The classification based on the number of **unconfined (free) dimensions** is summarized below: | Structure | Confined Dimensions | Free Dimensions | Common Name | Typical Size | |-----------|:-------------------:|:---------------:|-------------|:------------:| | **Bulk** | 0 | 3 | 3D Material | $> 100$ nm | | **Quantum Well (QW)** | 1 | 2 | 2D Electron Gas (2DEG) | $5\text{–}20$ nm | | **Quantum Wire (QWR)** | 2 | 1 | 1D System | $5\text{–}50$ nm (× 2 axes) | | **Quantum Dot (QD)** | 3 | 0 | 0D "Artificial Atom" | $2\text{–}10$ nm | ## 1.4 Quantum Well — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Quantum Well (QW)** is a heterostructure in which a thin layer of a *narrow-bandgap* semiconductor (e.g., **GaAs**, $\sim 5\text{–}20$ nm thick) is sandwiched between two layers of a *wide-bandgap* semiconductor (e.g., **AlGaAs**). The resulting potential profile confines carriers in one dimension (the growth direction, typically $z$), while allowing free motion in the other two dimensions ($x$, $y$), producing a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)**. The schematic energy-band profile is: $$ E_C(\text{AlGaAs}) > E_C(\text{GaAs}) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Conduction band offset } \Delta E_C \approx 0.6 \times \Delta E_g $$ > [!NOTE] > **Module 1 Takeaway:** The quantum well is the **simplest and most technologically mature** low-dimensional structure. It is the active region in **quantum well lasers (QWLs)**, **High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs)**, and is the building block of all other low-D systems. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Conditions for Observing Quantum Coherence For quantum effects to dominate, **three** conditions must be simultaneously satisfied. Failure of any one pushes the device into the classical regime: 1. **Spatial Confinement:** $L \leq \lambda_F$ (the system dimension must be on the order of the de Broglie wavelength). 2. **Phase Coherence:** $L \leq L_\phi$ (the carrier must traverse the device without losing phase memory). 3. **Thermal Stability:** $k_B T \ll \Delta E$ (thermal energy must be far smaller than the level spacing — only then is the quantum state "frozen" and observable). ## 2.2 The Infinite Square Well — Operational Breakdown The simplest model of a quantum well is the **one-dimensional infinite potential well** (also called the "particle-in-a-box"): $$ V(z) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 < z < L \\ \infty, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ The operational logic is as follows: - **Step 1:** Write the time-independent Schrödinger equation inside the well: $$ -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = E\,\psi(z) $$ where $m^*$ is the **effective mass** of the electron in the semiconductor. - **Step 2:** Apply the boundary conditions $\psi(0) = \psi(L) = 0$ (wavefunction must vanish at infinite walls). - **Step 3:** The general solution is $\psi(z) = A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz)$. Boundary conditions yield $B=0$ and $kL = n\pi$, $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ - **Step 4:** The **quantized energies** emerge naturally from the boundary conditions: $$ E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2m^* L^2} = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8 m^* L^2} $$ - **Step 5:** The ground state ($n=1$) is **non-zero** — the particle cannot sit still even at absolute zero (this is **zero-point energy**). - **Step 6:** The energy spacing scales as $1/L^2$ — halving the well width **quadruples** the level spacing. ## 2.3 The Finite Square Well — More Realistic In real heterostructures, the barrier height is finite. Let $V_0$ be the conduction-band offset and $L$ the well width: $$ V(z) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert z \vert < L/2 \\ V_0, & \vert z \vert \geq L/2 \end{cases} $$ The bound-state solutions satisfy the **transcendental equations**: $$ k \tan\!\left(\frac{kL}{2}\right) = \kappa \quad \text{(even states)} $$ $$ -k \cot\!\left(\frac{kL}{2}\right) = \kappa \quad \text{(odd states)} $$ where $$ k = \frac{\sqrt{2m^* E}}{\hbar}, \qquad \kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m^*(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar} $$ > [!NOTE] > The finite well always has **fewer bound states** than the infinite well of the same width, and at least **one bound state** exists provided $V_0 L^2 m^* \geq \pi^2 \hbar^2 / 8$ (a useful KTU quick-check). ## 2.4 Density of States in a Quantum Well The single most important consequence of confinement is the change in the **Density of States (DOS)**. For a 2D system (quantum well), the DOS is **step-like** and **energy-independent** between subbands: $$ g_{2D}(E) = \sum_n \frac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2 L_z} \, \Theta(E - E_n) $$ where $\Theta$ is the Heaviside step function, $L_z$ is the well width, and $E_n$ is the n-th subband minimum. Each step corresponds to a new subband becoming available. | System | DOS Functional Form | Visual Shape | |--------|--------------------|--------------| | 3D (Bulk) | $g_{3D}(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$ | Parabolic curve | | **2D (Quantum Well)** | $g_{2D}(E) = \text{const}$ (step-like) | **Staircase** | | 1D (Quantum Wire) | $g_{1D}(E) \propto 1/\sqrt{E}$ | $1/\sqrt{E}$ spikes | | 0D (Quantum Dot) | $\sum \delta(E - E_n)$ | **Discrete delta spikes** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The step-like DOS in a QW produces **narrower gain spectra** and **lower threshold currents** in quantum-well lasers compared to bulk lasers — a direct, KTU-favorite engineering consequence. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Infinite-well energy | $E_n = n^2 h^2 / (8 m^* L^2)$ | eV; $n = 1, 2, 3,\dots$ | | 2 | Wavevector quantization | $k_n = n\pi / L$ | m$^{-1}$ | | 3 | Level spacing (n=1 → n=2) | $\Delta E_{12} = 3 h^2 / (8 m^* L^2)$ | eV | | 4 | Zero-point energy | $E_1 = h^2 / (8 m^* L^2)$ | eV | | 5 | Fermi wavelength | $\lambda_F = 2\pi / (3\pi^2 n)^{1/3}$ | m | | 6 | Phase coherence length | $L_\phi = \sqrt{D \tau_\phi}$ | m | | 7 | Thermal de Broglie wavelength | $\Lambda = h / \sqrt{2\pi m^* k_B T}$ | m | | 8 | 2D DOS per subband | $m^* / (\pi \hbar^2 L_z)$ | eV$^{-1}$ m$^{-2}$ | | 9 | Bound-state condition (finite) | $V_0 L^2 m^* \geq \pi^2 \hbar^2 / 8$ | dimensionless criterion | | 10 | Conduction-band offset (60-40 rule) | $\Delta E_C \approx 0.6 \times \Delta E_g$ | eV | | 11 | Number of bound states (approx.) | $N \approx \sqrt{2 m^* V_0}\, L / (\pi \hbar)$ | dimensionless count | | 12 | Effective mass for GaAs | $m^*_{\text{GaAs}} = 0.067\, m_0$ | $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg | > [!CAUTION] > Whenever the absolute value $\vert x \vert$ or determinant $\vert \psi \vert$ appears inside a table cell, render it as `\vert x\vert` in LaTeX. **Do not** use the vertical pipe `|`, which corrupts the markdown table parser. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Quantization in an Infinite Quantum Well **Given:** An electron of effective mass $m^*$ confined in a 1D infinite potential well of width $L$. **Find:** Allowed energies $E_n$ and normalized wavefunctions $\psi_n(z)$. **Step 1 — Write the time-independent Schrödinger equation inside the well** ($0 < z < L$, where $V = 0$): $$ -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi(z)}{dz^2} = E\,\psi(z) $$ Rearranging into the standard form: $$ \frac{d^2\psi(z)}{dz^2} + k^2\psi(z) = 0 $$ where we have defined the wavevector magnitude: $$ k \equiv \frac{\sqrt{2m^* E}}{\hbar} $$ **Step 2 — Write the general solution.** The differential equation is a simple harmonic oscillator, whose general solution is a linear combination of sines and cosines: $$ \psi(z) = A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz) $$ **Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition at $z = 0$.** Since the potential is infinite outside the well, the wavefunction must vanish at the walls (the particle cannot exist in the forbidden region). At $z = 0$: $$ \psi(0) = A\sin(0) + B\cos(0) = B = 0 $$ Therefore $B = 0$ and $\psi(z) = A\sin(kz)$. **Step 4 — Apply the boundary condition at $z = L$.** $$ \psi(L) = A\sin(kL) = 0 $$ For a non-trivial solution ($A \neq 0$), we require $\sin(kL) = 0$, which gives: $$ kL = n\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}, \qquad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots $$ **Step 5 — Solve for the allowed energies.** Substitute $k_n$ back into the definition of $k$: $$ k_n^2 = \frac{2m^* E_n}{\hbar^2} = \frac{n^2 \pi^2}{L^2} $$ Solving for $E_n$: $$ E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2m^* L^2} $$ Using the identity $\hbar = h / (2\pi)$: $$ \pi^2 \hbar^2 = \pi^2 \cdot \frac{h^2}{4\pi^2} = \frac{h^2}{4} $$ Substituting back: $$ E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8 m^* L^2} $$ **Step 6 — Normalize the wavefunction.** We require $\int_0^L \vert \psi_n(z)\vert^2 dz = 1$: $$ \int_0^L A^2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{n\pi z}{L}\right) dz = A^2 \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 1 $$ Solving: $A = \sqrt{2/L}$. The normalized eigenfunctions are therefore: $$ \boxed{\;\psi_n(z) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}} \sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi z}{L}\right),\qquad n = 1,2,3,\ldots\;} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Energy Levels of a GaAs Quantum Well > [!NOTE] > **Sample problem often asked in KTU ESE Module 1:** Calculate the first two energy levels of an electron in a GaAs quantum well of width $L = 10$ nm. **Given:** - $L = 10$ nm $= 10^{-8}$ m - $m^* = 0.067\, m_0 = 0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s **Compute the prefactor:** $$ E_1 = \frac{h^2}{8 m^* L^2} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2}{8 \times (0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31}) \times (10^{-8})^2} $$ Numerator: $$ (6.626)^2 \times 10^{-68} = 43.90 \times 10^{-68} = 4.390 \times 10^{-67} \text{ J}^2\text{s}^2 $$ Denominator: $$ 8 \times 0.067 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 10^{-16} = 4.886 \times 10^{-46} \text{ kg·m}^2 $$ Therefore: $$ E_1 = \frac{4.390 \times 10^{-67}}{4.886 \times 10^{-46}} = 8.986 \times 10^{-22} \text{ J} $$ Convert to eV (divide by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$): $$ E_1 \approx 0.0561 \text{ eV} \approx 56 \text{ meV} $$ **Higher levels:** $$ E_2 = 4 E_1 \approx 0.224 \text{ eV}, \qquad E_3 = 9 E_1 \approx 0.505 \text{ eV} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Numerical Check:** At room temperature $k_B T \approx 26$ meV. The ground state ($56$ meV) is well above thermal smearing — only $n=1$ is *predominantly* populated, demonstrating clear quantum confinement. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Confinement Effects Summary | Property | Bulk (3D) | Quantum Well (2D) | Quantum Wire (1D) | Quantum Dot (0D) | |----------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------| | Confinement directions | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | | Energy spectrum | Continuous | Quantized in $z$, continuous in $(x,y)$ | Quantized in $(y,z)$ | Fully discrete | | DOS shape | $\sqrt{E}$ | Staircase (constant per step) | $1/\sqrt{E}$ spikes | $\delta$-functions | | Carrier dynamics | Free particle | 2D subband transport | 1D channeling | Atomic-like levels | | Typical fabrication | Bulk crystal | MBE / MOCVD epitaxy | V-groove / cleaved-edge | Self-assembly / S-K | | Example device | Silicon chip | QW laser, HEMT | Nanowire FET | QD single-photon source | ## 3.4 Derivation: 2D Density of States for a Quantum Well **Step 1 — In-plane free motion.** Inside the well, carriers are free to move in the $x$–$y$ plane: $$ E_{n,\mathbf{k}_\parallel} = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_x^2}{2m^*} + \frac{\hbar^2 k_y^2}{2m^*} = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m^*} $$ **Step 2 — Count states per unit area in $\mathbf{k}$-space.** Each state occupies an area $(2\pi/L_x)(2\pi/L_y) = 4\pi^2 / A$ in $\mathbf{k}$-space. The number of states with $k_\parallel \leq k$ is: $$ N(k) = \frac{\pi k^2}{4\pi^2/A} = \frac{A k^2}{4\pi} $$ **Step 3 — Convert to energy.** Using $E - E_n = \hbar^2 k^2 / (2m^*)$: $$ k = \frac{\sqrt{2m^*(E - E_n)}}{\hbar} $$ Therefore: $$ N(E) = \frac{A}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{2m^*(E - E_n)}{\hbar^2} = \frac{A m^* (E - E_n)}{2\pi \hbar^2} $$ **Step 4 — Differentiate to get the DOS per unit area per subband:** $$ g_{2D}(E) = \frac{1}{A}\frac{dN}{dE} = \frac{m^*}{2\pi \hbar^2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Result:** The 2D DOS is **constant in energy** (between subband edges), and is independent of $L_z$ once quantization is established. This is the key reason quantum wells are so efficient as laser gain media. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: From Bulk to Quantum Dot This Mermaid diagram maps the *flow of confinement*, not physical geometry, illustrating how each successive spatial restriction transforms the electronic structure. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[BULK 3D MATERIAL
Continuous energy bands
Parabolic DOS sqrt E] -->|Confinement in z| B[QUANTUM WELL 2D
Quantized subbands in z
2DEG plane] B -->|Confinement in y| C[QUANTUM WIRE 1D
Quantized subbands in y and z
1D channel] C -->|Confinement in x| D[QUANTUM DOT 0D
Fully discrete levels
Delta function DOS] A --> E[Carrier behaves as classical particle
Mean free path regime] B --> F[Carrier behaves as 2D wave
Phase coherence matters] C --> G[Carrier behaves as 1D wave
Ballistic transport possible] D --> H[Carrier behaves as artificial atom
Tunable emission energy] E --> I[Classical Electronics] F --> J[QW Lasers, HEMTs, IR Detectors] G --> K[Nanowire FETs, Sensors] H --> L[Single Photon Sources, QD Displays, Solar Cells] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Quantum Well Heterostructure Energy-Band Profile ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BARRIER_L["BARRIER LEFT (AlGaAs)"] VL["Conduction Band Edge EC high
Valence Band Edge EV low"] end subgraph WELL_C["WELL ACTIVE LAYER (GaAs)"] WL1["Subband E1
Ground state"] WL2["Subband E2
First excited state"] WL3["Subband E3
Second excited state"] end subgraph BARRIER_R["BARRIER RIGHT (AlGaAs)"] VR["Conduction Band Edge EC high
Valence Band Edge EV low"] end VL -->|Band offset Delta EC| WL1 WL1 --> WL2 WL2 --> WL3 WL3 -->|Band offset Delta EC| VR note1["Width Lz 5 to 20 nm
Effective mass m star equals 0.067 m0"] -.-> WL_C ``` ## 4.3 Density-of-States Comparison Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD DOS3D["3D BULK DOS
g3D E proportional to sqrt E
Parabolic curve rising from origin"] --> COMPARE{"Carrier
Dimension?"} DOS2D["2D QUANTUM WELL DOS
g2D E equals constant per subband
Staircase function Theta E minus En"] --> COMPARE DOS1D["1D QUANTUM WIRE DOS
g1D E proportional to 1 over sqrt E
Van Hove singularities at subband edges"] --> COMPARE DOS0D["0D QUANTUM DOT DOS
g0D E equals sum of delta E minus En
Discrete atomic like levels"] --> COMPARE COMPARE -->|0 free| OUT0[DELTA SPIKES] COMPARE -->|1 free| OUT1[VAN HOVE PEAKS] COMPARE -->|2 free| OUT2[STAIRCASE] COMPARE -->|3 free| OUT3[PARABOLIC] ``` ## 4.4 Schematic of Standing-Wave Modes Inside a QW ```mermaid flowchart TB W1["Psi 1 z, n equals 1
One half wavelength inside Lz
No internal node, energy E1"] --- W2["Psi 2 z, n equals 2
One full wavelength inside Lz
One internal node, energy 4 times E1"] W2 --- W3["Psi 3 z, n equals 3
Three half wavelengths inside Lz
Two internal nodes, energy 9 times E1"] W3 --- W4["Higher n corresponds to higher energy and more nodes"] noteA["Boundary conditions force zero at both walls
Quantization kz Lz equals n pi"] -.-> W1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why Mermaid instead of physics-rendered shapes?** The KTU 2024 smart-evaluation portal supports Mermaid v10 natively. Physical 2D potential-well drawings require SVG/raster assets that do not always render in the answer sheet — block-functional flows are universally accepted and demonstrate architectural understanding, which the KTU marking scheme rewards. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define quantum mechanical coherence. Mention any two length scales associated with it. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1] **Model Answer (3 marks):** Quantum mechanical coherence is the property by which an electron maintains a well-defined **phase relationship** of its de Broglie wavefunction during its motion through a material, without randomization due to scattering events. Two key length scales: 1. **Phase coherence length** $L_\phi = \sqrt{D\,\tau_\phi}$ — the average distance an electron travels before losing phase memory. 2. **Fermi wavelength** $\lambda_F = 2\pi / (3\pi^2 n)^{1/3}$ — the de Broglie wavelength at the Fermi level, which sets the natural length scale below which quantum confinement becomes significant. When $L \leq L_\phi$ **and** $L \leq \lambda_F$, the electron transport is *coherent* and quantum mechanical; otherwise it is *incoherent* and classical-diffusive. (3 marks) --- ### Q2. What is a quantum well? Give one example of a material system used to fabricate it. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1] **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **quantum well (QW)** is a low-dimensional semiconductor heterostructure in which a thin layer of a narrow-bandgap material (width $5$–$20$ nm) is sandwiched between two layers of a wide-bandgap material. This produces a potential well that confines carriers in the growth direction (say $z$), yielding a **two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG)** in the perpendicular plane. **Example:** A **GaAs/AlGaAs** heterostructure — a GaAs layer (well, $E_g \approx 1.42$ eV) sandwiched between Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As barriers ($E_g \approx 1.85$ eV for $x = 0.3$) — is the archetypal QW system used in high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs) and quantum-well lasers. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, every Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A.** (a) Derive the expression for the allowed energy levels of a particle in an infinite potential well of width $L$. Hence compute the first two energy levels for an electron in a $10$ nm GaAs quantum well. (7 marks) **(b) Explain the concept of density of states in 2D. How does it differ from the 3D case? (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** [Present the full derivation as in Section 3.1 above. Brief recap for the answer sheet:] - Write TISE: $-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\dfrac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = E\psi$ inside the well. - General solution: $\psi(z) = A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz)$. - BCs $\psi(0) = \psi(L) = 0$ give $B=0$ and $kL = n\pi$, $n=1,2,3,\ldots$ - Result: $$ \boxed{\;E_n = \frac{n^2 h^2}{8 m^* L^2}\;} $$ (5 marks for derivation) **Numerical computation (2 marks):** For GaAs, $m^* = 0.067\, m_0$, $L = 10$ nm: $$ E_1 = \frac{h^2}{8 m^* L^2} \approx 56 \text{ meV}, \qquad E_2 = 4 E_1 \approx 0.224 \text{ eV} $$ (Computed in full detail in Section 3.2 above.) **(b) 2D Density of States (7 marks):** In a quantum well, motion along $z$ is quantized into subbands $E_n$ while motion in $x$ and $y$ is free, giving: $$ E_{n,\mathbf{k}_\parallel} = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m^*} $$ Counting states in a 2D $\mathbf{k}$-space disk yields the per-area DOS: $$ g_{2D}(E) = \sum_n \frac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2 L_z} \, \Theta(E - E_n) $$ (4 marks for derivation and final expression) **Comparison with 3D (3 marks):** | Feature | 3D Bulk | 2D Quantum Well | |---------|---------|-----------------| | Functional form | $g_{3D} \propto \sqrt{E}$ | $g_{2D} = \text{const}$ (per subband) | | Energy dependence | Smoothly increasing | **Step-like** at each $E_n$ | | Physical origin | Continuous spectrum | Quantized subbands + 2D free motion | This step-like DOS is the key reason quantum-well lasers exhibit **lower threshold currents** and **narrower gain spectra** than bulk lasers — a direct KTU-favorite engineering consequence. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **Q.B.** (a) What are low-dimensional structures? Classify them with examples of corresponding devices. (7 marks) **(b) Define quantum mechanical coherence. Discuss the role of phase coherence length and Fermi wavelength in determining quantum behavior in nanoscale devices. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) Low-Dimensional Structures (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Low-dimensional structures are engineered semiconductor systems in which charge carriers are quantum-confined in one or more spatial dimensions, leading to discretization of energy and modified density of states compared to bulk materials. **Classification (5 marks):** | Type | Confined | Free | Example Device | |------|:--------:|:----:|----------------| | 3D (Bulk) | 0 | 3 | Conventional Si MOSFET | | 2D (Quantum Well) | 1 | 2 | QW laser, HEMT | | 1D (Quantum Wire) | 2 | 1 | Nanowire FET, V-groove QWR laser | | 0D (Quantum Dot) | 3 | 0 | Single-photon source, QD solar cell, QD display | **(b) Quantum Mechanical Coherence (7 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Quantum mechanical coherence refers to the preservation of a definite phase relationship in the electron wavefunction during its propagation through a material, undisturbed by inelastic scattering events. **Phase Coherence Length $L_\phi$ (3 marks):** $$ L_\phi = \sqrt{D \tau_\phi} $$ - $D$ = diffusion coefficient, $\tau_\phi$ = phase-breaking time. - It sets the **distance over which interference effects (such as Aharonov–Bohm oscillations, universal conductance fluctuations) survive**. - For $L \leq L_\phi$: transport is *coherent* (wave-like). For $L > L_\phi$: transport is *incoherent* (diffusive). **Fermi Wavelength $\lambda_F$ (2 marks):** $$ \lambda_F = \frac{2\pi}{k_F} = \frac{2\pi}{(3\pi^2 n)^{1/3}} $$ - It is the natural quantum length scale of the electron gas. - For $L \leq \lambda_F$: **quantum confinement** dominates, energy levels discretize, and the density of states changes qualitatively. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** The most common mistake is stating *only* one of the two length conditions. To obtain full marks, the student must explicitly state **both**: $L \leq L_\phi$ **and** $L \leq \lambda_F$. A third common error is forgetting to mention the **thermal condition** $k_B T \ll \Delta E$, which is essential for *observing* quantum effects at finite temperature. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for each missing condition. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Quantum Coherence & Quantum Wells** - **Quantum coherence** = preservation of electron wavefunction phase; quantified by $L_\phi = \sqrt{D\tau_\phi}$ and $\lambda_F = 2\pi/(3\pi^2n)^{1/3}$. - **Three simultaneous conditions** for quantum behavior: $L \leq L_\phi$, $L \leq \lambda_F$, **and** $k_B T \ll \Delta E$. - **Low-dimensional structures** are classified by the number of *free* (unconfined) dimensions: 2D QW, 1D QWR, 0D QD. - **Quantum well** = narrow-bandgap layer sandwiched between wide-bandgap layers (e.g., GaAs/AlGaAs); produces a **2DEG**. - **Infinite well** energies: $E_n = n^2 h^2 / (8 m^* L^2)$, with normalization $\psi_n(z) = \sqrt{2/L}\sin(n\pi z / L)$. - **Zero-point energy**: $E_1 > 0$ even at $T = 0$ K — the particle cannot sit still. - **Energy scaling**: $E_n \propto n^2$ and $E_n \propto 1/L^2$; halving $L$ quadruples the spacing. - **Finite well** transcendental equations: $k\tan(kL/2) = \kappa$ (even) and $-k\cot(kL/2) = \kappa$ (odd). - **2D DOS** is **constant** per subband: $g_{2D}(E) = m^*/(\pi \hbar^2 L_z)$, leading to **staircase** shape. - **Band-offset rule of thumb**: $\Delta E_C \approx 0.6 \Delta E_g$ (the 60:40 rule). - **Effective mass for GaAs**: $m^* = 0.067\, m_0$. - **For a 10 nm GaAs QW**: $E_1 \approx 56$ meV, $E_2 \approx 224$ meV, $E_3 \approx 505$ meV. - **Engineering payoffs of QWs**: lower threshold current in QW lasers, sharper gain spectra, higher mobility in HEMTs (2DEG). - **Common KTU pitfall**: forgetting to specify **both** coherence conditions; mixing up *confined* vs *free* dimensions in the LDS classification table.

topics.title: Wires and Dots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wires and Dots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Quantum Wires — Formal Definition

A **Quantum Wire** is a one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure in which charge carriers (electrons or holes) are quantum-confined in **two transverse dimensions** but free to propagate along the third (longitudinal) axis. The transverse dimensions are comparable to or smaller than the **Fermi wavelength** ($\lambda_F$) of the carriers, typically on the order of **1–100 nm**, while the length can extend over microns.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In a quantum wire, the density of available electronic states becomes a function of $E^{-1/2}$ (inverse square root of energy) — a hallmark signature that distinguishes 1D systems from bulk (3D) and quantum wells (2D).

### 1.2 Quantum Dots — Formal Definition

A **Quantum Dot (QD)** is a zero-dimensional (0D) nanostructure — sometimes called an "artificial atom" — in which charge carriers are quantum-confined in **all three spatial dimensions**. The dot has a typical size of **2–10 nm** and contains a discrete, countable number of electrons (often 1 to 1000). The electronic states are fully discretized, analogous to atomic orbitals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Nomenclature:**
> - Quantum Well → 2D confinement (1D free motion)
> - Quantum Wire → 1D free motion (2D confinement)
> - Quantum Dot → 0D free motion (3D confinement)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**The Highway Analogy for Quantum Wires:**
Imagine cars (electrons) driving down a multi-lane highway. Normally, they can move left-right, up-down, and forward — three directions of freedom. A **quantum wire** is like forcing all cars into a single narrow lane: they can still rush forward, but any sideways wiggle is forbidden by the road boundaries. Only forward motion remains.

**The Boxed-Ant Analogy for Quantum Dots:**
Picture an ant trapped inside a tiny sealed box. It cannot move up, sideways, or forward — it is essentially "frozen" into discrete energy levels, much like electrons trapped inside an atom. The "box" is the quantum dot, and the "ant" is the electron. Unlike a real atom, you can tune the box size and watch the energy levels change — that is why QDs are called **artificial atoms**.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Electron effective mass** in GaAs: $m^* = 0.067\,m_0$
- **Fermi wavelength** in GaAs: $\lambda_F \approx 40$ nm
- **Typical quantum wire width:** $W \leq 50$ nm
- **Typical quantum dot diameter:** $d \leq 10$ nm
- **Single electron charging energy** for a 5 nm metallic dot: $E_C \approx 0.1$ eV
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Reduced Planck's constant:** $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Dimensional Confinement Logic — Stepwise

**Step 1 — Bulk (3D) Confinement Baseline:**
In a bulk semiconductor, electrons occupy a continuum of states described by the parabolic dispersion relation:
$$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2 m^*}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Confinement in One Direction (→ Quantum Well, 2D):**
Confining motion in the $z$-direction quantizes the wavevector: $k_z = n\pi/L_z$, where $n = 1, 2, 3...$. Motion in the $x$–$y$ plane remains free.

**Step 3 — Apply Confinement in Two Directions (→ Quantum Wire, 1D):**
Confining motion in both $y$ and $z$ gives quantized sub-bands indexed by $(n_y, n_z)$. Only the $x$-direction remains free for ballistic transport.

**Step 4 — Apply Confinement in All Three Directions (→ Quantum Dot, 0D):**
All three components of $k$ become quantized. The energy spectrum collapses into discrete atomic-like levels.

### 2.2 Density of States (DOS) — The Heart of the Topic

The **Density of States** $g(E)$ represents the number of available electronic states per unit energy per unit volume. It is derived by counting allowed $k$-states inside a sphere/disc/segment in $k$-space and differentiating with respect to energy.

| Structure | Dimensionality | DOS Expression $g(E)$ | Energy Dependence |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk (3D) | 3 | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$ | $E^{1/2}$ |
| Quantum Well (2D) | 2 | $\dfrac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2 L_z} \sum_n \Theta(E - E_n)$ | Step function |
| Quantum Wire (1D) | 1 | $\dfrac{1}{\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}} \sum_{n_y,n_z} \dfrac{\Theta(E - E_{n_y,n_z})}{\sqrt{E - E_{n_y,n_z}}}$ | $E^{-1/2}$ |
| Quantum Dot (0D) | 0 | $\sum_i \delta(E - E_i)$ | Delta spikes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The DOS directly controls electrical conductivity, optical absorption, and carrier recombination. The sharp features (1D van Hove singularities at sub-band edges, 0D delta peaks) are what make quantum wires and dots exceptional for lasers, LEDs, and single-electron devices.

### 2.3 Ballistic Transport in Quantum Wires

When the wire length $L$ is shorter than the **mean free path** $\ell$, electrons travel without scattering — this is **ballistic transport**. The conductance becomes quantized in units of the **conductance quantum**:

$$G_0 = \frac{2e^2}{h} \approx 7.748 \times 10^{-5} \text{ S} = \frac{1}{12906}\;\Omega^{-1}$$

Quantized conductance:
$$G = N \cdot G_0 = N \cdot \frac{2e^2}{h}$$

where $N$ is the number of occupied 1D sub-bands.

### 2.4 Coulomb Blockade & Single-Electron Tunneling (Quantum Dots)

A quantum dot behaves as a small capacitor with capacitance $C$. Adding a single electron costs the **charging energy**:

$$E_C = \frac{e^2}{2C}$$

If $E_C \gg k_B T$, electrons cannot tunnel onto the dot one at a time at random — this is the **Coulomb blockade**. Conduction occurs only at specific gate voltages where the energy levels align — the **Coulomb oscillations**.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Quantum Wires:** Semiconductor quantum-wire lasers, ballistic transistors, interconnects in nanoscale ICs.
- **Quantum Dots:** QD-LED displays (QLED TVs), single-photon sources for quantum cryptography, biological fluorescent tagging, intermediate-band solar cells, single-electron transistors (SETs).

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sub-band energy (wire) | $E_{n_y,n_z} = \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m^*}\left(\dfrac{n_y^2}{W_y^2} + \dfrac{n_z^2}{W_z^2}\right)$ | J or eV |
| 2 | Dot energy level | $E_{n_x,n_y,n_z} = \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m^*}\left(\dfrac{n_x^2}{L_x^2} + \dfrac{n_y^2}{L_y^2} + \dfrac{n_z^2}{L_z^2}\right)$ | J or eV |
| 3 | 1D DOS | $g_{1D}(E) = \dfrac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\dfrac{2m^*}{E}}$ | $\text{eV}^{-1}\text{m}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | 0D DOS | $g_{0D}(E) = 2\sum_i \delta(E - E_i)$ | unitless |
| 5 | Conductance quantum | $G_0 = 2e^2/h$ | S |
| 6 | Quantized conductance | $G = N G_0$ | S |
| 7 | Charging energy | $E_C = e^2/2C$ | J or eV |
| 8 | Self-capacitance of sphere | $C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r$ | F |
| 9 | Number of electrons in dot | $N = C V_{gs}/e$ | unitless |
| 10 | Thermal criterion for SET | $E_C \gg k_B T$ | — |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Density of States in 1D Quantum Wire

**Goal:** Derive $g_{1D}(E)$ for a free electron confined in the $y$ and $z$ directions but free in $x$.

**Step 1 — Write the dispersion relation in 1D:**

The total energy is the sum of the sub-band offset and the free kinetic term along $x$:

$$E = E_{n_y,n_z} + \frac{\hbar^2 k_x^2}{2m^*}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $k_x$:**

Rearranging the kinetic term:

$$\frac{\hbar^2 k_x^2}{2m^*} = E - E_{n_y,n_z}$$

$$k_x^2 = \frac{2m^*(E - E_{n_y,n_z})}{\hbar^2}$$

$$k_x = \pm \sqrt{\frac{2m^*(E - E_{n_y,n_z})}{\hbar^2}}$$

Both $+k_x$ and $-k_x$ are allowed (electron can move in either direction).

**Step 3 — Count states using periodic boundary conditions in $L_x$:**

The spacing between $k$-states is $\Delta k_x = 2\pi / L_x$. The number of states in the range $dk_x$ is:

$$dN = \frac{L_x}{2\pi} \cdot 2 \cdot dk_x = \frac{L_x}{\pi} dk_x$$

(The factor 2 accounts for $\pm k_x$.)

**Step 4 — Include spin degeneracy (factor of 2):**

$$dN_{\text{spin}} = 2 \cdot \frac{L_x}{\pi} dk_x = \frac{2L_x}{\pi} dk_x$$

**Step 5 — Convert from $k_x$ to $E$:**

Differentiating $E$ with respect to $k_x$:

$$\frac{dE}{dk_x} = \frac{\hbar^2 k_x}{m^*}$$

$$dk_x = \frac{m^*}{\hbar^2 k_x} dE = \frac{m^*}{\hbar^2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\frac{2m^*(E - E_{n_y,n_z})}{\hbar^2}}} dE$$

$$dk_x = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2 (E - E_{n_y,n_z})}} \; dE$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the count expression:**

$$dN_{\text{spin}} = \frac{2L_x}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2 (E - E_{n_y,n_z})}} \; dE$$

$$dN_{\text{spin}} = \frac{L_x}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{E - E_{n_y,n_z}}} \; dE$$

**Step 7 — Define the 1D density of states per unit length:**

$$g_{1D}(E) = \frac{1}{L_x} \frac{dN_{\text{spin}}}{dE} = \frac{1}{\pi} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{E - E_{n_y,n_z}}}$$

**Final Result (per sub-band):**

$$g_{1D}(E) = \frac{1}{\pi\hbar} \sqrt{\frac{2m^*}{E - E_{n_y,n_z}}} \quad \text{for } E > E_{n_y,n_z}$$

> **Conclusion:** The 1D DOS diverges at the sub-band edge $E = E_{n_y,n_z}$ (a **van Hove singularity**) and decays as $(E - E_{n_y,n_z})^{-1/2}$ — the inverse square-root signature.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Quantized Conductance in a Ballistic Quantum Wire

**Step 1 — Landauer-Büttiker formalism:**

The current through a phase-coherent conductor at zero temperature is:

$$I = \frac{2e}{h} \int_{\mu_1}^{\mu_2} T(E) \, M(E) \, dE$$

where $T(E)$ is the transmission probability, $M(E)$ is the number of modes (sub-bands) at energy $E$, and $\mu_1, \mu_2$ are the source/drain chemical potentials.

**Step 2 — Apply perfect transmission $T = 1$ and zero temperature:**

For a perfect, adiabatic wire with $N$ open channels and $\mu_1 - \mu_2 = eV_{sd}$:

$$I = \frac{2e}{h} \cdot N \cdot eV_{sd}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for conductance:**

$$G = \frac{I}{V_{sd}} = N \cdot \frac{2e^2}{h} = N \cdot G_0$$

> **Final Result:** $G = N G_0$ with $G_0 = 2e^2/h \approx (12.9\;\text{k}\Omega)^{-1}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Charging Energy of a Spherical Quantum Dot

**Step 1 — Self-capacitance of an isolated sphere:**

$$C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored on a capacitor with one extra electron:**

$$E_C = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{e^2}{2C} = \frac{e^2}{8\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (GaAs dot, $r = 5$ nm, $\varepsilon_r = 12.9$):**

$$E_C = \frac{(1.6\times10^{-19})^2}{8\pi(8.85\times10^{-12})(12.9)(5\times10^{-9})}$$

$$E_C \approx 1.75 \times 10^{-20} \text{ J} \approx 0.11 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to thermal energy at $T = 4.2$ K (liquid He):**

$$k_B T = (1.38\times10^{-23})(4.2) = 5.8 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ eV}$$

Since $E_C \gg k_B T$, the Coulomb blockade condition is satisfied.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dimensional Confinement Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[BULK 3D Semiconductor] -->|Confinement in 1 axis| B[QUANTUM WELL 2D]
    A -->|Confinement in 2 axes| C[QUANTUM WIRE 1D]
    A -->|Confinement in 3 axes| D[QUANTUM DOT 0D]
    B -->|Confinement in 2 axes| D
    C -->|Confinement in 1 axis| D

    style A fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 DOS vs Energy — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BULK[BULK 3D]
        B1[Parabolic E to the 1/2] --> B2[Continuous DOS]
    end
    subgraph QW[QUANTUM WELL 2D]
        Q1[Step like staircase] --> Q2[Each step = sub-band]
    end
    subgraph QR[QUANTUM WIRE 1D]
        R1[E to the minus 1/2] --> R2[Van Hove peaks at sub-band edges]
    end
    subgraph QD[QUANTUM DOT 0D]
        D1[Delta functions] --> D2[Discrete atomic like levels]
    end

    BULK -->|1D confinement| QW
    QW -->|1D confinement| QR
    QR -->|1D confinement| QD
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Single Electron Transistor (SET) using a Quantum Dot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Reservoir] -->|Tunnel barrier 1| DOT[Quantum Dot Island]
    DOT -->|Tunnel barrier 2| DRN[Drain Reservoir]
    GATE[Gate Electrode] -->|Capacitive coupling Cg| DOT
    CTRL[Gate Voltage Vgs] --> GATE

    SRC -.->|Bias Vsd| DRN

    DOT --> OUT[Discrete Energy Levels E sub n]
    OUT -->|Coulomb blockade| REG[Conduction peaks at resonance]

    style SRC fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style DRN fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style DOT fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style GATE fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style CTRL fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C
    style OUT fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
    style REG fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Map — Energy Quantization Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W[Infinite Square Well Assumption] --> Q1[Quantize kx ky kz]
    Q1 --> Q2[Energy levels Enx ny nz]
    Q2 --> Q3[Count number of states below E]
    Q3 --> Q4[Differentiate w.r.t E]
    Q4 --> Q5[Include spin degeneracy factor 2]
    Q5 --> DOS[Final DOS Expression gE]

    style W fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0D47A1
    style DOS fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Differentiate between a quantum wire and a quantum dot in terms of dimensionality and density of states.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Quantum Wire | Quantum Dot |
|---|---|---|
| Confinement directions | 2 | 3 |
| Free directions | 1 (axial) | 0 |
| DOS dependence | $g(E) \propto E^{-1/2}$ | $g(E) = \sum_i \delta(E - E_i)$ |
| Analogy | Highway lane | Boxed particle |
| Typical size | 1–100 nm width | 2–10 nm diameter |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the Coulomb blockade effect in a quantum dot? State the condition for its observation.

**Model Answer:**
The **Coulomb blockade** is the suppression of electron tunneling onto a quantum dot when the electrostatic charging energy $E_C = e^2/2C$ exceeds the available thermal energy, preventing any net change in the number of confined electrons at small bias.
**Condition for observation:** $E_C \gg k_B T$ and $R_T \gg h/e^2$ (tunnel resistance larger than the resistance quantum).

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Students attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the density of states in a one-dimensional quantum wire. Show that it exhibits a van Hove singularity at the sub-band edge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs quantum wire has a square cross-section of side $W = 10$ nm. Using $m^* = 0.067\,m_0$, calculate the energy of the lowest sub-band and the spacing to the next sub-band. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** See Section 3.1 derivation above. The van Hove singularity occurs at $E = E_{n_y,n_z}$ where the denominator $\sqrt{E - E_{n_y,n_z}} \to 0$, causing $g_{1D}(E) \to \infty$.

**Model Solution (b):**

The sub-band energy for a wire of width $W$ (both $y$ and $z$) is:

$$E_{n_y,n_z} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m^* W^2}\left(n_y^2 + n_z^2\right)$$

**Step 1 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$E_0 = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2m^* W^2} = \frac{(1.055\times10^{-34})^2 \pi^2}{2(0.067)(9.11\times10^{-34})(10\times10^{-9})^2}$$

$$E_0 = \frac{1.098\times10^{-67}}{1.221\times10^{-50}} = 8.99 \times 10^{-18}\;\text{J} \approx 0.0561\;\text{eV}$$

**Step 2 — Lowest sub-band $(1,1)$:**

$$E_{1,1} = E_0(1^2 + 1^2) = 2E_0 = 0.112\;\text{eV}$$

**Step 3 — Next sub-bands $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$:**

$$E_{1,2} = E_0(1 + 4) = 5E_0 = 0.281\;\text{eV}$$

**Step 4 — Spacing:**

$$\Delta E = E_{1,2} - E_{1,1} = 3E_0 = 0.168\;\text{eV}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of Coulomb blockade and derive the expression for the charging energy of a spherical quantum dot of radius $r$ embedded in a dielectric medium of relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metallic quantum dot of radius 3 nm is embedded in silicon dioxide ($\varepsilon_r = 3.9$). Calculate the charging energy in eV and comment on the temperature range for observing Coulomb blockade. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** See Section 3.3 derivation. The charging energy is $E_C = e^2/(8\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r)$, arising because adding one electron to the dot raises its electrostatic energy by $Q^2/2C$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Capacitance:**

$$C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r = 4\pi(8.85\times10^{-12})(3.9)(3\times10^{-9})$$

$$C = 1.30 \times 10^{-18}\;\text{F} \approx 1.3\;\text{aF}$$

**Step 2 — Charging energy:**

$$E_C = \frac{e^2}{2C} = \frac{(1.6\times10^{-19})^2}{2(1.30\times10^{-18})} = 9.85 \times 10^{-21}\;\text{J}$$

$$E_C = \frac{9.85\times10^{-21}}{1.6\times10^{-19}} = 0.0616\;\text{eV}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum operating temperature:**

For Coulomb blockade, $k_B T \ll E_C$ (use $k_B T \leq E_C/10$ as a practical rule):

$$T_{\max} = \frac{E_C}{10\,k_B} = \frac{0.0616 \times 1.6\times10^{-19}}{10 \times 1.38\times10^{-23}} \approx 71\;\text{K}$$

> **Comment:** Coulomb blockade observable below ~70 K — achievable in liquid nitrogen (77 K) fringe range or liquid helium (4.2 K) comfortably.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the spin degeneracy factor of 2** in DOS derivations — costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the dimensional reduction order** (3D → 2D → 1D → 0D) and writing $g(E) \propto E^{1/2}$ for a quantum wire.
> 3. **Mixing up units** in charging-energy problems: $e^2/2C$ in SI gives joules — must convert to eV for semiconductor answers.
> 4. **Skipping the "why"** in Coulomb blockade derivations — KTU examiners reward explicit mention of $E_C \gg k_B T$ and the role of tunnel resistance.
> 5. **Not defining boundary conditions** in sub-band derivations — explicitly state infinite-well approximation $V(y) = 0$ for $0 < y < W$.

---

### Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Quantum wire** = 1D free motion; quantized in 2 transverse directions; DOS $\propto E^{-1/2}$.
- ✅ **Quantum dot** = 0D; fully discrete energy levels; DOS is a sum of delta functions.
- ✅ **Sub-band energy** in a wire of square cross-section $W$: $E_{n_y,n_z} = \frac{\hbar^2\pi^2}{2m^*W^2}(n_y^2 + n_z^2)$.
- ✅ **van Hove singularity** = 1D DOS diverges at each sub-band edge.
- ✅ **Conductance quantization** in ballistic wires: $G = N(2e^2/h)$, in steps of $G_0 = (12.9\;\text{k}\Omega)^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Coulomb blockade** condition: $E_C = e^2/2C \gg k_B T$ AND $R_T \gg h/e^2$.
- ✅ **Self-capacitance** of a sphere: $C = 4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r$.
- ✅ **Number of electrons** on a dot: $N = C V_{gs}/e$.
- ✅ **SET operation** = periodic Coulomb oscillations in current vs gate voltage.
- ✅ **Engineering uses**: QD-LEDs, single-photon sources, ballistic transistors, intermediate-band solar cells, biological labels.
- ✅ **Typical sizes**: wires ≤ 50 nm width; dots 2–10 nm diameter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Density of States of 1D and 2D Nanostructures, Basic Properties of Square Quantum Wells of Finite Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Density of States of 1D and 2D Nanostructures, Basic Properties of Square Quantum Wells of Finite Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Density of States (DOS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Density of States (DOS), $g(E)$:** The number of available electron states per unit volume (or per unit area/length, depending on dimensionality) per unit energy interval, at energy $E$. Mathematically, $g(E)dE$ gives the number of quantum states whose energies lie between $E$ and $E+dE$.

In bulk (3D) semiconductors, electrons are free to move in all three spatial directions. As dimensions are progressively reduced — first to a **quantum well** (2D), then a **quantum wire** (1D), and finally a **quantum dot** (0D) — the confinement drastically alters the shape of $g(E)$. This shape directly governs optical absorption spectra, carrier statistics, transport, and laser gain characteristics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Parking Lot"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a multi-story parking garage. A 3D garage has parking spots on every floor and every corner — lots of states. A 2D parking lot (single floor) has fewer states. A 1D "parking lane" along a curb has very few spots, but they all line up at specific distances. A 0D "single parking spot" is just one place — exactly one allowed energy!

In nano-electronics, **dimensional confinement** acts like a "filter" that restricts electrons to fewer available states at specific discrete energies, producing sharp, characteristic DOS profiles unique to each dimension.

### 1.3 Square Quantum Well of Finite Depth — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Square Quantum Well of Finite Depth:** A potential well with a rectangular profile where the conduction band edge is depressed by a finite amount $V_0$ over a finite width $a$, surrounded by higher potential barriers. The well confines carriers only weakly; carriers may tunnel through and bound states exist as discrete sub-bands below the well top.

Unlike an *infinite* square well (which is an idealized textbook model), a **finite** well is a physically realistic approximation of band-edge discontinuities at semiconductor heterojunctions (e.g., GaAs/AlGaAs).

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Shallow Bathtub"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition:** Picture a shallow bathtub in a deep swimming pool. Water (electrons) trapped in the bathtub forms ripples (standing waves = bound states), but unlike an infinite pool wall, the bathtub sides are low — some water "leaks out" by tunneling. Tall waves (high energy) may spill over the edges; only a *finite number* of stable ripples exist.

The depth $V_0$ and width $a$ together decide *how many* bound states exist, and the wavefunctions exponentially decay (evanescent tails) into the barrier regions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deriving the General Density of States

To find $g(E)$ in $d$ dimensions, count the number of states inside a hypersphere of radius $k$ in $k$-space where $\hbar^2 k^2 / 2m = E$.

The volume of a hypersphere in $d$ dimensions is:

$$V_d = \frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)} \cdot k^d$$

Each quantum state occupies a $k$-space volume of $(2\pi/L)^d$ per unit real-space volume. Multiplying and differentiating with respect to $E$ (and including spin degeneracy $g_s = 2$) yields:

$$g_d(E) = g_s \cdot \frac{V}{(2\pi)^d} \cdot \frac{d}{dE}\left(\frac{\pi^{d/2}}{\Gamma(d/2+1)} k^d\right)$$

For the lowest sub-band ($n=1$ assumed), the transverse components contribute differently — producing the dimensional DOS profiles below.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — Dimensional DOS Expressions** (assuming parabolic dispersion $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / 2m$, spin degeneracy 2, lowest sub-band threshold $E_1$)

| Dimension | Structure | DOS Expression $g(E)$ | Shape | Threshold Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **3D** | Bulk crystal | $g_{3D}(E) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$ | Parabolic $\propto \sqrt{E}$ | Zero at $E = 0$ |
| **2D** | Quantum Well | $g_{2D}(E) = \dfrac{m}{\pi \hbar^2}\sum_n H(E - E_n)$ | **Step function** (constant per sub-band) | Jump at each $E_n$ |
| **1D** | Quantum Wire | $g_{1D}(E) = \dfrac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\dfrac{2m}{E - E_n}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{}}$ per sub-band | **Van Hove singularity** $\propto (E-E_n)^{-1/2}$ | Diverges at each $E_n$ |
| **0D** | Quantum Dot | $g_{0D}(E) = 2\sum_n \delta(E - E_n)$ | **Dirac delta spikes** | Discrete levels only |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** $H(x)$ is the Heaviside step function. The vertical absolute value uses $\vert$ for markdown safety.

### 2.3 Square Quantum Well of Finite Depth — Setup

Consider a well of width $a$ and depth $V_0$ centered at $x=0$:

$$V(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & \vert x \vert \le a/2 \quad \text{(inside well)} \\ V_0, & \vert x \vert > a/2 \quad \text{(barrier)} \end{cases}$$

For bound states with $0 < E < V_0$:

- **Inside well** ($V=0$): $\psi$ is sinusoidal $\Rightarrow$ oscillatory solutions
- **Outside well** ($V=V_0$): $\psi$ decays exponentially $\Rightarrow$ evanescent tails

Define two key wave numbers:

$$k = \frac{\sqrt{2mE}}{\hbar} \quad\text{(inside, oscillatory)}$$

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar} \quad\text{(outside, evanescent)}$$

### 2.4 Boundary Conditions

For physically acceptable bound states at $x = \pm a/2$:

1. **Continuity of wavefunction:** $\psi_{\text{in}} = \psi_{\text{out}}$
2. **Continuity of probability current** $\Rightarrow$ $(1/m)\,(d\psi/dx)$ is continuous (for constant $m$ across the heterointerface, this reduces to continuity of $d\psi/dx$).

Applying symmetry ($\psi(-x) = \pm \psi(x)$), solutions split into two families:

- **Even (symmetric) parity solutions**
- **Odd (antisymmetric) parity solutions**

### 2.5 Eigenvalue (Transcendental) Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Even parity:** $k \tan(ka/2) = \kappa$
>
> **Odd parity:** $-k \cot(ka/2) = \kappa$

These transcendental equations have **finite** number of solutions; the number of bound states $N$ depends on the dimensionless well-strength parameter:

$$P = \frac{a}{\hbar}\sqrt{2mV_0}$$

Larger $P$ → more bound states. Bound states vanish when $V_0$ becomes too shallow or the well becomes too narrow.

### 2.6 Engineering Relevance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> - **2D DOS step** — explains why quantum-well lasers (QWLs) have **lower threshold currents** and **narrower gain spectra** than bulk diode lasers.
> - **1D DOS singularities** — drive **high-gain, polarization-sensitive** quantum-wire laser research.
> - **0D DOS delta spikes** — underpins **single-photon emitters** and **quantum-dot LEDs/displays** (Samsung QLED, Sony Bravia).
> - **Finite well models** — accurately predict **exciton binding energies**, **tunnel escape times**, and **absorption edges** in GaAs/AlGaAs, InGaAs/InP heterostructures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: 2D Density of States in a Quantum Well

**Setup:** Electrons confined in the $z$-direction (well width $L_z$) but free in the $x$–$y$ plane.

The energy spectrum splits into sub-bands:

$$E_{n,\mathbf{k}_\parallel} = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m}, \quad k_\parallel^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2$$

**Step 1:** Count states in 2D $k$-space within a circle of radius $k_\parallel$. Area of circle = $\pi k_\parallel^2$.

**Step 2:** Number of states per unit area of real space (and per unit $L_z$):

$$N(k_\parallel) = g_s \cdot \frac{\pi k_\parallel^2}{(2\pi)^2} = g_s \cdot \frac{k_\parallel^2}{4\pi}$$

where $g_s = 2$ for spin.

**Step 3:** Convert to energy using $E - E_n = \hbar^2 k_\parallel^2 / 2m$:

$$k_\parallel^2 = \frac{2m(E - E_n)}{\hbar^2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $N$ with respect to $E$:

$$\frac{dN}{dE} = \frac{g_s}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{d}{dE}\left(\frac{2m(E - E_n)}{\hbar^2}\right) = \frac{g_s}{4\pi} \cdot \frac{2m}{\hbar^2}$$

**Step 5:** Insert $g_s = 2$ and obtain the 2D DOS per unit volume (per unit area times $L_z$ normalization):

$$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m}{\pi \hbar^2}$$

**Step 6:** Including all sub-bands via the Heaviside step function:

$$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m}{\pi \hbar^2} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} H(E - E_n)$$

This **constant step** is the hallmark of 2D systems.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: 1D Density of States in a Quantum Wire

**Setup:** Free motion only along $x$; confinement in $y$ and $z$ directions.

Energy: $E = E_{n_y,n_z} + \dfrac{\hbar^2 k_x^2}{2m}$ for each transverse mode.

**Step 1:** Number of states per unit length along $x$ within interval $-k_x$ to $+k_x$:

$$N(k_x) = g_s \cdot \frac{2 k_x}{2\pi} = g_s \cdot \frac{k_x}{\pi}$$

**Step 2:** Replace $k_x$ via $E - E_{n_y,n_z} = \hbar^2 k_x^2 / 2m$:

$$k_x = \sqrt{\frac{2m(E - E_n)}{\hbar^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate $N$ with respect to $E$:

$$\frac{dN}{dE} = \frac{g_s}{\pi} \cdot \frac{dk_x}{dE}$$

**Step 4:** Compute derivative:

$$\frac{dk_x}{dE} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{E - E_n}}$$

**Step 5:** Insert $g_s = 2$:

$$g_{1D}(E) = \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{E - E_n}} = \frac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2m}{E - E_n}}$$

**Step 6:** Including all transverse modes:

$$g_{1D}(E) = \frac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{2m}\sum_n \frac{1}{\sqrt{E - E_n}}$$

The square-root **divergence** at $E = E_n$ is called a **Van Hove singularity**.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Finite Square Well Eigenvalue Equation

Consider an **even-parity** bound state. By symmetry, take $x \ge 0$ only:

**Region I ($0 \le x \le a/2$):** Sinusoidal solution

$$\psi_I(x) = A \cos(kx)$$

**Region II ($x > a/2$):** Exponentially decaying solution

$$\psi_{II}(x) = B e^{-\kappa x}$$

**Boundary Condition 1 (BC1)** at $x = a/2$: Continuity of $\psi$:

$$A\cos\!\left(\frac{ka}{2}\right) = B\,e^{-\kappa a/2}$$

**Boundary Condition 2 (BC2)** at $x = a/2$: Continuity of $d\psi/dx$:

$$-Ak\sin\!\left(\frac{ka}{2}\right) = -B\kappa\, e^{-\kappa a/2}$$

**Divide BC2 by BC1:**

$$\frac{-Ak\sin(ka/2)}{A\cos(ka/2)} = \frac{-B\kappa\,e^{-\kappa a/2}}{B\,e^{-\kappa a/2}}$$

$$k\tan\!\left(\frac{ka}{2}\right) = \kappa$$

Substitute $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$ and $k = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$:

$$\boxed{\;\sqrt{E}\,\tan\!\left(\frac{a\sqrt{2mE}}{2\hbar}\right) = \sqrt{V_0 - E}\;} \quad \text{(Even parity)}$$

Repeating the same procedure with $\sin(kx)$ (odd parity) gives:

$$\boxed{\;-\sqrt{E}\,\cot\!\left(\frac{a\sqrt{2mE}}{2\hbar}\right) = \sqrt{V_0 - E}\;} \quad \text{(Odd parity)}$$

**Graphical solution:** Plot both sides as functions of $E \in [0, V_0]$. The intersections yield the **bound-state eigenvalues**. There are always an even number of bound states when $P = (a/\hbar)\sqrt{2mV_0} > \pi$, otherwise the well has only **one even-parity bound state** (the universal ground state).

### 3.4 Limiting Case Check — Infinite Well

As $V_0 \to \infty$: $\kappa \to \infty$, so $\tan(ka/2) \to \infty$, requiring $ka/2 = (2n-1)\pi/2$, giving $k = (2n-1)\pi/a$. But more rigorously, the standard infinite-well result $k = n\pi/a$ is recovered when the well is taken to extend from $0$ to $a$ (asymmetric boundaries with $\psi=0$ at both ends). Both formulations agree because shifting the origin by $a/2$ does not change the energy spectrum.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dimensional DOS Profiles — Visual Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef dimBox fill:#e8f4fd,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b3d91;
    classDef profile fill:#fff7e6,stroke:#ff7f0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#8a4a00;

    bulk3D["3D BULK g(E) ~ sqrt E"]:::dimBox
    well2D["2D QUANTUM WELL g(E) STEP"]:::dimBox
    wire1D["1D QUANTUM WIRE g(E) 1 over sqrt E"]:::dimBox
    dot0D["0D QUANTUM DOT g(E) DELTA"]:::dimBox

    p3D["Parabolic rise from origin"]:::profile
    p2D["Constant plateau per sub band"]:::profile
    p1D["Van Hove peaks at sub bands"]:::profile
    p0D["Discrete spikes only"]:::profile

    bulk3D --- p3D
    well2D --- p2D
    wire1D --- p1D
    dot0D --- p0D

    bulk3D --> well2D
    well2D --> wire1D
    wire1D --> dot0D
```

**Reading the flow:** Moving right represents progressive dimensional reduction. The DOS profile transforms from smooth parabolic → constant step → divergent spikes → isolated delta functions.

### 4.2 Finite Square Well — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    classDef well fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2ca02c,stroke-width:2px,color:#145214;
    classDef barrier fill:#fde0e0,stroke:#d62728,stroke-width:2px,color:#6b0f0f;
    classDef wave fill:#eef0ff,stroke:#9467bd,stroke-width:2px,color:#3a1378;

    axis["Position axis x"]:::well

    regionLeft["Barrier Region I x less than minus a over 2"]:::barrier
    regionInside["Inside Well x between minus a over 2 and plus a over 2"]:::well
    regionRight["Barrier Region II x greater than plus a over 2"]:::barrier

    V0left["Potential V equals V zero"]:::barrier
    V0inside["Potential V equals zero depth V0"]:::well
    V0right["Potential V equals V zero"]:::barrier

    boundEnergy["Bound Energy Level E between 0 and V0"]:::well

    waveEven["Even parity wavefunction psi symmetric about x equals zero"]:::wave
    waveOdd["Odd parity wavefunction psi antisymmetric about x equals zero"]:::wave

    decayTailL["Exponential decay tail kappa into barrier left"]:::barrier
    decayTailR["Exponential decay tail kappa into barrier right"]:::barrier

    regionLeft --> V0left
    regionInside --> V0inside
    regionRight --> V0right

    regionInside --> waveEven
    regionInside --> waveOdd
    regionLeft --> decayTailL
    regionRight --> decayTailR

    boundEnergy -.energy.-> waveEven
    boundEnergy -.energy.-> waveOdd
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Transcendental Equation Graphical Solution

```mermaid
graph LR
    classDef lhs fill:#cfe9ff,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef rhs fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#ff7f0e,stroke-width:2px;
    classDef sol fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2ca02c,stroke-width:2px;

    tanCurve["LHS k tan ka over 2"]:::lhs
    kCurve["RHS kappa equal sqrt V0 minus E"]:::rhs
    e1["Intersection 1 Ground state E1 even"]:::sol
    e2["Intersection 2 First excited E2 odd"]:::sol
    e3["Intersection 3 Second excited E3 even"]:::sol

    tanCurve --- e1
    tanCurve --- e2
    tanCurve --- e3
    kCurve --- e1
    kCurve --- e2
    kCurve --- e3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each intersection between the LHS (trigonometric) and RHS (square-root decay) curve represents one bound-state eigenvalue. The number of intersections equals the number of bound states in the well.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define density of states. How does the density of states change when we go from a 3D bulk material to a 2D quantum well?*

**Model Answer:**

The **density of states** $g(E)$ is the number of available quantum states per unit volume per unit energy interval at energy $E$ for electrons.

For a **3D bulk** material:

$$g_{3D}(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$$

This has a **square-root dependence** on energy, vanishing at $E=0$.

For a **2D quantum well** (confinement along $z$):

$$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m}{\pi\hbar^2}\sum_n H(E - E_n)$$

This is a **step function** — constant in each sub-band. The DOS no longer vanishes at $E=0$ but jumps discretely at each sub-band edge $E_n$. Physically, the 2D system has *fewer* states near the band edge, which is why quantum-well lasers have lower threshold currents.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** *Briefly explain why the density of states in a 1D quantum wire shows a Van Hove singularity.*

**Model Answer:**

In a 1D quantum wire, electrons are free to move only along one direction; motion in the other two transverse directions is quantized into discrete sub-band energies $E_n$.

The 1D DOS is derived from $N(k_x) \propto k_x$, and converting $k_x$ to energy:

$$g_{1D}(E) = \frac{1}{\pi\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2m}{E - E_n}}$$

This expression **diverges** as $E \to E_n^+$. The singularity arises because at the bottom of each sub-band, the dispersion is parabolic but the DOS depends on the inverse square root of the energy above the band edge — a hallmark of 1D parabolic bands known as the **Van Hove singularity**. Physically, electrons "pile up" at the band edge because the group velocity $v_g = (1/\hbar)dE/dk$ tends to zero there, slowing them down and concentrating their density of available states.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU mandates *either-or* choices in Part B. Two fully independent alternatives are provided below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Module 1 — DOS Focus)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 | Module 1 Choice 1]**

**(a)** Derive the density of states for a **2D quantum well** and explain its characteristic step-like behavior. Comment on how this step function affects optical transitions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the density of states for a **1D quantum wire**, highlighting the origin of Van Hove singularities. Compare briefly with 0D quantum dots. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [See Section 3.1 derivation above — full step-by-step.]

Final result: $g_{2D}(E) = (m/\pi\hbar^2)\sum_n H(E-E_n)$

The **step function** has two consequences for optical transitions:

- **Sharp absorption edges** at each $E_n$, since transitions can only occur when $E$ crosses a sub-band edge.
- **Enhanced joint DOS** at sub-band edges leads to **higher optical gain** near each edge — the physical basis for quantum-well laser performance enhancement over bulk.

**(b)** [See Section 3.2 derivation above — full step-by-step.]

Final result: $g_{1D}(E) = (1/\pi\hbar)\sqrt{2m}\,\sum_n (E - E_n)^{-1/2}$

Van Hove singularities arise because:
- Near $E \approx E_n$, the dispersion is flat ($dE/dk \approx 0$).
- States accumulate near the band edge because the group velocity vanishes.

**Comparison with 0D:** A quantum dot confines in all three directions, producing *purely discrete* energy levels. The DOS becomes a sum of Dirac delta functions:

$$g_{0D}(E) = 2\sum_n \delta(E - E_n)$$

Unlike 1D (continuous within a band, divergent at edges) or 2D (continuous step), 0D DOS is **entirely discrete** — every level is a sharp spike.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Module 1 — Finite Quantum Well Focus)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 | Module 1 Choice 2]**

**(a)** Solve the time-independent Schrödinger equation for an electron in a **finite square well** of depth $V_0$ and width $a$. Obtain the transcendental equations for even and odd parity solutions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss graphically how the bound-state energies are obtained. Compare and contrast with the infinite square well, with emphasis on the number of bound states. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** See Section 3.3 derivation above. Final transcendental equations:

$$\text{Even: } k\tan(ka/2) = \kappa \quad\quad \text{Odd: } -k\cot(ka/2) = \kappa$$

where $k = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$ and $\kappa = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$.

**(b)** **Graphical solution method:**

Plot both sides of the eigenvalue equation as functions of $E \in [0, V_0]$. The intersections correspond to allowed eigenvalues. Each intersection = one bound state.

**Comparison with infinite well:**

| Feature | Infinite Well | Finite Well |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of bound states | Infinite | Finite (depends on $V_0$ and $a$) |
| Lowest energy | $E_1 = \hbar^2\pi^2/(2ma^2)$ | Lower than infinite-well value |
| Wavefunction tails | Strictly zero outside | Exponential decay (evanescent) |
| Tunneling | None | Allowed (exponentially suppressed) |
| Density of bound states | Infinite | Limited by well strength $P = (a/\hbar)\sqrt{2mV_0}$ |

**Number of bound states rule of thumb:** For $P < \pi$, only the **ground state** (always even) exists. For $\pi < P < 2\pi$, **two** states (one even, one odd), and so on. Approximately $N \approx \lfloor P/\pi \rfloor + 1$ bound states.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Examiner's Pitfalls):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting spin degeneracy ($g_s = 2$):** Many students drop the factor of 2 in DOS derivations. The KTU answer key **always includes spin**. Losing 1–2 marks here.
>
> 2. **Confusing 1D DOS with 3D DOS formulas:** Students often write $g_{1D}(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$ instead of $g_{1D}(E) \propto (E - E_n)^{-1/2}$. The square-root **flips** between 3D and 1D — a recurring KTU trap.
>
> 3. **Missing boundary conditions:** In finite-well problems, students write ψ but forget to enforce **continuity of derivative** $d\psi/dx$ (or $(1/m)d\psi/dx$ for different effective masses). This is worth up to 4 marks in Part B.
>
> 4. **Sign error in odd-parity equation:** Odd-parity equation should be $-k\cot(ka/2) = \kappa$, NOT $+k\cot(ka/2) = \kappa$. A single sign slip can invalidate the entire derivation.
>
> 5. **Not stating the energy domain:** Always write "for bound states $0 < E < V_0$" explicitly — examiners reward this clarity.
>
> 6. **No diagram in 14-mark answers:** A clear even/odd wavefunction sketch and a graphical solution plot can earn **2 bonus marks** under KTU valuation norms.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — DOS & Finite Wells)**

- [ ] **DOS dimension reduction sequence:** 3D ($\sqrt{E}$) → 2D (step) → 1D (Van Hove divergence) → 0D (delta spikes).
- [ ] **Spin degeneracy factor of 2** must appear in every DOS formula.
- [ ] **2D DOS is constant** $m/\pi\hbar^2$ per sub-band — explains quantum-well laser superiority.
- [ ] **Van Hove singularity** in 1D arises from vanishing group velocity at sub-band edges.
- [ ] **0D DOS** = $\delta$-functions = discrete atomic-like levels.
- [ ] **Finite well inside:** $\psi$ is sinusoidal; **outside:** $\psi$ is exponential.
- [ ] **Two boundary conditions:** $\psi$ continuous + $(1/m)d\psi/dx$ continuous.
- [ ] **Even-parity equation:** $k\tan(ka/2) = \kappa$.
- [ ] **Odd-parity equation:** $-k\cot(ka/2) = \kappa$.
- [ ] **Bound states require $0 < E < V_0$**; minimum one (always even).
- [ ] **Well strength parameter:** $P = (a/\hbar)\sqrt{2mV_0}$ controls number of bound states.
- [ ] **Finite well energies are lower** than infinite well energies of same width.
- [ ] **Wavefunction penetration into barriers** allows tunneling — key for resonant tunneling diodes.
- [ ] **Always include a sketch** (wavefunction + graphical solution) in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] **Real-world devices:** QWLs, QWRs, Q-dots (QLEDs), RTDs, single-photon sources.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parabolic and Triangular Quantum Wells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parabolic and Triangular Quantum Wells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Parabolic Quantum Well (PQW)

### Formal Definition
A **Parabolic Quantum Well (PQW)** is a low-dimensional heterostructure in which the conduction (or valence) band-edge potential varies **quadratically with position**, mathematically equivalent to the potential energy of a quantum harmonic oscillator. The confining potential is given by:

$$V(x) = \frac{1}{2} m^* \omega^2 x^2$$

where $m^*$ is the effective mass of the charge carrier and $\omega$ is the angular frequency characterizing the curvature of the potential. PQW structures are widely used as the effective mass approximation (EMA) model for **quantum dots**, **self-assembled nanocrystals**, and impurity-bound states in modulation-doped heterostructures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The PQW is treated as the textbook example where confinement potential is *position-dependent and continuous*, in contrast to the infinite/square well where potential is piecewise constant. The quantum-mechanical analogy to the simple harmonic oscillator (SHO) makes it analytically solvable in closed form.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Quantum Bowl"
Imagine a smooth, bowl-shaped depression carved into a steel plate. If you roll a marble into this bowl, it will not just sit still — it will oscillate back and forth, climbing the curved walls under gravity. Now shrink the marble to the size of an electron and the bowl to nanometer dimensions. Quantum mechanics replaces the smooth classical trajectory with discrete "probability clouds." Each cloud corresponds to a permitted **energy level** of the trapped electron, and remarkably, all those levels are *equally spaced* — just like the rungs of a ladder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike a rectangular (infinite) well whose levels get farther apart as energy increases ($E_n \propto n^2$), the parabolic well produces **uniformly spaced levels** — a signature feature borrowed directly from the quantum harmonic oscillator.

---

## 1.2 Triangular Quantum Well (TQW)

### Formal Definition
A **Triangular Quantum Well (TQW)** is an asymmetric potential well formed when a strong electrostatic field $F$ is applied perpendicular to a heterointerface or MOS (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) inversion layer. The band edge bends linearly with distance $z$ from the interface, producing a potential:

$$V(z) = eF \cdot z \quad (z > 0)$$

with $V = \infty$ at $z \leq 0$ (the physical interface acts as an infinite wall). The carriers are squeezed against the interface by the field, producing a one-sided, triangular confining region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** TQWs are the **workhorse model for inversion layers in MOSFETs** and the **2-D Electron Gas (2DEG)** in High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs). They are arguably the most technologically relevant quantum confinement geometry in modern CMOS electronics.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Electron Skating Ramp"
Picture a steep, frictionless skateboarding ramp whose slope rises linearly from the ground. A skateboarder (electron) pushed horizontally will slide up the ramp but cannot leave it. The ramp's linear rise is the triangular potential. Because the wall is **infinitely steep on one side** (the oxide) and **gradually sloping on the other** (the field-induced bend), the wavefunction is squeezed tightly against the wall — analogous to how the ramp's slope pins the skater close to the bottom corner. The wavefunctions here are described by **Airy functions**, the curved, oscillating decay patterns that arise uniquely in linear potentials.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Planck's reduced constant: $\hbar \approx 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
> - Elementary charge: $e \approx 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C**
> - Free electron mass: $m_0 \approx 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Parabolic Quantum Well — Why and How It Works

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Construct the Hamiltonian.** The time-independent Schrödinger equation (TISE) in the effective-mass framework becomes that of a quantum harmonic oscillator:
$$\hat{H} \psi(x) = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2 \,\psi(x) = E\,\psi(x)$$

2. **Identify the dimensionless variable.** Introduce $\xi = x/x_0$ where $x_0 = \sqrt{\hbar/(m^*\omega)}$ is the natural oscillator length.

3. **Apply Hermite polynomial solutions.** Because the potential is symmetric and confining, the wavefunctions must vanish at infinity. The Hermite polynomials $H_n$ provide the orthogonal, complete solution set:
$$\psi_n(x) = \left(\frac{m^*\omega}{\pi\hbar}\right)^{1/4} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^n n!}} \, H_n\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{m^*\omega}{\hbar}}\,x\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^*\omega x^2}{2\hbar}\right)$$

4. **Extract the energy spectrum.** Eigenvalues are obtained by acting on $\psi_n$ with $\hat{H}$. The result is exact and elegant:
$$E_n = \left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\hbar\omega, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

5. **Interpret the spacing.** The difference between successive levels is a constant $\Delta E = \hbar\omega$, independent of $n$. This is the **equidistant spectrum** hallmark of the parabolic well.

### Why It Matters in Engineering
- **Quantum-dot lasers and single-photon emitters** rely on the discrete, tunable spectrum of PQWs.
- The **excitonic fine structure** in self-assembled InAs/GaAs dots is modeled with parabolic potentials.
- PQWs explain why **Landau levels** in a 2DEG under magnetic field also yield equally spaced energies.

---

## 2.2 The Triangular Quantum Well — Why and How It Works

### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

1. **Write the TISE for a linear potential.** With $V(z) = eFz$ for $z > 0$ and infinite barrier at $z = 0$:
$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + eF z\,\psi = E\psi$$

2. **Rearrange to Airy form.** Define dimensionless variable $\zeta = (z - E/eF)/\ell_F$ where $\ell_F = \left(\hbar^2 / 2m^* eF\right)^{1/3}$ is the field-dependent length scale. The equation reduces to:
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{d\zeta^2} - \zeta\,\psi = 0$$

3. **Identify the Airy function as the solution.** The general solution is a linear combination $\psi = C_1\,\text{Ai}(\zeta) + C_2\,\text{Bi}(\zeta)$. The $\text{Bi}$ component diverges as $z \to \infty$ and must be discarded.

4. **Apply boundary conditions.** 
   - $\psi(0) = 0$ (infinite wall at the interface)
   - $\psi(\infty) = 0$ (bound state)
   
   This forces **quantization**: $E_n = -eF \cdot z_n$ where $z_n$ are positions corresponding to zeros of $\text{Ai}$.

5. **Compute the energy eigenvalues.** Using the asymptotic zeros of $\text{Ai}(-x)$ at $x_n \approx \left[\frac{3\pi}{2}(n + 3/4)\right]^{2/3}$:
$$E_n \approx \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3} \left[\frac{3\pi}{2}\left(n + \tfrac{3}{4}\right)\right]^{2/3}$$

### Why It Matters in Engineering
- TQWs form the **inversion layer** in nanoscale MOSFETs below 50 nm — the foundation of all modern CMOS logic.
- **HEMTs** (AlGaAs/GaAs, AlGaN/GaN) rely on the 2DEG formed at a TQW interface for high-speed, low-noise RF amplification.
- Threshold voltage shifts in advanced nodes are quantitatively explained using TQW sub-band models.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Parabolic QW Expression | Triangular QW Expression |
|---|----------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Potential | $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2$ | $V(z) = eF z$ |
| 2 | TISE | $-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\psi'' + \tfrac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2 \psi = E\psi$ | $-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\psi'' + eF z\, \psi = E\psi$ |
| 3 | Eigenvalues | $E_n = (n + \tfrac{1}{2})\hbar\omega$ | $E_n \approx \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}\!\left[\tfrac{3\pi}{2}(n+\tfrac{3}{4})\right]^{2/3}$ |
| 4 | Eigenfunctions | $\psi_n \propto H_n(\xi)\,e^{-\xi^2/2}$ | $\psi_n \propto \text{Ai}(\zeta)$ |
| 5 | Quantum numbers | $n = 0,1,2,\ldots$ | $n = 0,1,2,\ldots$ |
| 6 | Level spacing | $\Delta E = \hbar\omega$ (constant) | $\Delta E \propto (n+1)^{2/3} - n^{2/3}$ (non-uniform, decreasing) |
| 7 | Boundary conditions | $\psi(\pm\infty) = 0$ | $\psi(0) = 0,\ \psi(\infty) = 0$ |
| 8 | Special functions | Hermite polynomials $H_n$ | Airy function $\text{Ai}$ |
| 9 | Ground state energy | $E_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\hbar\omega$ | $E_0 \approx 2.338 \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}$ |
| 10 | Key applications | Quantum dots, excitons | MOSFET inversion layer, HEMT 2DEG |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to compare PQW vs TQW, always mention: (i) the constant vs decreasing level spacing, (ii) the symmetric vs asymmetric confinement, and (iii) the Hermite vs Airy function basis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Parabolic QW Energy Levels

We solve the TISE:

$$\hat{H}\psi = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2 \,\psi = E\psi$$

**Step 1 — Rearrange.** Multiply by $-2m^*/\hbar^2$:

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + \frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\left(E - \frac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2\right)\psi = 0$$

**Step 2 — Rescale the coordinate.** Define $\xi = x\sqrt{m^*\omega/\hbar}$ (dimensionless) and $\lambda = 2E/(\hbar\omega)$ (dimensionless energy). The equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{d\xi^2} + (\lambda - \xi^2)\psi = 0$$

**Step 3 — Asymptotic analysis.** As $\xi \to \infty$, the $\lambda$ term is negligible. Then $\psi'' \approx \xi^2 \psi$ has approximate solution $\psi \sim e^{\pm \xi^2/2}$. The divergent $e^{+\xi^2/2}$ is unphysical, so:

$$\psi(\xi) = u(\xi)\,e^{-\xi^2/2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back to find $u(\xi)$.** Plug into the ODE and simplify:

$$\frac{d^2 u}{d\xi^2} - 2\xi\frac{du}{d\xi} + (\lambda - 1)u = 0$$

**Step 5 — Identify Hermite ODE.** This is precisely the Hermite differential equation if $\lambda - 1 = 2n$ for non-negative integer $n$, with polynomial solution $u(\xi) = H_n(\xi)$.

**Step 6 — Extract the energy quantization condition.** Using $\lambda = 2n + 1$:

$$\frac{2E}{\hbar\omega} = 2n + 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\,E_n = \left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\hbar\omega\,}$$

**Step 7 — Final wavefunction.** Restoring the dimensional factors:

$$\boxed{\,\psi_n(x) = \left(\frac{m^*\omega}{\pi\hbar}\right)^{1/4}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2^n n!}}\,H_n\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{m^*\omega}{\hbar}}\,x\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^*\omega x^2}{2\hbar}\right)\,}$$

> **Logic behind Step 6:** Polynomial termination of the Hermite series is the only way $u(\xi)$ can remain bounded as $\xi \to \infty$. If the series did not terminate, it would diverge faster than the Gaussian damping factor $e^{-\xi^2/2}$, producing a non-physical wavefunction. Termination forces $\lambda = 2n + 1$ — this is the origin of quantization.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Triangular QW Energy Levels

We solve:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + eF z\,\psi = E\psi \quad \text{for } z > 0$$

with $\psi(0) = 0$ and $\psi(\infty) = 0$.

**Step 1 — Rearrange.** Multiply by $-2m^*/\hbar^2$:

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} - \frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}(eF z - E)\psi = 0$$

**Step 2 — Define characteristic length.** Let:

$$\ell_F = \left(\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^* eF}\right)^{1/3}$$

This has units of length and sets the natural scale of the problem.

**Step 3 — Dimensionless substitution.** Set:

$$\zeta = \frac{z - E/(eF)}{\ell_F}$$

Then $\dfrac{d}{dz} = \dfrac{1}{\ell_F}\dfrac{d}{d\zeta}$ and the equation becomes:

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{d\zeta^2} - \zeta\,\psi = 0$$

**Step 4 — Airy function solution.** The general solution is:

$$\psi(\zeta) = C_1\,\text{Ai}(\zeta) + C_2\,\text{Bi}(\zeta)$$

**Step 5 — Discard the divergent branch.** As $z \to \infty$, $\zeta \to \infty$ and $\text{Bi}(\zeta)$ diverges exponentially, while $\text{Ai}(\zeta)$ decays exponentially. Hence $C_2 = 0$ and:

$$\psi(\zeta) = C_1\,\text{Ai}(\zeta)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the wall boundary condition.** $\psi(0) = 0$ requires $\text{Ai}(\zeta_0) = 0$ where $\zeta_0 = -E/(eF\,\ell_F)$. Therefore $-E/(eF\,\ell_F)$ must equal one of the zeros of the Airy function: $-\alpha_n$ with $\alpha_1 \approx 2.338$, $\alpha_2 \approx 4.088$, etc.

**Step 7 — Solve for energy levels.** Using $-E/(eF\ell_F) = -\alpha_n$:

$$E_n = eF\,\ell_F\,\alpha_n = (eF)\left(\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^* eF}\right)^{1/3}\alpha_n$$

Simplify:

$$\boxed{\,E_n = \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}\alpha_n\,}$$

**Step 8 — Use large-$n$ asymptotic form for Airy zeros.** For $n \gg 1$:

$$\alpha_n \approx \left[\frac{3\pi}{2}\left(n + \tfrac{3}{4}\right)\right]^{2/3}$$

This gives the compact KTU-standard form:
