$$\boxed{\,E_n \approx \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}\left[\frac{3\pi}{2}\left(n + \tfrac{3}{4}\right)\right]^{2/3}\,}$$

**Step 9 — Verify ground state energy.** With $n=0$ and $\alpha_0 = 2.338$:

$$E_0 \approx 2.338 \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}$$

> **Logic behind Step 8:** The Airy function $\text{Ai}(-x)$ oscillates with progressively shorter wavelength as $x$ grows. For large $n$, a WKB-style argument gives the asymptotic phase integral $\int_0^{x_n}\sqrt{x}\,dx = \pi(n+3/4)/2$, leading to the $3\pi/2 \cdot (n+3/4)$ form.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Demonstration — Field-Scaling of TQW Levels

For GaAs ($m^* = 0.067\,m_0$) at $F = 10^7$ V/m:

$$\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*} = \frac{(1.054\times10^{-34})^2 (1.602\times10^{-19})^2 (10^7)^2}{2(0.067)(9.109\times10^{-31})}$$

$$= \frac{(1.111\times10^{-68})(2.566\times10^{-38})(10^{14})}{1.221\times10^{-31}} = \frac{2.851\times10^{-92}}{1.221\times10^{-31}}$$

$$= 2.335\times10^{-61}\ \text{J}^{1/3}\cdot\text{energy units}$$

Taking cube root and converting to eV:

$$E_0 \approx 2.338 \times (2.335\times10^{-61})^{1/3} \approx 2.338 \times (6.13\times10^{-21})\ \text{J} \approx 1.43\times10^{-20}\ \text{J} \approx 89.4\ \text{meV}$$

> **Interpretation:** The ground-state sub-band lies ~89 meV above the interface, comfortably above $k_BT \approx 25.7$ meV at room temperature — explaining why **2DEG transport** in HEMTs is robust against thermal excitation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram: Functional Architecture of Confined States in PQW vs TQW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Physical System] --> B0{Choose Geometry}

    B0 -->|Quadratic confinement| P1[Parabolic QW]
    B0 -->|Linear field-induced confinement| T1[Triangular QW]

    P1 --> P2[Potential: 1/2 m* omega squared x squared]
    P2 --> P3[Schrodinger Equation: SHO form]
    P3 --> P4[Hermite polynomial solution Hn]
    P4 --> P5[Equidistant Energy Levels: En equals n plus 1/2 times hbar omega]
    P5 --> P6[Output: Equally spaced sub-bands]

    T1 --> T2[Potential: eFz with infinite wall at z=0]
    T2 --> T3[Schrodinger Equation: Linear potential]
    T3 --> T4[Airy function solution Ai]
    T4 --> T5[Quantization via Airy zeros: alpha n]
    T5 --> T6[Output: Sub-bands with decreasing spacing]

    P6 --> App1[Application: Quantum Dot Lasers]
    P6 --> App2[Application: Excitonic Devices]

    T6 --> App3[Application: MOSFET Inversion Layer]
    T6 --> App4[Application: HEMT 2DEG Channel]

    style P5 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style T5 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style App1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900
    style App3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Solving Procedure for Quantum Well Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Identify the well geometry]) --> Q1{Is V quadratic in x?}

    Q1 -->|Yes| SP1[Step 1: Write harmonic oscillator TISE]
    SP1 --> SP2[Step 2: Rescale to dimensionless xi]
    SP2 --> SP3[Step 3: Apply Hermite polynomial basis]
    SP3 --> SP4[Step 4: Quantize via polynomial termination]
    SP4 --> Result1[Result: En equals n plus 1/2 times hbar omega]

    Q1 -->|No - linear| ST1[Step 1: Write linear-potential TISE]
    ST1 --> ST2[Step 2: Define field length scale]
    ST2 --> ST3[Step 3: Substitute to Airy form]
    ST3 --> ST4[Step 4: Apply wall boundary condition]
    ST4 --> ST5[Step 5: Use Airy function zeros]
    ST5 --> Result2[Result: En equals prefactor times alpha n]

    Result1 --> Final[Plot sub-bands and wavefunctions]
    Result2 --> Final

    style Result1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style Result2 fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style Final fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Blue boxes = Parabolic QW pathway; Orange boxes = Triangular QW pathway; Green boxes = Final synthesis. Each node uses alphanumeric identifiers (e.g., `P5`, `T5`) that comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards (no reserved keywords as standalone node names).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Distinguish between a parabolic quantum well and a triangular quantum well with respect to (i) potential profile, (ii) energy-level spacing, and (iii) special functions involved in solving the Schrödinger equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Parabolic QW | Triangular QW |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| (i) Potential | $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2$ — symmetric, quadratic | $V(z) = eF z$ — asymmetric, linear; $V = \infty$ at $z=0$ |
| (ii) Level spacing | Uniform: $\Delta E = \hbar\omega$ | Non-uniform: spacing decreases with $n$ as $E_n \propto n^{2/3}$ |
| (iii) Special functions | Hermite polynomials $H_n$ | Airy functions $\text{Ai}$ |

(1 mark each for the three correct entries.)

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Why are triangular quantum wells considered the most physically realistic model for inversion layers in nanoscale MOSFETs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Gate field analogy:** The strong perpendicular electric field $F$ from the gate bends the conduction band linearly with depth, exactly matching the triangular potential $V(z) = eF z$. (1 mark)
2. **Oxide interface wall:** The Si–SiO$_2$ interface acts as an effective infinite barrier at $z = 0$, enforcing the one-sided confinement. (1 mark)
3. **2DEG formation:** Solving with Airy functions reproduces the experimentally observed sub-band energies and population of the 2-D Electron Gas. (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Derive the energy eigenvalues and eigenfunctions for an electron confined in a parabolic quantum well with potential $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2$. Show that the resulting energy levels are equally spaced. Discuss the technological significance of this equal spacing. (14 marks)**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Derivation of Eigenvalues (7 marks)

**Step 1: Write the TISE.** (1 mark)
$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + \frac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2\,\psi = E\psi$$

**Step 2: Rescale.** Let $\xi = x\sqrt{m^*\omega/\hbar}$ and $\lambda = 2E/(\hbar\omega)$. (1 mark)

**Step 3: Obtain the ODE in standard form.** (1 mark)
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{d\xi^2} + (\lambda - \xi^2)\psi = 0$$

**Step 4: Asymptotic analysis and substitution** $\psi = u(\xi)\,e^{-\xi^2/2}$. (1 mark)
$$\frac{d^2 u}{d\xi^2} - 2\xi\frac{du}{d\xi} + (\lambda - 1)u = 0$$

**Step 5: Identify as Hermite equation and apply termination.** (1 mark)
$$\lambda - 1 = 2n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda = 2n + 1$$

**Step 6: Solve for energy.** (1 mark)
$$\boxed{E_n = \left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)\hbar\omega}$$

**Step 7: Note equal spacing.** $\Delta E = \hbar\omega$ constant — **equal spacing proven**. (1 mark)

#### Sub-Part (b) — Eigenfunctions and Technological Significance (7 marks)

**Eigenfunctions (3 marks):**
$$\psi_n(x) = \left(\frac{m^*\omega}{\pi\hbar}\right)^{1/4}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2^n n!}}\,H_n\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{m^*\omega}{\hbar}}\,x\right) \exp\!\left(-\frac{m^*\omega x^2}{2\hbar}\right)$$

with $H_0 = 1$, $H_1 = 2\xi$, $H_2 = 4\xi^2 - 2$, etc. The Gaussian envelope $\exp(-m^*\omega x^2/2\hbar)$ ensures $\psi \to 0$ as $x \to \pm\infty$.

**Technological significance (4 marks — any 4 of the following):**
1. Models **quantum dots** used in single-photon sources for quantum cryptography.
2. Predicts **equidistant excitonic transitions** in self-assembled InAs/GaAs nanocrystals.
3. Provides the framework for **Landau levels** in 2DEG under perpendicular B-field (since $V \propto x^2$).
4. Underlies **phonon engineering** in polar semiconductors where electron–LO-phonon coupling maps to a parabolic model.
5. Enables **single-mode quantum-dot lasers** with temperature-insensitive gain due to uniform level spacing.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**A triangular quantum well is formed at the Si/SiO$_2$ interface of a MOSFET with effective mass $m^* = 0.5\,m_0$ and effective electric field $F = 5 \times 10^7$ V/m. (a) Derive the energy eigenvalues using Airy function analysis. (b) Calculate the ground-state energy in eV. (c) Explain why higher sub-bands are more closely spaced than the lower ones. (14 marks)**

#### Sub-Part (a) — Derivation Using Airy Functions (7 marks)

**Step 1: TISE.** (1 mark)
$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + eF z\,\psi = E\psi, \quad \psi(0) = 0$$

**Step 2: Define field length.** (1 mark)
$$\ell_F = \left(\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^* eF}\right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 3: Substitute $\zeta = (z - E/eF)/\ell_F$.** (1 mark)
$$\frac{d^2\psi}{d\zeta^2} - \zeta\,\psi = 0$$

**Step 4: General solution.** (1 mark)
$$\psi(\zeta) = C_1\,\text{Ai}(\zeta) + C_2\,\text{Bi}(\zeta)$$

**Step 5: Boundary conditions.** Discard $C_2$ (divergence at infinity). Apply $\psi(0) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Ai}(-E/(eF\ell_F)) = 0$. (1 mark)

**Step 6: Quantization.** Set $-E/(eF\ell_F) = -\alpha_n$ where $\alpha_n$ are zeros of $\text{Ai}(-x)$. (1 mark)

**Step 7: Final eigenvalue.** (1 mark)
$$\boxed{E_n = \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}\alpha_n \approx \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}\left[\frac{3\pi}{2}\!\left(n+\tfrac{3}{4}\right)\right]^{2/3}}$$

#### Sub-Part (b) — Numerical Calculation (3 marks)

Compute the prefactor with $m^* = 0.5 m_0 = 4.555\times10^{-31}$ kg and $F = 5\times10^7$ V/m: (1 mark)

$$\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*} = \frac{(1.054\times10^{-34})^2 (1.602\times10^{-19})^2 (5\times10^7)^2}{2(4.555\times10^{-31})}$$

$$= \frac{(1.111\times10^{-68})(2.566\times10^{-38})(2.5\times10^{15})}{9.110\times10^{-31}} = 7.83\times10^{-61}\ \text{J}^{1/3}\text{-units}$$

Cube root: $(7.83\times10^{-61})^{1/3} = 9.22\times10^{-21}$ J. (1 mark)

Ground state ($n=0$, $\alpha_0 \approx 2.338$):

$$E_0 = 2.338 \times 9.22\times10^{-21}\ \text{J} = 2.156\times10^{-20}\ \text{J} \approx 0.135\ \text{eV}$$

(1 mark)

#### Sub-Part (c) — Non-Uniform Spacing Explanation (4 marks)

The eigenvalue scales as $E_n \propto (n+3/4)^{2/3}$. The spacing between consecutive levels is:

$$\Delta E_n = E_{n+1} - E_n \propto (n+1+3/4)^{2/3} - (n+3/4)^{2/3}$$

Using $(1+x)^{2/3} \approx 1 + (2/3)x$ for small $x$:

$$\Delta E_n \propto \frac{2}{3}(n+3/4)^{-1/3}$$

(2 marks) Hence $\Delta E_n$ **decreases** as $n^{-1/3}$. Physically, the triangular potential grows linearly, so each successive level fits into a narrower "wedge" of the linear ramp, producing progressively smaller separations. (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $+3/4$ phase** in the asymptotic Airy zero formula — students often write $3\pi n/2$ instead of $3\pi(n+3/4)/2$, losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Hermite with Laguerre polynomials** — Hermite is for PQW (1D Cartesian); Laguerre is for hydrogen atom or 2D harmonic oscillator in polar coordinates.
> 3. **Units inconsistency** — when computing $E_0$ numerically, mixing CGS and SI gives wrong answers; stick to SI throughout.
> 4. **Forgetting the Gaussian envelope** in the PQW wavefunction — the full answer requires both $H_n$ AND the $e^{-x^2/2}$ factor.
> 5. **Wrong boundary condition at $z=0$** — students sometimes forget the infinite-wall condition $\psi(0)=0$ for the TQW, which is essential for quantization.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parabolic QW potential:** $V(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}m^*\omega^2 x^2$ — symmetric, quadratic, analytically equivalent to the quantum harmonic oscillator.
- **PQW eigenvalues:** $E_n = (n+\tfrac{1}{2})\hbar\omega$ — **uniformly spaced** by $\hbar\omega$.
- **PQW eigenfunctions:** Gaussian envelope × Hermite polynomial $H_n(\xi)$.
- **Triangular QW potential:** $V(z) = eF z$ with $V=\infty$ at $z=0$ — asymmetric, linear, models MOSFET inversion layers.
- **TQW eigenvalues:** $E_n \approx \left(\frac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{1/3}\!\left[\frac{3\pi}{2}(n+\tfrac{3}{4})\right]^{2/3}$ — **non-uniformly spaced**, decreasing with $n$.
- **TQW eigenfunctions:** Pure Airy function $\text{Ai}(\zeta)$, no polynomial envelope.
- **Airy zeros:** $\alpha_0 \approx 2.338$, $\alpha_1 \approx 4.088$, $\alpha_2 \approx 5.520$ (use only the first three if numerical).
- **Technology link:** PQW → Quantum dots & excitonic devices; TQW → MOSFET/HEMT 2DEG channels.
- **Key distinction:** PQW has Hermite functions (discrete polynomial times Gaussian); TQW has Airy functions (oscillatory + decaying).
- **Exam mantra:** "**Hermite for Harmonic, Airy for Asymmetric.**"
- **Dimensional check:** Both $E_n$ formulas must reduce to Joules; always verify $\ell_F$ carries the correct units of length before plugging numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Methods of Fabrication of Nano-layers: Physical Vapour Deposition - Evaporation and Sputtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods of Fabrication of Nano-layers: Physical Vapour Deposition - Evaporation and Sputtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) — Formal Definition

**Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD)** is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition technique in which a solid target material is converted into its vapour phase by purely *physical* means (without chemical reactions in the gas phase), transported across a low-pressure region, and condensed onto a cooler substrate to form a nano-scale or micro-scale layer.

In the context of **nano-electronics fabrication**, PVD is the workhorse for depositing metallic interconnects (Al, Cu, Au, Ti), dielectric stacks (SiO₂, Al₂O₃ via reactive PVD), and semiconductor layers (Si, Ge) with thicknesses ranging from a few **nanometres (1 nm = 10⁻⁹ m)** to several micrometres.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** PVD is broadly classified into **Evaporation** and **Sputtering**. Students must understand the *physics* of the vapour generation step, because the kinetic energy of incoming atoms dictates film quality, adhesion, and step coverage.

## 1.2 The Two Principal PVD Sub-Families

### A) Vacuum Thermal Evaporation (VTE)
The source material is heated (resistively or by an electron beam) until its **vapour pressure** exceeds the chamber pressure. Atoms leave the surface with thermal energies of roughly **0.1 eV to 0.3 eV** and travel ballistically to the substrate.

### B) Sputtering (Plasma-Assisted PVD)
A low-pressure inert gas (typically **Ar**, base pressure ~ 10⁻⁷ Torr, working pressure ~ 10⁻³ – 10⁻² Torr) is ionised into a plasma. Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated onto the (negatively biased) target, knocking out target atoms via *momentum transfer*. These ejected atoms (energy **1–100 eV**) travel to the substrate and condense.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Ways to Spray Paint a Wall"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Painting Atoms onto a Surface**
>
> Imagine you want to spray a thin coloured mist onto a cold glass wall from across the room.
>
> - **Evaporation = The Candle Analogy:** You melt the paint (solid) into vapour by *heating it*. The vapour drifts across the room and settles on the cold wall. The "paint molecules" arrive *gently* (low energy) — like a soft mist from a candle. The catch: only materials with low melting points cooperate easily.
>
> - **Sputtering = The Billiards Analogy:** You fire tiny invisible "billiard balls" (Ar⁺ ions) at a solid block of paint. The impact *knocks* paint atoms off the block — they fly across the room and stick to the wall. The "paint atoms" arrive *harder* (high energy) — they embed better, stick more uniformly, and even coat the *sides* of tiny grooves on the wall. Any material works — even tungsten or ceramics.
>
> **Moral of the analogy:** Both methods end with a thin film on the substrate. The *mechanism* of getting atoms into the vapour phase is fundamentally different — heat it vs. knock it.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann constant | k_B | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Governs vapour-pressure temperature dependence |
| Avogadro's number | N_A | **6.022 × 10²³ mol⁻¹** | Converts between molar and atomic flux |
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Used in Clausius–Clapeyron relations |
| Atomic mass unit | u | **1.66 × 10⁻²⁷ kg** | Mass of a typical atom |
| Base vacuum for PVD | P_base | **10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁸ Torr** | Needed to keep mean free path large |
| 1 Torr in SI | — | **133.322 Pa** | Pressure conversion |

## 1.5 Why PVD Matters in Nano-Electronics

- CMOS gate electrodes (TiN, TaN) are deposited by **reactive sputtering**.
- Copper interconnect seed layers use **PVD** because CVD precursors for Cu are unstable.
- Nano-imprint lithography requires **uniform evaporation** of a release layer.
- Quantum dots and 2D-material heterostructures rely on **ultra-thin (< 5 nm)** PVD films.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vacuum Thermal Evaporation — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Load** the source material (pellets, wires, or powder) into a **crucible** or **filament** (W, Mo, Ta boats).
2. **Pump down** the chamber to base vacuum (~ 10⁻⁶ Torr) to eliminate O₂ and H₂O contamination.
3. **Heat** the source resistively. The temperature is ramped until the material's **vapour pressure ≈ 10⁻² Torr** (the practical evaporation threshold).
4. **Vaporisation** occurs. Atoms leave the melt surface with a **Maxwell–Boltzmann** energy distribution.
5. **Ballistic transport** — atoms travel in straight lines (mean free path ≫ source-to-substrate distance).
6. **Condensation** — atoms strike the cooler substrate, lose energy via phonon coupling, and form a thin film.
7. **Film growth modes** — Volmer–Weber (island), Frank–van der Merwe (layer), or Stranski–Krastanov (mixed), depending on surface energy balance.

### Evaporation Variants
- **Resistive (Filament) Evaporation:** Source heated by Joule heating; limited to materials melting below ~ 1500 °C.
- **Electron-Beam Evaporation (E-beam):** A focused e-beam (5–10 keV) locally melts the source. Achieves > 3000 °C; can evaporate W, Mo, SiO₂.
- **Flash Evaporation:** Powder fed continuously onto a hot filament — used for compound films (e.g., NiCr).
- **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE):** Ultra-high-vacuum (~ 10⁻¹⁰ Torr) cousin of evaporation with RHEED monitoring — yields monolayer precision.

## 2.2 Sputtering — Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Load** target (cathode) and substrate (anode or grounded) into the chamber; pump to base vacuum.
2. **Introduce** inert working gas (Ar) at ~ 10⁻² Torr.
3. **Apply** a high negative DC voltage (–300 V to –1000 V) to the target → plasma ignites.
4. **Plasma formation** — free electrons collide with Ar, generating Ar⁺ ions and secondary electrons.
5. **Ion bombardment** — Ar⁺ ions are accelerated across the dark space (cathode sheath) into the target.
6. **Momentum transfer** — incoming ion transfers kinetic energy to a target surface atom via a *collision cascade*. If the transferred energy exceeds the **surface binding energy (E_sb ≈ 3–30 eV)**, the atom is ejected — this is the **sputtered atom**.
7. **Transport** — sputtered atoms diffuse through the plasma (multiple collisions) and reach the substrate.
8. **Deposition** — atoms condense on the substrate; a few atomic layers later, a continuous film forms.

### Sputtering Variants
- **DC Diode Sputtering** — simple, but only works with conductive targets.
- **RF Sputtering (13.56 MHz)** — uses AC to alternate sputtering/electron neutralisation → enables **dielectric** targets (SiO₂, Al₂O₃).
- **Magnetron Sputtering** — magnets confine secondary electrons near the target → denser plasma, lower operating voltage, **higher deposition rates** (industry standard).
- **Reactive Sputtering** — add O₂ or N₂ to deposit oxides/nitrides (TiO₂, SiNₓ).
- **Ion-Beam Sputtering (IBS)** — separate ion gun; ultra-clean, low-pressure operation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in all tables to avoid Markdown breakage.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_d = \dfrac{p}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \dfrac{A_s}{L^2} \cdot \cos\theta$ | **Deposition rate** (atoms / unit area / sec) from a point source | Evaporation rate calculation |
| 2 | $p = p_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T}\right)$ | **Vapour pressure** (Clausius–Clapeyron form) | Choosing source temperature |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P}$ | **Mean free path** of a gas atom | Deciding chamber pressure |
| 4 | $Y = \dfrac{\text{Ejected atoms}}{\text{Incident ion}} \approx 0.04\,(\alpha\,M_t/M_i)\,U_0 \left(1 - \left(\dfrac{E_{th}}{E}\right)^{1/2}\right)$ | **Sputtering yield** (semi-empirical, Matsunami–Meyer) | Estimating sputter rates |
| 5 | $P_{kin} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | **Kinetic energy** of an incoming particle | Comparing energy scales |
| 6 | $T_{adhesion} \propto E_{kin}^{1/2}$ | Higher $E_{kin}$ → better film adhesion | Evaporation vs. sputtering trade-off |
| 7 | $v_{dep} = \dfrac{Y \cdot J_i \cdot M_t}{\rho \cdot N_A}$ | **Deposition rate** from sputtering (m/s) | System-design problems |
| 8 | $q = \dfrac{\Lambda\,V_{target}}{d}$ | **Ion flux** to target (Child–Langmuir-like) | Plasma density reasoning |
| 9 | $\theta_{sub} \approx \dfrac{d}{h}$ | **Step coverage** angle of incident flux | Evaporation shadowing |
| 10 | $E_{th} \approx \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma(1-\gamma)} \cdot (1+\gamma)$ | **Threshold energy** for sputtering | Material-selection questions |

Where:
- $p$ = equilibrium vapour pressure (Pa)
- $m$ = mass of a vapour atom (kg)
- $A_s$ = source area, $L$ = source-to-substrate distance
- $\Delta H_{vap}$ = enthalpy of vaporisation (J/mol)
- $d$ = atomic diameter (m), $P$ = chamber pressure (Pa)
- $Y$ = sputtering yield (dimensionless), $U_0$ = surface binding energy (eV)
- $M_t, M_i$ = atomic masses of target atom and incident ion
- $E_{th}$ = threshold energy for sputtering (typically 10–40 eV)
- $J_i$ = ion current density (A/m²), $\rho$ = film density

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Semiconductor industry:** Intel's 90 nm and below nodes use **PVD Cu seed layers** before electroplating; adhesion is poor with evaporation.
- **Optical coatings:** Anti-reflective coatings on lenses use **e-beam evaporation** (precise index control).
- **MEMS/NEMS:** Cantilever fabrication uses **sputtered Au** as a biocompatible electrode layer.
- **Magnetic storage:** Hard-disk read heads use **magnetron-sputtered** CoCrPt alloys.
- **Photovoltaics:** CIGS thin-film solar cells use co-evaporation of Cu, In, Ga, Se.

## 2.5 Evaporation vs. Sputtering — Direct Comparison

| Parameter | Thermal Evaporation | Sputtering |
|---|---|---|
| Vapour generation | Heating (thermal energy) | Momentum transfer (kinetic) |
| Particle energy at substrate | **~ 0.1 eV** (low) | **~ 1–100 eV** (high) |
| Step coverage | Poor (line-of-sight) | Excellent (directional fill) |
| Adhesion | Weak | Strong |
| Deposition rate | High (up to nm/s) | Moderate |
| Film density | Lower, porous | Higher, dense |
| Compatible materials | Low-melting-point metals, some oxides | All metals, alloys, compounds |
| Chamber pressure | ~ 10⁻⁶ Torr | ~ 10⁻³ Torr |
| Damage to substrate | None | Ion bombardment damage possible |
| Cost & complexity | Low | Higher (power supply, gas flow) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Evaporation Deposition Rate (Knudsen / Hertz–Knudsen Equation)

We derive the rate at which mass leaves a unit area of a hot source per second.

**Step 1 — Number density at the source surface.**
Using the ideal-gas law at temperature $T$ and pressure $p$:

$$
n = \dfrac{p}{k_B T}
$$

This is the number of atoms per unit volume at the surface that can escape.

**Step 2 — Average velocity component normal to the surface (1-D Maxwell–Boltzmann).**
Only the z-component of velocity contributes to atoms leaving the surface. The mean of $\vert v_z \vert$ for a Maxwellian gas is:

$$
\langle \vert v_z \vert \rangle = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_B T}{2\pi m}}
$$

**Step 3 — Effusion flux (Hertz–Knudsen equation).**
The flux of atoms leaving the surface per unit area per second is the product of density and mean normal velocity:

$$
\Phi_{evap} = n \cdot \langle \vert v_z \vert \rangle = \dfrac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}
$$

This is the **Hertz–Knudsen evaporation flux**. Units: atoms / (m² · s).

**Step 4 — Convert pressure to mass flux.**
Multiply by mass per atom $m$:

$$
R_{mass} = m \Phi_{evap} = p \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2\pi k_B T}}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to **thickness** deposition rate at a substrate located distance $L$ away, with cosine distribution from a small-area source.**

A point source emits isotropically; the fraction intercepted by a substrate of area $A_s$ at distance $L$ is $\dfrac{A_s \cos\theta}{4\pi L^2}$. For a substrate facing the source directly ($\theta = 0$):

$$
R_{dep} = \dfrac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}} \cdot \dfrac{A_s}{L^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Real-world numerical check.**
For **Aluminium** at $T = 1500$ K:
- $p \approx 10$ Pa
- $m_{Al} = 4.48 \times 10^{-26}$ kg
- $k_B T = 2.07 \times 10^{-20}$ J

$$
\Phi_{evap} = \dfrac{10}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 4.48\times 10^{-26} \cdot 2.07\times 10^{-20}}} \approx 9.2 \times 10^{21}\ \text{atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

This corresponds to ~ **1 nm/s** deposition rate on a substrate 30 cm away — typical lab value.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Mean Free Path of a Vapour Atom

For a vapour atom (diameter $d_1$) travelling through a residual gas (diameter $d_2$) at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$:

**Step 1 — Collision cross-section.**
Hard-sphere collision cross-section:

$$
\sigma = \pi \left(\dfrac{d_1 + d_2}{2}\right)^{2} = \pi d^{2}\quad (\text{for } d_1 \approx d_2 \equiv d)
$$

**Step 2 — Mean free path = mean distance between collisions.**
Mean free path = mean speed / collision frequency. After simplification:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}\, n \sigma}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ideal-gas density** $n = P / (k_B T)$:

$$
\lambda = \dfrac{k_B T}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2}\,P}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical check at $10^{-6}$ Torr.**
For Al vapour ($d \approx 2.86 \times 10^{-10}$ m), at room temperature, $P = 10^{-6}$ Torr $= 1.33 \times 10^{-4}$ Pa:

$$
\lambda \approx \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \cdot 300}{\sqrt{2}\,\pi (2.86\times10^{-10})^{2} \cdot 1.33\times 10^{-4}} \approx 72\ \text{m}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 72 m mean free path means atoms travel ballistically — that's why PVD chambers must be < 1 m in dimension to maintain vacuum.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Sputtering Yield Threshold (Simplified)

Sputtering is a binary-collision-cascade phenomenon.

**Step 1 — Maximum energy transferable in a head-on elastic collision.**

$$
T_m = \gamma E, \quad \text{where}\ \gamma = \dfrac{4 M_t M_i}{(M_t + M_i)^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Threshold condition: maximum transferable energy = surface binding energy.**

$$
\gamma E_{th} = U_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{th} = \dfrac{U_s}{\gamma}
$$

This is the **minimum ion energy** to dislodge an atom. For Ar⁺ on Cu ($M_t \approx 63.5$, $M_i \approx 40$):

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{4 \cdot 63.5 \cdot 40}{(63.5 + 40)^{2}} \approx 0.98
$$

So $E_{th} \approx U_s / 0.98 \approx U_s$. For Cu, $U_s \approx 3.5$ eV ⇒ $E_{th} \approx 3.5$ eV (matches experimental ~ 17 eV — the simple model underestimates because of cascade energy losses).

**Step 3 — Semi-empirical Matsunami–Meyer yield** (used in industry):

$$
Y(E) = 0.042\, \dfrac{\alpha\, Q\, s_n(\varepsilon)}{U_s}\left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{th}}{E}}\right)
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the reduced energy and $Q, s_n$ are tabulated nuclear stopping parameters. This expression gives $Y \sim 1$ to $10$ for typical metals at $E \sim 500$ eV.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which PVD Method?

| Substrate Topography | Desired Film Property | Recommended Method |
|---|---|---|
| Planar wafer, ≤ 10 nm film | Ultra-pure metal | **E-beam evaporation** |
| Trenches with aspect ratio > 2:1 | Conformal coverage | **Magnetron sputtering** or **ionised PVD** |
| Dielectric (SiO₂, AlN) film | Stoichiometric compound | **RF / reactive sputtering** |
| Compound semiconductor (GaAs) | Avoid ion damage | **MBE / thermal evaporation** |
| Insulating substrate | High deposition rate | **RF magnetron sputtering** |
| Alloy film with uniform composition | Avoid fractionation | **Co-sputtering or flash evaporation** |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 5 nm Au film is to be deposited by thermal evaporation. The source-to-substrate distance is 25 cm, source area = 1 cm², and the vapour pressure of Au at the operating temperature is 1 Pa. Compute the deposition rate and time required. (Given: $m_{Au} = 3.27 \times 10^{-25}$ kg, $T = 1800$ K, density of Au film = 19.3 g/cm³.)

**Solution.**

$$
k_B T = 1.38\times 10^{-23} \times 1800 = 2.484\times10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

$$
\Phi_{evap} = \dfrac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 3.27\times10^{-25} \cdot 2.484\times10^{-20}}}
$$

$$
\Phi_{evap} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5.10 \times 10^{-44}}} = \dfrac{1}{7.14 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 1.4 \times 10^{21}\ \text{atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

Geometric dilution factor:

$$
f = \dfrac{A_s}{L^2} = \dfrac{1\times10^{-4}}{0.25^{2}} = 1.6 \times 10^{-3}
$$

Mass flux at substrate:

$$
R_{mass} = \Phi_{evap} \cdot m \cdot f = 1.4\times10^{21} \cdot 3.27\times10^{-25} \cdot 1.6\times10^{-3} = 7.33\times10^{-7}\ \text{kg/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

Convert to thickness rate, using density $\rho = 19300$ kg/m³:

$$
v_{dep} = \dfrac{R_{mass}}{\rho} = \dfrac{7.33\times10^{-7}}{19300} \approx 3.8 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m/s} = 0.038\ \text{nm/s}
$$

For 5 nm film:

$$
t_{dep} = \dfrac{5}{0.038} \approx 132\ \text{s} \approx 2.2\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Answer:** Deposition rate ≈ 0.04 nm/s; total time ≈ 2.2 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PVD System

The following diagram captures the **major functional blocks** of a generic PVD system, indicating signal/energy/material flows rather than attempting a precise mechanical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Vacuum Pump Stack"] -->|P_base 1e-6 Torr| chA["Process Chamber"]
    chA -->|Ar Inlet| massF["Mass Flow Controller"]
    massF -->|Ar at 1e-2 Torr| chA
    powA["DC or RF Power Supply"] -->|bias -300 to -1000 V| tgtA["Target Cathode"]
    chA -->|plasma ignition| tgtA
    tgtA -->|Ar+ bombardment| chA
    chA -->|sputtered atoms| subA["Substrate Holder Anode"]
    subA -->|heat 200 C| subA
    subA -->|condensation| filmA["Nano Layer on Substrate"]
    monA["Quartz Crystal Monitor"] -->|rate feedback| ctrlA["Process Controller"]
    ctrlA -->|rate setpoint| powA
    srcA -.-> ctrlA
    massF -.-> ctrlA
```

## 4.2 Block Architecture of an Electron-Beam Evaporation System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    egun["Electron Gun Tungsten Filament 5 to 10 keV"] -->|e beam| cruA["Water Cooled Copper Crucible"]
    cruA -->|local melt 3000 C| srcM["Source Material Pellets"]
    srcM -->|vapour| trfA["Vapour Transport Line of Sight"]
    trfA -->|condensation| subA["Rotating Substrate Holder"]
    subA -->|heated 100 to 300 C| filmA["Evaporated Nano Film"]
    xrc["XTC Quartz Crystal Rate Monitor"] -->|feedback| ctrlA["PID Process Controller"]
    ctrlA -->|power regulation| egun
    vac["Cryo or Turbomolecular Pump"] -->|base vacuum 1e-7 Torr| chA["Vacuum Chamber"]
    chA --> xrc
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing Evaporation vs. Sputtering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Choose PVD Method for Nano Layer"] --> q1{"Target material is dielectric or insulator"}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Need low ion damage"}
    q1 -- No  --> q3{"Substrate has deep trenches AR greater than 2"}
    q2 -- Yes --> pathA["E beam Evaporation or MBE"]
    q2 -- No  --> pathB["RF or Reactive Sputtering"]
    q3 -- Yes --> pathC["Magnetron or Ionised PVD"]
    q3 -- No  --> q4{"Need ultra pure monolayer control"}
    q4 -- Yes --> pathD["MBE"]
    q4 -- No  --> q5{"High deposition rate for thick films"}
    q5 -- Yes --> pathE["E beam Evaporation"]
    q5 -- No  --> pathF["DC Magnetron Sputtering"]
```

## 4.4 Process Sequence — Step Coverage Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Thermal Evaporation Line of Sight"]
        a1["Source"] -->|straight ray| b1["Substrate Top Only"]
        b1 --> c1["Poor Sidewall Coverage"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Sputtering Diffuse Multi Direction"]
        a2["Target"] -->|sputtered atoms| b2["Substrate Top and Sidewalls"]
        b2 --> c2["Conformal Coverage"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid and not a CAD drawing?** Mermaid cannot accurately render photorealistic cross-sections of vacuum chambers. The block-level architecture above faithfully captures **functional** relationships (which subsystem energises which, which signal flows where) — this is what KTU examiners typically test in 14-mark descriptive questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**Define Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD). List any two advantages of PVD over Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD).**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

Physical Vapour Deposition (PVD) is a vacuum-based thin-film deposition technique in which a solid source material is converted into its vapour phase by *purely physical means* (thermal heating or momentum transfer via ion bombardment), transported across a low-pressure chamber, and condensed onto a cooler substrate to form a nano-layer.

**Two advantages over CVD:**
1. **No toxic gaseous by-products** — PVD is environmentally cleaner than CVD (e.g., no SiH₄, WF₆, or HCl fumes).
2. **Higher purity films** — absence of chemical precursors eliminates unwanted C, H, or O contamination; preferred for ultra-pure metal layers in CMOS.

*(1 mark: definition, 2 × 1 mark: advantages.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Compare thermal evaporation and sputtering with respect to (i) energy of deposited atoms and (ii) step coverage.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Thermal Evaporation | Sputtering |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Energy of deposited atoms | Very low (~ **0.1 eV**) — atoms leave with thermal energy | High (~ **1–100 eV**) — atoms ejected by momentum transfer cascade |
| (ii) Step coverage | Poor — strictly **line-of-sight**; trenches receive little flux on sidewalls | Excellent — multi-directional scattering in plasma; good **sidewall and bottom coverage** |

Higher energy in sputtering promotes better **adhesion** and **film density**, but may cause ion-bombardment damage to sensitive substrates.

*(2 marks: comparison, 1 mark: application comment.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must attempt **one full 14-mark question** from a choice of two. Each question below is divided into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **thermal evaporation** with a neat block diagram. Discuss **e-beam evaporation** and state why it is preferred for high-melting-point materials. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **Hertz–Knudsen evaporation flux equation** and compute the deposition rate of Al on a substrate 20 cm away from a 0.5 cm² source, given that the vapour pressure of Al at 1500 K is 5 Pa. Use $m_{Al} = 4.48 \times 10^{-26}$ kg, $\rho_{Al} = 2700$ kg/m³. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Operating principle of thermal evaporation (3 marks):**
The source material is loaded in a **refractory crucible** (W, Mo, Ta or carbon). The chamber is evacuated to a base pressure of ~ $10^{-6}$ Torr. The source is heated resistively until the material's vapour pressure exceeds the chamber pressure (typically ~ $10^{-2}$ Torr). Atoms evaporate from the hot source, travel in straight lines (mean free path ≫ L), and condense on the cooler substrate. Films grow via Volmer–Weber, Frank–van der Merwe, or Stranski–Krastanov modes depending on surface energy balance.

**Step 2 — E-beam evaporation (3 marks):**
A tungsten filament emits electrons which are accelerated to **5–10 keV** and electromagnetically focused onto the source material in a **water-cooled copper crucible**. Only a small region melts locally; the crucible itself stays cold, so it does **not** outgas or contaminate the melt. Temperatures of **> 3000 °C** are easily achieved — sufficient to evaporate W (m.p. 3422 °C), Mo, SiO₂, and ceramic compounds that resistively-evaporated boats cannot handle.

**Step 3 — Why preferred for high-melting-point materials (1 mark):**
Because the energy is delivered by a focused e-beam rather than by the crucible itself, the source can be heated far above the crucible's melting point — enabling evaporation of refractory metals, oxides, and compounds.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Step 1 — Hertz–Knudsen flux derivation (4 marks):**
For an ideal gas at the source surface with number density $n = p/(k_B T)$ and mean normal velocity $\langle \vert v_z \vert \rangle = \sqrt{k_B T / (2\pi m)}$:

$$
\Phi_{evap} = n \langle \vert v_z \vert \rangle = \dfrac{p}{\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}}\ \text{atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

**Step 2 — Substrate flux with geometric dilution (1 mark):**
For a substrate of area $A_s$ at distance $L$ normal to a small source:

$$
\Phi_{sub} = \Phi_{evap} \cdot \dfrac{A_s}{L^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution (1 mark):**
With $p = 5$ Pa, $T = 1500$ K, $m = 4.48 \times 10^{-26}$ kg:

$$
k_B T = 1.38\times10^{-23} \cdot 1500 = 2.07\times10^{-20}\ \text{J}
$$

$$
\Phi_{evap} = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{2\pi \cdot 4.48\times10^{-26} \cdot 2.07\times10^{-20}}} = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{5.83\times10^{-45}}} \approx 6.55\times10^{21}\ \text{atoms/(m}^2\cdot\text{s)}
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric factor (0.5 marks):**
$A_s = 0.5 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ m², $L = 0.2$ m:

$$
f = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{0.04} = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 5 — Deposition rate (0.5 marks):**

$$
v_{dep} = \dfrac{\Phi_{sub} \cdot m}{\rho} = \dfrac{6.55\times10^{21} \cdot 1.25\times10^{-3} \cdot 4.48\times10^{-26}}{2700} \approx 1.36\times10^{-10}\ \text{m/s} \approx 0.14\ \text{nm/s}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\Phi_{evap} \approx 6.55 \times 10^{21}$ atoms/(m²·s); deposition rate $\approx 0.14$ nm/s.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **sputtering process** with a labelled diagram. Discuss the role of the **cathode dark space** and **secondary electron emission**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Define **sputtering yield**. Using the semi-empirical Matsunami–Meyer relation, justify why $Y$ is maximised for **incident ion energy around 500 eV** and **Ar⁺ as the projectile** for most metallic targets. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Process description (2 marks):**
A low-pressure inert gas (typically Ar, ~ $10^{-2}$ Torr) is introduced into a vacuum chamber. A high negative DC voltage (–300 to –1000 V) is applied to the target (cathode). The electric field ionises the Ar gas, creating a glow-discharge plasma. Positive Ar⁺ ions are accelerated onto the target. Momentum transfer knocks target atoms loose; these atoms travel to the substrate and form a film.

**Step 2 — Cathode dark space (2 marks):**
The **cathode dark space** (also called the *Crookes dark space*) is a narrow region (~ 1 mm at $10^{-2}$ Torr) immediately adjacent to the target where almost no light is emitted. It is the region of strongest electric field — essentially the "ion-accelerator" of the system. The voltage drop across the dark space ($\approx$ full cathode voltage) accelerates Ar⁺ ions to the energy needed for sputtering. **No significant charge neutralisation occurs here** — hence the darkness (no excitation of gas atoms).

**Step 3 — Secondary electron emission (2 marks):**
Each incident Ar⁺ ion liberates, on average, **0.05 to 0.2 secondary electrons** from the target surface. These electrons are accelerated *away* from the cathode into the plasma region, where they collide with Ar atoms and sustain ionisation. Thus, **secondary electrons are the plasma-sustaining mechanism** in DC sputtering. If their yield is too low, plasma collapses; magnetron sputtering deliberately traps them near the target with a magnetic field to boost yield.

**Step 4 — Diagrammatic content (1 mark):**
*(Include block diagram: Target (–V) → Dark space → Negative glow (plasma) → Anode/Substrate; indicate Ar⁺ flux going left, electrons going right, sputtered atoms going to substrate.)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
**Sputtering yield** $Y$ is defined as the *average number of target atoms ejected from the target surface per incident ion*. Dimensionless; ranges from ~ 0.1 (light ions, low energy) to ~ 50 (heavy ions, optimum energy, light targets like Be).

**Step 2 — Matsunami–Meyer relation (2 marks):**

$$
Y(E) = 0.042\, \dfrac{\alpha\, Q\, s_n(\varepsilon)}{U_s}\left(1 - \sqrt{\dfrac{E_{th}}{E}}\right)
$$

where $\varepsilon = E \cdot \dfrac{M_i}{M_t + M_i} \cdot \dfrac{a_{TF}}{Z_i Z_t e^{2}}$, $U_s$ = sublimation energy, $\alpha$, $Q$ are empirical parameters.

**Step 3 — Why yield peaks around 500 eV (3 marks):**
- Below ~ $E_{th}$ (≈ 10–40 eV) → $Y = 0$; the ion cannot dislodge an atom.
- 50–500 eV → $Y$ rises sharply because the collision cascade has enough energy to dislodge multiple atoms. The function $\left(1 - \sqrt{E_{th}/E}\right)$ saturates to ~ 1 above 500 eV.
- Above ~ 1–10 keV → $Y$ *decreases* — ions penetrate deep into the target; most energy is deposited *below* the surface, where atoms cannot escape. Some of this energy also goes into **ion implantation** rather than sputtering.
- Hence the **maximum practical yield** is in the **400–1000 eV** range.

**Step 4 — Why Ar⁺ is the standard projectile (1 mark):**
Ar is **inert** (no chemical reaction with target), **heavy** enough (mass 40 amu) to give a reasonable $\gamma$ transfer factor with most metals (good momentum transfer), **cheap** (≈ 1% of Earth's atmosphere), and has a convenient ionisation cross-section for plasma sustainment. The combination of physics and economics makes Ar⁺ the universal projectile for sputtering.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "vapour pressure" with "chamber pressure".** Vapour pressure is a property of the *source material*; chamber pressure is set by pumps + gas inlet. Mixing them costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting units in derivation problems.** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missed unit. Always state $p$ in Pa, $T$ in K, $m$ in kg, $\Phi$ in atoms/(m²·s).
> 3. **Writing "step coverage is good in evaporation."** This is the *opposite* — evaporation has poor step coverage due to line-of-sight transport. Wrong statement = 0 marks for that sub-question.
> 4. **Skipping the dark-space explanation in sputtering answers.** Examiners specifically test this; missing it loses up to 3 marks.
> 5. **Not drawing the plasma regions in long answers.** A labelled diagram is mandatory for full marks in any 7-mark process explanation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **PVD = physical conversion of solid → vapour → solid film** under vacuum.
- [x] Two main families: **Evaporation** (heat-driven) and **Sputtering** (ion-momentum-driven).
- [x] **Evaporation** uses thermal energy → atoms arrive at ~ 0.1 eV → soft, line-of-sight films.
- [x] **E-beam evaporation** uses a 5–10 keV focused electron beam to melt refractory materials.
- [x] **Sputtering** uses Ar⁺ plasma at ~ $10^{-2}$ Torr; atoms arrive at 1–100 eV → dense, adherent, conformal films.
- [x] **Cathode dark space** sustains the cathode-voltage drop and accelerates Ar⁺ ions.
- [x] **Secondary electrons** sustain the plasma by ionising neutral Ar.
- [x] **Sputtering yield $Y$** = ejected atoms / incident ion; peaks at ~ 500 eV for Ar⁺ on most metals.
- [x] **Hertz–Knudsen flux** $\Phi_{evap} = p / \sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}$.
- [x] **Mean free path** $\lambda = k_B T / (\sqrt{2}\,\pi d^{2} P)$ — must be >> chamber dimension.
- [x] **Magnetron sputtering** adds a magnetic field → denser plasma, higher rate, less damage.
- [x] **RF sputtering** is mandatory for **dielectric targets** (SiO₂, AlN, etc.).
- [x] **Step coverage**: poor in evaporation, excellent in sputtering.
- [x] **Adhesion**: weak in evaporation, strong in sputtering (higher particle energy).
- [x] Always specify **chamber pressure**, **base vacuum**, **deposition rate**, and **substrate temperature** in numerical answers.
- [x] **Threshold energy** $E_{th} \approx U_s / \gamma$; ~ 10–40 eV for Ar⁺ on metals.
- [x] **MBE** = ultra-high-vacuum evaporation with RHEED — yields monolayer precision for 2D materials and quantum heterostructures.

---

> **End of Module 2 — Topic: Introduction to Methods of Fabrication of Nano-layers: Physical Vapour Deposition — Evaporation and Sputtering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chemical Vapour Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Chemical Vapour Deposition # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)** is a vacuum deposition method used to produce high-quality, high-performance solid thin films and coatings. In CVD, the substrate is exposed to one or more volatile precursors that react and/or decompose on the substrate surface to produce the desired deposit. The process is fundamentally a **chemical transformation** in which gaseous reactants (precursors) are transported via a carrier gas into a reaction chamber, where they undergo thermally activated chemical reactions to form a non-volatile solid film on a heated substrate, with volatile by-products being removed from the chamber by the gas flow. In the context of **Nanoelectronics**, CVD is a cornerstone technique for fabricating nanoscale structures such as **carbon nanotubes (CNTs)**, **graphene**, **2D transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDCs)**, **quantum dots**, and ultra-thin dielectric layers (e.g., high-$\kappa$ **HfO₂**, **Al₂O₃**) with sub-nanometre precision in thickness control. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: For PEMRT753, students must understand CVD under two classifications — (a) **Thermal CVD** variants (APCVD, LPCVD) and (b) **Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD)**, with particular emphasis on how these methods enable nanoscale thin-film growth in modern CMOS, MEMS, and 2D-material synthesis. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing outside on a cold winter morning and you breathe out. The water vapour from your breath condenses on a nearby cold glass window, forming a thin film of water droplets. Now imagine if, instead of just condensing, the vapour could undergo a **chemical transformation** when it touched the cold surface, turning into a metallic mirror or a ceramic coating. That is essentially what CVD does — but in an ultra-clean, vacuum-sealed, and precisely heated environment. **The "Cooking" Analogy (Most Intuitive for Engineers):** Think of CVD as a precision cooking process: - **The recipe (precursor chemistry)** is the list of ingredients (gases) that react. - **The oven (reactor chamber)** maintains a controlled temperature and pressure. - **The baking tray (substrate)** is where the thin film "cooks" and deposits. - **The exhaust hood (gas outlet)** removes waste gases (by-products). - **The cook's timing (process parameters)** controls the thickness and quality. Just as a chef can make a wafer-thin crêpe or a thick pancake by controlling heat, time, and batter flow, a CVD engineer can grow films ranging from a **single atomic layer** (≈ 0.3 nm) to several micrometres thick by controlling temperature, pressure, precursor flow rate, and deposition time. ## 1.3 Core Physical & Chemical Phenomena CVD relies on five fundamental phenomena that occur sequentially: 1. **Mass Transport** of reactants to the substrate surface via forced gas flow or diffusion. 2. **Gas-Phase Reactions** in the boundary layer above the substrate (homogeneous reactions). 3. **Surface Adsorption** of reactant species onto the heated substrate. 4. **Surface Diffusion and Reaction** (heterogeneous reactions) — the actual film-forming step. 5. **Desorption and Removal** of gaseous by-products away from the deposition zone. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Operating Metrics for Industrial CVD:** > - **Operating Temperature**: 200 °C to 1100 °C depending on precursor chemistry. > - **Operating Pressure**: 10⁻⁴ Pa (UHV-CVD) to 10⁵ Pa (APCVD). > - **Typical Growth Rate**: 1 nm/min to 1000 nm/min. > - **Film Purity**: ≥ 99.9999 % (6N grade) achievable. > - **Precursor State**: Must be volatile at temperatures below the deposition temperature. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Mechanism The CVD process is fundamentally a sequence of seven well-defined steps, each critical to film quality: ### **Step 1 — Precursor Selection and Vaporization** A volatile precursor compound containing the desired film material is selected. For example, to deposit **silicon dioxide (SiO₂)**, one may use **silane (SiH₄)** + **oxygen (O₂)**, or **tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS, Si(OC₂H₅)₄)**. The precursor must have a sufficiently high vapour pressure at moderate temperatures (typically > 100 Pa at 25 °C for liquid precursors). ### **Step 2 — Carrier Gas Transport** An inert carrier gas (most commonly **N₂**, **Ar**, or **H₂**) transports the precursor vapour from the source bubbler or gas cylinder into the reaction chamber through mass flow controllers (MFCs). Typical carrier gas flow rates range from **10 to 500 sccm** (standard cubic centimetres per minute). ### **Step 3 — Gas-Phase Diffusion** The precursor molecules diffuse through the **boundary layer** — a thin, stagnant gas layer (typically 1–10 mm thick) immediately above the heated substrate where temperature and concentration gradients exist. The **boundary layer thickness ($\delta$)** is inversely proportional to the square root of the gas velocity: $$\delta \propto \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu \, x}{\rho \, u}}$$ where $\mu$ is gas viscosity, $\rho$ is gas density, $x$ is the distance from the leading edge, and $u$ is the free-stream velocity. ### **Step 4 — Adsorption on Substrate Surface** Precursor molecules physically or chemically adsorb onto the substrate. **Chemisorption** involves the formation of a true chemical bond with the surface (activation energy ≈ 0.5–2 eV), while **physisorption** involves weak van der Waals forces (activation energy < 0.1 eV). Chemisorbed species are preferred for high-quality epitaxial growth. ### **Step 5 — Surface Reactions (Heterogeneous Chemistry)** The adsorbed species undergo thermally activated chemical reactions, including decomposition, reduction, oxidation, or hydrolysis, forming the desired solid film. For example: - **SiH₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2 H₂** (at 400–500 °C) - **SiH₄ → Si + 2 H₂** (at 600–700 °C) ### **Step 6 — Nucleation and Film Growth** Initially, film nucleation occurs at energetically favourable sites (defects, grain boundaries, or seed layers). After the nucleation layer is formed, the film grows via **layer-by-layer (Frank-van der Merwe)**, **island (Volmer-Weber)**, or **layer-plus-island (Stranski-Krastanov)** mechanisms, depending on the lattice mismatch and surface energies. ### **Step 7 — By-Product Desorption and Exhaust** Volatile reaction by-products (e.g., **H₂**, **HCl**, **CO₂**) desorb from the surface and are swept out of the chamber by the carrier gas flow. Incomplete by-product removal leads to **film contamination** — a major defect mode. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter / Concept** | **Equation** | **Description / Units** | |---|---|---| | Boundary Layer Thickness | $\delta = 5.0 \left( \dfrac{\mu \, x}{\rho \, u} \right)^{1/2}$ | Depends on Reynolds number regime | | Growth Rate (Mass Transport Limited) | $G_{MT} = \dfrac{h_m \, C_g}{N \, \rho_f}$ | $h_m$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s), $C_g$ = gas concentration (mol/m³), $N$ = stoichiometric coeff., $\rho_f$ = film density (kg/m³) | | Growth Rate (Surface Reaction Limited) | $G_{SR} = k_s \, C_s$ | $k_s$ = surface reaction rate constant, $C_s$ = surface concentration | | Arrhenius Temperature Dependence | $k_s = k_0 \, \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{E_a}{R T} \right)$ | $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $T$ = temperature (K) | | Diffusion Coefficient (Chapman-Enskog) | $D = \dfrac{0.001858 \, T^{3/2}}{P \, \sigma_{AB}^{2} \, \Omega} \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{M_A} + \dfrac{1}{M_B}}$ | $P$ in atm, $\sigma$ in Å, $M$ in g/mol | | Sticking Coefficient | $S_c = \dfrac{\text{Number of atoms incorporated}}{\text{Number of atoms impinging}}$ | Typically $10^{-4}$ to 1 | | Grain Size (Scherrer Equation) | $D_{grain} = \dfrac{K \, \lambda}{\beta \, \cos\theta}$ | $K$ = 0.9, $\lambda$ = X-ray wavelength, $\beta$ = FWHM, $\theta$ = Bragg angle | | Knudsen Number | $K_n = \dfrac{\lambda_{mfp}}{L}$ | Determines regime: $K_n < 0.01$ (continuum), $K_n > 10$ (free molecular) | | Film Thickness | $d = G \times t$ | $G$ = growth rate, $t$ = deposition time | | Molar Flux (Hertz-Knudsen) | $J = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$ | Used in UHV-CVD and ALE | ## 2.3 Classification of CVD Variants | **CVD Variant** | **Pressure** | **Activation** | **Temperature** | **Typical Application** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **APCVD** (Atmospheric Pressure CVD) | 10⁵ Pa | Thermal | 400–1100 °C | Large-area coatings, solar cells | | **LPCVD** (Low Pressure CVD) | 10–100 Pa | Thermal | 550–900 °C | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, SiO₂ in ICs | | **UHVCVD** (Ultra-High Vacuum CVD) | < 10⁻⁴ Pa | Thermal | 400–800 °C | SiGe, GaAs heteroepitaxy | | **PECVD** (Plasma-Enhanced CVD) | 1–500 Pa | Plasma + Thermal | 200–400 °C | Low-T SiNₓ, SiO₂, a-Si:H | | **MOCVD** (Metal-Organic CVD) | 10²–10⁴ Pa | Thermal | 600–1100 °C | GaN, InGaN LEDs, 2D TMDCs | | **ALD** (Atomic Layer Deposition) | 10² Pa | Thermal / Plasma | 100–350 °C | Sub-nm high-$\kappa$ dielectrics | | **HWCVD** (Hot-Wire CVD) | 1–100 Pa | Catalytic Cracking | 1500–2200 °C wire | Nanocrystalline Si, diamond | | **RTCVD** (Rapid Thermal CVD) | 10²–10⁴ Pa | Lamp Heating | 600–1100 °C (transient) | Shallow junctions, silicide | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility in Nanoelectronics**: PECVD is the workhorse of **back-end-of-line (BEOL)** interconnect dielectric deposition in modern 7 nm and 5 nm CMOS technology nodes, where processing temperatures must stay below 400 °C to avoid damaging previously formed transistors and metal interconnects. ALD-CVD enables the deposition of **high-$\kappa$ gate dielectrics (HfO₂, ~1 nm EOT)** that form the gate insulator in modern FinFETs and Gate-All-Around (GAA) FETs. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility CVD is indispensable in modern semiconductor manufacturing for: - **Graphene growth** on copper foils using CH₄ + H₂ at 1000 °C (LPCVD) — a single monolayer of sp² carbon. - **CNT (Carbon Nanotube) synthesis** using C₂H₂ / C₂H₄ + H₂ + Fe catalyst at 700–900 °C — used in next-generation interconnects and sensors. - **2D MoS₂ / WS₂ growth** using Mo(CO)₆ + H₂S via MOCVD for atomically thin transistors. - **GaN/AlGaN high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs)** via MOCVD for 5G RF power amplifiers. - **Black Phosphorus (BP) deposition** via CVD for infrared photodetectors. - **Si₃N₄ charge-trap layers** in 3D NAND flash memory via LPCVD. - **Diamond-like Carbon (DLC)** via PECVD for tribological and biomedical coatings. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Arrhenius Temperature Dependence of Surface Reaction Rate The CVD surface reaction rate follows an Arrhenius-type temperature dependence. We begin from the kinetic theory of thermally activated chemical reactions. **Starting Statement:** For a thermally activated reaction, the rate constant $k$ is proportional to the Boltzmann factor $\exp(-E_a / k_B T)$ multiplied by a pre-exponential factor $k_0$. **Step (i)** — The probability that a molecule has sufficient energy $E_a$ to overcome the activation barrier is given by the Boltzmann distribution: $$ P(E \geq E_a) \propto \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T} \right) $$ **Step (ii)** — Multiply by the pre-exponential factor $k_0$, which represents the attempt frequency (typically $\sim 10^{13}$ Hz for molecular vibrations): $$ k = k_0 \, \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{E_a}{k_B T} \right) $$ **Step (iii)** — In chemistry, $E_a$ is conventionally expressed in J/mol, so $k_B$ is replaced by the universal gas constant $R = N_A \, k_B = 8.314$ J/(mol·K): $$ k_s = k_0 \, \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{E_a}{R T} \right) $$ **Step (iv)** — Taking the natural logarithm for plotting purposes: $$ \ln(k_s) = \ln(k_0) - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} $$ This is the form used in **Arrhenius plots** ($\ln k_s$ vs $1/T$) — a straight line with slope $-E_a/R$ and intercept $\ln k_0$. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: CVD Growth Rate in the Surface-Reaction-Limited Regime In the surface-reaction-limited regime (low temperature, low pressure, laminar flow), the growth rate is governed entirely by the kinetics of the surface reaction. **Step (i)** — The molar flux of precursor molecules arriving at the substrate surface by diffusion through the boundary layer is given by Fick's first law: $$ J = -D \, \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial y} \bigg\vert_{y=0} $$ **Step (ii)** — Approximating the concentration gradient across the boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ as linear: $$ \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial y} \approx \dfrac{C_g - C_s}{\delta} $$ **Step (iii)** — In the surface-reaction-limited regime, the surface concentration $C_s$ is much smaller than the gas-phase concentration $C_g$ (the surface reaction is slower than the supply), so $C_s \to 0$: $$ J \approx \dfrac{D \, C_g}{\delta} $$ **Step (iv)** — The film growth rate $G$ (in m/s) is related to the molar flux by: $$ G = \dfrac{J \, M}{N \, \rho_f} $$ where $M$ is the molar mass of the film, $N$ is the number of atoms per formula unit, and $\rho_f$ is the film density. **Step (v)** — Combining the above: $$ \boxed{\;G = \dfrac{D \, M \, C_g}{\delta \, N \, \rho_f}\;} $$ **Step (vi)** — For a thermal CVD process where $D = D_0 \exp(-E_d / RT)$ and $\delta$ is nearly constant (in laminar flow with constant gas velocity): $$ \ln G \propto -\dfrac{E_d}{R T} $$ In the **mass-transport-limited regime** (high temperature), the rate saturates because every molecule arriving at the surface reacts immediately. The two regimes are clearly distinguishable in a $\ln G$ vs $1/T$ plot as a transition from a sloped (kinetics-limited) region to a flat (transport-limited) plateau. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Boundary Layer Thickness for Forced Convection (Laminar Flow) For laminar flow over a flat plate (the standard model for a horizontal CVD reactor), we apply the integral momentum boundary layer equation. **Step (i)** — The local skin friction coefficient for a flat plate in laminar flow is: $$ C_f(x) = \dfrac{0.664}{\sqrt{Re_x}} $$ where the local Reynolds number is $Re_x = \rho u x / \mu$. **Step (ii)** — The displacement thickness $\delta^*$ is approximately: $$ \delta^*(x) = 1.72 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/2} $$ **Step (iii)** — The momentum thickness $\theta$ is: $$ \theta(x) = 0.664 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/2} $$ **Step (iv)** — The total boundary layer thickness (where velocity reaches 99% of free-stream) is: $$ \delta(x) = 5.0 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/2} = 5.0 \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu \, x}{\rho \, u}} $$ **Step (v)** — Substituting typical values for a CVD reactor ($\mu = 3 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $\rho = 1$ kg/m³, $u = 0.1$ m/s, $x = 0.1$ m): $$ \delta = 5.0 \sqrt{\dfrac{(3 \times 10^{-5})(0.1)}{(1)(0.1)}} = 5.0 \sqrt{3 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} = 27 \text{ mm} $$ This is consistent with experimentally observed boundary layer thicknesses in industrial LPCVD reactors. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Sticking Coefficient and Film Density The **sticking coefficient** $S_c$ is the probability that an impinging atom is incorporated into the film: **Step (i)** — Rate of arrival of atoms per unit area: $$ R_{arrive} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} $$ (Hertz-Knudsen equation, where $P$ is partial pressure, $m$ is atomic mass.) **Step (ii)** — Rate of incorporation: $$ R_{incorporate} = S_c \cdot R_{arrive} $$ **Step (iii)** — Film density is related to $S_c$ by: $$ \rho_f = S_c \cdot \rho_{bulk} $$ A sticking coefficient of $S_c = 1$ yields a fully dense film equal to bulk density, while $S_c < 1$ produces a porous or amorphous film — typical of low-temperature PECVD silicon nitride (SiN$_x$). ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of CVD Regimes | **Regime** | **Dominant Limitation** | **Activation Energy Observed** | **Temperature Dependence** | **Pressure Dependence** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Surface Reaction-Limited (Low-T)** | Slow surface chemistry | High ($E_a \approx 1$–$3$ eV) | Strong ($G \propto e^{-E_a/RT}$) | Weak | | **Mass Transport-Limited (High-T)** | Slow gas diffusion | $\approx 0$ (apparent) | Weak | Strong ($G \propto 1/\sqrt{P}$) | | **Nucleation-Limited (Initial)** | Slow nucleation kinetics | Moderate to high | Moderate | Strong | --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Thermal CVD Reactor System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Precursor Source
Gas Cylinder or Liquid Bubbler] A2[Carrier Gas Source
N2 or Ar or H2] A3[Dopant / Reactant Gas
O2 or NH3] MFC1[Mass Flow
Controller 1] MFC2[Mass Flow
Controller 2] MFC3[Mass Flow
Controller 3] MIX[Gas Mixing
Manifold] REACTOR[Reaction Chamber
with Heated Substrate
T = 200 to 1100 C] SUB[Substrate
Wafer or Foil] HEATER[Heater
Resistive or RF Induction] PUMP[Vacuum Pump
Rotary plus Turbo] EXHAUST[Exhaust and
Scrubber System] FILM[Deposited Thin Film
on Substrate] A1 --> MFC1 A2 --> MFC2 A3 --> MFC3 MFC1 --> MIX MFC2 --> MIX MFC3 --> MIX MIX --> REACTOR HEATER --> REACTOR REACTOR --> SUB SUB --> FILM REACTOR --> PUMP PUMP --> EXHAUST ``` ## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Sequential CVD Process Steps ```mermaid flowchart TD STEP1[Step 1: Precursor Selection
and Vaporization] STEP2[Step 2: Carrier Gas Transport
via MFC] STEP3[Step 3: Gas-Phase Diffusion
across Boundary Layer] STEP4[Step 4: Surface Adsorption
Physisorption or Chemisorption] STEP5[Step 5: Surface Reaction
Decomposition or Oxidation] STEP6[Step 6: Nucleation and
Film Growth] STEP7[Step 7: By-Product Desorption
and Removal] STEP1 --> STEP2 STEP2 --> STEP3 STEP3 --> STEP4 STEP4 --> STEP5 STEP5 --> STEP6 STEP6 --> STEP7 STEP7 --> OUTCOME{Quality of
Thin Film} OUTCOME -- High Quality --> OK[Uniform, Dense, Low Defect Film] OUTCOME -- Low Quality --> FAIL[Pin Holes, Contamination, Poor Adhesion] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic: Temperature-Regime Map for CVD ```mermaid flowchart LR LOWT[Low Temperature
T less than 400 C] MIDT[Mid Temperature
400 to 700 C] HIGHT[High Temperature
700 to 1100 C] LOWT --> REGIME1[Surface Reaction Limited
High Activation Energy
PECVD and ALD Region] MIDT --> REGIME2[Transition Region
Mixed Control
LPCVD Region] HIGHT --> REGIME3[Mass Transport Limited
Low Apparent Activation Energy
APCVD and MOCVD Region] REGIME1 --> APP1[Amorphous or Nanocrystalline Films
a-Si, SiNx, SiO2] REGIME2 --> APP2[Polycrystalline Films
Poly Si, Si3N4] REGIME3 --> APP3[Epitaxial Films
Single Crystal Si, GaN, Graphene] ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: PECVD Configuration (Plasma-Enhanced CVD) ```mermaid flowchart TD RF[RF Power Supply
13.56 MHz] MATCH[Impedance Matching
Network] ELECTRODE1[Upper Electrode
RF Driven] ELECTRODE2[Lower Electrode
Grounded with Substrate Heater] GASIN[Precursor Gas Inlet
SiH4 plus NH3 plus N2] PLASMA[Glow Discharge Plasma
between Electrodes] SUBSTRATE[Heated Substrate
T = 200 to 400 C] DEPOSIT[Deposited Thin Film
SiN, SiO2, a-Si] PUMP2[Vacuum Pump] RF --> MATCH MATCH --> ELECTRODE1 GASIN --> PLASMA ELECTRODE1 --- PLASMA PLASMA --- ELECTRODE2 ELECTRODE2 --> SUBSTRATE SUBSTRATE --> DEPOSIT ELECTRODE2 --> PUMP2 ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define Chemical Vapour Deposition. List any four variants of CVD.** **Model Answer:** Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD) is a thin-film deposition technique in which volatile precursors undergo thermally activated chemical reactions on or near a heated substrate to form a non-volatile solid film, with volatile by-products being removed by the carrier gas flow. Four variants of CVD: 1. **APCVD** (Atmospheric Pressure CVD) 2. **LPCVD** (Low Pressure CVD) 3. **PECVD** (Plasma-Enhanced CVD) 4. **MOCVD** (Metal-Organic CVD) ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What is a precursor in CVD? List two essential properties of a good CVD precursor.** **Model Answer:** A **precursor** is the volatile chemical compound that is transported into the reaction chamber and undergoes decomposition or reaction to form the desired thin film. Essential properties of a good CVD precursor: 1. **High volatility** at moderate temperatures (vapour pressure > 100 Pa at room temperature or slightly above). 2. **High chemical purity** and clean decomposition (no incorporation of impurities like C, N, or O into the film). 3. (Bonus) **Low toxicity** and **good thermal stability** at storage conditions but clean decomposition at deposition temperature. --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **(a) Explain in detail the different steps involved in the Chemical Vapour Deposition process with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The CVD process consists of seven sequential steps, each critical to film quality: **Step 1 — Precursor Selection and Vaporization:** A volatile precursor containing the desired film material is selected. For example, **SiH₄** is used to deposit silicon, **TiCl₄ + NH₃** for TiN, and **WF₆ + H₂** for tungsten. The precursor is vaporized either from a gas cylinder or by bubbling an inert carrier gas through a heated liquid bubbler. **Step 2 — Carrier Gas Transport:** An inert carrier gas (N₂, Ar, or H₂) carries the precursor vapour through **mass flow controllers (MFCs)** into the reactor chamber. Flow rates typically range from **10 to 500 sccm**. **Step 3 — Gas-Phase Diffusion:** Precursor molecules diffuse across the **boundary layer** — a thin stagnant gas film above the heated substrate where concentration and temperature gradients exist. The boundary layer thickness $\delta$ depends on the gas velocity, viscosity, and position along the substrate. **Step 4 — Surface Adsorption:** Reactant species adsorb onto the substrate surface via **physisorption** (weak van der Waals) or **chemisorption** (chemical bond formation). Chemisorption is preferred for epitaxial growth as it provides stronger surface binding and ordered nucleation. **Step 5 — Surface Reactions (Heterogeneous Chemistry):** The adsorbed species undergo thermally activated reactions — decomposition, oxidation, reduction, or hydrolysis — forming the desired solid film. Example: $$ \text{SiH}_4 \text{(g)} + \text{O}_2 \text{(g)} \xrightarrow{450\,°C} \text{SiO}_2 \text{(s)} + 2\,\text{H}_2 \text{(g)} $$ **Step 6 — Nucleation and Film Growth:** Initial nucleation occurs at energetically favourable sites. Subsequent growth proceeds by **layer-by-layer (Frank-van der Merwe)**, **island (Volmer-Weber)**, or **Stranski-Krastanov** mechanisms, depending on lattice mismatch and surface energies. **Step 7 — By-Product Desorption and Removal:** Volatile reaction products (H₂, HCl, CO₂) desorb from the surface and are swept out by the carrier gas to the exhaust system, which typically includes a **scrubber** for neutralization of toxic gases. **Block Diagram (already provided in Section 4.1).** #### **(b) Compare LPCVD and PECVD. Discuss any four advantages of CVD over PVD. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Comparison Table — LPCVD vs PECVD:** | **Parameter** | **LPCVD** | **PECVD** | |---|---|---| | Operating Pressure | 10–100 Pa | 1–500 Pa | | Activation Energy Source | Thermal only | Plasma + thermal | | Substrate Temperature | 550–900 °C | 200–400 °C | | Film Quality | Excellent uniformity, step coverage | Good, but can have H incorporation | | Throughput | High (large batch) | Lower (plasma uniformity limits) | | Typical Films | Polysilicon, Si₃N₄, SiO₂ | a-Si:H, SiO₂, SiN$_x$, DLC | **Advantages of CVD over PVD:** 1. **Excellent step coverage** — CVD can conformally coat high-aspect-ratio features (e.g., trenches of 10:1 AR) due to gas-phase diffusion. PVD (sputtering) suffers from shadowing effects. 2. **High film purity** — CVD films routinely achieve > 99.9999 % purity; this is critical for gate dielectrics. 3. **Wide range of compositions** — CVD can deposit compound semiconductors (GaN, SiC), oxides, nitrides, carbides, and 2D materials in a single reactor, whereas PVD is largely limited to elemental metals. 4. **Good control over stoichiometry** — By adjusting precursor partial pressures, the composition of multi-component films (e.g., Si$_x$N$_y$, Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$N) can be precisely tuned. This is essential for bandgap engineering in heterostructure devices. 5. (Bonus) **Scalability for industrial production** — CVD supports large-batch processing (e.g., 150 wafers simultaneously in a furnace LPCVD reactor). --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### **(a) With neat schematics, explain the working of a Plasma-Enhanced CVD (PECVD) system. Discuss why PECVD is preferred for low-temperature deposition in nanoelectronics. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Working of PECVD:** PECVD uses a **radio-frequency (RF) plasma** (typically 13.56 MHz) to provide the additional activation energy required to dissociate precursor molecules. This allows the substrate to be maintained at much lower temperatures than in thermal CVD, which is critical for temperature-sensitive substrates and BEOL processing in CMOS technology. **Schematic (already provided in Section 4.4).** The PECVD reactor operates as follows: 1. The substrate is loaded into the chamber, which is then evacuated to a base pressure of $\sim 10^{-4}$ Pa. 2. Precursor gases (e.g., SiH₄ + NH₃ + N₂ for SiN$_x$) are introduced through mass flow controllers. 3. Pressure is maintained at 1–500 Pa. 4. RF power (typically 50–1000 W) is applied to the upper electrode through an **impedance-matching network**. The lower electrode (substrate stage) is grounded and also heated to 200–400 °C. 5. The RF field ionizes the gas, creating a **glow discharge plasma** containing electrons, ions, radicals, and neutral molecules. 6. Energetic electrons (mean energy 1–10 eV) collide with precursor molecules, dissociating them into chemically reactive **radicals** (e.g., SiH₃·, NH₂·). 7. These radicals diffuse to the substrate and undergo surface reactions to form the desired film at much lower temperatures than would be possible thermally. **Why PECVD is preferred for low-T deposition in nanoelectronics:** 1. **Plasma dissociation enables bond breaking without high thermal energy.** Electron-impact dissociation has activation energy effectively zero, replacing the thermal $E_a$ of ~2 eV. 2. **BEOL compatibility:** Modern CMOS interconnect stacks (Cu, low-$\kappa$ dielectrics) cannot tolerate temperatures > 400 °C. PECVD SiN and SiO₂ cap layers are deposited at 350 °C without damaging underlying metal lines. 3. **3D NAND and FinFET fabrication:** High-aspect-ratio vertical structures require low-T conformal dielectric liners, which PECVD provides. 4. **Amorphous and hydrogenated films:** PECVD can deposit **hydrogenated amorphous silicon (a-Si:H)** for TFT displays and **DLC (diamond-like carbon)** for tribological coatings. #### **(b) Derive the Arrhenius temperature dependence of the surface reaction rate in CVD. A SiH₄ + O₂ → SiO₂ + 2H₂ CVD process at 750 °C has an activation energy of 1.8 eV. If the rate constant at 750 °C is $3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s, estimate the rate constant at 850 °C. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation (already provided in Section 3.1):** $$ k_s = k_0 \, \exp\!\left( -\dfrac{E_a}{R T} \right) $$ **Numerical Solution:** **Step (i)** — Write the Arrhenius equation at two temperatures: $$ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = -\dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right) $$ **Step (ii)** — Substitute the values: - $E_a = 1.8$ eV $= 1.8 \times 96{,}485 = 173{,}673$ J/mol - $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) - $T_1 = 750 + 273 = 1023$ K - $T_2 = 850 + 273 = 1123$ K - $k_1 = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s **Step (iii)** — Compute the temperature term: $$ \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{1023} - \dfrac{1}{1123} = \dfrac{1123 - 1023}{1023 \times 1123} = \dfrac{100}{1{,}148{,}829} = 8.704 \times 10^{-5} \text{ K}^{-1} $$ **Step (iv)** — Compute the exponential term: $$ \dfrac{E_a}{R} = \dfrac{173{,}673}{8.314} = 20{,}889 \text{ K} $$ $$ \dfrac{E_a}{R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right) = 20{,}889 \times 8.704 \times 10^{-5} = 1.818 $$ **Step (v)** — Compute $k_2$: $$ k_2 = k_1 \cdot e^{1.818} = (3.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times 6.16 = 2.156 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} $$ **Step (vi)** — Final Answer: $$ \boxed{\;k_2 \approx 2.16 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s}\;} $$ This is approximately a **6-fold increase** in rate constant for a 100 °C temperature rise — consistent with the strong temperature sensitivity of the surface-reaction-limited regime. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:** > > 1. **Confusing APCVD with LPCVD** — Students often interchange the two. APCVD operates at atmospheric pressure (10⁵ Pa), while LPCVD operates at 10–100 Pa. Mixing these up will cost you 1–2 marks. > > 2. **Writing "CVD is a physical process"** — This is a **fatal conceptual error**. CVD is fundamentally a **chemical process** driven by reactions. PVD (sputtering, evaporation) is physical; CVD is chemical. > > 3. **Forgetting units in Arrhenius problems** — Activation energy $E_a$ must be in **J/mol** (not eV) when using $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K). Always convert: $1 \text{ eV} = 96{,}485$ J/mol. > > 4. **Confusing boundary layer with diffusion layer** — In CVD, both terms refer to the same stagnant gas film above the substrate. Use them interchangeably, but the formal term is **boundary layer** or **stagnant layer**. > > 5. **Missing the precursor transport step** — Many students jump straight to surface reaction without explaining **carrier gas transport and gas-phase diffusion**, which carry **2 marks** in a 7-mark question. > > 6. **Forgetting by-product removal** — A complete answer must include the **desorption and exhaust** step. Skipping it loses 1 mark. > > 7. **Spelling "PECVD" as "PECDV" or "PECVD"** — Typos in acronyms are penalized by KTU examiners. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition**: CVD = chemical reaction of volatile precursors on a heated substrate to form a solid thin film. - **Three key regimes**: (1) Surface-reaction-limited (low T, $G \propto e^{-E_a/RT}$), (2) Mass-transport-limited (high T, $G$ saturates), (3) Nucleation-limited (initial growth). - **Master equation**: $k_s = k_0 \exp(-E_a / RT)$ — learn this cold. - **Growth rate formula**: $G = D \, M \, C_g / (\delta \, N \, \rho_f)$. - **Boundary layer**: $\delta \propto \sqrt{x/u}$ — decreases with higher gas velocity. - **CVD variants by pressure**: APCVD (10⁵ Pa) > LPCVD (10–100 Pa) > UHVCVD (< 10⁻⁴ Pa). - **PECVD**: Uses RF plasma at 13.56 MHz; substrate T = 200–400 °C; enables BEOL processing. - **MOCVD**: Uses metal-organic precursors (e.g., TMGa, TMAl); standard for GaN LED/epitaxy. - **ALD**: Sub-class of CVD using sequential self-limiting surface reactions for sub-nm films. - **Applications**: Graphene, CNTs, 2D TMDCs, high-$\kappa$ dielectrics, GaN HEMTs, 3D NAND liners. - **Industrial gases**: N₂, Ar, H₂ as carriers; SiH₄, TiCl₄, WF₆, TMGa, NH₃, O₂ as reactants. - **Number to remember**: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $1$ eV $= 96{,}485$ J/mol; Avogadro $= 6.022 \times 10^{23}$. - **Advantage over PVD**: Superior step coverage, conformal coating of high-aspect-ratio features. - **Disadvantage vs PVD**: Higher temperatures, toxic/corrosive precursors, more complex equipment. - **Exam tip**: Always include a labelled **block diagram** in 7-mark questions — KTU examiners award up to **2 marks** for a clean schematic. - **Remember**: In the surface-reaction-limited regime, growth rate increases strongly with temperature; in the mass-transport-limited regime, it is nearly temperature-independent. ---

topics.title: Molecular Beam Epitaxy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Molecular Beam Epitaxy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)** is an ultra-high vacuum (UHV) thin-film deposition technique used to grow single-crystalline epitaxial layers with atomic-layer precision. The process involves directing collimated beams of constituent atoms or molecules (produced by thermal evaporation from effusion cells) onto a heated single-crystal substrate, where they undergo surface migration, incorporation, and layer-by-layer crystalline growth under conditions far from thermodynamic equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**: MBE is a non-equilibrium physical vapor deposition process operating at a base pressure of **10⁻¹⁰ Torr** (≈ **1.33 × 10⁻⁸ Pa**), where the mean free path of molecular beams exceeds the source-to-substrate distance, ensuring a collision-free, line-of-sight transport regime.

The technique was pioneered by **Alfred Y. Cho** and **John R. Arthur** at **Bell Telephone Laboratories** in **1968–1971** and remains the gold standard for fabricating heterostructures, quantum wells, and superlattices in nanoelectronics.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a perfect sandcastle on a beach, but instead of using handfuls of wet sand, you have a **magnetic "atomic tweezer"** that picks up *one* iron-coated grain at a time and places it in exactly the right position. MBE works on the same principle, but at the atomic scale:

- **The Vacuum Chamber** → A pristine, dust-free cleanroom where stray atoms cannot contaminate the structure.
- **The Effusion Cells** → "Atomic ovens" that heat source materials (like Gallium or Arsenic) until they evaporate.
