# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Quantum Well and explain the concept of quantum confinement. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Quantum Well (QW)** is a heterostructure nanodevice in which charge carriers are confined in one spatial dimension to a length scale comparable to their de Broglie wavelength (typically 1–100 nm). It is fabricated by sandwiching a thin layer of a low-bandgap semiconductor (e.g., GaAs) between two layers of a higher-bandgap material (e.g., AlGaAs).

**Quantum confinement** refers to the spatial restriction of carriers in one direction, forcing their wavefunctions to form standing waves. This quantizes the energy in the confinement direction into discrete sub-bands $E_n$, while carriers remain free in the other two dimensions. The energy of the n-th level in an infinite well is:

$$
E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^* L^2}
$$

The corresponding density of states becomes a step function, leading to enhanced optical and electronic properties exploited in QW lasers and HEMTs.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between 3D, 2D, 1D, and 0D density of states. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Dimensionality | System | DOS Expression | Shape |
|----------------|--------|----------------|-------|
| 3D | Bulk semiconductor | $g_{3D}(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$ | Parabolic rise |
| 2D | Quantum Well | $g_{2D}(E) = m^*/\pi\hbar^2$ (constant) | Step function |
| 1D | Quantum Wire | $g_{1D}(E) \propto 1/\sqrt{E}$ | Divergent peaks |
| 0D | Quantum Dot | $g_{0D}(E) \propto \delta(E - E_n)$ | Discrete delta spikes |

The progressive change from a continuous to discrete DOS as dimensionality decreases is the foundation of nanostructure physics.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — Question A

**(a)** Derive the energy eigenvalues and normalized wavefunctions for an electron confined in an infinite one-dimensional potential well of width $L$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A GaAs quantum well ($m^* = 0.067\,m_0$) has width $L = 8$ nm. Calculate the ground-state energy $E_1$, first excited state $E_2$, and the energy required to excite an electron from $E_1$ to $E_2$. Compare this with $k_B T$ at 300 K and comment. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Inside the well, the Schrödinger equation is:

$$
-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = E\psi \quad \text{for } 0 \le z \le L
$$

**Step 2:** The general solution is $\psi(z) = A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz)$, where $k = \sqrt{2m^*E}/\hbar$.

**Step 3:** Applying $\psi(0) = 0$ yields $B = 0$. Applying $\psi(L) = 0$ yields $\sin(kL) = 0$, so:

$$
k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

**Step 4:** Energy eigenvalues:

$$
E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2m^* L^2}
$$

**Step 5:** Normalization:

$$
\int_0^L A^2 \sin^2\!\left(\frac{n\pi z}{L}\right) dz = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\psi_n(z) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L}}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi z}{L}\right); \qquad E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2m^* L^2}
$$

### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $L = 8$ nm $= 8 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- $m^* = 0.067 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg $= 6.103 \times 10^{-32}$ kg
- $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s

**Step 1 — Compute $E_1$:**

$$
E_1 = \frac{\pi^2 (1.055 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2 \times 6.103 \times 10^{-32} \times (8 \times 10^{-9})^2}
$$

$$
E_1 = \frac{1.0985 \times 10^{-67}}{7.812 \times 10^{-48}} = 1.406 \times 10^{-20}\,\text{J}
$$

Converting to eV:

$$
E_1 = \frac{1.406 \times 10^{-20}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.0878\,\text{eV}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $E_2$:**

$$
E_2 = 4 E_1 = 4 \times 0.0878 = 0.3512\,\text{eV}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute transition energy:**

$$
\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = 3 E_1 = 0.2634\,\text{eV}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with thermal energy:**

$$
k_B T \text{ at } 300\,\text{K} = 0.0259\,\text{eV}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment:**

Since $\Delta E = 0.263\,\text{eV} \gg k_B T = 0.026\,\text{eV}$ (by a factor of ≈ 10), thermal excitations at room temperature **cannot** populate the second sub-band. The ground-state carriers are robustly confined. This is why quantum well lasers exhibit **temperature-stable threshold currents** and why intersub-band transitions in QWs are observable at room temperature — they are well-separated in energy from the thermal bath.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` — Question B

**(a)** With a neat energy-band diagram, explain the formation of a quantum well in a GaAs/AlGaAs heterostructure. Discuss how the conduction band offset $\Delta E_C$ gives rise to bound sub-band states. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the 2D density of states for a quantum well and explain why the DOS becomes a step function. Sketch and compare the DOS of bulk (3D) vs quantum well (2D) materials. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for (a):

A GaAs/AlGaAs quantum well is formed by epitaxially growing a thin layer of **GaAs** (bandgap $E_g = 1.42$ eV) between two thick layers of **Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As** (bandgap $E_g \approx 1.85$ eV for $x = 0.3$). Due to the band alignment, the conduction band edge of GaAs lies **below** that of AlGaAs by $\Delta E_C \approx 0.30$ eV (for $x = 0.3$, the band offset ratio is approximately 60:40 for conduction:valence).

Electrons in the conduction band see this as a **rectangular potential well** of depth $V_0 = \Delta E_C$ and width $L$. Solving the Schrödinger equation inside the well yields discrete bound sub-bands $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_N$ where $N$ depends on $L$ and $V_0$. The wavefunction **penetrates** slightly into the AlGaAs barriers (for finite $V_0$), but remains predominantly localized in the GaAs layer.

The bound states satisfy the transcendental equation:

$$
k\tan(kL/2) = \kappa \quad \text{(even parity)}
$$

As $L$ decreases or $V_0$ increases, more states become bound and more tightly confined — this is the **quantum size effect**.

### Model Solution for (b):

The 2D DOS is derived by counting in-plane k-states. For a single sub-band:

$$
g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2} \quad \text{for } E > E_n
$$

For multiple sub-bands, the total DOS is:

$$
g_{2D}^{total}(E) = \frac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2}\sum_{n=1}^{N}\Theta(E - E_n)
$$

**Why a step function?** Because each sub-band has a **constant** DOS contribution once it is energetically accessible ($E > E_n$). Below $E_1$, no states exist (forbidden zone); between $E_1$ and $E_2$, one sub-band contributes a constant; between $E_2$ and $E_3$, two sub-bands contribute; and so on.

**Comparison sketch (described):**

- **3D (bulk):** Smooth parabolic curve $g_{3D}(E) \propto \sqrt{E}$, no gaps.
- **2D (QW):** Step-like staircase with flat plateaus at heights $m^*/\pi\hbar^2, 2m^*/\pi\hbar^2, \ldots$.

The step-function DOS concentrates carriers at specific energies, enhancing the **joint density of states** for optical transitions and resulting in sharper absorption edges and lower laser thresholds.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the effective mass $m^*$:** Students frequently write $E_n = n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2 / (2m_0 L^2)$ using the free electron mass. Always substitute the **semiconductor effective mass** (e.g., 0.067 $m_0$ for GaAs).
> 
> 2. **Mixing up quantization and free directions:** QWs confine carriers in **ONE** direction only. The other two remain free with continuous kinetic energy. Writing "1D system" loses 1 mark.
> 
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Express final energies in **eV**, not joules, when the question asks for "the energy in eV." Use $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
> 
> 4. **Normalization skips:** The full derivation must include the **normalization step** $A = \sqrt{2/L}$. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 
> 5. **Infinite vs. Finite confusion:** The infinite well has **all** $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ as bound states. The finite well has only a **finite** number. State this distinction explicitly.
> 
> 6. **Boundary conditions:** Always write $\psi(0) = \psi(L) = 0$ **before** deriving the quantization condition. Examiners want to see the logic chain.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quantum Wells**

- **Definition:** Quantum Well = 1D-confined carrier system (2D electron gas).
- **Infinite well energy:** $E_n = \dfrac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^* L^2}$.
- **Infinite well wavefunction:** $\psi_n(z) = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{L}}\sin\!\left(\dfrac{n\pi z}{L}\right)$.
- **Zero-point energy:** $E_1 \neq 0$ — even ground state has confinement energy.
- **Quantization rule:** $kL = n\pi$ — half-wavelengths must "fit" the well.
- **Effective mass rule:** Always use $m^*$, never $m_0$, for semiconductor QWs.
- **Finite well:** Bound states satisfy transcendental equations; only finite number bound.
- **2D DOS:** Constant $g_{2D}(E) = m^*/\pi\hbar^2$ per sub-band.
- **Total 2D DOS:** Staircase function summing step contributions from each sub-band.
- **Quantum size effect:** $E_1 \propto 1/L^2$ — narrower wells → higher confinement energy.
- **Real-world uses:** QW lasers, HEMTs, QWIPs, MQW solar cells, QCSE modulators.
- **Material systems:** GaAs/AlGaAs, InGaAs/InP, Si/SiO$_2$, CdTe/HgCdTe.
- **Typical L:** 5–20 nm (much smaller than de Broglie wavelength at 300 K).
- **Comparison chain:** 3D DOS parabolic → 2D step → 1D peak → 0D delta — memorize this progression.
- **Energy scale check:** For GaAs QW with $L = 10$ nm, $E_1 \approx 56$ meV; $\Delta E_{1\to 2} \approx 168$ meV — much larger than $k_B T$ at room temperature.
- **Exam mantra:** *Always state quantization direction, always use effective mass, always normalize the wavefunction, always convert to eV.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Multiple Quantum Well (MQW)** is a periodic, low-dimensional heterostructure composed of alternating ultrathin layers (typically **2–20 nm**) of a narrow-bandgap semiconductor (the *well* material) sandwiched between layers of a wider-bandgap semiconductor (the *barrier* material). The structure is termed "multiple" because the active region contains **many stacked quantum wells** rather than a single one. The well thickness $L_w$ is comparable to or smaller than the **de Broglie wavelength** of the charge carrier ($\lambda_d \approx 20\text{–}50$ nm), producing **quantum confinement** in the growth direction (z-axis) while carriers remain free to move in the in-plane x-y plane, resulting in a **2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** or 2-Dimensional Hole Gas (2DHG) inside each well.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Critical Distinction**
> - **Multiple Quantum Well (MQW):** Barriers are **thick** ($L_b \gtrsim 10$ nm) so that wavefunctions in adjacent wells **do NOT couple**. Each well behaves as an independent 1-D potential well.
> - **Superlattice (SL):** Barriers are **thin** ($L_b \lesssim 5$ nm) so that wavefunctions **tunnel** through the barriers, splitting each discrete level into a **miniband** (Kroemer–Esaki formulation, 1970).

The potential energy profile $V(z)$ is a **periodic square-wave function** along the growth direction:

$$V(z) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \le z \le L_w \quad (\text{well region}) \\ V_0, & L_w \le z \le L_w + L_b \quad (\text{barrier region}) \end{cases}$$

with a period $d = L_w + L_b$ repeated $N$ times.

## 1.2 Band Alignment & Material Classification

MQWs are classified based on the relative band offsets of the two semiconductors:

| Type | Conduction Band | Valence Band | Example |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Type-I (straddling)** | Well | Well | GaAs / Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As, InGaAs / InP |
| **Type-II (staggered)** | Well | Barrier | InP / InGaAsSb, Si / SiGe |
| **Type-II (misaligned)** | Barrier | Well | GaSb / InAs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type-I MQWs** are the most common in optoelectronics because **both electrons and holes are confined in the same physical region**, maximizing wavefunction overlap and optical transition probability (radiative recombination efficiency).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Staircase Analogy

Imagine a multi-story building (the **MQW stack**) where:
- Each **floor** is a *quantum well* — a low-energy living space where the resident (electron) is free to walk in any direction (x-y plane) but cannot leave the floor vertically.
- Each **floor ceiling/ceiling thickness** ($L_b$) is the *barrier* — if the ceiling is thick (concrete slab), floors are isolated (MQW); if the ceiling is thin (glass partition), sounds (wavefunctions) leak through to neighbors (Superlattice).
- The **building's height** determines the **number of stacked wells** $N$.
- The **elevator button energy** is the *conduction band offset* $\Delta E_c$.

When light (photon) shines on the building, electrons get "promoted" from the ground floor (valence band) to a higher floor (conduction band) — this is the **interband transition** in a QW laser. The energy of the photon equals the *stair height* (bandgap $E_g$ + confinement energy $E_n$).

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (for reference)

- **Reduced Planck constant** $\hbar = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Electron rest mass** $m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg
- **Effective mass of GaAs** $m^* = 0.067\,m_0$
- **Boltzmann constant** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Elementary charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of Quantum Confinement in MQWs

The energy band diagram of a bulk 3-D semiconductor is parabolic and continuous. When the well thickness $L_w$ becomes comparable to the **de Broglie wavelength** $\lambda_d = h/p = h/\sqrt{2m^*E}$ of the thermalized carrier, the carrier's wavefunction in the z-direction is **forced to form standing waves**. This imposes the boundary conditions:

1. The wavefunction $\psi(z)$ must vanish at the well boundaries (for an infinite well) → $\psi(0) = \psi(L_w) = 0$.
2. Only discrete sinusoidal modes are allowed → quantized energy levels.

**The 'Why':** Heisenberg's uncertainty principle dictates that confining a particle to a region of size $L_w$ produces momentum uncertainty $\Delta p_z \sim \hbar/L_w$, and hence kinetic energy $E_n \sim \hbar^2/(2m^*L_w^2)$.

**The 'How':** Solving the time-independent Schrödinger equation with the boundary conditions yields the quantized subband energies.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physical Operation

- **Step 1 — Epitaxial Growth:** MQWs are grown by **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)** or **Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition (MOCVD)** with sub-monolayer precision. Each layer thickness is controlled to within **±0.1 nm**.
- **Step 2 — Confinement Setup:** The conduction band offset $\Delta E_c$ and valence band offset $\Delta E_v$ create a potential well of depth $V_0$. For GaAs/Al$_{0.3}$Ga$_{0.7}$As, $\Delta E_c \approx 0.24$ eV, $\Delta E_v \approx 0.16$ eV (60:40 rule).
- **Step 3 — Quantization:** Carrier wavefunctions become sinusoidal in the well and decaying exponentials in the barrier.
- **Step 4 — Subband Formation:** Each well supports discrete subbands $E_1, E_2, \dots$ for electrons and $E_{hh1}, E_{lh1}, \dots$ for heavy and light holes.
- **Step 5 — Optical/Electrical Response:** Devices exploit transitions between subbands (intersubband) or across the modified effective bandgap (interband).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units / Constants |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_n = \dfrac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^* L_w^2}$ | Energy of $n$-th subband (infinite well) | $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ |
| 2 | $g_{2D}(E) = \dfrac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2} \sum_n \Theta(E - E_n)$ | 2-D Density of States (step function) | $m^*$ in kg, $\Theta$ is Heaviside step |
| 3 | $g_{3D}(E) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$ | 3-D bulk DOS (parabolic, for comparison) | $\text{eV}^{-1}\,\text{m}^{-3}$ |
| 4 | $\Delta E_{1\to 2} = \dfrac{3\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^* L_w^2}$ | Intersubband transition energy (n=1→2) | Joules |
| 5 | $E_g^{eff} = E_g + E_1^e + E_1^{hh}$ | Effective bandgap of QW | eV |
| 6 | $\lambda_{photon} = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g^{eff} \text{ [eV]}}\; \mu\text{m}$ | Emission wavelength | $\mu$m |
| 7 | $V_0 = q\,\Delta E_c$ (electron well depth) | Barrier height in eV | eV |
| 8 | $k\,L_w\tan(kL_w/2) = \sqrt{2m^*V_0/\hbar^2 - k^2}\,L_w$ | Transcendental eqn. (finite well, even) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $E_F} = \dfrac{n_s \pi \hbar^2}{m^*}$ | Fermi level in 2DEG (per subband) | $n_s$ = sheet carrier density (m$^{-2}$) |
| 10 | $N_{total} = N_w \cdot L_w$ | Active material "thickness equivalent" | $N_w$ = number of wells |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is DOS step-like?** In a 2-D system, the in-plane wavevector $\vec{k}_\parallel = (k_x, k_y)$ is continuous (2 degrees of freedom), but energy depends only on $\vert \vec{k}_\parallel \vert^2$. Counting states in k-space gives a **constant** density per unit area per unit energy. Each subband $E_n$ contributes one step, producing the famous **staircase DOS** of MQWs.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **QW Lasers:** MQW active regions reduce threshold current by ~**3–5×** and increase differential gain compared to bulk DH lasers. The 980 nm pump laser for Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers (EDFAs) uses InGaAs/AlGaAs MQWs.
- **High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs):** The undoped AlGaAs/InGaAs MQW channel hosts a high-mobility 2DEG ($\mu \sim 10^4$ cm²/V·s), used in satellite receivers and radar.
- **Quantum-Well Infrared Photodetectors (QWIPs):** Exploit intersubband absorption in doped MQWs for thermal imaging in the **3–14 μm** range.
- **Blue & Green LEDs:** GaN/InGaN MQWs are the active region of modern solid-state lighting (Nobel Prize 2014, Nakamura, Akasaki, Amano).
- **MQW Solar Cells:** Intermediate-band solar cells using MQW stack to harvest sub-bandgap photons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Energy Levels in an Infinite Quantum Well

**Problem Setup:** Solve the time-independent Schrödinger equation for a particle of effective mass $m^*$ trapped in an infinite 1-D potential well of width $L_w$.

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi(z)}{dz^2} = E\psi(z), \quad 0 \le z \le L_w$$

with boundary conditions $\psi(0) = 0$ and $\psi(L_w) = 0$ (wavefunction vanishes at infinitely high walls).

**Step 1 — Rearrange the Schrödinger equation:**

$$\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + k^2\psi = 0, \quad \text{where } k^2 = \frac{2m^*E}{\hbar^2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the general solution:**

$$\psi(z) = A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz)$$

**Step 3 — Apply $\psi(0) = 0$:**

$$\psi(0) = A\sin(0) + B\cos(0) = B = 0$$

Thus, $\psi(z) = A\sin(kz)$.

**Step 4 — Apply $\psi(L_w) = 0$:**

$$A\sin(kL_w) = 0$$

For a non-trivial solution ($A \ne 0$), we require:

$$\sin(kL_w) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad kL_w = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the wavevector $k_n$:**

$$k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L_w}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back to obtain the quantized energy $E_n$:**

$$E_n = \frac{\hbar^2 k_n^2}{2m^*} = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\left(\frac{n\pi}{L_w}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{E_n = \frac{n^2 \pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m^* L_w^2}}$$

**Step 7 — Normalization (for completeness):**

$$\int_0^{L_w} \vert A\sin(k_n z)\vert^2 dz = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L_w}}$$

Hence the normalized eigenfunctions are:

$$\psi_n(z) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L_w}}\,\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi z}{L_w}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** Energy scales as $1/L_w^2$ — halving the well width **quadruples** the subband energy (critical for tuning laser wavelength).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — 2-D Density of States for MQWs

**Goal:** Show that a particle confined in $z$ but free in $x, y$ has a **constant (step-like) DOS** in energy.

**Step 1 — Total energy of carrier in n-th subband:**

$$E = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_x^2}{2m^*} + \frac{\hbar^2 k_y^2}{2m^*} = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m^*}$$

where $k_\parallel^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2$.

**Step 2 — Count states in k-space within area $A$ (periodic boundary conditions):**

Each allowed state occupies an area $(2\pi/A)$ in the 2-D k-plane. Therefore, the number of states with $k_\parallel \le k$:

$$N(k) = 2 \times \frac{\pi k^2}{(2\pi/A)} = \frac{A k^2}{2\pi}$$

(The factor 2 accounts for spin degeneracy.)

**Step 3 — Express $k$ in terms of energy $E$ above the subband edge $E_n$:**

$$k = \sqrt{\frac{2m^*(E - E_n)}{\hbar^2}}, \quad E > E_n$$

**Step 4 — Number of states per unit area with energy less than $E$:**

$$\frac{N(E)}{A} = \frac{m^*(E - E_n)}{2\pi \hbar^2}, \quad E > E_n$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate with respect to $E$ to obtain the 2-D DOS per unit area:**

$$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{d(N/A)}{dE} = \frac{m^*}{2\pi\hbar^2} \times 2 = \frac{m^*}{\pi\hbar^2}, \quad E > E_n$$

$$\boxed{g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m^*}{\pi\hbar^2} \sum_{n=1}^\infty \Theta(E - E_n)}$$

**Step 6 — Total DOS = sum of step contributions:**

$$g_{2D}^{total}(E) = \frac{m^*}{\pi\hbar^2}\big[\Theta(E-E_1) + \Theta(E-E_2) + \Theta(E-E_3) + \cdots\big]$$

**Step 7 — Comparison with bulk 3-D DOS:**

$$g_{3D}(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E} \quad \text{(smoothly rising)}$$

> The 2-D DOS is **flat** between subband edges and **jumps** by $m^*/(\pi\hbar^2)$ at each $E_n$. This staircase structure **peaks the gain spectrum** in QW lasers, dramatically reducing threshold current.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Effective Bandgap of a MQW

**Step 1 — The bulk bandgap is $E_g$ (e.g., 1.42 eV for GaAs at 300 K).**

**Step 2 — Quantization pushes the lowest electron level **up** by $E_1^e$:**

$$E_1^e = \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m_e^* L_w^2}$$

**Step 3 — Quantization pushes the highest heavy-hole level **down** by $E_1^{hh}$:**

$$E_1^{hh} = \frac{\pi^2 \hbar^2}{2 m_{hh}^* L_w^2}$$

**Step 4 — The effective bandgap (lowest interband transition) is therefore:**

$$E_g^{eff} = E_g + E_1^e + E_1^{hh} = E_g + \frac{\pi^2\hbar^2}{2L_w^2}\!\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_{hh}^*}\right)$$

**Step 5 — The corresponding photon emission wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g^{eff}} = \frac{1.24\;\mu\text{m·eV}}{E_g^{eff}\,(\text{eV})}$$

**Numerical Example (GaAs QW, $L_w = 10$ nm, $m_e^* = 0.067\,m_0$, $m_{hh}^* = 0.50\,m_0$):**

$$E_1^e = \frac{\pi^2 \times (1.0546 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2 \times 0.067 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (10 \times 10^{-9})^2} = 5.74 \times 10^{-22}\;\text{J} = 35.8\;\text{meV}$$

$$E_1^{hh} = \frac{\pi^2 \times (1.0546 \times 10^{-34})^2}{2 \times 0.50 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times (10 \times 10^{-9})^2} = 4.81 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J} = 3.0\;\text{meV}$$

$$E_g^{eff} = 1.420 + 0.0358 + 0.0030 = 1.459\;\text{eV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda \approx 850\;\text{nm}$$

This is the standard **850 nm GaAs/AlGaAs QW laser wavelength** used in CD players and short-range fiber optics.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Criterion for MQW vs Superlattice (Coupling Test)

**Step 1 — Penetration depth of wavefunction in barrier (evanescent decay length):**

$$\kappa = \frac{\sqrt{2m^*(V_0 - E_n)}}{\hbar}$$

**Step 2 — For the wavefunction to be essentially zero at the next well's boundary:**

$$\kappa L_b \gtrsim 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_b \gtrsim \frac{3\hbar}{\sqrt{2m^*(V_0 - E_1)}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example (GaAs/AlGaAs, $V_0 - E_1 \approx 0.20$ eV):**

$$L_b^{min} \approx \frac{3 \times 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \times 0.067 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.20 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}} \approx 8.5\;\text{nm}$$

> Therefore, for this system, **$L_b > 10$ nm** ensures independent wells (MQW behavior), while **$L_b < 5$ nm** leads to inter-well coupling and **miniband formation** (Superlattice regime).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — MQW Band Diagram & State Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Substrate GaAs] --> B[Buffer Layer 500 nm]
    B --> C[Barrier AlGaAs 10 nm]
    C --> D[Well GaAs 8 nm]
    D --> E[Barrier AlGaAs 10 nm]
    E --> F[Well GaAs 8 nm]
    F --> G[Barrier AlGaAs 10 nm]
    G --> H[Well GaAs 8 nm]
    H --> I[Barrier AlGaAs 10 nm]
    I --> J[Cap Layer GaAs 100 nm]
    D --> K[Subband E1 e]
    D --> L[Subband E2 e]
    F --> M[Subband E1 e]
    H --> N[Subband E1 e]
    K -.independent wells.-> M
    M -.independent wells.-> N
```

**Interpretation:** Three identical GaAs wells (8 nm) separated by 10 nm AlGaAs barriers → $\kappa L_b > 3$ ensures no wavefunction coupling → standard MQW laser active region.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a MQW Laser

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BIAS[Biasing Module]
        V1[Forward Bias 1.5 to 2.5 V]
    end
    subgraph MQW[Multiple Quantum Well Active Region]
        W1[Well 1 with Subband E1 E2]
        W2[Well 2 with Subband E1 E2]
        W3[Well N with Subband E1 E2]
    end
    subgraph CONFINEMENT[Optical Confinement]
        CL1[Upper Cladding AlGaAs p type]
        CL2[Lower Cladding AlGaAs n type]
    end
    subgraph CAVITY[Fabry Perot Cavity]
        MIR1[Mirror R equals 0.3]
        MIR2[Mirror R equals 0.95]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Photon Output]
        PH[Stimulated Emission lambda equals 850 nm]
    end
    V1 --> MQW
    MQW --> CONFINEMENT
    CONFINEMENT --> CAVITY
    CAVITY --> OUTPUT
    W1 -.parallel gain.-> W2
    W2 -.parallel gain.-> W3
```

**Interpretation:** Multiple QWs share the optical mode in the cavity; the staircase DOS concentrates gain at the band-edge, giving a **higher differential gain** and **lower threshold current** than bulk DH lasers.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison Chart — MQW vs Superlattice vs Bulk

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulk Semiconductor] -->|3D DOS parabolic| A1[g3D proportional to sqrt E]
    A -->|thin film| B[Single Quantum Well]
    B -->|stack N wells| C[Multiple Quantum Well]
    C -->|thin barrier coupling| D[Superlattice]
    B -->|2D DOS staircase| B1[g2D constant per subband]
    C -->|isolated subbands| C1[Discrete levels in each well]
    D -->|miniband formation| D1[Dispersion E versus kz]
    A1 --> A2[Continuous spectrum]
    B1 --> B2[Sharp optical transitions]
    C1 --> C2[High gain peak]
    D1 --> D2[Negative differential resistance Bloch oscillator]
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Energy Level Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONDUCTION[Conduction Band]
        CE1[Ec offset equals 0.24 eV]
        E2[Subband E2]
        E1[Subband E1 ground]
    end
    subgraph VALENCE[Valence Band]
        VH1[Ev offset equals 0.16 eV]
        HH1[Heavy hole HH1]
        HH2[Heavy hole HH2]
    end
    E1 -.E1e plus E1hh.-> HH1
    E2 -.intersubband transition.-> E1
    E1 -.interband laser transition.-> HH1
    E2 -.interband laser transition.-> HH2
    CE1 --- E2
    E2 --- E1
    E1 --- HH1
    HH1 --- HH2
    HH2 --- VH1
```

**Interpretation:** The blue arrows show the **two principal optical transitions**:
1. **Interband** ($E_1 \to HH_1$): used in QW lasers/LEDs.
2. **Intersubband** ($E_2 \to E_1$): used in QWIPs and quantum cascade lasers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**Define a Multiple Quantum Well (MQW) structure. How does it differ from a Superlattice?**

**Model Answer:**

A Multiple Quantum Well (MQW) is a periodic heterostructure consisting of alternating ultrathin layers of a narrow-bandgap semiconductor (the *well*, width $L_w \sim 2\text{–}20$ nm) and a wider-bandgap semiconductor (the *barrier*, width $L_b \gtrsim 10$ nm). Carriers are quantum-confined in the z-direction, forming 2-D subbands in each well.

| Feature | MQW | Superlattice |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Barrier thickness $L_b$ | Thick ($\gtrsim 10$ nm) | Thin ($\lesssim 5$ nm) |
| Inter-well coupling | Negligible | Strong |
| Energy spectrum | Discrete levels per well | **Minibands** (Bloch-like) |
| Transport | Independent wells | Coherent tunneling |

**(3 marks — KTU key: definition 1, MQW characteristics 1, MQW vs SL distinction 1)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**Explain the step-like density of states in a 2-D MQW system and state its significance in semiconductor lasers.**

**Model Answer:**

In a MQW, the carrier is free to move in the x-y plane (continuous $k_x, k_y$) but confined in z. The total energy is:

$$E = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m^*}$$

Counting the number of states in a circular 2-D k-space region of area $\pi k^2$ per subband gives:

$$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{m^*}{\pi\hbar^2} \sum_n \Theta(E - E_n)$$

**Significance:** Unlike the 3-D bulk DOS which rises as $\sqrt{E}$, the 2-D DOS is **constant** between subband edges and **jumps** at each $E_n$. In a laser, this **concentrates the gain spectrum** at a single energy, yielding a **higher peak gain** and a **3–5× lower threshold current** than bulk double-heterojunction lasers.

**(3 marks — KTU key: derivation outline 1, staircase shape 1, laser significance 1)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]

**Derive the quantized energy levels for an electron in an infinite quantum well of width $L_w$. Hence explain how MQWs are used in quantum-well lasers and HEMTs.**

#### **Part (a) — Derivation of Quantized Energy (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

Consider an electron of effective mass $m^*$ confined in an infinite potential well of width $L_w$ along the z-direction:

$$V(z) = 0, \quad 0 \le z \le L_w; \qquad V(z) = \infty, \quad \text{elsewhere}$$

The time-independent Schrödinger equation inside the well:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = E\psi$$

$$\Rightarrow \frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + k^2\psi = 0, \quad k = \frac{\sqrt{2m^*E}}{\hbar}$$

General solution:

$$\psi(z) = A\sin(kz) + B\cos(kz)$$

Boundary condition $\psi(0) = 0 \Rightarrow B = 0$, so $\psi(z) = A\sin(kz)$.

Boundary condition $\psi(L_w) = 0 \Rightarrow A\sin(kL_w) = 0$, so $kL_w = n\pi$ ($n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$).

Therefore:

$$k_n = \frac{n\pi}{L_w} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_n = \frac{\hbar^2 k_n^2}{2m^*} = \frac{n^2\pi^2\hbar^2}{2m^*L_w^2}$$

The normalized eigenfunctions:

$$\psi_n(z) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{L_w}}\sin\!\left(\frac{n\pi z}{L_w}\right)$$

**(7 marks — KTU key: Schrödinger eqn 1, general solution 1, boundary conditions 2, final expression 2, normalization 1)**

#### **Part (b) — MQW Applications (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

**1. Quantum-Well (QW) Lasers (4 marks):**
- The MQW active region (e.g., 3–5 GaAs wells with AlGaAs barriers) replaces the bulk active layer of a DH laser.
- The 2-D staircase DOS **peaks the gain** at the band edge, providing higher differential gain $\partial g/\partial n$.
- Result: threshold current density drops from $\sim 1$ kA/cm² (bulk) to $\sim 50$ A/cm² (MQW); temperature stability improves (high $T_0$); wavelength tunability by varying $L_w$ (smaller $L_w$ → shorter $\lambda$).

**2. High Electron Mobility Transistors (HEMTs) (3 marks):**
- The MQW structure (e.g., AlGaAs/InGaAs) creates a triangular potential well at the heterointerface.
- The 2DEG that forms in the undoped InGaAs well is **spatially separated from ionized donors** (modulation doping), drastically reducing ionized-impurity scattering.
- Room-temperature mobility reaches $\mu \sim 10{,}000$ cm²/V·s (vs. $\sim 8000$ cm²/V·s in doped channels), enabling **low-noise, high-frequency** microwave and mm-wave applications (radar, satellite, 5G).

**(7 marks — KTU key: QW laser mechanism 2, advantages 1, HEMT mechanism 2, mobility reasoning 2)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the band structure and energy levels of a Multiple Quantum Well. Discuss the type-I and type-II classifications with examples.
**(b)** Starting from the Schrödinger equation, derive the 2-D density of states of a MQW and compare it with the 3-D bulk density of states. Mention **two** device applications where the staircase DOS is exploited.

#### **Part (a) — Band Structure & Classification (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

A MQW consists of alternating layers of semiconductors A (well, bandgap $E_g^A$) and B (barrier, bandgap $E_g^B > E_g^A$) grown along the z-axis. The conduction band drops by $\Delta E_c$ and the valence band rises by $\Delta E_v$ inside the well, forming a periodic square-wave potential.

[See Section 4.1 and 4.4 Mermaid diagrams for schematic band structure.]

**Type-I (Straddling) — Most Common:**
- Both electrons and holes confined in the **same well** material.
- Example: **GaAs/Al$_x$Ga$_{1-x}$As**, **InGaAs/InP**, **InGaN/GaN (blue LEDs)**.
- Use: Lasers, LEDs, photodetectors, HEMTs.

**Type-II (Staggered):**
- Electrons confined in material A; holes confined in material B (or vice versa).
- Examples: **InP/InGaAsSb** (long-wavelength detectors), **GaSb/InAs** (T2SLs for MWIR).
- Use: Infrared detectors, cascade lasers.

**Type-II (Misaligned/Broken-Gap):**
- Conduction band of B lies **below** valence band of A → carriers spontaneously separate.
- Example: **InAs/GaSb** broken-gap superlattice.
- Use: Novel infrared devices, topological insulator studies.

**(7 marks — KTU key: band diagram 2, Type-I + example 2, Type-II + example 2, device relevance 1)**

#### **Part (b) — 2-D DOS Derivation & Applications (7 Marks)**

**Solution:**

For a carrier in the $n$-th subband of a MQW well:

$$E = E_n + \frac{\hbar^2 k_\parallel^2}{2m^*}, \quad k_\parallel^2 = k_x^2 + k_y^2$$

Number of states in a 2-D k-space circle of radius $k$ (area $\pi k^2$, including spin degeneracy):

$$N(E) = 2 \times \frac{\pi k^2}{(2\pi)^2/A} = \frac{A k^2}{2\pi}$$

Substituting $k^2 = 2m^*(E - E_n)/\hbar^2$:

$$\frac{N(E)}{A} = \frac{m^*(E - E_n)}{\pi\hbar^2}, \quad E > E_n$$

DOS per unit area:

$$g_{2D}(E) = \frac{d(N/A)}{dE} = \frac{m^*}{\pi\hbar^2}\,\Theta(E - E_n)$$

Summing over all subbands:

$$g_{2D}^{total}(E) = \frac{m^*}{\pi\hbar^2}\big[\Theta(E - E_1) + \Theta(E - E_2) + \Theta(E - E_3) + \cdots\big]$$

**Comparison with 3-D Bulk:**

$$g_{3D}(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$$

The 2-D DOS is **constant between subbands and rises in steps**, while the 3-D DOS rises as $\sqrt{E}$.

**Two device applications exploiting the staircase DOS:**

1. **Quantum-Well Lasers (QW Lasers):** The peaked DOS at $E_1$ concentrates the gain spectrum, reducing threshold current and improving temperature stability.
2. **Quantum Cascade Lasers (QCLs):** Intersubband transitions between MQW subbands emit mid- and far-infrared photons. The sharp, narrow DOS enables **single-mode, wavelength-engineered** emission from 3 µm to 300 µm.

**(7 marks — KTU key: setup 1, counting states 1, final expression 2, comparison 1, two applications 2)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting boundary conditions** when deriving $E_n$ → easy 2-mark loss. Always explicitly state $\psi(0) = \psi(L_w) = 0$.
> 2. **Mixing up MQW vs Superlattice** → KTU examiners dock 1 mark for any confusion. Remember: MQW = isolated wells (thick barriers); SL = coupled wells (minibands).
> 3. **Confusing intersubband vs interband transitions** in QWIPs vs QW lasers. Intersubband = within conduction band (infrared); interband = across the gap (visible/NIR).
> 4. **Not stating the 60:40 rule** for GaAs/AlGaAs band offsets: $\Delta E_c / \Delta E_v \approx 60/40$ — a common KTU sub-question.
> 5. **Ignoring the spin degeneracy factor of 2** in DOS derivations.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **MQW Definition:** Periodic stack of quantum wells with thick barriers (no coupling).
- **Superlattice Definition:** MQW with thin barriers → minibands form.
- **Infinite well energy:** $E_n = n^2\pi^2\hbar^2/(2m^*L_w^2)$.
- **2-D DOS:** $g_{2D}(E) = m^*/(\pi\hbar^2)$ per subband — **staircase** (vs. 3-D $\sqrt{E}$).
- **Effective bandgap:** $E_g^{eff} = E_g + E_1^e + E_1^{hh}$ (increases with decreasing $L_w$ → **blue shift**).
- **Intersubband transition:** $E_{n\to n+1} = (2n+1)\pi^2\hbar^2/(2m^*L_w^2)$.
- **Type-I vs Type-II:** Type-I — both carriers in same well (most common). Type-II — carriers in different wells.
- **MQW vs SQW:** MQWs amplify the active volume and saturable absorption; SQW has lower gain per photon.
- **MBE / MOCVD:** Growth techniques giving sub-nm precision.
- **Key applications:** QW lasers, HEMTs, QWIPs, QCLs, blue/green LEDs, MQW solar cells.
- **Material systems:** GaAs/AlGaAs (850 nm), InGaAs/InP (1550 nm), InGaN/GaN (blue/green).
- **Coupling criterion:** $\kappa L_b \gtrsim 3$ → MQW; $\kappa L_b \lesssim 1$ → SL.
- **60:40 rule (GaAs/AlGaAs):** $\Delta E_c \approx 0.6\,\Delta E_g$; $\Delta E_v \approx 0.4\,\Delta E_g$.
- **Differential gain boost:** Staircase DOS → $\partial g/\partial n$ up to ~**10×** higher than bulk → low-threshold lasers.
- **Effective mass table to memorize (GaAs):** $m_e^* = 0.067\,m_0$, $m_{hh}^* = 0.50\,m_0$, $m_{lh}^* = 0.087\,m_0$.
- **Emission wavelength formula:** $\lambda\,(\mu\text{m}) = 1.24 / E_g^{eff}\,(\text{eV})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Whenever a question asks "Discuss the significance of MQWs," always mention **three** things: (1) **staircase DOS → high gain**, (2) **blue shift of effective bandgap → wavelength tuning**, and (3) **size quantization → design of intersubband devices (QWIPs, QCLs)**. Examiners consistently reward all three.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modulation Doped Quantum Wells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Modulation Doped Quantum Wells ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Modulation Doped Quantum Well (MDQW)** is a heterostructure semiconductor device in which the dopant (donor or acceptor) atoms are placed in a **wider bandgap barrier material** that is physically separated from a **narrower bandgap quantum well region**. The conduction band offset (or valence band offset) at the heterointerface creates a triangular potential well, and the carriers (electrons for n-type, holes for p-type) supplied by the remote dopants **fall into and accumulate inside the well**, forming a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** or Two-Dimensional Hole Gas (2DHG). The canonical KTU-syllabus example is the **GaAs/AlₓGa₁₋ₓAs heterojunction**, where Si donors are placed inside the AlGaAs barrier, leaving the undoped GaAs well as the carrier conducting channel. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Historical Milestone:** Modulation doping was invented in **1978** by R. Dingle, H. L. Störmer, A. C. Gossard, and W. Wiegmann at **Bell Laboratories**. The discovery earned Störmer (along with Tsui and Laughlin) the **1998 Nobel Prize in Physics** for the Fractional Quantum Hall Effect, which was observed in a modulation-doped 2DEG. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **"The Fish Pond and the Feeding Pipe" Analogy** Imagine a narrow, deep **fish pond (the quantum well)** carved into a flat landscape, located far away from a **feeding pipe (the donor layer)** that pours fish (electrons) into the ground. 1. The **pond** (GaAs well) has low ground level — fish naturally roll down into it by gravity (the conduction band offset acts as a "hill"). 2. The **feeding pipe** (Si-doped AlGaAs) is uphill, on the raised terrace. 3. Fish poured from the pipe **swim downhill** into the pond — but **the feeding pipe itself never enters the pond**. 4. Because the pipe (ionized donors) is *spatially separated* from the fish (electrons), the fish do not collide with the pipe's walls. **Result:** The fish can swim at **extremely high speed** without being slowed down — analogous to **ultrahigh-mobility electrons** in the 2DEG. > This spatial separation of *ionized impurity centers* from *mobile carriers* is the **single most important design trick** in all of high-speed nanoelectronics. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics - **Lattice mismatch (GaAs/AlAs):** ≈ **0.13%** — nearly lattice-matched, allowing atomically sharp interfaces. - **Conduction band offset (ΔEc) at GaAs/Al₀.₃Ga₀.₀₇As:** ≈ **0.23 eV**. - **Typical 2DEG sheet density (nₛ):** **1 × 10¹⁵ m⁻² to 5 × 10¹⁵ m⁻²**. - **Low-temperature mobility (μ):** can exceed **10⁷ cm²/(V·s)** in ultra-clean MBE-grown samples. - **Effective mass of electron in GaAs:** m* ≈ **0.067 m₀**. - **Relative permittivity of GaAs:** εᵣ ≈ **12.9**. - **Elementary charge:** e ≈ **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**. - **Reduced Planck constant:** ℏ ≈ **1.055 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — Why It Works The success of modulation doping rests on **three coupled physics facts**: 1. **Bandgap engineering via heterostructure:** The wide-bandgap AlGaAs (Eg ≈ 1.85 eV for x = 0.3) presents a higher conduction band edge than the narrow-gap GaAs (Eg ≈ 1.42 eV). The discontinuity at the interface, ΔEc, forms a triangular notch. 2. **Thermodynamic equilibrium charge transfer:** Donor electrons in AlGaAs seek the lowest available energy. They lower their energy by *spatially transferring* into the GaAs side, leaving behind a sheet of positive donor ions in AlGaAs. 3. **Self-consistent field formation:** The transferred electrons create a sheet of negative charge; the leftover donor ions form a sheet of positive charge. Together they create a strong local electric field **F**, which **bends the conduction band** at the interface into a triangular well. Solving this self-consistently yields the **Fang–Howard variational ground state**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why mobility skyrockets at low temperature:** > At **T → 0 K**, phonon (lattice vibration) scattering vanishes. The *only* remaining scattering mechanism for the 2DEG is ionized impurity scattering. Because the donor ions sit in the AlGaAs barrier, **far from the wavefunction of the 2DEG** (which is localized within ~3–10 nm of the interface), the Coulomb scattering matrix element is dramatically reduced. Mobility can therefore reach values **~100× higher** than in a bulk-doped sample. ### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility Modulation-doped quantum wells are the **active channel** of virtually every modern high-speed and low-noise transistor: - **HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor)** — also called MODFET, TEGFET, or SDHT. - **pHEMT** (pseudomorphic HEMT) — adds an InGaAs well for higher electron velocity. - **mHEMT** (metamorphic HEMT) — used in mmWave and satellite receivers. - **Foundational structure for the integer and fractional quantum Hall effects** (1980, 1982). - **Quantum cascade lasers, quantum-dot IR photodetectors, single-electron transistors, and spin qubits in semiconductor quantum dots** all leverage this heterostructure. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sheet carrier density (ideal, charge balance) | $n_s = \dfrac{\varepsilon \, F_s}{e}$ | m⁻² ; $F_s$ = surface field | | 2 | Triangular-well subband energy (Stern–Howard) | $E_n \approx \left(\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\right)^{\!\!1/3} \!\cdot\! \left[\dfrac{3\pi e F}{2}\!\left(n + \tfrac{3}{4}\right)\right]^{2/3}$ | eV ; $n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$ | | 3 | Fang–Howard variational parameter | $b = \left[\dfrac{12\,m^*\,e^2\,n_s}{\varepsilon\,\hbar^2}\right]^{1/3}$ | m⁻¹ | | 4 | Ground-state (n=0) energy | $E_0 \approx \dfrac{9}{8}\!\left(\dfrac{e^2 \hbar F}{\sqrt{2 m^*}}\right)^{2/3}$ | eV (variational result) | | 5 | Effective well width (Fang–Howard) | $\langle z \rangle = 3/b$ | nm | | 6 | 2DEG areal density (band-offset model) | $n_s \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon \, \Delta E_c}{e^2 (d + \Delta d)}$ | m⁻² ; $d$ = spacer thickness | | 7 | Drude mobility relation | $\mu = \dfrac{e \tau}{m^*}$ | m²/(V·s) ; $\tau$ = scattering time | | 8 | Conductivity of 2DEG | $\sigma = n_s \, e \, \mu$ | S/m or S/□ (sheet) | | 9 | Cyclotron frequency (Quantum Hall) | $\omega_c = eB/m^*$ | rad/s | | 10 | Quantized Hall resistance | $R_{xy} = h/(i\,e^2)$ | Ω ; $i$ = integer / fractional index | > **Boundary conditions used:** > - At the heterointerface (z = 0): $\psi(0) = 0$ (infinite-barrier limit) or Airy-function matching (finite barrier). > - Deep inside the well (z → ∞): $\psi(z) \to 0$. > - Deep in the barrier (z → −∞): $\psi(z) \to 0$. > - Far from interface: electric field $F \to 0$ in the well interior; $F$ is **piecewise constant** in the depletion approximation. > **Numerical constants for quick plug-in:** > - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m > - $\hbar^2/m_0 = 3.81 \times 10^{-38}$ J·m²·rad²/s² (not needed if m* used directly) > - $m^*_{\text{GaAs}} = 0.067\, m_0 = 6.0 \times 10^{-32}$ kg ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Triangular Quantum Well from Poisson's Equation We consider an n-type modulation-doped AlGaAs/GaAs structure. Donors with density $N_d$ occupy $0 < z < d_s$ (where $d_s$ is the spacer thickness). The GaAs well occupies $z < 0$. We treat the 2DEG as a sheet of charge at $z = 0$. **Step 1 — Set up the 1-D Poisson equation** in the GaAs region ($z < 0$), assuming no free carriers and no charge there: $$\dfrac{d^2 V(z)}{dz^2} = -\dfrac{\rho(z)}{\varepsilon} = 0$$ Since the 2DEG has already been accounted for as the boundary condition at $z = 0$, the field on the GaAs side is **uniform and constant**: $$\dfrac{dV}{dz} = -F = \text{const.}$$ **Step 2 — Integrate to get the band bending (potential energy)** in the well: $$V(z) = -eFz \quad \text{for } z < 0$$ **Step 3 — Convert potential energy to conduction band edge** by adding the bulk conduction band level $E_c^{(0)}$ of GaAs: $$E_c(z) = E_c^{(0)} - eFz$$ This is a **linearly rising (triangular) potential** as we move into the well. The slope is $eF$, which gives the well its "triangular" name. **Step 4 — Quantify F using the boundary condition from Gauss's law** at $z = 0$. The displacement discontinuity equals the sheet charge: $$F = \dfrac{e \, n_s}{\varepsilon}$$ This couples the field to the carrier density — the very heart of self-consistency. > **Physical interpretation:** The electric field $F$ bends the band by ~$\Delta E_c$ over a length scale of $L \approx \Delta E_c / (eF)$, typically **5–15 nm** at the interface. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Ground State Energy via Fang–Howard Variational Method The Hamiltonian for an electron in the triangular well (z < 0) with infinite barrier at z = 0 is: $$\hat{H} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\dfrac{d^2}{dz^2} + eFz$$ **Step 1 — Choose a trial wavefunction** (Fang–Howard, 1966) satisfying $\psi(0) = 0$ and decaying as $z \to \infty$: $$\psi(z) = \sqrt{\dfrac{b^3}{2}}\, z \, e^{-bz/2}$$ The prefactor ensures $\int_0^{\infty} |\psi|^2 dz = 1$. The parameter $b > 0$ is the variational parameter we will optimize. **Step 2 — Compute the kinetic energy expectation value:** $$\langle T \rangle = \int_0^{\infty} \psi^* \!\left(-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\dfrac{d^2}{dz^2}\right)\! \psi \, dz$$ First derivative: $$\dfrac{d\psi}{dz} = \sqrt{\dfrac{b^3}{2}}\, e^{-bz/2}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{bz}{2}\right)$$ Second derivative: $$\dfrac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} = \sqrt{\dfrac{b^3}{2}}\, e^{-bz/2}\!\left(\dfrac{b^2 z}{4} - b\right)$$ Therefore: $$\langle T \rangle = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\!\int_0^{\infty}\!\psi \cdot \dfrac{d^2\psi}{dz^2}\,dz$$ After integration (using $\int_0^\infty z^2 e^{-bz} dz = 2/b^3$ etc.): $$\langle T \rangle = \dfrac{\hbar^2 b^2}{8 m^*}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the potential energy expectation value:** $$\langle U \rangle = eF \int_0^{\infty}\! z \,\vert \psi\vert^2 dz = eF \cdot \dfrac{b^3}{2}\int_0^{\infty}\! z^2 e^{-bz} dz = eF \cdot \dfrac{b^3}{2} \cdot \dfrac{2}{b^3} = \dfrac{3eF}{b}$$ **Step 4 — Total variational energy:** $$E(b) = \langle T \rangle + \langle U \rangle = \dfrac{\hbar^2 b^2}{8 m^*} + \dfrac{3eF}{b}$$ **Step 5 — Minimize with respect to b:** $$\dfrac{dE}{db} = \dfrac{\hbar^2 b}{4 m^*} - \dfrac{3eF}{b^2} = 0$$ Solving: $$b^3 = \dfrac{12\,m^*\,eF}{\hbar^2}$$ This is precisely the Fang–Howard optimal parameter quoted in the formula sheet (with $F = e n_s / \varepsilon$). **Step 6 — Substitute back to get the ground-state energy:** $$E_0 = \dfrac{\hbar^2 b^2}{8 m^*} + \dfrac{3eF}{b} = \dfrac{\hbar^2}{8m^*}\!\left(\dfrac{12 m^* eF}{\hbar^2}\right)^{2/3} + 3eF\!\left(\dfrac{\hbar^2}{12 m^* eF}\right)^{1/3}$$ After simplification: $$E_0 = \dfrac{9}{8}\!\left(\dfrac{e^2 \hbar^2 F^2}{2 m^*}\right)^{1/3} \cdot \text{(factor)} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; E_0 \approx \left(\dfrac{9 e \hbar F}{8}\right)^{\!2/3}\!\dfrac{1}{2m^*}$$ or equivalently in the canonical Stern form: $$E_0 \approx \left(\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\right)^{\!\!1/3}\!\left[\dfrac{9\pi eF}{8}\right]^{2/3}$$ This is the **ground subband energy** measured from the GaAs conduction band edge at the interface. > [!NOTE] > **Exponent Rule Check:** $E_0 \propto F^{2/3}$ — the ground-state energy grows only slowly with the field. Doubling $F$ increases $E_0$ by a factor of $\sim 1.59$, not $2$. This sub-linear dependence is characteristic of triangular quantum wells (as opposed to parabolic wells where $E \propto \sqrt{F}$). --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Higher Subbands (Airy's Function Solution) For excited states ($n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$), the exact solution of the Schrödinger equation in a linear potential is the **Airy function Ai**: $$\psi_n(z) = C_n \, \text{Ai}\!\left(\dfrac{z}{z_0} - a_n\right)$$ where $z_0 = \left(\hbar^2 / 2 m^* e F\right)^{1/3}$ is the characteristic length, and $a_n$ are the Airy zeros (negative numbers). The energy quantization is: $$E_n = -eF \cdot a_n \cdot z_0 = a_n\!\left(\dfrac{\hbar^2 e^2 F^2}{2m^*}\right)^{\!\!1/3}$$ with the first few zeros: $a_0 \approx +2.338$, $a_1 \approx +4.088$, $a_2 \approx +5.521$. Using the leading-order approximation $a_n \approx \left[\tfrac{3\pi}{2}(n+\tfrac{3}{4})\right]^{2/3}$ recovers the Stern–Howard expression quoted in the Formula Sheet. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of MDQW vs Bulk-Doped Channel | Parameter | Bulk-doped GaAs channel | Modulation-doped GaAs/AlGaAs QW | |---|---|---| | Dopant location | Same region as carriers | Spatially separated in AlGaAs | | Scattering at 4 K | High (ionized impurity) | Very low | | Mobility at 77 K | ~ 2 × 10⁵ cm²/(V·s) | ~ 10⁶ cm²/(V·s) | | Mobility at 4 K | ~ 5 × 10⁴ cm²/(V·s) | ~ 10⁷ cm²/(V·s) | | Carrier profile | 3-D bulk | 2-D sheet (quantum confined in z) | | Subband structure | None | Multiple quantized levels | | Confinement direction | None | z-direction (perpendicular to interface) | | Temperature stability | Poor mobility roll-off | Better, but persistent photoconductivity issue | | Primary failure mode | Impurity + phonon scattering | DX centers, parallel conduction | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Band Diagram of a Modulation-Doped GaAs/AlGaAs QW ```mermaid flowchart LR A["AlGaAs Barrier Layer
Wide Eg = 1.85 eV
Si DONORS placed here
z greater than 0"] B["Spacer Layer
Undoped AlGaAs
Thickness d_s approx 5 to 20 nm
Prevents impurity scattering"] C["Heterointerface
z = 0
Conduction band offset
delta Ec approx 0.23 eV"] D["GaAs Quantum Well
Narrow Eg = 1.42 eV
UNDOPED
z less than 0"] E["Triangular Potential Well
Formed by band bending
Slope equals eF per m
Confines 2DEG"] F["2DEG Sheet
n_s approx 1 to 5 times 10 to power 15 per sq m
Located within 5 to 10 nm of interface
Electrons donated from AlGaAs"] G["Donor Ions left behind
Positive sheet charge in AlGaAs
Creates the electric field F"] A -- "step down at interface" --> C C -- "linear band bending" --> D C -- "creates triangular well" --> E E -- "traps electrons" --> F A -- "Si donors ionize" --> G G -- "electric field F" --> E A -. "physically separated by spacer" .-> B B -. "prevents impurity scattering" .-> D ``` > **How to read this diagram:** Trace the path from the donor layer → through the spacer → to the heterointerface → into the triangular well. The arrows show that **energy flows downward** (electrons fall), while **Coulomb interaction flows back** (the donor ions create the very field that confines the 2DEG — a beautiful self-consistency). ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a HEMT Built on the MDQW ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph S1["SOURCE REGION"] SO["Ohmic Contact
Source"] end subgraph S2["CHANNEL REGION 2DEG"] CH["Modulation-Doped Quantum Well
GaAs undoped channel
AlGaAs Si-doped supply layer
Spacer prevents scattering"] end subgraph S3["GATE CONTROL"] GA["Schottky Gate
Metal contact on AlGaAs"] GB["Depletion Region
formed under reverse bias"] end subgraph S4["DRAIN REGION"] DR["Ohmic Contact
Drain"] end SO --> CH CH --> DR GA --> GB GB -. "modulates n_s
in channel below" .-> CH ``` > **How to read this diagram:** Electrons enter at the Source, travel ballistically through the 2DEG channel (low scattering!), are modulated by the field under the Gate, and exit at the Drain. This is the operating principle of every HEMT in your mobile phone's RF front-end. ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Step-by-Step Operating Cycle of Modulation Doping ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Step 1 MBE Growth
Deposit undoped GaAs on substrate"] P2["Step 2 Spacer Layer
Grow thin undoped AlGaAs
Thickness d_s approx 5 to 20 nm"] P3["Step 3 Doping Layer
Grow Si-doped AlGaAs
ND approx 10 to power 24 per cubic m"] P4["Step 4 Cap Layer
Undoped GaAs on top
Prevents oxidation"] P5["Step 5 Cooling to 300 K
Si donors ionize
Release electrons"] P6["Step 6 Charge Transfer
Electrons fall into triangular well
at GaAs AlGaAs interface"] P7["Step 7 Self-Consistent Equilibrium
Electric field F balances charge
Subbands E0 E1 E2 form"] P8["Step 8 Result
2DEG established
n_s approx 10 to power 15 per sq m
mu skyrockets at low T"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 ``` ### 4.4 Fallback — Equivalent-Circuit of the 2DEG Channel For topics requiring physical cross-section drawings (e.g., TEM/SEM schematics), the equivalent "block-level" architecture: | Physical Layer | Function | Engineering Role | |---|---|---| | Substrate (semi-insulating GaAs) | Mechanical support, isolation | Carrier confinement to channel | | Buffer (undoped GaAs) | Trap-gettering layer | Reduces backscattering | | Channel (undoped GaAs) | Hosts 2DEG | High-mobility transport | | Spacer (undoped AlGaAs) | Tunnel barrier against dopants | Reduces ionized impurity scattering | | Donor supply (Si-doped AlGaAs) | Remote dopant reservoir | Supplies carriers | | Cap (Si-doped GaAs) | Ohmic contact facilitation | Low-resistance source/drain | ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition) #### **Q1. Define modulation doping. Why is it essential for high-electron-mobility transistors (HEMTs)?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Modulation doping is a technique in which **donor (or acceptor) impurities are placed in a wider-bandgap barrier material**, physically separated from the narrow-bandgap conducting channel. Electrons from the donors transfer into the lower-energy channel, forming a **2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** at the heterointerface. It is **essential for HEMTs** because the spatial separation of ionized donors from mobile carriers **drastically reduces ionized impurity scattering**, allowing electron mobility to reach values of $\sim 10^7$ cm²/(V·s) at cryogenic temperatures. This translates directly into **higher transconductance, lower noise figure, and higher unity-gain cutoff frequency $f_T$** for the transistor. --- #### **Q2. What is the Fang–Howard wavefunction? Write its mathematical form and physical significance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The Fang–Howard wavefunction is a **variational trial function** that approximates the ground-state envelope of an electron in the **triangular potential well** formed at a modulation-doped heterointerface. $$\psi_0(z) = \sqrt{\dfrac{b^3}{2}}\, z\, e^{-bz/2}, \quad z \ge 0$$ where the variational parameter $b$ (the inverse decay length) is: $$b = \left[\dfrac{12\,m^*\,e^2\,n_s}{\varepsilon\,\hbar^2}\right]^{1/3}$$ **Significance:** It captures the key physical feature that the 2DEG wavefunction **penetrates only ~3–10 nm into the well**, peaking at $z = 2/b$ and decaying exponentially. This is what makes the spatial separation from dopants effective in suppressing scattering. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Question Paper Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a) Derive the Fang–Howard ground-state energy for a modulation-doped 2DEG, starting from the variational principle.** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Hamiltonian of the triangular well:** (1 mark) $$\hat{H} = -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m^*}\dfrac{d^2}{dz^2} + eFz$$ **Step 2 — Trial wavefunction** with variational parameter $b$: (1 mark) $$\psi(z) = \sqrt{\dfrac{b^3}{2}}\, z \, e^{-bz/2}$$ **Step 3 — Compute kinetic energy expectation value:** (2 marks) $$\langle T \rangle = \dfrac{\hbar^2 b^2}{8 m^*}$$ (Show: $\int z e^{-bz/2} \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{dz^2}(z e^{-bz/2}) dz = -b^2/4$ times the normalization.) **Step 4 — Compute potential energy expectation value:** (1 mark) $$\langle U \rangle = \dfrac{3eF}{b}$$ **Step 5 — Total variational energy and minimization:** (1 mark) $$\dfrac{dE}{db} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = \left[\dfrac{12\,m^*\,eF}{\hbar^2}\right]^{1/3}$$ $$E_0 = \dfrac{9}{8}\!\left(\dfrac{e^2 \hbar^2 F^2}{2 m^*}\right)^{1/3}$$ **Step 6 — Numerical evaluation** for GaAs with $n_s = 5 \times 10^{15}$ /m²: (1 mark) $F = e n_s / \varepsilon = (1.6 \times 10^{-19})(5 \times 10^{15})/(12.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \approx 7.0 \times 10^{6}$ V/m $b \approx 1.7 \times 10^{8}$ /m, so $\langle z \rangle = 3/b \approx 18$ nm — wavefunction penetrates ~18 nm into GaAs. $E_0 \approx 28$ meV — well above $k_B T$ at 4 K, justifying the **degenerate 2DEG** treatment. --- #### **(b) Explain the conduction band diagram of a modulation-doped GaAs/AlGaAs heterostructure. Show how the 2DEG is formed and discuss its role in achieving high mobility.** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Solution:** 1. **Band diagram description** (2 marks): - AlGaAs has $E_g \approx 1.85$ eV; GaAs has $E_g \approx 1.42$ eV. - At equilibrium, the conduction band offset $\Delta E_c \approx 0.23$ eV (for x = 0.3) creates a step. - Fermi level is pinned at the Si donor level in AlGaAs and equals the subband energy $E_0$ in the well. 2. **Triangular well formation** (2 marks): - Electrons transferred from ionized Si donors in AlGaAs form a 2D sheet at $z = 0$. - The remaining positive donor sheet creates field $F = e n_s/\varepsilon$, bending the GaAs conduction band linearly into a triangular shape. - Quantized subbands $E_0, E_1, E_2, \ldots$ form, separated by $\sim 20$–$30$ meV. 3. **Why mobility is high** (2 marks): - **Reduced ionized impurity scattering:** The Coulomb potential of donors falls off as $1/(\varepsilon R)$, where $R$ is the 3D distance from the ion. Since the electron's probability density is at $z \sim 2/b \sim 10$ nm and the donor is at $z \sim d_s \sim 10$–$20$ nm in the barrier, $R \ge d_s + 2/b$. By increasing $d_s$, one tunes the scattering rate to nearly zero. - **No alloy scattering** (in pure GaAs channel). - **Reduced phonon scattering at low T** complements the design. 4. **Mobility numbers (evidence):** (1 mark) - Typical 77 K mobility in undoped GaAs/AlGaAs MDQW: $1$–$2 \times 10^6$ cm²/(V·s). - Best reported (Pfeiffer–West, 2008): $\mu > 3 \times 10^7$ cm²/(V·s). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** #### **(a) With neat sketches, explain the principle of operation of a HEMT. Show why a modulation-doped channel gives superior high-frequency performance compared to a conventional MESFET.** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Solution:** **1. HEMT structure (sketch described):** (2 marks) - Top: Ohmic source (S) and drain (D) contacts alloyed into a heavily-doped cap layer. - Middle: Schottky gate (G) metal deposited directly on AlGaAs surface. - Bottom: Undoped GaAs channel hosting the 2DEG, sitting on AlGaAs buffer and substrate. - Si-doped AlGaAs "supply layer" above the channel; spacer layer in between. **2. Operating principle:** (2 marks) - Negative gate voltage depletes the 2DEG beneath the gate; $n_s$ decreases approximately linearly with $|V_{GS}|$. - Drain current $I_D = q n_s \, W \, v_{sat}$ where $v_{sat}$ is the saturation velocity in GaAs ($\sim 1.3 \times 10^5$ m/s). - Transconductance $g_m = \partial I_D / \partial V_{GS} \propto \partial n_s/\partial V_{GS}$ is very high because $n_s$ is controlled by a thin (10–30 nm) AlGaAs layer (high capacitance per unit area). **3. Why HEMT > MESFET at high frequency:** (2 marks) - **Higher mobility** → higher velocity → higher $g_m$. - **Reduced parasitic resistance** in the source-gate and gate-drain regions. - **Lower noise figure** due to suppressed intervalley scattering. - **Higher $f_T$ and $f_{max}$** by factors of 2–5×. **4. Quantitative example:** (1 mark) For a 0.25 µm gate HEMT: $f_T \approx 100$ GHz; for a similar MESFET: $f_T \approx 30$ GHz. --- #### **(b) Discuss the role of the spacer layer in a modulation-doped heterostructure. What happens if the spacer thickness is too small or too large?** *(7 marks)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Solution:** **1. Definition and role of the spacer:** (2 marks) The **spacer layer** is an **undoped AlGaAs region** of thickness $d_s$ (typically 5–20 nm) inserted between the Si-doped AlGaAs supply layer and the GaAs channel. Its function is to **physically increase the donor-to-electron separation** without reducing the sheet carrier density. The 2DEG's wavefunction (Fang–Howard) penetrates into the AlGaAs side by an amount $\sim 1/b$, but the **probability of finding the electron at the donor ion location is exponentially suppressed**, leading to dramatically reduced Coulomb matrix elements for scattering. **2. Effect of too-small spacer ($d_s < 3$ nm):** (2 marks) - Donor ions come too close to the 2DEG → strong **ionized impurity scattering** → mobility collapses. - Enhanced **parallel conduction** in the doped AlGaAs layer, degrading low-temperature transport. - Possible **threshold voltage shift** and increased gate leakage. **3. Effect of too-large spacer ($d_s > 30$ nm):** (2 marks) - Carrier transfer efficiency drops exponentially: $n_s \propto \exp(-\alpha d_s)$. - Reduced sheet carrier density means **lower current drive** and **higher access resistance**. - Gate loses electrostatic control over the channel. - For InGaAs-based pseudomorphic HEMTs, $d_s$ must remain below the critical thickness to avoid strain relaxation. **4. Optimal design:** (1 mark) For GaAs/AlGaAs at 77 K, the optimum is typically $d_s \approx 5$–$10$ nm, balancing scattering reduction against carrier density loss. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks on this topic — read carefully:** > > 1. **Do NOT confuse "modulation doping" with "delta doping."** Delta doping places dopants in a single atomic plane *within* the well — scattering is *high*. Modulation doping places dopants *in the barrier* — scattering is *low*. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > > 2. **Do NOT forget to state the boundary conditions** $\psi(0) = 0$ and $\psi(\infty) = 0$ when writing the Fang–Howard wavefunction. Marks are deducted for unnormalized or unphysical trial functions. > > 3. **Do NOT compute $\langle T \rangle$ and $\langle U \rangle$ in the wrong units.** Always work in SI (Joules, kg) for intermediate steps and convert to eV at the end. > > 4. **Do NOT omit the spacer layer in your band-diagram sketch.** Drawing the AlGaAs donor layer touching the GaAs channel will lose you full marks — the spacer is the *defining feature*. > > 5. **Do NOT write $m^*$ without specifying** that it is the **GaAs effective mass** ($0.067\,m_0$), not the free-electron mass. > > 6. **In numerical problems**, many students incorrectly use $E_g$ instead of $\Delta E_c$ for the conduction band offset — the latter is **~60–70% of $E_g$**, depending on the alloy. --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Modulation doping = remote doping in the wider-bandgap barrier**, separated from the conducting channel. - ✅ **Famous system:** GaAs/AlGaAs with Si donors; 2DEG forms at the heterointerface. - ✅ **Three conditions for 2DEG:** (i) heterojunction band offset, (ii) carrier transfer from donors, (iii) self-consistent field creating triangular well. - ✅ **Self-consistency loop:** $F \to E_0 \to n_s \to F$ — solved iteratively via Poisson–Schrödinger. - ✅ **Key equation:** $n_s = \varepsilon F / e$ (sheet charge density from Gauss's law). - ✅ **Fang–Howard wavefunction:** $\psi(z) = \sqrt{b^3/2}\,z\,e^{-bz/2}$, optimal $b = (12 m^* e F/\hbar^2)^{1/3}$. - ✅ **Triangular-well subband energy:** $E_n \approx (\hbar^2/2m^*)^{1/3} \cdot [3\pi eF(n+3/4)/2]^{2/3}$. - ✅ **Spacer layer** is the secret weapon — it controls the trade-off between carrier density and mobility. - ✅ **Real-world impact:** HEMT, pHEMT, mHEMT, quantum Hall physics, QCLs, qubits. - ✅ **Nobel connection:** 1998 Nobel Prize (Störmer, Tsui, Laughlin) for FQHE in modulation-doped 2DEGs. - ✅ **At low T, mobility is limited by ionized impurity scattering** — addressed by modulation doping, not by temperature. - ✅ **Typical numerical values to memorize:** $m^*_{\text{GaAs}} = 0.067 m_0$, $\varepsilon_r = 12.9$, $\Delta E_c \approx 0.23$ eV (x=0.3). - ✅ **Störmer's rule of thumb:** At 4 K, every 1 nm of additional spacer can boost mobility by ~30%, but costs ~5% of $n_s$. - ✅ **Compared to MESFET:** HEMT has ~5× higher $f_T$, ~10× lower noise figure, ~2× higher power-added efficiency. - ✅ **Failure modes to remember:** DX centers in AlGaAs, parallel conduction, persistent photoconductivity. > **One-line mantra for the exam:** > *"Dope remote, confine tight, separate well — and the 2DEG will reward you with ultrahigh mobility."*

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Concept of Superlattices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Superlattice Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> A *superlattice* is an artificially engineered periodic structure formed by alternating layers of two or more different semiconductor materials, where the period of the artificial potential (typically **1 nm to 30 nm**) is comparable to or smaller than the **de Broglie wavelength** of an electron in the crystal. This artificial periodicity creates a new, larger unit cell that produces additional minibands and minigaps in the electronic band structure that are not present in the parent bulk materials.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy: The "Staircase of Energy"

Imagine a normal crystal lattice as a **perfectly smooth marble floor** — an electron can roll across it freely because the atomic potentials are uniform and identical everywhere.

Now, picture a **superlattice** as a staircase carved into that same floor. Each step represents a different semiconductor material (say, GaAs followed by AlGaAs, then GaAs again, and so on). The electron now encounters a *new* periodic pattern superimposed on the atomic one — a "super-period." This super-period is what gives rise to **mini-bands** and **mini-gaps** — smaller versions of the familiar conduction and valence bands.

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Period of superlattice (d)** | Sum of well width + barrier width |
| **Well material** | Lower bandgap semiconductor (e.g., GaAs) |
| **Barrier material** | Wider bandgap semiconductor (e.g., AlGaAs) |
| **Miniband** | Allowed energy range created by super-periodicity |
| **Minigap** | Forbidden energy range created by super-periodicity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is artificial periodicity useful?**
> In natural crystals, lattice constants are fixed by nature (~0.3–0.6 nm). In superlattices, engineers *choose* the period. This tunability allows the design of **tailor-made electronic and optical properties** — the foundation of devices like Quantum Cascade Lasers (QCLs), Resonant Tunneling Diodes (RTDs), and Infrared Detectors.

---

## 1.2 The Kronig-Penney Model of a Superlattice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kronig-Penney Model Definition:**
> The *Kronig-Penney (KP) model* is a simplified one-dimensional quantum-mechanical model that treats the periodic potential of a crystal (or superlattice) as a series of rectangular potential wells and barriers. The model yields a transcendental equation whose solutions define the allowed and forbidden energy bands for an electron moving through the periodic structure.

For a **superlattice**, the KP model is applied where the period `d = a + b` consists of:
- A **well of width `a`** with potential $V = 0$
- A **barrier of width `b`** with potential $V = V_0$

The boundary condition from Bloch's theorem produces the famous KP equation:

$$\cos(kd) = \cos(\alpha a)\cosh(\beta b) + \frac{\alpha^2 - \beta^2}{2\alpha\beta}\sin(\alpha a)\sinh(\beta b)$$

where:
- $\alpha = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$
- $\beta = \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$
- $k$ is the Bloch wavevector

---

## 1.3 Transport of Charge in Nanostructures

In nanostructures (quantum wells, wires, dots, and superlattices), charge transport is governed by **quantum mechanical phenomena** rather than classical drift-diffusion. The key transport regimes are:

| Transport Regime | Mechanism | Typical Length Scale |
|------------------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Ballistic Transport** | No scattering within the structure | Mean free path > device length |
| **Quasi-ballistic** | Few scattering events | Mean free path ≈ device length |
| **Diffusive Transport** | Frequent scattering | Mean free path < device length |
| **Tunneling Transport** | Quantum tunneling through barrier | Barrier width < ~3 nm |
| **Hopping Transport** | Phonon-assisted tunneling between localized states | Disordered/amorphous systems |

> [!NOTE]
> In superlattices, transport is particularly interesting because electrons can move via **miniband conduction** (Bloch oscillations) or via **sequential tunneling** between adjacent quantum wells, depending on the barrier thickness.

---

## 1.4 Electron Scattering Mechanisms

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electron Scattering Definition:**
> Electron scattering refers to any process that changes the electron's momentum (direction or magnitude) or energy. Scattering is characterized by a **scattering rate (1/τ)** or **mean free path (λ = v·τ)**, where τ is the relaxation time.

In bulk semiconductors, scattering is dominated by:
- **Phonon scattering** (acoustic & optical)
- **Ionized impurity scattering**
- **Neutral impurity scattering**

In **nanostructures**, additional mechanisms become dominant:

| Scattering Mechanism | Origin | Dominant At |
|---------------------|--------|-------------|
| **Boundary/Interface Scattering** | Confinement walls, heterointerfaces | Strong in nanowires, 2DEGs |
| **Surface Roughness Scattering** | Atomic-scale imperfections at interfaces | Strong in thin quantum wells |
| **Quantum Confinement Scattering** | Quantized subband transitions | All low-dimensional structures |
| **Dislocation Scattering** | Lattice mismatch defects | Strained-layer superlattices |
| **Electron-Electron Scattering** | Carrier-carrier Coulomb interaction | High carrier density |

# 📗 SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown — Superlattice Band Engineering

### 🔹 Step-by-Step Logic of Superlattice Formation

1. **Start with a bulk semiconductor** → Has continuous conduction and valence bands separated by bandgap $E_g$.
2. **Grow alternating thin layers** of two semiconductors with different $E_g$ values using Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) or Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition (MOCVD).
3. **Choose layer thickness** $a$ (well) and $b$ (barrier) such that $d = a + b$ ≈ de Broglie wavelength.
4. **Confinement in the growth direction** → Electrons form discrete **subbands** in each well.
5. **Tunneling coupling between wells** → Discrete levels broaden into **minibands** separated by **minigaps**.
6. **Mini Brillouin Zone** forms with $-\pi/d \leq k_z \leq +\pi/d$.

### 🔹 The "Why" Behind Each Step
- **Step 1–2:** Different materials create a modulated potential $V(z)$ that the electron "sees" as periodic.
- **Step 3:** If layers are too thick → electrons are fully localized (no miniband). If too thin → weak modulation.
- **Step 4–5:** Quantum confinement plus inter-well coupling is the *origin* of minibands.
- **Step 6:** The artificial period `d` defines a new, smaller Brillouin zone.

---

## 2.2 Kronig-Penney Model — The Core Mathematics

### 🔹 Setup of the Problem

We solve the 1-D time-independent Schrödinger equation:

$$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m}\frac{d^2\psi}{dz^2} + V(z)\psi = E\psi$$

In **Region I (well, 0 < z < a)**: $V = 0$
In **Region II (barrier, -b < z < 0)**: $V = V_0$

The general solutions are:

**Region I:** $\psi_I(z) = A e^{i\alpha z} + B e^{-i\alpha z}$, where $\alpha = \sqrt{2mE}/\hbar$

**Region II:** $\psi_{II}(z) = C e^{\beta z} + D e^{-\beta z}$, where $\beta = \sqrt{2m(V_0-E)}/\hbar$

Applying Bloch's theorem: $\psi(z+d) = \psi(z) e^{ikd}$ and matching wavefunctions and derivatives at the boundaries, we obtain the **KP equation** (derivation in Section 3).

---

## 2.3 Scattering Rate Analysis

The total scattering rate in Matthiessen's rule approximation:

$$\frac{1}{\tau_{total}} = \frac{1}{\tau_{phonon}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{impurity}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{boundary}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{surface}} + \frac{1}{\tau_{e-e}}$$

Mobility is related by:

$$\mu = \frac{q\langle\tau\rangle}{m^*}$$

where $m^*$ is the effective mass and $q$ is the electron charge.

---

## 2.4 📊 KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!NOTE]
> **Bookmark this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU question on this topic!**

| # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Units / Symbols |
|---|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $d = a + b$ | Superlattice period | nm |

| 3 | $\beta = \sqrt{2m(V_0-E)}/\hbar$ | Decay constant in barrier | m⁻¹ |
| 4 | $\cos(kd) = \cos(\alpha a)\cosh(\beta b) + \dfrac{\alpha^2-\beta^2}{2\alpha\beta}\sin(\alpha a)\sinh(\beta b)$ | **Kronig-Penney equation** | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P = \dfrac{\beta^2 a b}{2}$ (Kronig-Penney parameter) | Measures barrier strength | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_n \approx \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2 n^2}{2m^* a^2}$ | Particle-in-a-box subband | eV |
| 7 | $\Delta E_{miniband} \propto \exp(-\kappa b)$ | Miniband width | eV |
| 8 | $\lambda_{dB} = h/p = h/\sqrt{2m^*E}$ | de Broglie wavelength | nm |
| 9 | $\mu = q\tau/m^*$ | Drift mobility | cm²/(V·s) |

| 14 | $E_F = \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2 n_{2D}}{m^*}$ | 2-D Fermi energy | eV |
| 15 | $J = nqv_d = nq^2\tau E/m^*$ | Drift current density | A/m² |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Role of Superlattice Concept |
|-------------|-------------------------------|
| **Quantum Cascade Laser (QCL)** | Engineered minibands enable intersubband transitions in IR/mid-IR |
| **Resonant Tunneling Diode (RTD)** | Sharp tunneling peaks from KP miniband alignment |
| **High Electron Mobility Transistor (HEMT)** | Modulation doping in superlattice buffers reduces impurity scattering |
| **Thermoelectric devices** | Phonon scattering at interfaces reduces thermal conductivity (PGEC concept) |
| **Photovoltaic cells (multi-junction)** | Superlattice intermediate bands tune absorption |
| **Single-electron transistors** | Coulomb blockade in quantum dot superlattices |
