Cascaded MOBILE chain"] classDef clk fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef rtd fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef sig fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class VCLK,IN clk class RTD1,RTD2 rtd class NODE,FB,GND sig ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the zero-bias band profile, the emitter Fermi level sits between $E_1$ and $E_2$ of the well. At $V = V_P$, $E_F^l$ aligns with $E_1$ → resonance → peak. In the valley region, $E_F^l$ has fallen below $E_1$ but $E_F^r$ has also dropped, so the bias window $[E_F^r, E_F^l]$ no longer brackets $E_1$ → current collapses → NDR. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]** **Define resonant tunnelling. State the resonance condition for an electron tunnelling through a double-barrier quantum well structure.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** *Resonant tunnelling* is a quantum-mechanical transport process in which an electron wave propagates through a double-barrier quantum well (DBQW) heterostructure with near-unity transmission probability when the electron's incident energy matches a quantised sub-band level $E_n$ of the well. **Resonance condition (1.5 marks):** The resonance condition is $$ k_w L_w = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3,\ldots $$ where $k_w = \sqrt{2m^*E_n}/\hbar$ is the wavevector of the electron inside the well and $L_w$ is the well width. Equivalently, $E_n = n^2\pi^2\hbar^2/(2m^*L_w^2)$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)? Why does it appear in the I–V characteristics of a Resonant Tunnelling Diode?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition of NDR (1 mark):** NDR is the property by which the current through a two-terminal device **decreases** with increasing applied voltage over a finite bias interval, i.e., $dI/dV < 0$. **Origin in RTD (2 marks):** As the bias $V$ is increased, the emitter Fermi level $E_F^l$ is pulled down relative to the well sub-bands. When $E_F^l$ aligns with $E_1$ → **resonance** → peak current $I_P$ is reached. As $V$ is increased further, $E_1$ moves out of the bias window $[E_F^r, E_F^l]$ → off-resonance → transmission $T(E)$ drops sharply → current falls to the valley value $I_V$. Hence NDR appears between $V_P$ and $V_V$. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern for Module 3 (14 marks):** > Each Part-B question carries **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (e.g., (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). Students must answer **either Option A or Option B** in full. --- ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Theory + Derivation Track **Q.A (a) [7 marks]** *Derive the Tsu–Esaki formula for the tunnelling current density in a Resonant Tunnelling Diode. Clearly state the assumptions and explain the role of the transmission coefficient $T(E)$.* **Model Solution — Step-by-step (7 marks):** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | 1. State the physical setup and assumptions | 1 | (i) Coherent, elastic tunnelling; (ii) Effective-mass approximation; (iii) Transverse motion separable; (iv) Fermi–Dirac distributions $f_l, f_r$ in left/right reservoirs. | | 2. Write the net current as an imbalance of left-to-right and right-to-left tunnelling rates | 1 | $J \propto \int T(E)\,[f_l - f_r]\,dE$ | | 3. Perform the transverse $k_y, k_z$ integration to obtain the prefactor | 1 | $\dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\int dk_y dk_z = \dfrac{m^*}{2\pi\hbar^2}$ | | 4. Substitute to get the **general Tsu–Esaki equation** | 1.5 | $J = \dfrac{qm^*}{2\pi^2\hbar^3}\int T(E)\,[f_l - f_r]\,dE$ | | 5. Use the Fermi-function identity to obtain the **logarithmic form** | 1.5 | $J = \dfrac{q}{2\pi\hbar}\int T(E)\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 + e^{(E_F^l - E)/k_BT}}{1 + e^{(E_F^r - E)/k_BT}}\right]dE$ | | 6. Discuss the role of $T(E)$ | 1 | $T(E)$ acts as a **spectral filter** — current is large only when the bias window straddles a resonance peak of $T(E)$. This is the source of the NDR. | **Final boxed answer (0 marks, for clarity):** $$ \boxed{\,J = \dfrac{q}{2\pi\hbar}\int_{0}^{\infty} T(E)\,\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 + e^{(E_F^l - E)/k_BT}}{1 + e^{(E_F^r - E)/k_BT}}\right] dE\,} $$ --- **Q.A (b) [7 marks]** *With the help of a neatly labelled I–V characteristic, explain the operation of a Resonant Tunnelling Diode (RTD). Define the Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR) and discuss two factors that affect it.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | 1. Sketch the I–V curve with all four key markers | 2 | Mark $V_P$, $I_P$ (resonance peak); $V_V$, $I_V$ (valley); the NDR region between $V_P$ and $V_V$ (slope negative); and the post-valley rise (thermionic). | | 2. Describe the four regions of the I–V curve | 2 | (i) Pre-peak: off-resonant tunnelling; (ii) Peak: $E_1$ aligned with $E_F^l$; (iii) NDR: $E_1$ leaves the bias window; (iv) Post-valley: thermionic emission + next sub-band $E_2$ approaching resonance. | | 3. Define PVR | 1 | $\text{PVR} = I_P / I_V$ — figure of merit quantifying the contrast between on- and off-resonance tunnelling. | | 4. Two factors affecting PVR | 2 | **Factor 1 (Barrier):** Higher & thicker barriers → smaller $T_0$ → both $I_P$ and $I_V$ drop, but $I_V$ drops more (since valley current has a larger relative tunnelling component) → PVR ↑. **Factor 2 (Temperature):** Lower temperature → suppressed thermionic emission at the valley → $I_V$ ↓ more than $I_P$ → PVR ↑. (Alternative: well-width asymmetry, interface roughness.) | **Schematic I–V Curve Description (for the answer sheet):** - Y-axis: $I$ (or $J$), X-axis: $V$. - Curve starts near origin, rises monotonically to $(V_P, I_P)$, **slopes downward** from $(V_P, I_P)$ to $(V_V, I_V)$, then rises again to a second peak (corresponding to $E_2$) and saturates. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Device Physics + Applications Track (Alternative to Q.A) **Q.B (a) [7 marks]** *Explain the structure and operating principle of a Resonant Tunnelling Diode. With a neat energy-band diagram, describe what happens at zero bias, at peak voltage, and in the valley region.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | 1. Describe the physical layered structure | 1.5 | n⁺-emitter / undoped spacer / AlGaAs barrier (≈2–3 nm) / GaAs well (≈5 nm) / AlGaAs barrier (≈2–3 nm) / undoped spacer / n⁺-collector (AlGaAs/GaAs heterostructure). | | 2. Explain the principle of operation in words | 1.5 | Electrons from the emitter tunnelling through the DBQW achieve maximum transmission only at specific energies equal to the quantised sub-band levels $E_n$ of the well. The bias controls whether $E_F^l$ aligns with $E_n$ or not. | | 3. Zero-bias band diagram + explanation | 1 | Symmetric, flat-band condition; $E_F^l = E_F^r$; no net current. | | 4. Peak-voltage ($V = V_P$) band diagram + explanation | 1.5 | Conduction band tilts; $E_1$ aligns with $E_F^l$; resonance; current peaks. | | 5. Valley-region band diagram + explanation | 1.5 | $E_F^l$ has dropped below $E_1$; $E_1$ lies outside the bias window $[E_F^r, E_F^l]$; off-resonance; current falls sharply (NDR). | --- **Q.B (b) [7 marks]** *Discuss the applications of Resonant Tunnelling Diodes in nano-electronic circuits. Highlight any two emerging applications in detail.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** | Step | Marks | Content | |---|---|---| | 1. Brief introduction to why RTDs are useful | 1 | RTDs offer THz-speed switching, NDR-enabled compact logic, and natural multi-valued behaviour — making them ideal for nano-electronic integration. | | 2. **Application 1: THz Oscillators** | 2.5 | RTDs biased in the NDR region, embedded in a microstrip or waveguide resonator, produce sustained THz oscillations. Used in security imaging, spectroscopy, and ultra-broadband wireless links. Operating frequency $f \sim \Delta E/h$, where $\Delta E$ is the resonance FWHM. | | 3. **Application 2: Multi-Valued Logic / MOBILE** | 2.5 | Two RTDs in series (MOBILE) form a self-latching inverter under a clocked supply. By adjusting the relative areas of the two RTDs, the latch can be set to give ternary/quaternary output — enabling MVL arithmetic (e.g., 4-bit adders realised with ~30 % fewer gates). | | 4. Other applications (brief) | 1 | Quantum cascade lasers (intersub-band transitions), QWIPs (IR detectors), ADCs (NDR-based flash), neuromorphic computing (oscillatory neural networks). | --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Module 3, RT Transport)** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the Tsu–Esaki prefactor.** The correct prefactor is $q/(2\pi\hbar)$, not $q/(\pi\hbar)$ or $q/\hbar$. Loss of **1 mark** if the prefactor is wrong. > 2. **Confusing $E_n$ (sub-band) with $E_F$ (Fermi level).** In the band diagram, $E_1$ is a discrete level inside the well, whereas $E_F^l, E_F^r$ are the quasi-Fermi levels in the reservoirs. Examiners specifically test this distinction. > 3. **Drawing the I–V curve without negative slope.** Showing an RTD I–V that always rises (like a normal diode) costs **at least 2 marks**. > 4. **Wrong symmetry of the band diagram.** Many students draw an asymmetric DBQW under zero bias. It must be **symmetric at $V=0$**. > 5. **Stating PVR as $I_V/I_P$ instead of $I_P/I_V$.** Always $I_P \ge I_V$, so PVR is a number $\ge 1$. > 6. **Skipping the resonance condition** $k_w L_w = n\pi$ when asked for "the principle of resonant tunnelling" — it is the **single most important** sentence in the answer. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!NOTE] > **Must-Remember Points on Resonant Tunnelling Transport (Module 3)** > > ☐ Resonant tunnelling occurs in a **double-barrier quantum well (DBQW)** when incident energy equals a quantised well sub-band. > ☐ **Resonance condition:** $k_w L_w = n\pi$, equivalently $E_n = n^2\pi^2\hbar^2/(2m^*L_w^2)$. > ☐ **Transmission coefficient** $T(E)$ has a Lorentzian-shaped peak of (near) unity at each $E_n$. > ☐ **Tsu–Esaki current density:** $J = \dfrac{q}{2\pi\hbar}\int T(E)\ln\!\left[\dfrac{1 + e^{(E_F^l - E)/k_BT}}{1 + e^{(E_F^r - E)/k_BT}}\right]dE$. > ☐ The I–V curve shows a **peak** ($I_P$) and a **valley** ($I_V$) separated by a region of **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** where $dI/dV < 0$. > ☐ **PVR = $I_P / I_V$** is the key figure of merit. Room-temperature PVRs of 4–12 are typical for AlGaAs/GaAs RTDs. > ☐ Typical RTD structure: **n⁺-GaAs / spacer / AlGaAs barrier / GaAs well / AlGaAs barrier / spacer / n⁺-GaAs**. > ☐ RTDs enable **THz oscillators, MOBILE multi-valued logic, QWIPs, and quantum cascade lasers** — all exam-favourite applications. > ☐ Switching speed is set by tunnelling time $\tau \sim \hbar/\Delta E$ → femtosecond regime → THz operation. > ☐ The NDR arises because increasing $V$ eventually **misaligns** $E_1$ from the bias window $[E_F^r, E_F^l]$, collapsing $T(E)$ inside the integration range. > ☐ At low temperature ($T \to 0$) the logarithmic integrand becomes a step function over the bias window → current ≈ $\frac{q}{2\pi\hbar} \int_{E_F^r}^{E_F^l} T(E)\,dE$. > ☐ **Memory aid for KTU exam:** Always quote (i) the resonance condition, (ii) the Tsu–Esaki formula, and (iii) a labelled I–V curve with $V_P, I_P, V_V, I_V$ — together these are worth 80 % of the marks on any RTD question.

topics.title: Coulomb Blockade
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Coulomb Blockade

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Coulomb Blockade** is a fundamental quantum transport phenomenon observed in nano-scale electronic devices where the flow of single electrons through a small conducting *island* (such as a metallic nanoparticle, quantum dot, or single-molecule junction) coupled to source and drain electrodes via thin insulating **tunnel barriers** is energetically suppressed at low bias voltages and low temperatures.

Formally, Coulomb blockade occurs when the **electrostatic charging energy** required to add one excess electron to the island exceeds the energy supplied by the thermal bath ($k_B T$) and the applied source–drain bias ($eV_{ds}$). Under this condition, the island behaves like a *Coulomb island* whose electron population is quantized to integer values, and a finite threshold voltage must be exceeded before any steady current can flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In the orthodox (semi-classical) theory of single-electron tunneling (SET), Coulomb blockade is the cornerstone concept. The topic covers the energetics of charging, the conditions for observing single-electron effects, and the operation of the Single-Electron Transistor (SET).

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental constants are central to Coulomb blockade calculations and must be memorized by every KTU 2024 student:

- **Elementary charge:** $\mathbf{e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $\mathbf{k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}}$
- **Reduced Planck constant:** $\mathbf{\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J\cdot s}}$
- **Quantum resistance:** $\mathbf{R_Q = h/e^2 \approx 25.8\ \text{k}\Omega}$

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy (Water-Cup Model)

Imagine a **small cup** representing the Coulomb island, connected to a **water reservoir** by a thin straw (the tunnel junction). The cup is so small that **a single drop of water corresponds to a single electron**.

- The cup already holds some water. To push in one more drop, you must perform work against the electrostatic "back-pressure."
- If the energy of the incoming drop is **less than this back-pressure**, the drop simply **bounces back** — the cup is "blocked."
- Only when you apply enough external pressure (analogous to a **bias voltage** higher than the threshold $V_{th}$) can you force the drop in.
- The cup can then either hold 1 drop, 2 drops, 3 drops, ... but never a fractional drop — this is the **discreteness of charge**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in nanotechnology:** At macroscopic scales, the charging energy $E_c = e^2/2C$ is vanishingly small because $C$ is huge. At nano-scale, $C$ is on the order of $\mathbf{10^{-18}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-15}}$ F, making $E_c$ comparable to or larger than $k_B T$ even at room temperature in some cases.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The phenomenon of Coulomb blockade unfolds in the following structured way:

1. **Geometry Setup**: A nanoscale conducting island (often called a *Coulomb island* or *quantum dot*) is sandwiched between two metallic electrodes — **source (S)** and **drain (D)** — separated from each electrode by a thin insulating barrier through which electrons can **quantum-mechanically tunnel**.

2. **Capacitive Charging**: The entire structure forms a network of capacitors. The source forms a capacitor $C_S$, the drain forms $C_D$, and (optionally) a gate electrode forms $C_G$. The total island capacitance is $C_\Sigma = C_S + C_D + C_G$.

3. **Discrete Electron Addition**: To transfer one electron onto the island, charge $Q$ must increase by $e$. The electrostatic energy of the island changes by:
   $$\Delta U \;=\; \frac{Q^2}{2C_\Sigma}\;-\;\frac{(Q-e)^2}{2C_\Sigma}\;=\;\frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}\;-\;\frac{eQ}{C_\Sigma}$$

   The minimum (worst-case) energy required occurs when $Q = 0$, giving:
   $$E_c \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$
   This is called the **single-electron charging energy**.

4. **Energy Competition**: An electron can tunnel only if the energy decrease in the external circuit (supplied by bias $V_{ds}$ or thermal energy $k_B T$) is at least as large as $E_c$.

5. **Blockade Regime**: When $eV_{ds} < E_c$ (and $k_B T \ll E_c$), tunneling is energetically forbidden — current is suppressed to essentially zero. This is the **Coulomb blockade regime**.

6. **Onset of Conduction**: Once $eV_{ds} \geq E_c$, electrons can tunnel one at a time, producing a stepwise current known as the **Coulomb staircase**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Coulomb Blockade Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains **every high-yield formula** that has appeared in KTU past papers for this topic. Memorize each entry along with its physical meaning and units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Boundary Conditions | Standard Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Charging energy | $E_c = \dfrac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$ | Energy to add 1 electron to neutral island | Joules (J) or eV |
| 2 | Total island capacitance | $C_\Sigma = C_S + C_D + C_G$ | Sum of all capacitive couplings | Farads (F) |
| 3 | Threshold (offset) voltage | $V_{th} = \dfrac{e}{2 C_\Sigma}$ | Half-width of Coulomb gap at $V_G = 0$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | Full Coulomb gap width | $\Delta V = \dfrac{e}{C_\Sigma}$ | Source–drain bias range of zero current | Volts (V) |
| 5 | Equilibrium electron number | $n_0 = \dfrac{C_G V_G}{e}$ | Induced by gate voltage | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Gate-voltage periodicity | $\Delta V_G = \dfrac{e}{C_G}$ | Period of Coulomb oscillations | Volts (V) |
| 7 | Orthodox tunneling rate | $\Gamma = \dfrac{-\Delta E}{e^2 R_T \left[1 - \exp(\Delta E / k_B T)\right]}$ | Rate when $\Delta E < 0$ (downhill tunneling) | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 8 | Thermal condition | $k_B T \ll E_c$ | Required for charge quantization | $k_B T$ in eV |
| 9 | Tunnel-resistance condition | $R_T \gg R_Q = h / e^2 \approx 25.8\ \text{k}\Omega$ | Localization of charge on island | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 10 | Maximum operating temperature | $T_{\max} \approx \dfrac{e^2}{2 k_B C_\Sigma}$ | Above this, thermal smearing destroys blockade | Kelvin (K) |
| 11 | Free-energy difference (source→island) | $\Delta F = \dfrac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2n+1) - \dfrac{e}{C_\Sigma}\left(C_G V_G + C_D V_D - C_S V_S\right)$ | Per-tunneling-event energy change | Joules (J) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Pipe-Symbol Note (Valuation Tip):** Always write absolute values using $\vert\,\cdot\,\vert$ or $\mid\,\cdot\,\mid$ in LaTeX. Never use raw `|` symbols inside math, as examiners sometimes de-mask markdown tables and `|` may break.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Single-Electron Transistor (SET):** Used as an ultra-sensitive electrometer capable of detecting fractions of an electron charge; integrated in **room-temperature SET** prototypes with sub-10 nm islands.
- **Quantum Current Standards:** Single-electron pumps deliver an exact current $I = e f$ at frequency $f$, enabling the realization of the SI ampere from $e$ and $f$.
- **Coulomb-Blockade Thermometers (CBTs):** Arrays of metallic islands act as primary thermometers from 0.01 K to 30 K — used in cryogenic laboratories.
- **Low-Power Digital Logic:** SETs consume as little as $\sim 10^{-8}$ of the energy of a MOSFET per switching event, opening paths to **sub-10 aJ logic gates**.
- **Quantum Computing Readout:** SETs serve as sensitive charge sensors for semiconductor spin qubits and Majorana zero modes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charging Energy of a Coulomb Island

### Setup

Consider an isolated metallic island of self-capacitance $C$ (we first ignore source/drain/gate for clarity) carrying total excess charge $Q = -N e$, where $N$ is the number of extra electrons on the island.

### Step 1 — Energy stored in a charged conductor

By the classical electrostatic relation for an isolated conductor, the energy required to assemble charge $Q$ on a capacitor of capacitance $C$ is:

$$U(Q) \;=\; \frac{Q^2}{2C}$$

This is the **self-energy** of the charge configuration.

### Step 2 — Substitute the discrete charge

Since charge is quantized in units of $e$, set $Q = -Ne$:

$$U(N) \;=\; \frac{(-Ne)^2}{2C} \;=\; \frac{N^2 e^2}{2C}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the incremental energy for one more electron

To find the energy cost of adding **one** electron (transition $N \rightarrow N+1$):

$$\Delta U \;=\; U(N+1) \;-\; U(N)$$

$$\Delta U \;=\; \frac{(N+1)^2 e^2}{2C} \;-\; \frac{N^2 e^2}{2C}$$

$$\Delta U \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2C}\left[(N+1)^2 - N^2\right]$$

### Step 4 — Expand the square difference

$$(N+1)^2 - N^2 \;=\; N^2 + 2N + 1 - N^2 \;=\; 2N + 1$$

### Step 5 — Final expression

$$\Delta U \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2C}(2N + 1)$$

For a *neutral* island ($N = 0$), the **minimum** charging energy becomes:

$$E_c \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because $\Delta U$ depends on $N$, the "cost" of adding an electron is *larger* when the island already carries excess electrons. This growing cost is exactly what suppresses simultaneous multi-electron tunneling.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Threshold Voltage for Onset of Conduction

### Setup

Now connect the island to a source at potential $V_S = V/2$ and a drain at $V_D = -V/2$ through two equal tunnel junctions of capacitance $C_J$ each. The total island capacitance is $C_\Sigma = 2 C_J$.

We ask: **What is the minimum source–drain bias $V$ at which an electron can tunnel from source to island?**

### Step 1 — Energy of the island before tunneling (with $n$ electrons)

The island's electrostatic energy, including the bias contribution, is:

$$E_i \;=\; \frac{(-ne + q_0)^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

where $q_0$ is the **induced charge** from the external bias. By charge-neutrality (zero charge initially on island for simplicity), $q_0 = 0$ and the bias sources induce zero net charge on island in the symmetric case.

So before tunneling:

$$E_i \;=\; \frac{(-ne)^2}{2 C_\Sigma} \;=\; \frac{n^2 e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

### Step 2 — Energy of the island after one electron has tunneled onto it

Now the island has $n+1$ electrons:

$$E_f \;=\; \frac{[(n+1)e]^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

### Step 3 — Work done by the source electrode

When one electron (charge $-e$) leaves the source at potential $V/2$, the source's energy **decreases** by:

$$W_S \;=\; (-e)\cdot(V/2) \cdot (-1) \;=\; \frac{eV}{2}$$

(Note: the negative sign on $(-e)\cdot(V/2)$ represents the source's energy; removing one electron of charge $-e$ from the source at potential $V/2$ releases energy $eV/2$ to the tunneling process.)

### Step 4 — Total energy balance for the tunneling event

The total energy change of the universe (island + source) is:

$$\Delta E_{\text{total}} \;=\; (E_f - E_i) \;-\; W_S$$

$$\Delta E_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}\left[(n+1)^2 - n^2\right] \;-\; \frac{eV}{2}$$

$$\Delta E_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2n+1) \;-\; \frac{eV}{2}$$

### Step 5 — Find the threshold condition

For tunneling to be **energetically allowed**, we need $\Delta E_{\text{total}} \leq 0$. The worst case (largest positive $\Delta E$) is for $n = 0$:

$$0 \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma} \;-\; \frac{e V_{th}}{2}$$

$$V_{th} \;=\; \frac{e}{C_\Sigma}$$

If we use a **symmetric bias** about zero, the blockade exists for $|V_{ds}| < V_{th}/2$ and conduction starts at:

$$V_{ds,\text{onset}} \;=\; \frac{e}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

This is the famous **threshold voltage** of Coulomb blockade.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two forms appear:** $V_{th} = e/C_\Sigma$ refers to the *full* source-drain voltage range where no current flows (the Coulomb gap). $V_{ds,\text{onset}} = e/(2 C_\Sigma)$ refers to the half-width when source and drain are biased symmetrically about the island. Both forms are accepted in KTU valuation provided the bias configuration is clearly stated.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Free-Energy Difference with a Gate Electrode (SET Case)

### Setup

The **Single-Electron Transistor** adds a third electrode, the gate, coupled to the island through capacitance $C_G$ and held at voltage $V_G$. The total capacitance is now:

$$C_\Sigma \;=\; C_S + C_D + C_G$$

The charge on the island can be tuned continuously via $V_G$:

$$Q_0 \;=\; C_S V_S + C_D V_D + C_G V_G$$

### Step 1 — Free energy of the island with $n$ electrons

The Gibbs free energy is:

$$F(n) \;=\; \frac{(ne - Q_0)^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

### Step 2 — Free-energy change for one electron tunneling from source to island

For the transition $n \rightarrow n+1$ with the source held fixed at $V_S$:

$$\Delta F_{S \rightarrow I} \;=\; F(n+1) - F(n) - e V_S \cdot \frac{C_S}{C_\Sigma}$$

Expanding $F(n+1) - F(n)$:

$$F(n+1) - F(n) \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2n + 1) + \frac{e Q_0}{C_\Sigma} \cdot (\text{linear term correction})$$

Carrying out the full algebra gives:

$$\Delta F_{S \rightarrow I} \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2n+1) \;-\; \frac{e}{C_\Sigma}\left[C_G V_G + (C_D - C_S) \frac{V_{ds}}{2}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Simplification for symmetric junctions

If $C_S = C_D = C_J$ and $V_{ds}$ is applied symmetrically:

$$\Delta F_{S \rightarrow I} \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2n+1) \;-\; \frac{e C_G}{C_\Sigma} V_G$$

### Step 4 — Coulomb blockade condition

For tunneling to be forbidden:

$$\Delta F_{S \rightarrow I} \;>\; 0 \quad \text{for all integer } n$$

### Step 5 — Periodicity in gate voltage

As $V_G$ is swept, blockade is lifted periodically whenever:

$$\frac{e C_G V_G}{C_\Sigma} \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2n+1)$$

The **period** in gate voltage is:

$$\Delta V_G \;=\; \frac{e}{C_G}$$

This gives the famous **Coulomb oscillations** of SET conductance.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tunneling Rate (Orthodox Theory Master Equation)

### Orthodox Theory Assumptions

The **orthodox theory** of Averin and Likharev (1985–1991) — central to KTU 2024 syllabus — assumes:

1. Confinement energies are negligible (treat electrons as classical particles).
2. Tunneling events are instantaneous and uncorrelated.
3. Charge states are well-defined integers $n$.
4. Tunneling resistance $R_T \gg R_Q$.

### Tunneling Rate Formula

For an electron tunneling across junction $j$ that changes $n$ by $\pm 1$:

$$\Gamma_j^{\pm}(n) \;=\; \frac{1}{R_T C_\Sigma e^2}\;\frac{-\Delta F_j^{\pm}(n)}{\exp\left[\Delta F_j^{\pm}(n) / k_B T\right] - 1}$$

where:
- $\Gamma_j^+$: rate of electron *entering* island (forward tunneling)
- $\Gamma_j^-$: rate of electron *leaving* island (backward tunneling)
- $\Delta F_j^{\pm}(n)$: free-energy change for that event
- The denominator enforces detailed balance at thermal equilibrium

### Master Equation for the Charge State

The probability $P(n, t)$ that the island holds $n$ electrons evolves as:

$$\frac{dP(n)}{dt} \;=\; \sum_{j}\left[\Gamma_j^+(n-1)\,P(n-1) + \Gamma_j^-(n+1)\,P(n+1)\right] \;-\; \sum_{j}\left[\Gamma_j^+(n) + \Gamma_j^-(n)\right]P(n)$$

In the steady state $dP/dt = 0$, this becomes a linear system solvable for $P(n)$ and hence the DC current:

$$I_{ds} \;=\; e \sum_n P(n)\left[\Gamma_S^+(n) - \Gamma_S^-(n)\right]$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Coulomb Staircase (I–V Characteristics)

When $V_{ds}$ is comparable to or larger than $e/C_\Sigma$, multiple discrete current steps appear:

$$I \;=\; \frac{1}{R_T}\;\left(V_{ds} - V_{th,\text{eff}}\right)\;\text{for}\; V_{ds} > V_{th,\text{eff}}$$

Each step occurs when a new charge state becomes energetically accessible, producing the **Coulomb staircase** of equally spaced steps of height $\Delta I \approx e/(2 R_T C_\Sigma)$ at $T \to 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Unit Convention for KTU:** Always convert $E_c$ to electron-volts when comparing with $k_B T$, because $k_B T$ at room temperature (300 K) is $\mathbf{\approx 25.85\ \text{meV}}$, a value KTU examiners love to test.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Single-Electron Transistor (SET)

The physical cross-section of an SET is challenging to render as a Mermaid topology, so we present its **functional architecture** mapping all capacitive, electrostatic, and tunneling interactions:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSrc["SOURCE ELECTRODE\nPotential VS\nBias VDS via VS = plus VDS slash 2"]
    nodeDrain["DRAIN ELECTRODE\nPotential VD\nBias VDS via VD = minus VDS slash 2"]
    nodeGate["GATE ELECTRODE\nPotential VG\nTunable Control Input"]
    nodeIsland["COULOMB ISLAND\nMetallic Nanoparticle\nOr Semiconductor Quantum Dot\nCharge State n times e"]
    nodeTun1["TUNNEL JUNCTION 1\nInsulating Barrier\nCapacitance CS\nTunnel Resistance RT_S"]
    nodeTun2["TUNNEL JUNCTION 2\nInsulating Barrier\nCapacitance CD\nTunnel Resistance RT_D"]
    nodeGateCap["GATE CAPACITOR\nDielectric Layer\nCapacitance CG\nNo DC Current Flow"]
    nodeEnIsland["ELECTROSTATIC ENERGY\nU = Q squared over 2 C Sigma\nCharging Energy Ec = e squared over 2 C Sigma"]
    nodeEnBalance["FREE ENERGY BALANCE\nDelta F S to I and Delta F I to D\nComputed per Orthodox Theory"]

    nodeSrc --> nodeTun1
    nodeTun1 --> nodeIsland
    nodeIsland --> nodeTun2
    nodeTun2 --> nodeDrain
    nodeGate --> nodeGateCap
    nodeGateCap --> nodeIsland

    nodeIsland -.-> nodeEnIsland
    nodeEnIsland -.-> nodeEnBalance
    nodeTun1 -.-> nodeEnBalance
    nodeTun2 -.-> nodeEnBalance
    nodeGateCap -.-> nodeEnBalance
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Solid arrows** = physical / DC connections (current-carrying).
> - **Dotted arrows** = logical / mathematical dependencies used in the energy analysis.
> - The island is *electronically isolated* except through the two tunnel junctions; the gate couples only **capacitively**, hence no DC gate current flows.

## 4.2 Functional Energy-Band Flow During Blockade vs Conduction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["STATE A BLOCKADE\nDelta F greater than 0\nNo Net Tunneling Allowed\nCurrent I equals 0"]
    nodeB["STATE B THRESHOLD\nDelta F equals 0\nOnset of Single Electron Tunnel"]
    nodeC["STATE C CONDUCTION\nDelta F less than 0\nElectron Enters Island\nThen Exits to Drain"]
    nodeD["STATE D RESET\nIsland Returns to Charge State n\nReady for Next Cycle"]

    nodeA -->|Bias exceeds Vth| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Single electron hops on| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Single electron hops off| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Repeats at rate Gamma| nodeA
```

## 4.3 Operating-Regime Map of a Coulomb Island

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeRegimeA["REGIME A CLASSICAL\neV greater than kB T\ngreater than h slash RC\nNormal Ohmic Conduction\nCoulomb Effects Negligible"]
    nodeRegimeB["REGIME B COULOMB BLOCKADE\nkB T less than Ec\ngreater than eV greater than 0\nDiscrete Charge States\nI equals 0"]
    nodeRegimeC["REGIME C QUANTUM\neV greater than h slash tau\nDiscrete Energy Levels\nCoulomb plus Quantum Effects"]
    nodeRegimeD["REGIME D COHERENT\nT less than h slash kB T\ngreater than h slash e squared RT\nQuantum Coherence\nKondo Effect Possible"]

    nodeRegimeA -->|Decrease T or Increase R| nodeRegimeB
    nodeRegimeB -->|Decrease Island Size| nodeRegimeC
    nodeRegimeC -->|Further Cooling and Lower Disorder| nodeRegimeD
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked to *identify* the regime a device operates in, students must compute and verify **all three orthodox conditions** (charging energy, thermal, and resistance), not just one. A single missed condition often leads to part-mark deductions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define Coulomb blockade. List the three essential conditions for observing Coulomb blockade in a single-electron tunneling device.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Coulomb blockade is the phenomenon in which the addition of a single electron to a small conducting island (quantum dot) is energetically forbidden because the electrostatic charging energy $E_c = e^2/2C_\Sigma$ required exceeds the available thermal and bias energy.

**Conditions (2 marks — ½ mark each + ½ mark for explanation):**

1. **Charging-energy dominance:** $E_c = e^2/2 C_\Sigma \gg k_B T$. This ensures charge quantization is not thermally smeared.
2. **Tunnel-resistance dominance:** $R_T \gg R_Q = h/e^2 \approx 25.8\ \text{k}\Omega$. This ensures charge is well-localized on the island between tunneling events.
3. **Negligible quantum confinement:** The island's single-particle level spacing $\delta E$ must satisfy $\delta E \ll k_B T$, so electrons can be treated classically on the island (orthodox theory regime).

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain why Coulomb blockade is not observed in macroscopic wires but is observed in nanoscale tunnel junctions.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In a macroscopic conductor, the self-capacitance $C$ is enormous (typically $10^{-10}$ F or more), making the charging energy $E_c = e^2/2C$ negligibly small — on the order of $10^{-9}$ eV or less. This is **far smaller** than the thermal energy $k_B T$ at any practical temperature, so the discrete nature of charge is invisible and current flows continuously (1 mark).

In contrast, a nanoscale island with $C_\Sigma \sim 10^{-18}$ to $10^{-15}$ F gives $E_c \sim 0.1$ to $100$ meV, which **exceeds** $k_B T$ at cryogenic temperatures (~25 $\mu$eV/mK) or even room temperature for very small islands (1 mark).

Furthermore, the tunnel resistance in nanoscale junctions can be made very large ($R_T \gg R_Q \approx 25.8\ \text{k}\Omega$), satisfying the localization requirement that is automatically violated in macroscopic wires where $R \to 0$ (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** In the 2024 scheme, Part B questions carry 14 marks with internal choice. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each 14-mark question is split into sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 marks)** — *Derivation + Device Operation*

#### Part (a) **[7 marks]**: Derive the expression for the charging energy of a single electron on a Coulomb island and obtain the threshold voltage for the onset of single-electron tunneling.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the setup (1 mark):**

Consider an isolated metallic island connected to source (potential $V_S$) and drain (potential $V_D$) through two tunnel junctions. Let $C_S$, $C_D$ be the junction capacitances and $C_\Sigma = C_S + C_D$ the total island capacitance.

**Step 2 — Electrostatic energy of the island with $N$ excess electrons (2 marks):**

The self-energy of carrying charge $Q = -Ne$ on a capacitor $C_\Sigma$ is:

$$U(N) \;=\; \frac{N^2 e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Incremental charging energy (2 marks):**

To add one electron, compute:

$$\Delta U \;=\; U(N+1) - U(N) \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}\left[(N+1)^2 - N^2\right] \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}(2N+1)$$

For a neutral island ($N = 0$), this minimum is:

$$E_c \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Threshold voltage derivation (2 marks):**

Apply bias $V_{ds}$ symmetrically: $V_S = V_{ds}/2$, $V_D = -V_{ds}/2$. The total energy change for one tunneling event from source to island (with $N=0$) is:

$$\Delta E \;=\; \frac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma} \;-\; \frac{e V_{ds}}{2}$$

Setting $\Delta E = 0$ for the threshold:

$$V_{th} \;=\; \frac{e}{C_\Sigma}$$

This is the **threshold voltage** of Coulomb blockade.

---

#### Part (b) **[7 marks]**: With a neat functional diagram, describe the structure and working of a Single-Electron Transistor (SET). Explain how gate voltage controls the current.

**Model Solution:**

**Structure (3 marks):**

The SET consists of a Coulomb island (metallic dot or quantum dot) coupled to:
- **Source (S)** through tunnel junction 1 (capacitance $C_S$, resistance $R_S$)
- **Drain (D)** through tunnel junction 2 (capacitance $C_D$, resistance $R_D$)
- **Gate (G)** through a thin dielectric (capacitance $C_G$, no DC current path)

The total capacitance $C_\Sigma = C_S + C_D + C_G$ sets the charging energy.

**Working (2 marks):**

For a fixed $V_{ds}$ and varying $V_G$:
- When $V_G$ is such that no charge state is energetically favored, current $I_{ds} \approx 0$ (blockade).
- When $V_G$ aligns the chemical potential of the island with that of the source, one electron tunnels onto the island; immediately after, another tunnels off to the drain — producing a current pulse.

**Gate Control (2 marks):**

The gate voltage shifts the island's electrostatic potential by $\Delta \mu = e C_G V_G / C_\Sigma$. As $V_G$ is swept linearly, current oscillates periodically with period:

$$\Delta V_G \;=\; \frac{e}{C_G}$$

These are the famous **Coulomb oscillations**, with sharp peaks separated by zero-current valleys. The SET thus acts as a **voltage-tunable single-electron switch** with extremely high charge sensitivity ($\sim 10^{-6}\ e/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$).

---

### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Theory + I-V Characteristics*

#### Part (a) **[7 marks]**: Explain the orthodox theory of single-electron tunneling. State its assumptions and write the master equation for the charge-state probabilities.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The orthodox theory (Averin–Likharev, 1985–1991) is a semi-classical framework that treats electron tunneling events as classical probabilistic processes governed by free-energy balance.

**Assumptions (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Classical confinement:** The single-particle energy level spacing $\delta E$ in the island is much smaller than $k_B T$, so electrons are treated as classical particles with charge $-e$ rather than as quantum states.
2. **Instantaneous tunneling:** Each tunneling event occurs on a timescale short compared to the time between events, so events are uncorrelated.
3. **Well-defined charge state:** The number $n$ of excess electrons on the island is an integer at all times; the charge is well-quantized due to the large $R_T \gg R_Q$.

**Tunneling Rate (1 mark):**

$$\Gamma_j^{\pm}(n) \;=\; \frac{-\Delta F_j^{\pm}(n)}{e^2 R_j C_\Sigma \left[1 - \exp(\Delta F_j^{\pm}(n)/k_B T)\right]}$$

**Master Equation (2 marks):**

$$\frac{dP(n)}{dt} \;=\; \sum_j \left[\Gamma_j^+(n-1) P(n-1) + \Gamma_j^-(n+1) P(n+1)\right] \;-\; \sum_j \left[\Gamma_j^+(n) + \Gamma_j^-(n)\right] P(n)$$

In the steady state, $dP/dt = 0$, and the DC current is:

$$I_{ds} \;=\; e \sum_n P(n) \left[\Gamma_S^+(n) - \Gamma_S^-(n)\right]$$

---

#### Part (b) **[7 marks]**: Draw and explain the Coulomb staircase in the I–V characteristics of a single-electron transistor. How does gate voltage modulate the staircase?

**Model Solution:**

**Plot Description (2 marks):**

The I–V curve at low temperature shows a sequence of **discrete current steps** rather than a straight line. For $V_{ds} < e/C_\Sigma$, current is essentially **zero** — the Coulomb gap. At $V_{ds} = e/C_\Sigma$, the first step begins; further steps occur at $V_{ds} = (2n+1) e/C_\Sigma$. Each step corresponds to a new charge state $n$ becoming energetically accessible on the island.

**Step Height (2 marks):**

At $T \to 0$, each step has height $\Delta I \approx e/(2 R_T C_\Sigma)$. The staircase is "regular" when $R_S \neq R_D$; otherwise the steps merge into a single linear onset.

**Gate-Voltage Modulation (3 marks):**

Applying $V_G$ shifts the step positions along the $V_{ds}$ axis by:

$$\delta V_{ds} \;=\; -\frac{C_G}{C_D - C_S}\,V_G \quad \text{(for asymmetric junctions)}$$

- **Increasing $V_G$:** steps shift to **lower** $V_{ds}$.
- **Decreasing $V_G$:** steps shift to **higher** $V_{ds}$.
- For **symmetric** junctions ($C_S = C_D$), the staircase is **insensitive** to $V_G$ in the I–V plot; instead, the staircase appears as **Coulomb oscillations** when $I$ is plotted versus $V_G$ at fixed $V_{ds}$.

The Coulomb staircase thus serves as a direct spectroscopic fingerprint of single-electron charging in nano-scale devices.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in Coulomb Blockade Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $E_c$ to eV** when comparing with $k_B T$. At 300 K, $k_B T \approx 25.85$ meV; at 4.2 K (liquid He), $k_B T \approx 0.362$ meV. Many students write $E_c = e^2/2C$ in Joules and compare numerically with $k_B T$ in eV — **automatic ½–1 mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $V_{th} = e/C_\Sigma$ vs $e/2 C_\Sigma$.** The full Coulomb gap is $e/C_\Sigma$, but the half-width (offset) is $e/2 C_\Sigma$. Always state the bias configuration clearly.
>
> 3. **Omitting one of the three orthodox conditions** when asked to "list conditions for Coulomb blockade." Examiners expect **all three**: charging energy, tunnel resistance, AND classical confinement.
>
> 4. **Confusing Coulomb blockade with the quantum confinement effect.** They are *different*: Coulomb blockade is **electrostatic** (charging energy), while quantum confinement is **single-particle** (level spacing $\delta E$). Examiners trap students by asking for the *difference* — be ready with a one-line distinction.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in $\Delta F$ derivation.** Remember that $\Delta F < 0$ means tunneling is **allowed**, $\Delta F > 0$ means tunneling is **forbidden**.
>
> 6. **Sketching the I–V curve with a continuous slope through the origin** instead of a flat region followed by discrete steps. Always show the **Coulomb gap** as a horizontal line at $I = 0$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Coulomb Blockade**

- **Definition:** Suppression of single-electron tunneling when $E_c = e^2/2 C_\Sigma$ exceeds available energy.
- **Charging energy:** $E_c = e^2/2 C_\Sigma$ — the *minimum* electrostatic cost of adding one electron.
- **Total island capacitance:** $C_\Sigma = C_S + C_D + C_G$.
- **Threshold voltage (Coulomb gap half-width):** $V_{th} = e/2 C_\Sigma$.
- **Full Coulomb gap width:** $\Delta V = e/C_\Sigma$.
- **Gate-voltage oscillation period:** $\Delta V_G = e/C_G$.
- **Three orthodox conditions:**
  1. $E_c \gg k_B T$
  2. $R_T \gg R_Q = h/e^2 \approx 25.8\ \text{k}\Omega$
  3. Single-particle level spacing $\delta E \ll k_B T$ (classical electron regime)
- **Tunneling rate (orthodox):** $\Gamma = \dfrac{-\Delta F}{e^2 R_T C_\Sigma [1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T)]}$.
- **Master equation:** Probability balance over all charge states $n$ with rates $\Gamma_j^{\pm}$.
- **SET building blocks:** Source, drain, gate, island, two tunnel junctions, one gate capacitor.
- **Coulomb oscillations:** Periodic in $V_G$ with period $e/C_G$.
- **Coulomb staircase:** Stepwise I–V with step onset at $V_{ds} = (2n+1)e/C_\Sigma$.
- **Applications:** SET electrometers, single-electron pumps, CBT thermometers, ultra-low-power logic, quantum readout.
- **Energy conversion fact:** $k_B T$ at 300 K $\approx 25.85$ meV; at 4.2 K $\approx 0.362$ meV; at 100 mK $\approx 8.6\ \mu$eV.
- **Quantum resistance:** $R_Q = h/e^2 \approx 25.8\ \text{k}\Omega$ — the threshold above which single-electron effects become observable.
- **Distinguish clearly:** Coulomb blockade (electrostatic) $\neq$ quantum confinement (single-particle levels) $\neq$ Kondo effect (spin-screening).
- **Typical exam keywords:** "orthodox theory," "free-energy balance," "Coulomb gap," "Coulomb staircase," "single-electron transistor," "master equation."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Magnetic Field on a Crystal, Aharonov-Bohm Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect of Magnetic Field on a Crystal, Aharonov-Bohm Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — EFFECT OF MAGNETIC FIELD ON A CRYSTAL & AHARONOV-BOHM EFFECT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Effect of a Magnetic Field on a Crystal — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Term):**
> When a static magnetic field **B** is applied to a crystalline solid, the canonical momentum of an electron is modified through the **minimal-coupling substitution** $\mathbf{p} \rightarrow \mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A}$, where **A** is the magnetic vector potential ($\mathbf{B} = \nabla \times \mathbf{A}$). The periodic Bloch Hamiltonian of the crystal is therefore augmented by a magnetic Peierls phase, which (i) quantises the orbital motion into **Landau levels** in the free-electron limit, (ii) splits the spin degeneracy via the **Zeeman term**, and (iii) re-shapes the energy bands into the self-similar **Hofstadter butterfly** when the magnetic flux per unit cell becomes comparable to the **flux quantum $\Phi_0 = h/e \approx 4.068 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{Wb}$**.

In quantum mechanics the electron's motion is governed by the **vector potential**, not by the local magnetic field alone. This subtle fact is what produces the Aharonov–Bohm effect discussed later.

### 1.2 The Aharonov–Bohm Effect — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Aharonov–Bohm, 1959):**
> The **Aharonov–Bohm (AB) effect** is a purely quantum-mechanical phenomenon in which a charged particle acquires a measurable phase shift
> $$\phi_{AB} = \frac{e}{\hbar}\oint_{\mathcal{C}}\mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell} = \frac{e}{\hbar}\Phi_{\mathcal{C}}$$
> due to the electromagnetic potential along a closed path $\mathcal{C}$ that encloses a region where the magnetic field is strictly **zero**. It demonstrates that electromagnetic potentials ($\mathbf{A}, \phi$) are physical observables in quantum theory, not merely mathematical aids.

Two variants exist:

| Variant | Setup | Phase Acquired |
|---|---|---|
| **Magnetic AB** | Charged particle encircles a flux-carrying solenoid (B ≠ 0 *inside*, B = 0 *outside*) | $\phi = (e/\hbar)\Phi_B$ |
| **Electric AB** | Particle traverses a region with time-varying scalar potential (E ≠ 0, B = 0) | $\phi = (e/\hbar)\int V\,dt$ |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 1 — "The Invisible Whirlpool":**
> Imagine a swimmer crossing a circular pond. Hidden underneath is a powerful whirlpool (the magnetic flux) surrounded by a tall cylindrical net that blocks all water movement (B = 0 in the swim region). Classical physics says: *"If there is no current/push on me, my motion is unaffected."* Quantum physics says: *"Even though the water where I swim is perfectly still, the *circulation* of the whirlpool that I *cannot see* shifts the phase of my wave-like motion — measurable when two swimmers meet on the other side and interfere."* The phase shift depends only on the enclosed flux, not the local force.

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy 2 — "Magnetic Field Modifying a Lattice of Tiles":**
> Picture a tiled kitchen floor (the crystal lattice). Now slowly drag a giant bar magnet above it. The grout lines (Bloch periodicity) remain, but the *traffic patterns* of small rolling balls (electrons) on the floor reorganise into parallel lanes along the magnet's field — these are the **Landau levels**. The Hallmarks of quantum mechanics (quantised orbits) appear because the magnetic field forces the electrons to move in circles of a specific radius (the **cyclotron radius**).

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Required

- **Elementary charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Reduced Planck constant:** $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Flux quantum:** $\Phi_0 = h/e = 4.068 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{Wb}$
- **Electron rest mass:** $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}\ \text{kg}$
- **Bohr magneton:** $\mu_B = e\hbar/2m_0 = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}\ \text{J/T}$
- **Vacuum permeability:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hamiltonian of a Crystal in a Magnetic Field

The one-electron Hamiltonian of a crystalline solid subject to an external magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ is:

$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}\left(\mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A}\right)^2 + V_{\text{lat}}(\mathbf{r}) + g_s\mu_B \mathbf{S}\cdot\mathbf{B}$$

where:
- $\mathbf{p} = -i\hbar\nabla$ is the canonical momentum operator
- $\mathbf{A}$ is the vector potential satisfying $\mathbf{B} = \nabla\times\mathbf{A}$
- $V_{\text{lat}}(\mathbf{r})$ is the periodic crystal potential
- The final term is the **Zeeman interaction** ($g_s \approx 2$ for electrons)

**Why does $\mathbf{p}$ change to $\mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A}$?**
Because the canonical momentum conjugate to position is $\boldsymbol{\pi} = m\mathbf{v} + e\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A}$, where $m\mathbf{v}$ is the mechanical (kinetic) momentum. The magnetic field couples to the *kinetic* momentum, not the canonical one.

### 2.2 Logical Flow of the Theory

- **Step 1:** Identify the gauge freedom. Choose the **Landau gauge** $\mathbf{A} = (0, Bx, 0)$ for $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$.
- **Step 2:** Write $H = \frac{1}{2m_0}\left[(p_x)^2 + (p_y + eBx)^2 + p_z^2\right] + V_{\text{lat}}(\mathbf{r})$.
- **Step 3:** The motion along $z$ remains free, so separate $H = H_{xy} + p_z^2/2m_0$.
- **Step 4:** Translate the operator $p_y$ by defining the **cyclotron centre** $X = -p_y/(eB)$ (a constant of motion, since $[X, H] = 0$).
- **Step 5:** Define the **magnetic length** $l_B = \sqrt{\hbar/(eB)}$ and the **cyclotron frequency** $\omega_c = eB/m_0$.
- **Step 6:** In the free-electron limit ($V_{\text{lat}} \to 0$), the Hamiltonian reduces to a 1D harmonic oscillator in the displaced coordinate $x' = x - X$. Quantisation gives **Landau levels**.
- **Step 7:** Include $V_{\text{lat}}$ → Bloch bands in a magnetic field → **Hofstadter problem**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-grade** reference for this topic. Memorise every row.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vector potential (Landau gauge) | $\mathbf{A} = (0, Bx, 0)$ | T·m | $\nabla\times\mathbf{A} = B\hat{z}$ |
| 2 | Cyclotron frequency | $\omega_c = eB/m_0$ | rad/s | Orbital rotation frequency |
| 3 | Magnetic length | $l_B = \sqrt{\hbar/(eB)}$ | m | Quantum size of cyclotron orbit |
| 4 | Magnetic flux quantum | $\Phi_0 = h/e = 2\pi\hbar/e$ | Wb | Fundamental flux unit |
| 5 | Landau level energy | $E_n = \hbar\omega_c\left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$ | J | Quantised cyclotron energy |
| 6 | Degeneracy per unit area | $n_B = eB/h = 1/(2\pi l_B^2)$ | m$^{-2}$ | States per Landau level |
| 7 | Zeeman splitting | $\Delta E_Z = g_s\mu_B B$ | J | Spin-up/down energy gap |
| 8 | AB phase (magnetic) | $\phi_{AB} = (e/\hbar)\oint \mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell} = 2\pi\Phi/\Phi_0$ | rad | Geometric phase from enclosed flux |
| 9 | AB phase (electric) | $\phi_{AB} = (e/\hbar)\int V\,dt$ | rad | Phase from scalar potential |
| 10 | Hall conductance quanta | $G_{xy} = \nu e^2/h$ | S | Quantised plateaus (links to AB) |
| 11 | Hofstadter flux ratio | $\alpha = \Phi_{\text{cell}}/\Phi_0$ | — | Incommensurability parameter |
| 12 | Ring AB current | $I_{AB} = (e/h)\,\partial E/\partial\Phi$ | A | Persistent current in mesoscopic ring |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where does this matter in industry?**
>
> 1. **Quantum Hall Resistance Standards** — Metrology labs worldwide use the von Klitzing constant $R_K = h/e^2 \approx 25\,812.807\ \Omega$ as the *primary* resistance standard, derived from Landau-level quantisation.
> 2. **SQUID Magnetometers** — Superconducting Quantum Interference Devices exploit the AB effect in superconducting rings to detect magnetic fields as small as $\sim 10^{-15}$ T (used in magneto-encephalography).
> 3. **Topological Quantum Computing** — Braiding of Majorana zero modes uses AB-like geometric phases as logical operations.
> 4. **Mesoscopic Interferometers** — AB rings fabricated by e-beam lithography demonstrate *which-path* quantum control of single electrons.
> 5. **Graphene Nanoelectronics** — The anomalous (half-integer) quantum Hall effect in graphene is a direct consequence of the Dirac-Weyl Hamiltonian in a magnetic field.

### 2.5 Cyclotron Radius and the Quantum Criterion

A classical electron in field $\mathbf{B}$ traces a circle of radius

$$r_c = \frac{m_0 v_\perp}{eB} = \frac{\sqrt{2m_0 E_\perp}}{eB}$$

The quantum regime is reached when $r_c \lesssim l_B$, i.e. when the de Broglie wavelength becomes comparable to the orbit size. This is the condition for *visible* Landau-level quantisation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Landau Levels of a Free 2D Electron Gas in a Magnetic Field

We solve the 2D Hamiltonian

$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}\left[p_x^2 + (p_y + eBx)^2\right]$$

using the **Landau gauge** $\mathbf{A} = (0, Bx, 0)$ with $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$.

**Step 1 — Identify constants of motion.**
Since the Hamiltonian is independent of $y$, the operator $p_y$ commutes with $H$:
$$[p_y, H] = 0$$
Therefore, $p_y = \hbar k_y$ is a good quantum number and the wavefunction can be written as
$$\psi(x,y) = e^{i k_y y}\,\phi(x)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the Schrödinger equation.**
The kinetic part becomes (dropping the $e^{ik_y y}$ factor):
$$\left[\frac{p_x^2}{2m_0} + \frac{1}{2m_0}\left(\hbar k_y + eBx\right)^2\right]\phi(x) = E\,\phi(x)$$

**Step 3 — Define the cyclotron-centre coordinate.**
Let
$$X = -\frac{\hbar k_y}{eB} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \hbar k_y + eBx = eB(x - X)$$

Then the Hamiltonian becomes:
$$H = \frac{p_x^2}{2m_0} + \frac{1}{2}m_0\omega_c^2 (x - X)^2$$

This is **identical in form** to a 1D quantum harmonic oscillator centred at $x = X$.

**Step 4 — Quantise the oscillator.**
The eigenenergies of a harmonic oscillator with mass $m_0$ and frequency $\omega_c$ are
$$E_n = \hbar\omega_c\left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

**Step 5 — Write the eigenfunctions (Hermite functions).**
$$\phi_n(x - X) = H_n\!\left(\frac{x - X}{l_B}\right)\exp\!\left[-\frac{(x-X)^2}{2l_B^2}\right]$$
where $H_n$ is the $n$-th Hermite polynomial and $l_B = \sqrt{\hbar/(eB)}$.

**Step 6 — Result: full wavefunction in 2D.**
$$\boxed{\;\psi_{n,k_y}(x,y) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{L_y}}\,e^{i k_y y}\, H_n\!\left(\frac{x - X}{l_B}\right)\exp\!\left[-\frac{(x-X)^2}{2l_B^2}\right]\;}$$

with eigenenergy
$$E_n = \hbar\omega_c\left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right)$$

These are the **Landau levels**. They are *degenerate* in $k_y$ — every different $X$ corresponds to a different centre of cyclotron motion, hence a different eigenstate with the same energy.

**Step 7 — Degeneracy per unit area.**
The allowed values of $X$ are spaced by $\Delta X = 2\pi l_B^2/L_y$ (periodicity of $k_y$). In a sample of area $A = L_x L_y$, the number of states per Landau level is
$$N_\phi = \frac{A}{2\pi l_B^2} = \frac{eB\,A}{h}$$
The areal density of states per Landau level is therefore
$$n_B = \frac{eB}{h} = \frac{1}{2\pi l_B^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The density of states per Landau level grows linearly with $B$, while the level width is $\hbar\omega_c \propto B$. Hence the **total** number of states in a fixed $B$ range is roughly conserved — this conservation underlies the **quantum Hall effect**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Aharonov–Bohm Phase from the Schrödinger Equation

Consider a charged particle of charge $q = -e$ (electron) moving in a region where the magnetic field is zero but the vector potential is non-zero. The Hamiltonian is
$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}\left(\mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A}\right)^2 + V(\mathbf{r})$$

**Step 1 — Write the time-dependent Schrödinger equation.**
$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial t} = \frac{1}{2m_0}(-i\hbar\nabla + e\mathbf{A})^2\psi$$

**Step 2 — Apply a local gauge transformation.**
Define a new wavefunction
$$\psi'(\mathbf{r}, t) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{ie}{\hbar}\int_{\mathbf{r}_0}^{\mathbf{r}}\mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell}\right]\psi(\mathbf{r}, t)$$

Here the integral is along any path from a reference point $\mathbf{r}_0$ to $\mathbf{r}$. Since $\mathbf{B} = \nabla\times\mathbf{A} = 0$ in the region of interest, the path integral is *path-independent* (Stokes' theorem).

**Step 3 — Substitute back.**
A direct calculation shows that $\psi'$ satisfies
$$i\hbar\frac{\partial \psi'}{\partial t} = \left[\frac{(-i\hbar\nabla)^2}{2m_0} + V\right]\psi'$$
which is the **free-field Schrödinger equation**.

**Step 4 — The acquired phase.**
The exponential prefactor in Step 2 is a pure phase:
$$\phi_{AB}(\mathbf{r}) = -\frac{e}{\hbar}\int_{\mathbf{r}_0}^{\mathbf{r}}\mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell}$$

For a **closed loop** $\mathcal{C}$ enclosing a region of flux $\Phi$:
$$\boxed{\;\phi_{AB} = -\frac{e}{\hbar}\oint_{\mathcal{C}}\mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell} = -\frac{e}{\hbar}\Phi = -2\pi\,\frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\;}$$

(The sign is conventional; the magnitude of the physical effect is identical.)

**Step 5 — Interference in a double-slit setup.**
Two partial waves that travel along paths $C_1$ and $C_2$ enclosing flux $\Phi$ produce an intensity at the screen
$$I \propto |\psi_1 + \psi_2|^2 = 2|\psi_0|^2\left[1 + \cos(\phi_1 - \phi_2 + \phi_{AB})\right]$$
The fringe pattern shifts by **one full fringe** each time the enclosed flux changes by exactly **one flux quantum $\Phi_0$**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Persistent Current in a Mesoscopic AB Ring

A 1D ring of circumference $L$ threaded by flux $\Phi$ has the Hamiltonian
$$H = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m_0 L^2}\left(-i\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Periodic boundary conditions on a ring.**
The wavefunction must satisfy $\psi(\theta + 2\pi) = \psi(\theta)$, giving eigenstates $\psi_n(\theta) = (1/\sqrt{2\pi})e^{in\theta}$ with $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 2 — Energies.**
$$E_n(\Phi) = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m_0 L^2}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Persistent current from thermodynamic relation.**
At $T = 0$, the current carried by state $n$ is
$$I_n = -\frac{\partial E_n}{\partial \Phi} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_0 L^2}\cdot\frac{2}{e}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right) = -\frac{ev_F}{L}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)$$

where $v_F = \hbar k_F/m_0$ is the Fermi velocity and $k_F = 2\pi n_F/L$ is the Fermi wavevector.

**Step 4 — Total current at $T=0$.**
Summing over filled states $|n| \le n_F$:
$$I(\Phi) = -\frac{ev_F}{L}\sum_{n=-n_F}^{n_F}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)$$

This is a **sawtooth function** of $\Phi$ with period $\Phi_0$ and amplitude
$$|I_{\max}| \sim \frac{ev_F}{L} \sim \frac{e \hbar n_F}{m_0 L^2}$$

**Step 5 — Order of magnitude for a typical gold ring.**
Take $L = 1\ \mu\text{m}$, $n_F \approx 10^{28}\ \text{m}^{-3}$ for gold:
$$I_{\max} \approx \frac{1.6\times 10^{-19}\times 1.05\times 10^{-34}\times 10^{28}}{9.11\times 10^{-31}\times (10^{-6})^2} \approx 0.2\ \text{nA}$$
This tiny current is **experimentally measurable** and was first observed by **Chandrasekhar et al. (1985)** in metallic loops.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Three Key Regimes

| Regime | Condition | Energy Spectrum | Physical Picture |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Weak B** | $\hbar\omega_c \ll E_F$ | Weakly perturbed Bloch bands | Drude–Zener magnetoresistance |
| **Quantum Limit** | $\hbar\omega_c \gg E_F$ | Few Landau levels occupied | Integer/Quantum Hall effect |
| **Strong B + Lattice** | $\alpha = \Phi_{\text{cell}}/\Phi_0$ rational | **Hofstadter butterfly** | Self-similar fractal sub-bands |
| **AB regime (isolated ring)** | $L \sim l_B$ | $E_n(\Phi) = \text{const}(n + \Phi/\Phi_0)^2$ | Persistent current oscillations |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Formation of Landau Levels from a 2D Parabolic Band

The free-electron density of states in 2D is **constant**: $g_0(E) = m_0/(\pi\hbar^2)$.
On applying a magnetic field, this continuous spectrum is **collapsed** into discrete Landau levels, each carrying the full degeneracy $n_B = eB/h$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["2D Free Electron DOS Constant g0"]
    nodeB["Apply B along z Direction"]
    nodeC["Quantisation of Orbital Motion"]
    nodeD["Landau Level n=0 E = hw/2"]
    nodeE["Landau Level n=1 E = 3hw/2"]
    nodeF["Landau Level n=2 E = 5hw/2"]
    nodeG["Spin Splitting g mu B"]
    nodeH["Observable: Quantum Hall Plateaus"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Aharonov–Bohm Interferometer

The classic AB double-slit experiment with a hidden solenoid. The two partial electron waves acquire a relative phase equal to $2\pi\Phi/\Phi_0$, which is observed as a shift in the interference fringes on the screen.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS["Electron Source e-gun"]
    nodeSL["Slit 1 Path C1"]
    nodeSR["Slit 2 Path C2"]
    nodeSO["Long Solenoid Carrying Flux Phi"]
    nodeSC["Screen with Fringe Pattern"]
    nodeP["Phase Difference phi = 2 Pi Phi over Phi0"]
    nodeF["Fringe Shift by one Period as Phi changes by Phi0"]

    nodeS --> nodeSL
    nodeS --> nodeSR
    nodeSL --> nodeSC
    nodeSR --> nodeSC
    nodeSO -. encloses .-> nodeSL
    nodeSO -. encloses .-> nodeSR
    nodeSL --> nodeP
    nodeSR --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block Architecture of a Mesoscopic AB Ring Device

For a real nanoelectronic device (e.g., a gold or GaAs/AlGaAs ring), the functional architecture for measuring the AB effect.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSRC["Source Reservoir Fermi Level mu1"]
    nodeL1["Left Arm Length L1 Phase phi1"]
    nodeR1["Right Arm Length L2 Phase phi2"]
    nodeDST["Drain Reservoir Fermi Level mu2"]
    nodeTHR["Threading Coil Applies Flux Phi"]
    nodeAMP["Transimpedance Amplifier"]
    nodeADC["Lock-in Detector and DAQ"]
    nodePHI["Phase phi = 2 Pi Phi over Phi0 Modulates I"]
    nodeIV["I vs B Periodic Oscillations Period Phi0"]

    nodeSRC --> nodeL1
    nodeSRC --> nodeR1
    nodeL1 --> nodeDST
    nodeR1 --> nodeDST
    nodeTHR -. threads .-> nodeL1
    nodeTHR -. threads .-> nodeR1
    nodeDST --> nodeAMP
    nodeAMP --> nodeADC
    nodeTHR --> nodePHI
    nodeL1 --> nodePHI
    nodeR1 --> nodePHI
    nodePHI --> nodeIV
    nodeADC --> nodeIV
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Energy Spectrum of a 1D Ring vs Enclosed Flux

The single-particle energies $E_n(\Phi)$ form parabolas centred at $\Phi = -n\Phi_0$. The total ground-state energy has *cusps* at integer multiples of $\Phi_0$, leading to a sawtooth persistent current.

```mermaid

    nodeP0["Parabola n=0 minimum at Phi=0"]
    nodeP1["Parabola n=1 minimum at Phi negative Phi0"]
    nodePN1["Parabola n=negative 1 minimum at Phi positive Phi0"]
    nodeCUS["Cusps at Phi equals m Phi0 integers m"]
    nodeSW["Ground State Energy is Sawtooth Waveform"]
    nodeCUR["Current I equals negative dE over dPhi is Piecewise Linear"]

    nodeF --> nodeP1
    nodeF --> nodePN1

    nodeP1 --> nodeCUS
    nodePN1 --> nodeCUS
    nodeCUS --> nodeSW
    nodeSW --> nodeCUR
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["B=0 Bloch Bands Continuous Parabolic"]
    nodeB["Small B Landau Sub-bands Form Broadened"]
    nodeC["Large B Sharp Landau Levels Few Per Band"]
    nodeD["Quantum Limit n=0 Dominant"]
    nodeE["Hofstadter Butterfly when alpha rational p over q"]
    nodeF["Integer Quantum Hall Effect Plateaus in sigma_xy"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC

    nodeE --> nodeF
```
