<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign errors in the AB phase** — Students frequently write $\phi = e\Phi/\hbar$ but forget the sign of the electron charge $q = -e$. The phase itself is observable; the *sign* matters for interference direction.
> 2. **Confusing $\Phi_0 = h/e$ with $h/2e$** — The latter is the *superconducting* flux quantum. In nanoelectronics, $h/e$ is the operative one.
> 3. **Landau gauge vs symmetric gauge** — In the symmetric gauge $\mathbf{A} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}\times\mathbf{r}$ the Hamiltonian has *rotational* symmetry; in the Landau gauge it has *translational* symmetry along $y$. Mixing these leads to wrong degeneracy counts.
> 4. **Forgetting spin degeneracy in Landau level counting** — Each orbital Landau level holds $eB/h$ states *per spin*. Always multiply by 2 if the Zeeman term is negligible.
> 5. **Mixing the AB effect with the Hall effect** — The AB effect is *purely quantum interference*; the Hall effect involves a *real Lorentz force*. They are physically distinct phenomena that share only the underlying electromagnetism.
> 6. **Dimensional mistakes with $\hbar\omega_c$** — $\omega_c$ has units rad/s; multiplying by $\hbar$ gives joules. Convert to eV by dividing by $1.602\times 10^{-19}$ when required.

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Question Bank]**
Define the magnetic flux quantum $\Phi_0$ and write its value. Why is it a fundamental unit in mesoscopic physics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **magnetic flux quantum** is the smallest amount of magnetic flux that, when enclosed by a closed electronic path, produces a phase shift of $2\pi$ in the electron wavefunction.

$$\Phi_0 = \frac{h}{e} = \frac{2\pi\hbar}{e} \approx 4.068 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{Wb}$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
- It is the natural periodicity of all AB-related phenomena: persistent currents, AB oscillations, and the flux period of the Fraunhofer pattern in Josephson junctions all have period $\Phi_0$.
- It defines the magnetic length scale $l_B$ at which quantum effects of the magnetic field become important.
- The ratio $\Phi/\Phi_0$ (the **filling factor** when $\Phi$ is the flux per unit cell) controls the topology of the energy spectrum (Hofstadter butterfly).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question Bank]**
What are **Landau levels**? Write the energy expression for a 2D free electron gas in a uniform perpendicular magnetic field.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Landau levels** (1 mark) are the quantised energy levels of a charged particle confined to move in a plane perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field. They arise because the magnetic field forces the electron to perform circular cyclotron motion, and the quantization condition for the orbital angular momentum produces discrete radii.

**Energy expression (2 marks):**

$$E_n = \hbar\omega_c\left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right), \qquad \omega_c = \frac{eB}{m_0}, \qquad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

Each Landau level has a large degeneracy $N_\phi = eBA/h$ where $A$ is the sample area.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Starting from the Hamiltonian of a free electron in a uniform magnetic field, derive the **Landau level energy spectrum** for a 2D electron gas. Clearly state the assumptions, gauge choice, and the physical significance of the **magnetic length** $l_B$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Aharonov–Bohm phase** acquired by an electron travelling around a long solenoid carrying magnetic flux $\Phi$. Explain, with the help of an interference setup, how this phase is experimentally detected. Discuss why the AB effect proves that electromagnetic potentials are physical, not merely mathematical, constructs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks**

**1. Hamiltonian and gauge (2 marks):**
For an electron (charge $-e$) in a uniform field $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$, choose the **Landau gauge**:
$$\mathbf{A} = (0, Bx, 0) \quad\text{so that}\quad \nabla\times\mathbf{A} = B\hat{z}$$
The 2D Hamiltonian (no periodic potential, $V_{\text{lat}}=0$) is
$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}\left[p_x^2 + (p_y + eBx)^2\right]$$

**2. Separation of variables and constants of motion (1 mark):**
Because $H$ is independent of $y$, we have $[p_y, H] = 0$, so $p_y = \hbar k_y$ is a good quantum number. The eigenfunction is $\psi(x,y) = e^{i k_y y}\phi(x)$.

**3. Harmonic-oscillator identification (2 marks):**
Define the cyclotron-centre coordinate
$$X = -\frac{\hbar k_y}{eB}$$
Then
$$H = \frac{p_x^2}{2m_0} + \frac{1}{2}m_0\omega_c^2(x - X)^2, \quad \omega_c = \frac{eB}{m_0}$$
This is the Hamiltonian of a 1D harmonic oscillator of mass $m_0$ and frequency $\omega_c$, centred at $x = X$.

**4. Quantisation (1 mark):**
$$E_n = \hbar\omega_c\left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$
The corresponding eigenfunctions are
$$\phi_n(x - X) \propto H_n\!\left(\frac{x - X}{l_B}\right)\exp\!\left[-\frac{(x - X)^2}{2l_B^2}\right]$$

**5. Physical significance of $l_B$ (1 mark):**
The **magnetic length** is defined as
$$l_B = \sqrt{\frac{\hbar}{eB}}$$
For $B = 1\ \text{T}$, $l_B \approx 25.6\ \text{nm}$. It sets the spatial extent of the Landau-level wavefunction in the plane, and is the natural quantum length scale for all magnetic-field phenomena in a 2DEG.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks**

**1. Setup (1 mark):**
A long solenoid of radius $R$ carries flux $\Phi = \pi R^2 B$. Outside the solenoid, $\mathbf{B} = 0$ but $\mathbf{A} \neq 0$. For an ideal infinite solenoid:
$$\mathbf{A} = \frac{\Phi}{2\pi r}\hat{\phi}, \quad r > R$$
This satisfies $\nabla\times\mathbf{A} = 0$ for $r > R$ and $\int \mathbf{B}\cdot d\mathbf{a} = \Phi$.

**2. Wavefunction in a region with $\mathbf{B} = 0$ (1 mark):**
The Schrödinger Hamiltonian in the region $r > R$ is
$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}(-i\hbar\nabla + e\mathbf{A})^2$$
Although $\mathbf{A} \neq 0$, the *local* physics (spectrum) is unchanged because $\mathbf{B} = 0$ implies the operator $-i\hbar\nabla + e\mathbf{A}$ can be eliminated locally by a gauge transformation.

**3. Derivation of the phase (3 marks):**
Consider a wave-packet that traverses a closed path $\mathcal{C}$ encircling the solenoid. Comparing the wavefunction at the start and end of the path:
$$\psi_{\text{final}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{ie}{\hbar}\oint_{\mathcal{C}}\mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell}\right]\psi_{\text{initial}}$$
By Stokes' theorem:
$$\oint_{\mathcal{C}}\mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell} = \int_{\mathcal{S}}(\nabla\times\mathbf{A})\cdot d\mathbf{a} = \Phi$$
So the acquired phase is
$$\phi_{AB} = \frac{e\Phi}{\hbar} = 2\pi\,\frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}$$
For an electron, $q = -e$, so $\phi_{AB} = -2\pi\Phi/\Phi_0$.

**4. Experimental detection (1 mark):**
A **double-slit experiment** with the solenoid placed *behind* the slits: the two partial waves travel along $\mathcal{C}_1$ and $\mathcal{C}_2$ and interfere on a screen. The fringe pattern shifts by **one full period** each time $\Phi$ is increased by $\Phi_0$, even though the electrons never enter the region where $\mathbf{B} \neq 0$.

**5. Physical significance (1 mark):**
The interference shift depends only on $\Phi$ (a global integral of $\mathbf{A}$), not on the local field at the electron's position. This proves that the **vector potential** $\mathbf{A}$ — not just the magnetic field $\mathbf{B}$ — has direct physical significance in quantum mechanics. The effect was first measured unambiguously by **Tonomura et al. (1986)** using electron holography.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the effect of a magnetic field on the energy bands of a crystalline solid. With the aid of a clear sketch, describe the formation of **Landau sub-bands** and explain why the density of states becomes a series of $\delta$-function-like peaks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metallic ring of circumference $L = 2\ \mu\text{m}$ is threaded by a magnetic flux $\Phi$. Treating the ring as a strictly 1D system, derive the energy eigenvalues and show that the persistent current at zero temperature is a **sawtooth function of $\Phi$** with period $\Phi_0$. Estimate the maximum current amplitude for an electron density $n_s = 10^{28}\ \text{m}^{-1}$ (treated as a 1D line density). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks**

**1. Starting point (1 mark):**
With a uniform $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$ applied to a 3D crystal, the one-electron Hamiltonian is
$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}(\mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A})^2 + V_{\text{lat}}(\mathbf{r})$$
The vector potential breaks one of the translational symmetries of the lattice because $\mathbf{A}$ is *not* periodic. Only the **magnetic translation group** commutes with $H$.

**2. Free-electron limit (2 marks):**
For $V_{\text{lat}} = 0$, we recover the Landau levels:
$$E_n(k_z) = \hbar\omega_c\left(n + \tfrac{1}{2}\right) + \frac{\hbar^2 k_z^2}{2m_0}$$
The motion in $z$ remains free, so each level becomes a 1D parabola — a **Landau sub-band**.

**3. Inclusion of $V_{\text{lat}}$ (2 marks):**
In a periodic potential, Bloch's theorem must be replaced by the **magnetic translation group**. The ratio of flux per unit cell to flux quantum,
$$\alpha = \frac{\Phi_{\text{cell}}}{\Phi_0} = \frac{eBa^2}{h}$$
becomes a control parameter. For *rational* $\alpha = p/q$, the unit cell enlarges to $q$ original cells, and each Landau level splits into $q$ sub-bands — the **Hofstadter butterfly**.

**4. Density of states (2 marks):**
In the absence of broadening, the 2D DOS,
$$g_0(E) = \frac{m_0}{\pi\hbar^2}\ \text{(constant)}$$
collapses into a sum of $\delta$-functions:
$$g(E) = \frac{eB}{h}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\delta(E - E_n) \cdot 2\ \text{(spin)}$$
Each peak has weight $2eB/h$. With realistic disorder and temperature, the $\delta$-functions are broadened into **Gaussian or Lorentzian peaks**, which is the experimentally observed Landau-level fan.

**Sketch requirement:** The student should show the parabolic $E$ vs $k$ for $B=0$ transforming into discrete horizontal Landau levels at finite $B$, each with a 1D parabolic dispersion along $k_z$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks**

**1. Hamiltonian of the 1D ring (1 mark):**
A 1D ring of circumference $L$ with threaded flux $\Phi$ in Landau gauge-equivalent form (line vector potential $A = \Phi/L$):
$$H = \frac{1}{2m_0}\left(-i\hbar\frac{1}{L}\frac{\partial}{\partial\theta} + \frac{e\Phi}{L}\right)^2$$

**2. Eigenstates and eigenvalues (2 marks):**
The wavefunction is single-valued only if $\psi(\theta + 2\pi) = \psi(\theta)$, giving $\psi_n(\theta) = (1/\sqrt{2\pi})e^{in\theta}$ with integer $n$. The energy is
$$E_n(\Phi) = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m_0 L^2}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)^2, \quad n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

**3. Ground state at $T = 0$ (1 mark):**
Filling all states $|n| \le n_F$ where $n_F$ is determined by the line density $n_s$:
$$n_s = \frac{2n_F + 1}{L}\quad\text{(factor 2 for spin)}$$
The ground-state energy is
$$E_0(\Phi) = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m_0 L^2}\sum_{n=-n_F}^{n_F}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)^2$$

**4. Persistent current (1 mark):**
The current is
$$I(\Phi) = -\frac{\partial E_0}{\partial\Phi} = -\frac{\hbar^2}{m_0 L^2}\cdot\frac{1}{\Phi_0}\sum_{n=-n_F}^{n_F}\left(n + \frac{\Phi}{\Phi_0}\right)$$

**5. Sawtooth behaviour (1 mark):**
This is a **piecewise linear** function of $\Phi$. It is zero at half-integer multiples of $\Phi_0$, jumps at integer multiples, and is periodic with period $\Phi_0$. Plotting $I$ vs $\Phi$ gives a **sawtooth waveform** with cusps at $\Phi = m\Phi_0$.

**6. Numerical estimate (1 mark):**
The amplitude of the persistent current is
$$I_{\max} = \frac{ev_F}{L} = \frac{e\hbar}{m_0 L}\cdot\frac{2\pi n_F}{L}$$
For $L = 2\ \mu\text{m}$ and $n_s = 10^{28}\ \text{m}^{-1}$ (so $n_F \approx n_s L/2 = 10^{22}\ \text{states}$):
$$v_F = \frac{\hbar k_F}{m_0} = \frac{\hbar}{m_0}\cdot\frac{\pi n_s}{1} = \frac{1.05\times 10^{-34}\times \pi \times 10^{28}}{9.11\times 10^{-31}} \approx 3.6\times 10^{24}\ \text{m/s}$$
But this exceeds $c$, indicating the free-electron model is not realistic for such a high density. Using a more physical $v_F \approx 10^6\ \text{m/s}$ (typical Fermi velocity for a metal):
$$I_{\max} \approx \frac{1.6\times 10^{-19}\times 10^6}{2\times 10^{-6}} \approx 80\ \text{nA}$$
This is the order of magnitude experimentally observed in metallic mesoscopic rings.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Things to remember (exam-day essentials):**

- [x] **Minimal coupling substitution** is $\mathbf{p} \rightarrow \mathbf{p} + e\mathbf{A}$ (or $-e\mathbf{A}$ for electrons depending on convention).
- [x] **Landau gauge** $\mathbf{A} = (0, Bx, 0)$ is preferred for translation-invariant problems; **symmetric gauge** $\mathbf{A} = \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{B}\times\mathbf{r}$ for rotation-invariant problems.
- [x] **Cyclotron frequency** $\omega_c = eB/m_0$ and **magnetic length** $l_B = \sqrt{\hbar/(eB)}$.
- [x] **Landau levels:** $E_n = \hbar\omega_c(n + 1/2)$, degenerate over an area with $N_\phi = eBA/h$ states per level.
- [x] **Flux quantum** $\Phi_0 = h/e \approx 4.068\times 10^{-15}\ \text{Wb}$ (NOT $h/2e$, which is the superconducting one).
- [x] **Aharonov–Bohm phase** $\phi_{AB} = (e/\hbar)\oint \mathbf{A}\cdot d\boldsymbol{\ell} = 2\pi\Phi/\Phi_0$.
- [x] **AB effect** demonstrates physical reality of $\mathbf{A}$ and $\phi$, not just $\mathbf{E}$ and $\mathbf{B}$.
- [x] **Persistent current** in a mesoscopic ring is periodic in $\Phi$ with period $\Phi_0$ and sawtooth waveform.
- [x] **Hofstadter butterfly** emerges for Bloch electrons in a strong magnetic field when $\Phi_{\text{cell}}/\Phi_0$ is rational.
- [x] **Quantum Hall effect** plateaus in $\sigma_{xy} = \nu e^2/h$ arise from Landau-level quantisation + disorder.
- [x] **Zeeman term** $g_s\mu_B B$ lifts spin degeneracy of each Landau level (~$0.058$ meV/T for $g_s = 2$).
- [x] **Engineering applications:** von Klitzing resistance standard, SQUID magnetometers, mesoscopic interferometers, topological quantum bits.
- [x] **Common confusion:** AB ≠ Hall. AB is interference of a coherent particle with itself; Hall is a transport response of an ensemble.
- [x] **Sign convention:** For an *electron* (charge $-e$), the AB phase is $-2\pi\Phi/\Phi_0$.
- [x] **Magnetic length scale check:** $l_B = 25.6\ \text{nm}/\sqrt{B[\text{T}]}$.

> **End of Module 3 Topic: Effect of Magnetic Field on a Crystal & Aharonov–Bohm Effect**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Shubnikov-de Hass Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** The Shubnikov-de Hass Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Shubnikov–de Haas (SdH) Effect** is the quantum oscillatory phenomenon in which the **electrical resistivity (magnetoresistance)** of a metallic or semiconducting system exhibits a periodic variation as a function of an applied magnetic field **B**, observed at low temperatures and strong magnetic fields. It is the direct electrical transport analogue of the **de Haas–van Alphen (dHvA) effect**, where magnetisation oscillates instead of resistance.

Mathematically, the resistivity contains an oscillatory component:
$$\Delta \rho_{xx}(B) \propto \cos\left[ 2\pi \left( \frac{F}{B} - \frac{1}{2} + \beta \right) \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> The SdH effect is one of the most powerful **experimental probes of the Fermi surface** in bulk, 2-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG) systems, graphene, and topological materials. In nano-electronics, it is the *gold-standard* technique for measuring the effective mass, scattering time, and Berry phase of charge carriers in low-dimensional heterostructures (e.g. GaAs/AlGaAs, MoS₂, graphene).

## 1.2 The Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine electrons in a crystal behaving like **orbits around the sun**. When we apply no magnetic field, the orbits are random, and resistance has a normal value. Now, switch on a strong magnetic field. The electrons can no longer orbit freely — they are forced into **discrete, quantised circular "racetracks"** (called **Landau Levels**), like cars constrained to drive only on specific lanes of a circular track.

As the magnetic field is increased, the racetracks shrink and **occasionally a whole lane of allowed orbits passes exactly through the Fermi energy** (the "water level" of electrons). Every time a new lane aligns with the Fermi level, the available states for scattering suddenly change, and the resistance **jumps up and down** in a regular, wavelike pattern.

> [!NOTE] 
> **Real-world analogy:** Picture a multi-storey car park. Cars (electrons) can only park on specific floors (Landau levels). As you tilt the building (increase B), floor heights change. Each time a floor crosses the street level (Fermi energy), a wave of cars enters or leaves — producing a rhythmic pattern in the parking fee (resistance).

## 1.3 The Three Mandatory Conditions

For SdH oscillations to be observable, **all three** of the following experimental conditions must be satisfied simultaneously:

| Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| High magnetic field | $\hbar \omega_c \gg k_B T$ | Landau level spacing exceeds thermal blurring |
| Low temperature | $T \to 0$ regime, typically $T < 4.2\,\text{K}$ | Suppresses thermal smearing of Landau levels |
| High mobility | $\omega_c \tau \gg 1$ | Electron completes many cyclotron orbits before scattering |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> Failure of any one condition **destroys** the oscillations. This is why SdH measurements are performed in **dilution refrigerators and superconducting magnets** in low-temperature physics laboratories.

## 1.4 Historical Context & Relation to Nano-electronics

Discovered in **1930** by Lev Shubnikov and Wander Johannes de Haas in bismuth, the effect lay dormant for decades until the rise of **semiconductor heterostructures (2DEG)** in the 1970s–80s. In modern nano-electronics, the SdH effect is the canonical tool used to:
- Map the **Fermi surface topology** of topological insulators and Weyl semimetals.
- Measure the **Berry phase** of Dirac fermions in graphene ($\beta = 1/2$).
- Extract the **effective mass** in MOSFET inversion layers.
- Determine the **quantum lifetime** in 2D materials.

## 1.5 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are central to the SdH effect and must be memorised:

- **Reduced Planck's constant:** $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$
- **Bohr magneton:** $\mu_B = 9.274 \times 10^{-24}\,\text{J/T}$
- **Electron charge magnitude:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$
- **Standard unit of SdH frequency:** $\text{Tesla (T)}$ for $F$ when $A$ is in $\text{m}^{-2}$
- **Typical 2DEG densities:** $n_s \sim 10^{15} - 10^{16}\,\text{m}^{-2}$
- **Typical SdH frequencies in 2DEG:** $F \sim 10 - 100\,\text{T}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Origin: Landau Quantisation of Orbital Motion

In a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$, an electron's orbital motion in the $xy$-plane becomes quantised. The continuous parabolic density of states breaks up into discrete **Landau levels**:

$$E_n = \left( n + \frac{1}{2} \right)\hbar \omega_c + \frac{\hbar^2 k_z^2}{2m^*}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

where the **cyclotron frequency** is:
$$\omega_c = \frac{eB}{m^*}$$

Each Landau level has a degeneracy (number of available states per unit area):
$$g_L = \frac{eB}{h} = \frac{1}{2\pi l_B^2}$$

where $l_B = \sqrt{\hbar / eB}$ is the **magnetic length** — a natural length scale in nano-electronics that shrinks as $B$ increases.

## 2.2 Why Does Resistance Oscillate? — The Mechanism Step-by-Step

The SdH oscillation in resistivity is *not* a direct consequence of Landau quantisation of the spectrum alone; it arises because the **scattering probability depends on the density of states at the Fermi energy**, and this density oscillates with $B$.

- **Step 1:** At a particular $B$, only specific Landau levels cross the Fermi energy $E_F$.
- **Step 2:** When $E_F$ sits *exactly* in the middle of a Landau level, the density of states at $E_F$ is **maximum** → scattering rate is **maximum** → resistance is **maximum**.
- **Step 3:** When $E_F$ sits in a gap between Landau levels, the density of states is **minimum** → scattering rate is **minimum** → resistance is **minimum**.
- **Step 4:** As $B$ is swept, $E_F$ "bobs" up and down through the Landau ladder, producing **oscillatory** resistance with **1/B periodicity**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table contains every equation you must know to solve KTU 2024 problems on the SdH effect.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cyclotron frequency | $\omega_c = eB / m^*$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Landau level energy | $E_n = (n+1/2)\hbar\omega_c + \hbar^2 k_z^2 / 2m^*$ | J or eV |
| 3 | Landau level degeneracy per unit area | $g_L = eB/h = 1/(2\pi l_B^2)$ | $\text{m}^{-2}$ |
| 4 | Magnetic length | $l_B = \sqrt{\hbar / eB}$ | m |
| 5 | **Onsager relation** (SdH frequency) | $F = (\hbar / 2\pi e)\, A_{\text{ext}}$ | T (when $A$ in $\text{m}^{-2}$) |
| 6 | Oscillation phase | $\Phi = 2\pi(F/B - 1/2 + \beta)$ | rad |
| 7 | Dingle factor | $R_D = \exp(-2\pi^2 k_B T_D / \hbar\omega_c)$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Thermal factor | $R_T = X / \sinh X$, $X = 2\pi^2 k_B T / \hbar\omega_c$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Spin factor | $R_S = \cos(\pi g m^* / 2 m_e)$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | **Lifshitz–Kosevich (LK) amplitude** | $\Delta\rho \propto R_D \cdot R_T \cdot R_S$ | — |
| 11 | 2DEG density from SdH | $n_s = 2eF/h$ | $\text{m}^{-2}$ |
| 12 | Effective mass from LK | $A \propto T / \sinh X \implies m^*$ | kg |
| 13 | Dingle temperature | $T_D = \hbar / (2\pi k_B \tau_q)$ | K |
| 14 | Quantum mobility | $\mu_q = e \tau_q / m^*$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{V·s}$ |
| 15 | 1/B oscillation condition | $1/B_n = (n + \gamma)\, 2\pi e / \hbar A$ | $\text{T}^{-1}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Why is the Onsager relation the heart of the SdH effect?** It links a *directly measurable* quantity (the SdH frequency $F$, obtained by FFT of the oscillatory resistance vs $1/B$) to a *fundamental* property of the metal — the **extremal cross-sectional area of the Fermi surface** perpendicular to $\mathbf{B}$. This is the entire reason SdH is so important in nano-electronics.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in Modern Nano-electronics

- **GaAs/AlGaAs 2DEG:** Standard textbook system. SdH gives $n_s$, $m^*$, $\tau_q$ to within 1% accuracy.
- **Graphene (Dirac fermion system):** The Berry phase $\beta = 1/2$ produces a characteristic **phase shift of $\pi$** in SdH oscillations, providing a direct experimental signature of relativistic charge carriers.
- **Topological insulators (Bi$_2$Se$_3$, Bi$_2$Te$_3$):** Surface Dirac fermions show SdH with $\beta = 1/2$, distinguishing them from bulk carriers.
- **Weyl/Dirac semimetals:** SdH reveals the chiral anomaly and Fermi surface topology in 3D Dirac systems.
- **2D transition metal dichalcogenides (MoS$_2$, WSe$_2$):** SdH used to probe spin–orbit coupling-induced valley splitting.

## 2.5 Dimensional Sanity Check for the Onsager Relation

To confirm the Onsager relation $F = (\hbar/2\pi e)\,A$ is dimensionally consistent:
- $[\hbar] = \text{J·s}$, $[e] = \text{C}$, $[A] = \text{m}^{-2}$
- $[\hbar A / e] = \text{J·s} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} / \text{C} = \text{J·s} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s/A·s} = \text{V·s} \cdot \text{m}^{-2}$
- Since $\text{V·s/m}^2 = \text{T}$ (because $1\,\text{T} = 1\,\text{V·s/m}^2$), the equation is dimensionally sound.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Onsager Relation (KTU Favourite)

The Onsager relation is the most important derivation for the SdH effect. We derive it from the Bohr–Sommerfeld quantisation condition.

**Step 1: Classical electron motion in a magnetic field.**
In a uniform $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$, an electron performs a **helical orbit** with constant $k_z$ and circular motion in the $k_x k_y$ plane. The area of this orbit in $k$-space is related to the cyclotron frequency by:
$$A(E, k_z) = \pi k_\perp^2$$

**Step 2: Quantisation condition (Bohr–Sommerfeld).**
For a periodic orbit, the Bohr–Sommerfeld rule requires:
$$\oint \mathbf{p} \cdot d\mathbf{q} = (n + \gamma)\, 2\pi \hbar$$

For an electron in a magnetic field, the canonical momentum includes the vector potential contribution. The result is:
$$A(E, k_z) = \frac{2\pi e B}{\hbar} (n + \gamma)$$

where $\gamma = 1/2$ for the standard Landau problem (this gives the half-integer in $(n+1/2)\hbar\omega_c$).

**Step 3: Quantisation at the Fermi energy.**
The condition for the $n$-th Landau level to coincide with the Fermi energy $E_F$ is:
$$A(E_F, k_z) = \frac{2\pi e B_n}{\hbar}(n + \gamma)$$

For a 2D system, the orbit area is independent of $k_z$ and equals the Fermi circle area: $A(E_F) = \pi k_F^2$.

**Step 4: Identification of the SdH period.**
Successive Landau levels cross $E_F$ at fields $B_n$ satisfying:
$$\frac{1}{B_n} = \frac{2\pi e}{\hbar A}(n + \gamma)$$

This shows oscillations are **periodic in $1/B$**. The SdH frequency $F$ is defined as the inverse of the period in $1/B$:
$$F \equiv \frac{1}{\Delta(1/B)} = \frac{\hbar A}{2\pi e}$$

**Step 5: Final result (Onsager relation):**

$$\boxed{\, F = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi e}\, A_{\text{ext}} \,}$$

> [!NOTE] 
> For 3D Fermi surfaces, $A_{\text{ext}}$ is the **extremal** (maximum or minimum) cross-sectional area perpendicular to $\mathbf{B}$ — only these orbits contribute dominantly to the oscillations because of the stationary phase argument in the Lifshitz–Kosevich theory.

## 3.2 Derivation of the 2DEG Density from SdH Frequency

Given the LK formula, the **total 2D carrier density** is connected to the SdH frequency by:

**Step 1:** The area of a circular 2D Fermi surface is $A = \pi k_F^2$.

**Step 2:** The 2D density of states is $n_s = k_F^2 / (2\pi)$ (including spin degeneracy of 2).

**Step 3:** Substitute into the Onsager relation:

$$
F = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi e}\, \pi k_F^2
$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $k_F^2 = 2eF/\hbar$.

**Step 5:** Substitute into the density expression:

$$n_s = \frac{k_F^2}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2eF}{\hbar} = \frac{eF}{\pi \hbar}$$

**Step 6:** Rewrite using $h = 2\pi\hbar$:

$$\boxed{\, n_s = \frac{2eF}{h} \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> The factor of 2 in $2eF/h$ comes from spin degeneracy. For systems with strong spin–orbit coupling or valley degeneracy, this formula must be generalised to $n_s = g_v g_s eF/h$, where $g_v$ is the valley degeneracy and $g_s = 2$ is the spin degeneracy.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Cyclotron Effective Mass from the Temperature Dependence of SdH Amplitude

The **thermal damping factor** in the Lifshitz–Kosevich formula is:
$$R_T(B, T) = \frac{X}{\sinh X}, \quad X = \frac{2\pi^2 k_B T}{\hbar \omega_c} = \frac{2\pi^2 k_B T m^*}{\hbar e B}$$

The SdH amplitude at a fixed $B$ therefore scales as:
$$A(T) = A_0 \cdot \frac{X}{\sinh X}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural log of both sides (only the $R_T$ part depends on $T$):

$$\ln\left[\frac{A(T)}{A_0}\right] = \ln X - \ln(\sinh X)$$

**Step 2:** In the limit of low temperature and high $B$ such that $X \gg 1$:

$$\sinh X \approx \frac{1}{2} e^X$$

So:
$$\ln\left[\frac{A(T)}{A_0}\right] = \ln X - X - \ln 2 = \ln\left(\frac{X}{2}\right) - X$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:

$$\ln\left[\frac{A(T) \sinh X}{X}\right] = -X = -\frac{2\pi^2 k_B m^*}{\hbar e B} T$$

**Step 4:** Define the **Lifshitz–Kosevich (LK) mass plot** — plot $\ln[A(T)\sinh X/X]$ vs $T$ at fixed $B$. The slope gives $m^*$:

$$m^* = -\frac{\hbar e B}{2\pi^2 k_B} \times \text{slope}$$

> [!NOTE] 
> This is the standard experimental method to extract effective mass. The "mass plot" is *always* linear if the LK theory applies, and its slope yields $m^*$ directly. KTU examiners love this.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Dingle Temperature and Quantum Lifetime

The **Dingle factor** quantifies the smearing of Landau levels due to impurity scattering:
$$R_D = \exp\left(-\frac{2\pi^2 k_B T_D}{\hbar \omega_c}\right) = \exp\left(-\frac{\pi}{\omega_c \tau_q}\right)$$

**Step 1:** Taking the natural log:
$$\ln R_D = -\frac{2\pi^2 k_B T_D}{\hbar \omega_c} = -\frac{2\pi^2 k_B T_D m^*}{\hbar e B}$$

**Step 2:** Define the **Dingle plot** — plot $\ln[A(B)\cdot B \sinh X / X]$ vs $1/B$. The slope gives $T_D$:

$$T_D = -\frac{\hbar e}{2\pi^2 k_B m^*} \times \text{slope}$$

**Step 3:** The **quantum scattering time** is then:
$$\tau_q = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi k_B T_D}$$

**Step 4:** The **quantum mobility** is:
$$\mu_q = \frac{e \tau_q}{m^*} = \frac{e \hbar}{2\pi k_B T_D m^*}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Distinction from transport mobility:** The quantum (single-particle) lifetime $\tau_q$ differs from the transport lifetime $\tau_t$ by a factor of typically 5–100. In SdH, only $\tau_q$ appears because the oscillations sense *all* scattering events, while transport senses only *large-angle* scattering. This is a frequent KTU exam point.

## 3.5 Complete Lifshitz–Kosevich (LK) Formula — The Final Form

Combining all factors, the **full LK formula** for the oscillatory part of the resistivity is:

$$\Delta \rho_{xx} = \rho_0 \cdot \frac{5}{2}\left(\frac{B}{2F}\right)^{1/2} \cdot R_T \cdot R_D \cdot R_S \cdot \cos\left[ 2\pi\left( \frac{F}{B} - \frac{1}{2} + \beta \right) \right]$$

with the three multiplicative reduction factors:
$$R_T = \frac{2\pi^2 k_B T / \hbar\omega_c}{\sinh(2\pi^2 k_B T / \hbar\omega_c)}, \quad R_D = e^{-2\pi^2 k_B T_D / \hbar\omega_c}, \quad R_S = \cos\left(\frac{\pi g m^*}{2 m_e}\right)$$

> [!NOTE] 
> The **Berry phase $\beta$** in the phase term is the topological fingerprint. For ordinary (Schrödinger) fermions, $\beta = 0$. For Dirac fermions (graphene, topological surface states), $\beta = 1/2$, producing a $\pi$ phase shift visible in the Landau level fan diagram.

## 3.6 Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** In a GaAs/AlGaAs 2DEG, the SdH frequency is measured to be $F = 32.5\,\text{T}$ at $T = 1.5\,\text{K}$. Calculate the 2D electron density, the Fermi wavevector, and the Fermi energy. Given $m^* = 0.067\,m_e$.

**Solution Step 1 — Density:**

$$n_s = \frac{2eF}{h} = \frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times 32.5}{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$$

$$n_s = \frac{1.0413 \times 10^{-17}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34}} = 1.572 \times 10^{16}\,\text{m}^{-2}$$

**Solution Step 2 — Fermi wavevector:**

$$k_F = \sqrt{2\pi n_s} = \sqrt{2\pi \times 1.572 \times 10^{16}} = 3.143 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

**Solution Step 3 — Fermi energy:**

$$E_F = \frac{\hbar^2 k_F^2}{2 m^*} = \frac{(1.054 \times 10^{-34})^2 (3.143 \times 10^{8})^2}{2 \times 0.067 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}}$$

$$E_F = \frac{(1.111 \times 10^{-68})(9.879 \times 10^{16})}{1.221 \times 10^{-31}} = \frac{1.097 \times 10^{-51}}{1.221 \times 10^{-31}} = 8.99 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{J}$$

Converting to meV: $E_F = 8.99 \times 10^{-21} / 1.602 \times 10^{-22} \approx 56.1\,\text{meV}$.

> [!NOTE] 
> This $E_F \approx 56\,\text{meV}$ is very characteristic of modulation-doped GaAs 2DEG systems. A good sanity check for KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: SdH Measurement Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CRYO[Cryogenic Environment T less than 4.2 K]
        A[Sample Mount with 2DEG or Bulk Material]
        B[Superconducting Magnet Coil producing B up to 14 T]
        C[Thermometer and He Gas Flow Controller]
    end
    
    subgraph ELEC[Electronic Measurement Chain]
        D[Low Frequency AC Lock in Amplifier at 13 Hz]
        E[Constant Current Source 1 to 10 microamp]
        F[Voltage Probes for R_xx and R_xy]
    end
    
    subgraph DATA[Data Acquisition and Analysis]
        G[ADC and PC Storage at Field Steps of 0.01 T]
        H[Subtract Smooth Background Polynomial]
        I[FFT to Extract SdH Frequency F]
        J[Lifshitz Kosevich Fit to Extract m star and T_D]
    end
    
    B -->|Field Sweep| A
    C -->|Temp Control| A
    E -->|I_xx| A
    A -->|V_xx and V_xy| F
    F -->|Lock in Detection| D
    D -->|Signal| G
    G -->|Raw Data| H
    H -->|Background Removed| I
    I -->|F value| J
    J -->|m star T_D Berry Phase| DATA
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Physics of SdH Oscillation Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Electrons in Conduction Band of 2DEG or Bulk Metal] --> P1[Apply Static Magnetic Field B along z axis]
    P1 --> P2[Orbital Motion Quantised into Landau Levels]
    P2 --> P3[Energy Spectrum becomes Discrete with Spacing hbar omega_c]
    P3 --> P4[Fermi Level EF Modulated by B Sweep]
    P4 --> P5{Q1 Does a Landau Level Cross EF}
    P5 -->|Yes Level Centre at EF| P6[DOS at EF Maximum]
    P6 --> P7[Scattering Rate Maximum]
    P7 --> P8[Resistivity Maximum]
    P5 -->|No Gap at EF| P9[DOS at EF Minimum]
    P9 --> P10[Scattering Rate Minimum]
    P10 --> P11[Resistivity Minimum]
    P8 --> P12[Oscillatory Pattern in R_xx versus 1 over B]
    P11 --> P12
    P12 --> P13[FFT of R_xx versus 1 over B yields SdH Frequency F]
    P13 --> P14[Onsager Relation yields Fermi Surface Area A]
    P13 --> P15[LK Temperature Dependence yields m star]
    P13 --> P16[LK Field Dependence yields T_D and tau_q]
    P13 --> P17[Phase of Oscillation yields Berry Phase beta]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Information Content Extracted from SdH

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SdH[SdH Oscillatory Signal R_xx versus B] --> A1[Frequency Domain FFT Analysis]
    SdH --> A2[Amplitude versus T Analysis]
    SdH --> A3[Amplitude versus B Dingle Analysis]
    SdH --> A4[Phase Analysis via Landau Fan Diagram]
    
    A1 --> O1[Extremal Fermi Surface Area A_ext via Onsager Relation]
    A1 --> O2[2D Carrier Density n_s]
    A1 --> O3[Fermi Surface Anisotropy via Angular Dependence]
    
    A2 --> O4[Effective Mass m star]
    A2 --> O5[Many Body Mass Renormalisation]
    
    A3 --> O6[Dingle Temperature T_D]
    A3 --> O7[Quantum Lifetime tau_q]
    A3 --> O8[Quantum Mobility mu_q]
    
    A4 --> O9[Berry Phase beta for Topological Classification]
    A4 --> O10[Spin Splitting g factor]
    A4 --> O11[2D versus 3D Character from Phase]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Landau Level Fan Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LLAND[Landau Level Structure at Successive B]
        L1[B low Spacing Small Many Levels below EF]
        L2[B medium Spacing Medium Fewer Levels below EF]
        L3[B high Spacing Large Few Levels below EF]
    end
    
    LLAND --> FAN[Landau Fan Plot N integer versus 1 over B]
    FAN --> FIT[Linear Extrapolation to 1 over B equals zero]
    FIT --> INT[Intercept gives Berry Phase beta]
    
    INT -->|Intercept equals 0 point 5| TOPO[Topological Dirac System Graphene or TI]
    INT -->|Intercept equals 0| TRIV[Trivial Parabolic System GaAs 2DEG]
```

> [!NOTE] 
> The Landau fan diagram is constructed by assigning integer Landau indices $N$ to each oscillation peak (or trough) and plotting $N$ vs $1/B$. The $y$-intercept at $1/B = 0$ reveals the Berry phase: an intercept of $+1/2$ signals non-trivial topology, while $0$ indicates a conventional band structure.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Shubnikov–de Haas (SdH) effect. What are the essential experimental conditions required for its observation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Shubnikov–de Haas effect is the **oscillatory variation of the electrical resistivity (magnetoresistance) of a conductor as a function of an applied magnetic field $B$**, observed at low temperatures, arising from the quantisation of electron orbital motion into discrete Landau levels. **(1 mark)**

The three essential conditions for its observation are:
1. **Low temperature:** $k_B T \ll \hbar \omega_c$, typically $T < 4.2\,\text{K}$, so that thermal smearing does not wash out the Landau level structure. **(1 mark)**
2. **High magnetic field:** $\hbar \omega_c$ must be large enough that Landau levels are well-separated. **(0.5 mark)**
3. **High carrier mobility:** $\omega_c \tau \gg 1$, so that electrons complete many cyclotron orbits before being scattered. **(0.5 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State the Onsager relation for the Shubnikov–de Haas effect and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Onsager relation connects the SdH oscillation frequency $F$ to the extremal cross-sectional area of the Fermi surface $A_{\text{ext}}$ perpendicular to the applied magnetic field:
$$F = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi e}\, A_{\text{ext}} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**Physical significance:** It is the most important practical consequence of the SdH effect. Because $F$ can be measured directly from the FFT of the oscillatory resistivity, the Onsager relation allows experimental determination of the **Fermi surface area** — a fundamental property of the electronic structure. It is the basis for mapping Fermi surfaces of metals, 2DEGs, graphene, and topological materials. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**(a) Derive the Lifshitz–Kosevich (LK) formula for the amplitude of Shubnikov–de Haas oscillations, clearly identifying the physical origin of each factor: thermal, Dingle, and spin. (7 marks)**

**(b) A GaAs/AlGaAs 2DEG has an SdH frequency of $F = 28.4\,\text{T}$ measured at $T = 1.2\,\text{K}$. Calculate: (i) the 2D electron density $n_s$, (ii) the Fermi wavevector $k_F$, and (iii) the Fermi energy $E_F$ in meV. Take the effective mass as $m^* = 0.067\,m_e$. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of the LK Formula (7 marks):**

The LK formula gives the amplitude of the SdH oscillatory part of the magnetoresistivity:
$$\Delta\rho_{xx} \propto R_T \cdot R_D \cdot R_S \cdot \cos\left[2\pi\left(\frac{F}{B} - \frac{1}{2} + \beta\right)\right]$$

Each factor has a specific physical origin:

**(1) Thermal factor $R_T$ (2.5 marks):**
At finite temperature, the Fermi–Dirac distribution smears the sharpness of the Fermi surface over an energy window of width $\sim k_B T$. The thermal factor is:
$$R_T = \frac{X}{\sinh X}, \quad X = \frac{2\pi^2 k_B T}{\hbar \omega_c} = \frac{2\pi^2 k_B T m^*}{\hbar e B}$$

**Origin:** When $k_B T \gg \hbar \omega_c$, the smearing exceeds the Landau level spacing and the oscillations vanish ($R_T \to 0$). When $k_B T \ll \hbar \omega_c$, the smearing is small and oscillations survive ($R_T \to 1$).

**(2) Dingle factor $R_D$ (2.5 marks):**
Impurity and defect scattering broadens each Landau level into a Lorentzian of width $\Gamma = \hbar / 2\tau_q$. The exponential reduction is:
$$R_D = \exp\left(-\frac{2\pi^2 k_B T_D}{\hbar \omega_c}\right), \quad T_D = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi k_B \tau_q}$$

**Origin:** Short scattering times (low mobility) cause Landau levels to overlap, destroying the quantisation. $R_D$ vanishes as $\tau_q \to 0$.

**(3) Spin factor $R_S$ (2 marks):**
The Zeeman splitting of spin-up and spin-down Landau levels at the Fermi energy modulates the oscillation amplitude:
$$R_S = \cos\left(\frac{\pi g m^*}{2 m_e}\right)$$

**Origin:** Spin splitting can cause a Landau level of one spin species to coincide with $E_F$ while the other does not, leading to partial cancellation of the oscillation. When $g m^* = 2 m_e$, $R_S = -1$ (complete phase inversion, "spin zero").

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) 2D electron density (2.5 marks):**
$$n_s = \frac{2eF}{h} = \frac{2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 28.4}{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}$$
$$n_s = \frac{9.099 \times 10^{-18}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34}} = 1.373 \times 10^{16}\,\text{m}^{-2}$$

**(ii) Fermi wavevector (2 marks):**
$$k_F = \sqrt{2\pi n_s} = \sqrt{2\pi \times 1.373 \times 10^{16}}$$
$$k_F = \sqrt{8.628 \times 10^{16}} = 2.937 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m}^{-1}$$

**(iii) Fermi energy (2.5 marks):**
$$E_F = \frac{\hbar^2 k_F^2}{2 m^*} = \frac{(1.054 \times 10^{-34})^2 (2.937 \times 10^{8})^2}{2 \times 0.067 \times 9.109 \times 10^{-31}}$$
$$E_F = \frac{(1.111 \times 10^{-68})(8.626 \times 10^{16})}{1.221 \times 10^{-31}}$$
$$E_F = \frac{9.581 \times 10^{-52}}{1.221 \times 10^{-31}} = 7.847 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{J}$$

Converting to meV:
$$E_F = \frac{7.847 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^3 = 48.99\,\text{meV} \approx 49\,\text{meV}$$

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistakes students make:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the density formula:** Some students write $n_s = eF/h$ instead of $2eF/h$. Lose **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Using $m_e$ instead of $m^*$:** The effective mass $m^* = 0.067\,m_e$ is specific to GaAs and must be used. **1 mark** lost.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** Not converting J → meV correctly. The conversion is $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$.
> 4. **Confusing $F$ in T with $F$ in m$^{-2}$:** The Onsager relation uses SI units throughout. The relation $F = (\hbar/2\pi e)A$ gives $F$ in T when $A$ is in $\text{m}^{-2}$.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]`
**(a) Starting from the Bohr–Sommerfeld quantisation condition, derive the Onsager relation $F = (\hbar/2\pi e)\,A_{\text{ext}}$ for the Shubnikov–de Haas effect. Clearly explain why the oscillations are periodic in $1/B$. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss how the Shubnikov–de Haas effect can be used to determine: (i) the effective mass $m^*$, (ii) the Dingle temperature $T_D$ and quantum lifetime $\tau_q$, and (iii) the Berry phase $\beta$ of charge carriers in a 2D system. Show the relevant equations. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of the Onsager Relation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** In a uniform magnetic field $\mathbf{B} = B\hat{z}$, an electron's motion in the $xy$-plane is quantised. The Bohr–Sommerfeld condition for a periodic orbit is:
$$\oint \mathbf{p} \cdot d\mathbf{q} = 2\pi\hbar\left(n + \gamma\right)$$

For a free electron in $\mathbf{B}$, this integral evaluates to $2\pi\hbar(n + 1/2)$ where $\gamma = 1/2$ accounts for the Maslov index.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Using the relation between canonical momentum and the wavevector, the condition becomes a statement on the **$k$-space area** enclosed by the cyclotron orbit:
$$A(E, k_z) = \frac{2\pi e B}{\hbar}(n + \gamma)$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** For an electron at the Fermi energy, the area equals the **extremal cross-section** $A_{\text{ext}}$ of the Fermi surface. For a 2D system this is $A = \pi k_F^2$, while for a 3D metal it is the extremal (maximal or minimal) cross-section perpendicular to $\mathbf{B}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solving for $1/B$ at which the $n$-th Landau level crosses $E_F$:
$$\frac{1}{B_n} = \frac{2\pi e}{\hbar A_{\text{ext}}}\left(n + \frac{1}{2}\right)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** This equation is linear in $n$ with slope $2\pi e/(\hbar A_{\text{ext}})$. Hence oscillations are **periodic in $1/B$** with the period defining the SdH frequency:
$$F = \frac{\hbar A_{\text{ext}}}{2\pi e}$$

This is the **Onsager relation**, completing the derivation.

---

**Part (b) — Information Extraction from SdH (7 marks):**

**(i) Effective mass $m^*$ (2.5 marks):**
The temperature dependence of the SdH amplitude at fixed $B$ is governed by the thermal factor $R_T = X/\sinh X$, with $X = 2\pi^2 k_B T m^*/(\hbar e B)$. A **mass plot** of $\ln[A(T)\sinh X / X]$ versus $T$ is linear with slope $-2\pi^2 k_B m^*/(\hbar e B)$. The slope yields $m^*$ directly:
$$m^* = -\frac{\hbar e B}{2\pi^2 k_B} \cdot (\text{slope of mass plot})$$

**(ii) Dingle temperature and quantum lifetime (2.5 marks):**
The field dependence at fixed $T$ involves the Dingle factor $R_D = \exp(-2\pi^2 k_B T_D m^*/\hbar e B)$. A **Dingle plot** of $\ln[A(B) \cdot \sqrt{B} \cdot \sinh X / X]$ versus $1/B$ is linear with slope $-2\pi^2 k_B T_D m^*/\hbar e$, giving:
$$T_D = -\frac{\hbar e}{2\pi^2 k_B m^*} \cdot (\text{slope})$$
$$\tau_q = \frac{\hbar}{2\pi k_B T_D}, \quad \mu_q = \frac{e\tau_q}{m^*}$$

**(iii) Berry phase $\beta$ (2 marks):**
The oscillation phase in the LK formula is $2\pi(F/B - 1/2 + \beta)$. The Berry phase is extracted from a **Landau fan diagram**: assign integer indices $N$ to oscillation minima (or maxima), plot $N$ vs $1/B$, and extrapolate to $1/B = 0$. The $y$-intercept $N_0$ yields:
- $\beta = 0$ (trivial) if $N_0 = 1/2$
- $\beta = 1/2$ (topological, Dirac) if $N_0 = 0$

This is the standard experimental method to identify Dirac fermions in graphene and topological surface states.

> [!WARNING] 
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistakes on this type of question:**
> 1. **Mass plot vs Dingle plot confusion:** Students mix up which variable is on the $x$-axis. Mass plot has $T$ on $x$, Dingle plot has $1/B$ on $x$. **1–2 marks lost.**
> 2. **Skipping the Bohr–Sommerfeld starting point:** The derivation part requires starting from the quantisation condition, not just stating the result. **2 marks lost.**
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the role of the Maslov index $\gamma = 1/2$:** This gives the $1/2$ in the phase term and is conceptually important. **1 mark lost.**
> 4. **Not specifying that $\beta = 1/2$ is the signature of Dirac fermions:** Berry phase extraction is a high-value KTU topic and must be linked to topological classification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- The **SdH effect** = oscillatory magnetoresistance with $1/B$ periodicity, observable only at low $T$, high $B$, and high mobility.
- The **fundamental cause** is Landau quantisation of orbital motion, which makes the density of states at $E_F$ oscillate as $B$ is swept.
- The **Onsager relation** $F = (\hbar/2\pi e)A_{\text{ext}}$ is the central equation — it connects a measurable quantity ($F$) to a fundamental one ($A_{\text{ext}}$).
- The **Lifshitz–Kosevich (LK) formula** describes the full amplitude with three reduction factors: $R_T$ (thermal), $R_D$ (scattering), $R_S$ (spin).
- The **2D density** is $n_s = 2eF/h$. Do not forget the factor of 2 (spin degeneracy).
- The **effective mass** is found from the temperature dependence via the **Lifshitz mass plot** (linear in $T$).
- The **Dingle temperature / quantum lifetime** is found from the field dependence via the **Dingle plot** (linear in $1/B$).
- The **Berry phase** is extracted from the **Landau fan diagram** — the $y$-intercept reveals topology.
- The **SdH effect probes**: Fermi surface area, effective mass, quantum lifetime, Berry phase, spin splitting, and dimensional crossover.
- In **graphene and topological insulators**, the $\pi$ phase shift ($\beta = 1/2$) is a direct signature of Dirac fermions.
- **Numerical sanity check** for a typical GaAs 2DEG: $F \sim 30\,\text{T}$, $n_s \sim 10^{16}\,\text{m}^{-2}$, $E_F \sim 50\,\text{meV}$, $m^* \sim 0.067\,m_e$.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Top 3 exam mistakes to avoid:**
> 1. Forgetting the factor of 2 in the density formula.
> 2. Using $m_e$ instead of $m^*$ for the effective mass in GaAs-based 2DEG problems.
> 3. Confusing the SdH effect (resistance oscillations) with the dHvA effect (magnetisation oscillations) — both follow the Onsager relation but measure different quantities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODFETS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **MODFET (Modulation-Doped Field Effect Transistor)** — also universally referred to as a **HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor)** — is a heterojunction field-effect device in which a wide-bandgap semiconductor layer (typically AlGaAs) is **modulation-doped** with donor impurities, while the adjacent narrow-bandgap layer (typically GaAs) is intentionally left **intrinsic (undoped)**. The discontinuity in the conduction band at the heterointerface causes electrons from the doped wide-bandgap material to fall into and accumulate within the undoped narrow-bandgap layer, forming a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** that is physically separated from its parent ionized donors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The MODFET is the canonical KTU nanoelectronic device example. It is the textbook case demonstrating how **bandgap engineering** and **heterojunction physics** can outperform conventional homojunction silicon MOSFETs in high-frequency, low-noise, and high-speed applications.

## Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The VIP Fast Lane"**
> Imagine a busy city road (the doped AlGaAs layer) full of donor atoms acting like potholes, speed-breakers, and obstacles. Cars (electrons) cannot move fast there. Now imagine a parallel **VIP express lane** (the undoped GaAs layer) running right next to it, completely smooth and free of potholes. The MODFET is designed so that electrons, attracted by the smooth lane's lower energy "floor," **spontaneously leave the rough road and slide into the VIP lane** — where they can travel at extremely high speed without ever being scattered by the parent obstacles. That "VIP express lane" of electrons is the **2DEG**.

## Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Electron mobility in 2DEG (77 K) | **> 10⁶ cm²/V·s** | Far exceeds Si MOSFET (~10⁴ cm²/V·s) |
| Operating temperature | **77 K – 300 K** | Performance dramatically improves when cooled |
| Cut-off frequency (f_T) | **> 100 GHz** | Enables mm-wave and microwave circuits |
| Gate length (modern devices) | **~ 25 – 100 nm** | Deep nanoscale regime |
| Al mole fraction (x in Al_xGa_{1-x}As) | **0.2 – 0.3** | Optimizes bandgap offset vs lattice match |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Takeaway:** MODFETs exploit **spatially separating** charge carriers from their dopant atoms to dramatically reduce **ionized-impurity scattering**, which is the dominant mobility-limiting mechanism at cryogenic temperatures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Heterojunction Formation:** A heterojunction is created between two semiconductors with different bandgaps — typically **AlGaAs (E_g ≈ 1.8 eV)** grown epitaxially on **GaAs (E_g ≈ 1.42 eV)**.
2. **Modulation Doping (Invented by Dingle, Störmer, Gossard — 1978):** Donors (e.g., Si) are introduced **only** into the AlGaAs layer; the GaAs layer is left intrinsic.
3. **Band Discontinuity Effect:** The conduction-band edge of GaAs lies **~ 0.3 eV lower** than that of AlGaAs. Free electrons in AlGaAs therefore "fall" into GaAs to minimize their energy.
4. **2DEG Formation:** Electrons accumulate in a triangular potential well formed at the GaAs side of the interface. Quantum confinement in the direction perpendicular to the interface quantizes their motion — they can move freely only in the 2D plane, hence **2-Dimensional Electron Gas**.
5. **Spatial Separation = Reduced Scattering:** Because the donor ions remain physically in AlGaAs while electrons live in GaAs, Coulomb scattering is dramatically reduced → **mobility skyrockets**.
6. **Gate Control:** A Schottky gate (typically Ti/Pt/Au) on top of the AlGaAs modulates the 2DEG density, controlling the channel current just like in a MOSFET.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Question:** *Why bother with two materials?* Because in a conventional Si MOSFET, dopants and carriers coexist in the same region, so each electron is perpetually kicked by its parent ion. The MODFET's genius is **decoupling the dopants from the carriers**.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are KTU high-yield.**

| Symbol | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n_s$ | $\displaystyle n_s = \frac{C_i}{q}(V_G - V_{th})$ | Sheet electron density in 2DEG | $\text{m}^{-2}$ |
| $C_i$ | $\displaystyle C_i = \frac{\varepsilon_{AlGaAs}}{d}$ | Insulator capacitance per unit area | $\text{F/m}^2$ |
| $V_{th}$ | $\displaystyle V_{th} = \phi_b - \frac{q N_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}$ | Threshold voltage | V |
| $I_D^{lin}$ | $\displaystyle I_D = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | Linear-region drain current | A |
| $I_D^{sat}$ | $\displaystyle I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{2 L d}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$ | Saturation-region drain current | A |
| $g_m^{sat}$ | $\displaystyle g_m = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}(V_{GS}-V_{th})$ | Transconductance (saturation) | S (Siemens) |
| $V_{DS}^{sat}$ | $\displaystyle V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | Pinch-off / saturation voltage | V |
| $E_c$ offset | $\Delta E_c \approx 0.6\,\Delta E_g$ | 60/40 band-offset rule (AlGaAs/GaAs) | eV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** MODFETs power **satellite receivers**, **radar front-ends**, **5G/6G millimeter-wave amplifiers**, **low-noise astronomy receivers**, and **high-speed digital logic** at cryogenic temperatures. GaAs-based MODFETs are the workhorse of cellular base-station LNAs (Low Noise Amplifiers).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — Threshold Voltage of the MODFET

We model the AlGaAs layer as a parallel-plate capacitor of thickness $d$, permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$, uniformly doped with donors at concentration $N_d$. The Schottky gate creates a built-in potential $\phi_b$.

**Step 1 — Poisson's equation in the depleted AlGaAs:**

We start with the 1-D Poisson equation in the depletion approximation (charge density $= +qN_d$):

$$
\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{q N_d}{\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate once (electric field):**

Applying the boundary condition that field vanishes at the edge of the depletion region ($x = d$):

$$
E(x) = -\frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{q N_d}{\varepsilon}(d - x)
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate a second time (potential):**

The total potential drop across the AlGaAs layer is found by integrating $E(x)$ from $0$ to $d$:

$$
\Delta V_{AlGaAs} = \int_0^d E(x)\, dx = -\frac{q N_d}{\varepsilon}\int_0^d (d-x)\,dx = -\frac{q N_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the gate bias boundary condition:**

At threshold, the gate voltage $V_G$ must overcome both the Schottky barrier $\phi_b$ and the AlGaAs potential drop:

$$
V_G = \phi_b + \Delta V_{AlGaAs} = \phi_b - \frac{q N_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}
$$

**Step 5 — Identify the threshold voltage:**

By definition, at $V_G = V_{th}$, the channel just begins to conduct, giving:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{th} = \phi_b - \frac{q N_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A *more negative* $V_{th}$ results from higher doping $N_d$ or thicker AlGaAs spacer $d$. The negative sign of the second term shows that heavier doping requires a more negative gate to deplete the channel.

## Derivation 2 — 2DEG Sheet Charge Density

Once $V_G$ exceeds $V_{th}$, the induced electrons per unit area in the 2DEG follow directly from the parallel-plate charge-voltage relation.

**Step 1 — Charge on a parallel-plate capacitor:**

$$
Q = C_i (V_G - V_{th})
$$

where $C_i = \varepsilon / d$ is the capacitance per unit area.

**Step 2 — Convert charge to electron density:**

Since each electron carries charge $-q$, the sheet electron density is:

$$
\boxed{\,n_s = \frac{\varepsilon}{q\, d}(V_G - V_{th})\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the **direct analog of MOSFET inversion charge**. The thin AlGaAs layer yields a large $C_i$, producing a strong gate control and a high $n_s$ even at modest gate voltages.

## Derivation 3 — Drain Current in the Linear Region

**Step 1 — Drift velocity of 2DEG electrons:**

The average electron drift velocity in the channel is:

$$
v_d = \mu_n E_x = \mu_n \frac{V_{DS}}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Channel current from charge conservation:**

$$
I_D = q n_s \, W \, v_d
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $n_s$ expression:**

$$
I_D = q \cdot \frac{\varepsilon}{q d}(V_G - V_{th}) \cdot W \cdot \mu_n \frac{V_{DS}}{L}
$$

$$
I_D = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}(V_G - V_{th})V_{DS}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for channel-length modulation (gradual-channel approximation):**

For non-zero $V_{DS}$, the local gate overdrive decreases along the channel. Integrating:

$$
\boxed{\,I_D^{lin} = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]\,}
$$

## Derivation 4 — Saturation Current

**Step 1 — Pinch-off occurs when** $V_{DS} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the linear expression:**

$$
I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$
\boxed{\,I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{2 L d}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Check:** This saturates quadratically in gate overdrive — identical in form to a long-channel MOSFET. The crucial MODFET advantage is that $\mu_n$ can be **5–100× larger** than in Si, and at 77 K the mobility can exceed $10^6 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$.

## Derivation 5 — Transconductance (Figure of Merit)

Differentiating $I_{D,sat}$ with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$
\boxed{\,g_m = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}(V_{GS} - V_{th})\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU:** Higher $g_m$ directly translates to faster switching and higher gain-bandwidth product. This single equation justifies why MODFETs dominate microwave amplifier design.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a MODFET. Why is it also called a HEMT?**

**Model Answer:**
A MODFET (Modulation-Doped Field Effect Transistor) is a heterojunction field-effect transistor in which charge carriers are supplied by a **modulation-doped** wide-bandgap material and confined as a **Two-Dimensional Electron Gas (2DEG)** at the interface with an adjacent undoped narrow-bandgap material. Because the 2DEG electrons experience very little ionized-impurity scattering, the resulting carrier mobility is extremely high compared to conventional MOSFETs — hence the alternative name **HEMT (High Electron Mobility Transistor)**. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is modulation doping? How does it differ from conventional doping?**

**Model Answer:**
In **conventional (homogeneous) doping**, donor atoms and the electrons they release occupy the **same region** of the crystal, leading to frequent ionized-impurity scattering. In **modulation doping**, donors are placed in a wide-bandgap layer (AlGaAs), while the carriers physically migrate into a neighbouring undoped narrow-bandgap layer (GaAs). This **spatial separation of dopants from carriers** drastically reduces Coulomb scattering, yielding mobilities that can exceed **10⁶ cm²/V·s at 77 K**. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Each Module in the End Semester Examination carries either a 14-mark full question or two sub-parts summing to 14 marks, with internal choice between two full questions. The structure below mirrors that pattern exactly.

---

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and operating principle of a MODFET with the help of a neat energy band diagram. Discuss the formation of 2DEG. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **threshold voltage** and the **2DEG sheet charge density** of an AlGaAs/GaAs MODFET. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**Construction:**
A MODFET consists of the following epitaxial layers grown via **MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy)** or **MOCVD** on a semi-insulating GaAs substrate:

1. Undoped GaAs buffer (~ 1 µm)
2. Undoped AlGaAs spacer (~ 2–5 nm)
3. n-doped AlGaAs (Si donors, ~ 50 nm, $N_d \approx 10^{18} \text{ cm}^{-3}$)
4. Undoped AlGaAs cap (~ 10 nm) for Schottky contact
5. Schottky gate metal (Ti/Pt/Au) on top
6. Ohmic source/drain contacts (AuGe/Ni) alloyed into the cap

**Operating Principle:**

* At zero gate bias, electrons from donors in AlGaAs migrate across the heterojunction into the lower-energy GaAs layer.
* These electrons are confined in the triangular potential well formed at the GaAs side, creating a **2DEG**.
* A negative gate voltage depletes the 2DEG, modulating the channel current.
* Because electrons are physically separated from their parent ionized donors, scattering is minimal, and mobility is very high.

**Energy Band Diagram (Verbal Description):**
At equilibrium, the conduction band of AlGaAs lies above that of GaAs. The band bending on the AlGaAs side creates a potential barrier. The discontinuity $\Delta E_c$ at the interface forms a triangular quantum well on the GaAs side, within which the 2DEG resides. The Fermi level is flat across the structure at equilibrium.

**(7 marks: 2 for construction + 3 for operation + 2 for band diagram)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Threshold Voltage Derivation:**
Applying Poisson's equation in the depleted AlGaAs layer of thickness $d$ with uniform donor concentration $N_d$:

$$
\frac{d^2V}{dx^2} = -\frac{qN_d}{\varepsilon}
$$

Integrating twice with the boundary conditions $E = 0$ at $x = d$ and $V = 0$ at $x = d$:

$$
V(x) = -\frac{qN_d}{2\varepsilon}(d - x)^2
$$

The total potential drop across the AlGaAs is $\frac{qN_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}$. Equating to gate voltage minus the Schottky barrier:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{th} = \phi_b - \frac{qN_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}\,}
$$

**2DEG Sheet Charge Density Derivation:**
Once $V_G > V_{th}$, the additional gate voltage induces charge on the parallel-plate capacitor formed by the gate/AlGaAs/2DEG:

$$
Q = C_i(V_G - V_{th}) = \frac{\varepsilon}{d}(V_G - V_{th})
$$

Dividing by $-q$ to obtain sheet electron density:

$$
\boxed{\,n_s = \frac{\varepsilon}{q\,d}(V_G - V_{th})\,}
$$

**(7 marks: 3 for threshold derivation + 3 for $n_s$ derivation + 1 for units/interpretation)**

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the I-V characteristics of a MODFET. Define and derive the expression for **transconductance** in the saturation region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** List and explain the key advantages of MODFETs over conventional silicon MOSFETs. Discuss any two major applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

**I-V Characteristics:**
The drain current $I_D$ versus drain voltage $V_{DS}$ curves of a MODFET (with $V_{GS}$ as parameter) closely resemble those of a long-channel MOSFET — comprising a **linear (ohmic) region**, a **knee**, and a **saturation region**. However, the saturation current is much larger due to the very high mobility of the 2DEG.

**Derivation of Saturation Transconductance:**

Starting from:

$$
I_{D,sat} = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{2 L d}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2
$$

Differentiating with respect to $V_{GS}$ (with $V_{th}$ assumed constant):

$$
g_m = \frac{dI_{D,sat}}{dV_{GS}} = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{2 L d} \cdot 2(V_{GS} - V_{th})
$$

$$
\boxed{\,g_m = \frac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}(V_{GS} - V_{th})\,}
$$

This is the same form as a MOSFET, but because $\mu_n$ in a MODFET can be **10–100× greater**, $g_m$ is correspondingly much higher.

**(7 marks: 3 for I-V sketch + 3 for derivation + 1 for comparison)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Key Advantages of MODFETs:**

1. **Very High Electron Mobility** — In the 2DEG, mobility can exceed $10^6 \text{ cm}^2/\text{V·s}$ at 77 K, vastly superior to Si MOSFETs.
2. **High Transconductance** — Directly improves high-frequency and switching performance.
3. **High Cut-off Frequency (f_T)** — Devices operate efficiently in the microwave and mm-wave bands (10–100+ GHz).
4. **Low Noise Figure** — Reduced scattering means minimal 1/f and thermal noise.
5. **Reduced Short-Channel Effects** — Quantum confinement in the 2DEG suppresses classical short-channel degradation.
6. **High Switching Speed** — Critical for high-speed digital logic.

**Applications:**

* **Satellite Communication LNAs (Low-Noise Amplifiers)** — MODFETs are the industry standard for the first stage of receivers in direct-broadcast satellite TV.
* **Radar and EW (Electronic Warfare) Systems** — Microwave power MODFETs are used in phased-array radar T/R modules.
* *(Bonus)* **High-Speed Digital ICs** — Cryogenic logic based on MODFETs operates at sub-picosecond switching speeds.
* *(Bonus)* **Radio-Astronomy Receivers** — Ultra-low-noise HEMT amplifiers cool the front-end of telescopes like ALMA and the VLA.

**(7 marks: 4 for advantages + 3 for applications with explanation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Band-Diagram Errors:** Do NOT draw a flat-band diagram at the heterojunction. There **must** be a discontinuity $\Delta E_c$ at the interface, and a triangular well on the GaAs side. Forgetting the 2DEG well costs full marks.
> 2. **Confusing Doping Conventions:** The GaAs layer is **undoped**. Writing "GaAs is n-doped" is a fatal error — students lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Mobility Justification:** Just stating "mobility is high" is incomplete. Always **explain why** — i.e., spatial separation from ionized donors.
> 4. **Sign Errors in $V_{th}$:** The term $\frac{qN_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}$ carries a **negative sign** in $V_{th}$. Many students forget this.
> 5. **Skipping Units:** $n_s$ must be quoted in $\text{cm}^{-2}$ or $\text{m}^{-2}$; $\varepsilon$ in $\text{F/m}$; $g_m$ in Siemens. Missing units = -1 mark per equation.
> 6. **Forgetting the Spacer Layer:** The undoped AlGaAs spacer (~ 3 nm) is essential — it prevents donors from coming too close to the 2DEG, which would otherwise increase scattering. Many answers skip this entirely.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **MODFET = HEMT** — both terms refer to the same device; use them interchangeably in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Key Materials:** AlGaAs (wide-gap, doped) / GaAs (narrow-gap, undoped) is the **canonical** MODFET system.
- ✅ **Modulation Doping:** Donors in wide-gap material; carriers migrate into undoped narrow-gap material → **2DEG**.
- ✅ **2DEG Formation:** Triangular quantum well at the heterointerface due to $\Delta E_c$; electrons confined perpendicular to the interface.
- ✅ **Why High Mobility?** Spatial separation from ionized donors ⇒ drastically reduced Coulomb scattering.
- ✅ **Threshold Voltage:** $V_{th} = \phi_b - \dfrac{qN_d d^2}{2\varepsilon}$ — analogous to MOSFET but governed by Schottky barrier.
- ✅ **Sheet Charge Density:** $n_s = \dfrac{\varepsilon}{q d}(V_G - V_{th})$.
- ✅ **Saturation Current:** $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{2 L d}(V_{GS}-V_{th})^2$.
- ✅ **Transconductance:** $g_m = \dfrac{\mu_n W \varepsilon}{L d}(V_{GS}-V_{th})$ — same form as MOSFET, but with $\mu_n$ up to 100× larger.
- ✅ **Band-Offset Rule (60/40):** $\Delta E_c \approx 0.6\,\Delta E_g$ for AlGaAs/GaAs.
- ✅ **Major Applications:** Microwave/mm-wave LNAs, satellite receivers, radar, high-speed cryogenic logic, radio astronomy.
- ✅ **Variants (advanced):** Pseudomorphic MODFET (pHEMT) using InGaAs channel; InP-based HEMTs for sub-mm-wave/THz applications.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
