<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Single Electron Transistor (SET) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Single Electron Transistor (SET)** is a **three-terminal nanoscale switching device** based on the **Coulomb Blockade phenomenon**, in which the transfer of individual electrons through a small conducting "island" (quantum dot) is precisely controlled by a gate electrode via two quantum-mechanical tunnel junctions.

The SET was first experimentally demonstrated by **Fulton and Dolan** (1987) using metal-oxide junction structures, and theoretically described by **K. K. Likharev** under the **"Orthodox Theory of Single Electron Tunneling"**. It is considered the ultimate limit of CMOS scaling because it operates by controlling the motion of **a single electron**.

### 1.1.1 Standard KTU Syllabus Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| Island (Quantum Dot) | Central conductor with self-capacitance $C_{\Sigma}$ |
| Tunnel Junction | Thin insulator (~1–2 nm) allowing quantum tunneling |
| Coulomb Island | Conductor small enough that $e^2/2C_\Sigma \gg k_B T$ |
| Orthodox Theory | The framework governing sequential single-electron tunneling |
| Coulomb Blockade | Suppression of electron flow below a threshold voltage |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Fountain Coin Model"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy**: Think of the SET island as a *coin-collecting fountain* — it can hold exactly one coin at a time, and every time you drop a coin in, the previous one rolls out the other side. A small water valve (the **gate voltage**) controls exactly *when* this coin exchange is allowed. If the coin's weight is "felt" by the system, only one coin at a time can sit in the bowl — this is the **Coulomb Blockade**.

In simpler terms:
- The **island** = a tiny metallic bowl.
- The **two tunnel junctions** = two narrow channels (one for coin-in, one for coin-out).
- The **gate** = an external knob that fine-tunes the "weight" the bowl can hold.
- **Electrons behave like discrete coins** — they cannot be split, only enter/exit one-by-one.

This is the essence of **single-electron electronics** — information is carried not by continuous current flow, but by the *counted presence of individual electrons*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> These constants define the **operating regime** of every SET device:

- **Electron charge**: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
- **Boltzmann constant**: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
- **Capacitance energy scale**: A typical SET has $C_\Sigma \sim 10^{-18} \text{ F}$ → $E_C = e^2/2C_\Sigma \sim 80 \text{ meV}$
- **Operating temperature**: $T < e^2/2C_\Sigma k_B \sim 1\text{–}10 \text{ K}$
- **Tunnel resistance requirement**: $R_T > h/e^2 \approx 25.8 \text{ k}\Omega$ (to suppress quantum fluctuations)
- **Island dimensions**: Typically **2–100 nm** in diameter

The figure **$R_T > 25.8 \text{ k}\Omega$** (often called the **von Klitzing constant** $R_Q$) is a **bold** requirement — it ensures that quantum uncertainty does not blur the discrete charging effect.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of SET

The SET consists of:
1. **Source (S)** — electron reservoir
2. **Drain (D)** — electron reservoir
3. **Gate (G)** — electrostatic control terminal
4. **Island (I)** — central quantum dot
5. **Two tunnel junctions** with capacitances $C_1$ and $C_2$ and resistances $R_{T1}, R_{T2}$
6. **Gate capacitor** $C_G$

### 2.1.1 The Three-Step Logic of Operation

- **Step 1 — Charging Energy Buildup**: When an electron enters the island, it costs electrostatic energy
$$E_C = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$
where $C_\Sigma = C_1 + C_2 + C_G$ is the **total island capacitance**.

- **Step 2 — Coulomb Blockade**: At low temperature ($k_B T \ll E_C$), no current flows until the source-drain voltage exceeds the threshold
$$V_{th} = \frac{e}{2C_\Sigma}$$
This is the famous **Coulomb Staircase** onset.

- **Step 3 — Gate-Controlled Tunneling**: The gate voltage $V_G$ shifts the island's electrostatic potential $\phi$ by
$$\Delta\phi = \frac{e C_G}{C_\Sigma} V_G$$
allowing **single-electron tunneling events** to occur precisely one electron at a time.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every high-yield formula** for SET-based problems in KTU Module-4 examinations.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $C_\Sigma = C_1 + C_2 + C_G$ | Total island capacitance | F (farads) |
| 2 | $E_C = \dfrac{e^2}{2 C_\Sigma}$ | Single-electron charging energy | Joules (J); eV in practice |
| 3 | $V_{th} = \dfrac{e}{2 C_\Sigma}$ | Coulomb blockade threshold voltage | Volts |
| 4 | $R_T > R_Q = h/e^2 \approx 25.8\text{ k}\Omega$ | Tunnel resistance condition | Ohms |
| 5 | $k_B T \ll E_C = e^2/2C_\Sigma$ | Temperature criterion for blockade | Both in J |
| 6 | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{e C_G}{C_\Sigma} V_G$ | Gate-induced island potential shift | Volts |
| 7 | $V_{th}^{\text{shifted}} = \dfrac{e}{2 C_\Sigma} - \dfrac{e C_G}{C_\Sigma} V_G$ | Effective threshold with gate bias | Volts |
| 8 | $\Gamma_{1\to 2} = \dfrac{1}{e^2 R_{T1}} \dfrac{-\Delta F_{1\to 2}}{1 - \exp(\Delta F_{1\to 2}/k_B T)}$ | Orthodox tunneling rate (junction 1) | $s^{-1}$ |
| 9 | $n = \dfrac{C_G V_G}{e}$ | Induced fractional electron count on island | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta V_G^{\text{period}} = \dfrac{e}{C_G}$ | Gate voltage periodicity of Coulomb oscillations | Volts |
| 11 | $T_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{e^2}{2 k_B C_\Sigma}$ | Max operating temperature | Kelvin |
| 12 | $I_{SET} = e \Gamma$ | Single-electron current | Amperes |

---

## 2.3 The Orthodox Theory — Master Equations

> [!NOTE]
> The **Orthodox Theory** (Likharev, 1988) is the cornerstone framework for SET analysis in the KTU syllabus.

The probability $\sigma_n$ of finding $n$ excess electrons on the island satisfies the **Master Equation**:

$$\frac{d\sigma_n}{dt} = \sigma_{n+1}\Gamma_{n+1\to n}^{+} + \sigma_{n-1}\Gamma_{n-1\to n}^{-} - \sigma_n(\Gamma_{n\to n-1}^{+} + \Gamma_{n\to n+1}^{-})$$

where:
- $\Gamma_{n\to n-1}^{+}$ = tunneling *into* the island via junction "+" when $n$ electrons are present
- $\Gamma_{n\to n+1}^{-}$ = tunneling *out of* the island via junction "−"

The **free-energy change** for tunneling across junction $i$ is:

$$\Delta F_i = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma} \pm eV_i - eV_G \frac{C_G}{C_\Sigma}$$

**Real-World Engineering Utility**:
- SETs are used as **single-electron memory cells** (1-bit = 1 electron)
- Ultra-sensitive **electrometers** capable of detecting charges $\sim 10^{-6}\,e$
- Building blocks for **quantum-dot cellular automata (QDCA)**
- Future **sub-5 nm logic gates** when MOSFET scaling ends
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charging Energy of the Island

**Goal**: Show that the energy required to bring one extra electron onto the island is $E_C = e^2/2C_\Sigma$.

**Step 1**: The island has total capacitance $C_\Sigma$ to ground (when sources and gate are at zero).

**Step 2**: Initial charge on island: $Q_0 = 0$. The energy stored is:

$$E_0 = \frac{Q_0^2}{2C_\Sigma} = 0$$

**Step 3**: After one electron tunnels onto the island, charge becomes $Q_1 = -e$ (or $+e$ depending on sign convention). The energy is:

$$E_1 = \frac{(-e)^2}{2C_\Sigma} = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 4**: The energy cost (charging energy) is the difference:

$$E_C = E_1 - E_0 = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma} - 0 = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 5**: Numerical check for $C_\Sigma = 10^{-18}\text{ F}$:

$$E_C = \frac{(1.6\times 10^{-19})^2}{2 \times 10^{-18}} = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{-38}}{2 \times 10^{-18}} = 1.28 \times 10^{-20}\text{ J}$$

Converting to eV:

$$E_C = \frac{1.28\times 10^{-20}}{1.6\times 10^{-19}} = 0.08\text{ eV} = 80\text{ meV}$$

This matches the experimental threshold for SETs — a **bold** and important KTU numerical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coulomb Blockade Threshold Voltage

**Goal**: Find the minimum source-drain voltage $V_{DS}$ that allows current flow.

**Step 1**: With source-drain voltage $V_{DS}$ applied, junction 1 (source-island) has voltage $V_1 = V_{DS}/2$ and junction 2 has $V_2 = V_{DS}/2$ (symmetric case).

**Step 2**: When one electron crosses from source to drain, it gains energy $eV_{DS}$ from the source-drain bias.

**Step 3**: However, to enter the island, the electron must overcome the charging energy $E_C = e^2/2C_\Sigma$.

**Step 4**: For tunneling to occur:

$$eV_{DS} \geq E_C = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 5**: Solving for the threshold:

$$V_{th} = \frac{e^2/2C_\Sigma}{e} = \frac{e}{2C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 6**: Generalizing for an asymmetric junction ($C_1 \neq C_2$), the threshold shifts to:

$$V_{th} = \frac{e}{2(C_1 \parallel C_2)}$$

where $C_1 \parallel C_2 = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ is the parallel combination.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gate-Induced Periodicity

**Goal**: Derive the period of Coulomb oscillations in $V_G$.

**Step 1**: The gate induces a fractional charge $q = C_G V_G$ on the island.

**Step 2**: The effective number of induced electrons is:

$$n = \frac{C_G V_G}{e}$$

**Step 3**: The Coulomb blockade is lifted whenever $n$ is a half-integer (i.e., when the island's charge crosses $e/2$). This happens periodically with period:

$$\Delta V_G = \frac{e}{C_G}$$

**Step 4**: For $C_G = 5 \times 10^{-19}\text{ F}$:

$$\Delta V_G = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{5 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.32\text{ V}$$

**Step 5**: This periodicity is the **KTU high-yield concept** — it forms the basis of single-electron memory and quantum-dot logic.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tunneling Rate (Orthodox Formula)

**Goal**: Derive the tunneling rate $\Gamma$ across a junction with free-energy change $\Delta F$.

**Step 1**: From Fermi's Golden Rule for quantum tunneling:

$$\Gamma = \frac{1}{e^2 R_T} \cdot \frac{-\Delta F}{1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T)}$$

**Step 2**: Limit 1 — When $\Delta F \gg k_B T$ (low temperature, strong Coulomb blockade):

$$\Gamma \approx \frac{-\Delta F}{e^2 R_T}$$

since $1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T) \to 1$ and $\Delta F < 0$ implies $-\Delta F > 0$.

**Step 3**: Limit 2 — When $\Delta F \ll k_B T$ (high temperature, no blockade):

$$\Gamma \approx \frac{1}{e^2 R_T} \cdot k_B T = \frac{k_B T}{e^2 R_T}$$

because $(1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T)) \approx -\Delta F / k_B T$.

**Step 4**: The current through the SET at equilibrium is then:

$$I_{SET} = e \cdot (\Gamma_1 - \Gamma_2)$$

where $\Gamma_1, \Gamma_2$ are the rates for junction 1 and junction 2.

**Step 5**: Numerical example: For $R_T = 100\text{ k}\Omega$, $T = 4.2\text{ K}$ (helium temperature):

$$\Gamma \approx \frac{(1.38\times 10^{-23})(4.2)}{(1.6\times 10^{-19})^2 (10^5)} = \frac{5.8 \times 10^{-23}}{2.56 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{9}\text{ s}^{-1}$$

Current: $I = e\Gamma = 1.6\times 10^{-19} \times 2.27 \times 10^{9} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{-10}\text{ A} = 0.36\text{ nA}$ — a typical SET current scale.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Stability Diagram (Coulomb Diamonds)

**Goal**: Map the $(V_{DS}, V_G)$ plane to find regions of blockade vs current flow.

**Step 1**: For tunneling across junction $i$, blockade is lifted when:

$$V_{DS} \geq \frac{e}{2C_\Sigma} \pm \frac{C_G V_G}{C_2}\ (\text{or}\ C_1)$$

**Step 2**: The boundary lines in the $(V_{DS}, V_G)$ plane:

$$V_{DS}^{\pm} = \pm \frac{e}{2C_2} \pm \frac{C_G V_G}{C_2}$$

**Step 3**: These four lines bound **diamond-shaped regions** — the so-called **Coulomb diamonds**.

**Step 4**: Inside each diamond, the island has a fixed integer charge $n$, and current is blocked.

**Step 5**: At the diamond edges, an electron can tunnel in/out, producing a current peak.

**Step 6**: The diamonds are **periodic in $V_G$** with period $\Delta V_G = e/C_G$ (matches Derivation 3).

> [!NOTE]
> The **stability diagram** is a high-frequency KTU examination topic — students must sketch it correctly.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Summary

| Problem Type | Given | Use Formula | Output |
|--------------|-------|-------------|--------|
| Find charging energy | $C_\Sigma$ | $E_C = e^2/2C_\Sigma$ | Energy in J / eV |
| Find threshold $V$ | $C_\Sigma$ | $V_{th} = e/2C_\Sigma$ | Volts |
| Operating temperature | $C_\Sigma$ | $T_{max} = e^2/2k_B C_\Sigma$ | Kelvin |
| Gate period | $C_G$ | $\Delta V_G = e/C_G$ | Volts |
| Tunnel rate | $\Delta F, R_T, T$ | $\Gamma = \dfrac{-\Delta F}{e^2 R_T[1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T)]}$ | $s^{-1}$ |
| SET current | $\Gamma$ | $I = e\Gamma$ | Amperes |
| Stability region | $V_G, V_{DS}, C_1, C_2, C_G$ | Diamond plot | Diagram |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — SET Structure

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the SET's physical and electrical architecture in detail.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Source Reservoir] -->|Tunnel J1| junc1Node[Junction 1: C1 RT1]
    junc1Node -->|Electron Tunneling| islandNode[Quantum Island: Central Conductor]
    islandNode -->|Tunnel J2| junc2Node[Junction 2: C2 RT2]
    junc2Node --> drnA[Drain Reservoir]
    gateA[Gate Voltage VG] -->|Coupled via CG| islandNode
    islandNode -.Charges CΣ.-> capGnd[Total Island Capacitance CΣ = C1 + C2 + CG]
    capGnd -.-> gndNode[Ground Reference]

    classDef reservoir fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000;
    classDef junction fill:#ffe680,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000000;
    classDef island fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000000;
    classDef control fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000000;
    classDef cap fill:#f5d6e8,stroke:#7d3c98,color:#000000;

    class srcA,drnA reservoir;
    class junc1Node,junc2Node junction;
    class islandNode island;
    class gateA,gndNode control;
    class capGnd cap;
```

### Interpretation
- **Source Reservoir / Drain Reservoir**: Provide electrons; characterized by Fermi levels.
- **Junction 1 (C1, RT1)** & **Junction 2 (C2, RT2)**: Thin tunnel barriers; the only paths in/out of the island.
- **Quantum Island**: The heart of the SET; holds a discrete number of electrons.
- **Gate Voltage (VG)** + **Gate Capacitor (CG)**: Electrostatic control terminal.
- **CΣ** = C1 + C2 + CG: Combined capacitance defining charging energy.

---

## 4.2 Functional Operation Flow — Coulomb Blockade Cycle

This diagram shows the operational sequence of single-electron transport.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Initial State: n electrons on island] --> s2{Voltage condition: VDS greater than Vth}
    s2 -- No --> s1b[State persists: Coulomb Blockade ON]
    s1b --> s2
    s2 -- Yes --> s3[Electron tunnels from Source to Island]
    s3 --> s4[Island now has n plus 1 electrons]
    s4 --> s5[Charging energy EC paid]
    s5 --> s6[Electron tunnels from Island to Drain]
    s6 --> s7[Island returns to n electrons]
    s7 --> s8[Net result: One electron transferred Source to Drain]
    s8 --> s9{Cycle repeats via Gate modulation}
    s9 -- Yes --> s2
    s9 -- No --> s1

    classDef process fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1b4f72,color:#000000;
    classDef decision fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000000;
    classDef result fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000000;

    class s1,s3,s4,s5,s6,s7,s8 process;
    class s2,s9 decision;
    class s1b result;
```

---

## 4.3 Stability Diagram (Coulomb Diamonds) — Mermaid Representation

The Coulomb diamonds form the key experimental fingerprint of a SET.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    axisA[Horizontal Axis: VGS from negative to positive] --> axisB[Vertical Axis: VDS magnitude]
    axisB --> d1[Diamond 1: Region where n equals 0 electrons]
    d1 --> d2[Diamond 2: Region where n equals 1 electron]
    d2 --> d3[Diamond 3: Region where n equals 2 electrons]
    d3 --> d4[Diamond N: Region where n equals N minus 1 electrons]
    d4 --> edgeA[Diamond Edges: Current peaks due to single electron tunneling]
    edgeA --> blockA[Inside diamonds: Current is blocked Coulomb blockade active]

    classDef axes fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000,color:#000000;
    classDef diamond fill:#d4e6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000000;
    classDef edge fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000000;
    classDef block fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000000;

    class axisA,axisB axes;
    class d1,d2,d3,d4 diamond;
    class edgeA edge;
    class blockA block;
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Mermaid schematic** above maps the functional architecture of a SET — for actual pictorial rendering of Coulomb diamonds, students should refer to standard textbooks (e.g., Likharev's review on single-electron devices).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]**

### Q1. Define Single Electron Transistor. State the Coulomb Blockade condition. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:

A **Single Electron Transistor (SET)** is a nanoscale three-terminal switching device that uses controlled single-electron tunneling events through a small conducting **island**, separated from source and drain by two tunnel junctions, with a gate electrode electrostatically controlling the energy levels.

**Coulomb Blockade condition**:

$$\frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma} \gg k_B T \quad \text{and} \quad R_T \gg \frac{h}{e^2}$$

This means (i) the single-electron charging energy must greatly exceed the thermal energy, and (ii) the tunnel resistance must be large enough to suppress quantum fluctuations.

---

### Q2. What is the role of the gate in a SET? **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:

The **gate electrode**, coupled to the island via capacitance $C_G$, performs two critical functions:
1. **Modulates the electrostatic potential** of the island by $\Delta\phi = e C_G V_G / C_\Sigma$.
2. **Lifts the Coulomb Blockade periodically** — current peaks appear at gate voltages satisfying $V_G = (n + 1/2)e/C_G$, where $n$ is an integer.

This gate-controlled periodicity enables the SET to act as a single-electron switch or memory cell.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — Choose **ONE** of the following:

---

### 🔹 Question A — **(14 Marks)**

#### (a) Derive the expression for the charging energy and threshold voltage of a Single Electron Transistor. Show that the gate voltage periodicity is $e/C_G$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Charging Energy Derivation**:
When an extra electron is added to the island of capacitance $C_\Sigma$, the energy stored becomes:

$$E_1 = \frac{(-e)^2}{2C_\Sigma} = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$

The charging energy (initial state has zero energy) is:

$$E_C = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 2 — Threshold Voltage**:
For tunneling to occur, the source-drain voltage must supply at least the charging energy:

$$eV_{th} \geq E_C \Rightarrow V_{th} = \frac{e}{2C_\Sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Gate Periodicity**:
The gate induces a charge $q = C_G V_G$ on the island. The Coulomb blockade condition is lifted when $q$ shifts the island's effective charge by $e/2$:

$$C_G \Delta V_G = e \Rightarrow \Delta V_G = \frac{e}{C_G}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification**:
For $C_G = 1 \times 10^{-18}\text{ F}$:

$$\Delta V_G = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{10^{-18}} = 0.16\text{ V}$$

This confirms that SETs require very low gate voltages for operation.

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the operation of a Single Electron Transistor. Discuss the stability diagram (Coulomb diamonds). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Structure**:
The SET consists of:
- A **source reservoir** and **drain reservoir**.
- Two **tunnel junctions** with capacitances $C_1, C_2$ and resistances $R_{T1}, R_{T2}$.
- A central **quantum island** with self-capacitance $C_\Sigma = C_1 + C_2 + C_G$.
- A **gate terminal** coupled via $C_G$.

**Step 2 — Working Principle**:
- When $V_{DS} < V_{th} = e/2C_\Sigma$, the system is in **Coulomb blockade** — no current flows because the electrostatic cost of adding an electron exceeds the energy gained.
- When $V_{DS} \geq V_{th}$, electrons tunnel one at a time from source → island → drain, producing a **single-electron current** $I = e\Gamma$.
- The gate voltage $V_G$ shifts the blockade threshold, producing periodic current peaks (Coulomb oscillations) with period $\Delta V_G = e/C_G$.

**Step 3 — Coulomb Diamonds (Stability Diagram)**:
- In the $(V_{DS}, V_G)$ plane, regions of constant island charge $n$ form **diamond-shaped blocks**.
- Inside each diamond: Coulomb blockade is active, current ≈ 0.
- At diamond edges: one-electron tunneling is allowed, current peaks occur.
- The diamonds repeat with period $e/C_G$ along the $V_G$ axis.

**Step 4 — Importance**:
This stability diagram is the experimental fingerprint of SET behavior, used to extract $C_1, C_2, C_G$ directly from measurements.

---

### 🔹 Question B — **(14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### (a) Explain the Orthodox Theory of single-electron tunneling. Derive the tunneling rate expression. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Background**:
The **Orthodox Theory**, formulated by K. K. Likharev, describes SET operation in the classical limit where:
- $R_T \gg h/e^2$ (quantum fluctuations negligible)
- $C_\Sigma$ is small enough that charging energy dominates
- Electron energy quantization is ignored (continuous density of states)

**Step 2 — Free Energy Considerations**:
The change in free energy $\Delta F$ for tunneling of one electron across junction $i$ is:

$$\Delta F = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma} \pm eV_i - eV_G \frac{C_G}{C_\Sigma}$$

Tunneling is allowed only if $\Delta F < 0$.

**Step 3 — Tunneling Rate Derivation**:
From Fermi's Golden Rule combined with detailed balance:

$$\Gamma = \frac{1}{e^2 R_T} \cdot \frac{-\Delta F}{1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T)}$$

- At $T \to 0$: $\Gamma \to -\Delta F / e^2 R_T$
- At $T \to \infty$: $\Gamma \to k_B T / e^2 R_T$

**Step 4 — Master Equation**:
The probability $\sigma_n$ of $n$ electrons on the island evolves as:

$$\frac{d\sigma_n}{dt} = \sigma_{n+1}\Gamma_{n+1\to n}^{+} + \sigma_{n-1}\Gamma_{n-1\to n}^{-} - \sigma_n(\Gamma_{n\to n-1}^{+} + \Gamma_{n\to n+1}^{-})$$

In steady state ($d\sigma_n/dt = 0$), this gives the SET current.

#### (b) A SET has $C_1 = 5 \times 10^{-19}\text{ F}$, $C_2 = 3 \times 10^{-19}\text{ F}$, and $C_G = 2 \times 10^{-19}\text{ F}$. Calculate: (i) Total island capacitance, (ii) Charging energy, (iii) Threshold voltage, (iv) Gate voltage period. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Total Island Capacitance**:
$$C_\Sigma = C_1 + C_2 + C_G = 5\times 10^{-19} + 3\times 10^{-19} + 2\times 10^{-19} = 10\times 10^{-19}\text{ F} = 10^{-18}\text{ F}$$

**Step 2 — Charging Energy**:
$$E_C = \frac{e^2}{2C_\Sigma} = \frac{(1.6\times 10^{-19})^2}{2 \times 10^{-18}} = \frac{2.56\times 10^{-38}}{2\times 10^{-18}} = 1.28 \times 10^{-20}\text{ J}$$

Converting to eV:
$$E_C = \frac{1.28\times 10^{-20}}{1.6\times 10^{-19}} = 0.08\text{ eV} = 80\text{ meV}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Voltage**:
$$V_{th} = \frac{e}{2C_\Sigma} = \frac{1.6\times 10^{-19}}{2\times 10^{-18}} = 0.08\text{ V} = 80\text{ mV}$$

**Step 4 — Gate Voltage Period**:
$$\Delta V_G = \frac{e}{C_G} = \frac{1.6\times 10^{-19}}{2\times 10^{-19}} = 0.8\text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Operating Temperature Limit**:
$$T_{max} = \frac{E_C}{k_B} = \frac{1.28\times 10^{-20}}{1.38\times 10^{-23}} \approx 927\text{ K}$$

Wait — this seems too high. Re-check: actually $T_{max} = E_C / k_B$ is the **order-of-magnitude upper bound**. For practical blockade, we need $T < E_C / (10\,k_B) \approx 92\text{ K}$. Most SETs operate at **liquid helium temperature (4.2 K)** or lower.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** charging energy to eV when answering numerical questions — always state **both** J and eV.
> 2. **Confusing $C_\Sigma$ with $C_G$** — $C_\Sigma$ is the *total* capacitance; $C_G$ is *only* the gate capacitance.
> 3. **Wrong gate period formula** — students often write $\Delta V_G = e/C_\Sigma$ (incorrect); the correct formula uses $C_G$ alone.
> 4. **Skipping the temperature criterion** $k_B T \ll E_C$ — this is **mandatory** in any SET answer.
> 5. **Forgetting the $R_T > h/e^2$ condition** — the orthodox theory breaks down if this is violated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for SET — KTU Module 4:**

### Core Concepts
- **SET = Coulomb Blockade + Tunneling + Gate Control**
- Three terminals: **Source, Drain, Gate**
- Single island + two tunnel junctions
- Single-electron current: **$I = e\Gamma$**

### Must-Know Equations
- $C_\Sigma = C_1 + C_2 + C_G$
- $E_C = e^2/2C_\Sigma$ (charging energy)
- $V_{th} = e/2C_\Sigma$ (threshold voltage)
- $\Delta V_G = e/C_G$ (gate period)
- $R_T > h/e^2 \approx 25.8\text{ k}\Omega$
- $k_B T \ll E_C$ (temperature condition)
- $\Gamma = (-\Delta F)/[e^2 R_T(1 - \exp(\Delta F/k_B T))]$ (tunneling rate)

### Physical Regimes
- **Coulomb Blockade**: $V_{DS} < V_{th}$, $I = 0$
- **Single-electron tunneling**: $V_{DS} \geq V_{th}$, $I$ is quantized
- **Coulomb Diamonds**: stability diagram in $(V_{DS}, V_G)$ plane

### Operating Conditions
- **Temperature**: 0.1 K – 10 K (typically 4.2 K)
- **Island size**: 2–100 nm
- **Tunnel barrier**: 1–2 nm thick insulator
- **$R_T$**: 100 kΩ – 10 MΩ for stable operation

### Orthodox Theory Assumptions
- Continuous energy spectrum in electrodes (classical density of states)
- Quantum fluctuations negligible ($R_T \gg h/e^2$)
- Sequential tunneling only (no co-tunneling)
- Single electron charge treated as classical discrete unit

### Real-World Applications
- **Ultra-sensitive electrometers** (charge detectors)
- **Single-electron memory** (one electron = one bit)
- **Quantum computing** (charge qubits)
- **Thermometry** at sub-Kelvin temperatures
- **Logic gates** beyond CMOS scaling limits
- **Quantum-dot cellular automata (QDCA)**

### Key Numerical Values
- $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$
- $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\text{ J/K}$
- $R_Q = h/e^2 \approx 25.8\text{ k}\Omega$
- Typical $E_C \sim 1\text{–}100\text{ meV}$ for SET devices

### KTU Exam Focus Areas
- Numerical problems on $E_C$, $V_{th}$, $\Delta V_G$
- Derivation of charging energy and threshold voltage
- Explanation of Coulomb blockade mechanism
- Sketching and interpretation of Coulomb diamonds
- Comparison of SET vs conventional MOSFET
- Operating temperature and resistance conditions
- Applications in nanoelectronics

> **Final Tip**: Always check whether the question asks for **operating regime** (Coulomb blockade on/off), **gate periodicity**, or **numerical values** — these are the three most common KTU question types for SET.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNT Transistors - Properties of Graphene, Resonant Tunnel Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CNT Transistors - Properties of Graphene, Resonant Tunnel Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Graphene — The Mother of Carbon Nanomaterials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Graphene** is a single, two-dimensional (2D) sheet of $sp^2$-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a planar **honeycomb (hexagonal) lattice**. It is the fundamental building block of all other graphitic carbon allotropes — graphite, carbon nanotubes (rolled), and fullerenes (wrapped).

Each carbon atom contributes one of its four valence electrons to a delocalized **π-band** (perpendicular to the plane) that is responsible for graphene's extraordinary electronic properties. The remaining three electrons form strong covalent **σ-bonds** with the three nearest neighbors at an interatomic distance of $a_{cc} \approx \mathbf{1.42\ \text{Å}}$ and a lattice constant $a = \sqrt{3}\,a_{cc} \approx \mathbf{2.46\ \text{Å}}$.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy
Think of graphene as the **"chicken wire" of nanotechnology** — a perfectly flat, one-atom-thick mesh. If you roll this mesh, you get a **Carbon Nanotube (CNT)**. If you stack many sheets, you get **graphite**. If you wrap a small piece, you get a **buckyball (C₆₀)**. Hence, graphene is literally the *universal 2D Lego* of carbon nanoelectronics.

### 1.1.1 Why Graphene is Special — The Key Properties

| Property | Value / Behaviour | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier mobility (suspended) | $\mu \approx \mathbf{200{,}000\ cm^2/V\!\cdot\!s}$ | Ultra-fast transistors |
| Fermi velocity | $v_F \approx \mathbf{1 \times 10^6\ m/s}$ | Independent of $k$ — massless Dirac fermions |
| Thermal conductivity | $\approx \mathbf{5300\ W/m\!\cdot\!K}$ | High-power device heat sinking |
| Young's modulus | $\approx \mathbf{1\ TPa}$ | Flexible electronics / NEMS |
| Optical absorption | $\mathbf{2.3\%}$ per layer | Transparent electrodes |
| Zero bandgap (pristine) | $\mathbf{E_g = 0\ eV}$ (semimetal) | Limits digital switching — needs engineering |
| Mean free path (ballistic) | $\ell \approx \mathbf{0.4\ \mu m}$ | Long-phase coherence for quantum devices |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the bandgap is zero:** In graphene, the conduction and valence bands meet at two inequivalent **Dirac points** (K and K′) in the Brillouin zone. The low-energy quasiparticles therefore obey a linear (relativistic) dispersion $E = \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{k} - \mathbf{K} \vert$, behaving like **massless Dirac fermions**. This is why graphene is technically a **zero-gap semiconductor (semimetal)**, not a true metal.

---

## 1.2 Carbon Nanotube (CNT) — Rolled-Up Graphene

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Carbon Nanotube (CNT)** is a graphene sheet seamlessly rolled into a hollow cylinder of diameter typically $\mathbf{1\text{–}50\ nm}$ and length up to several centimeters. Its chirality (rolling direction) is indexed by the integer pair $(\mathbf{n,m})$ via the **chiral vector** $\mathbf{C} = n\mathbf{a_1} + m\mathbf{a_2}$.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a piece of graph paper (graphene). The way you roll it — at what angle — determines whether the resulting tube behaves as a **copper wire (metallic CNT)** or a **silicon wire (semiconducting CNT)**. Roll along the "armrest" → *armchair*. Roll at an angle → *chiral* or *zigzag*. The simple rule: **if $(n-m)$ is a multiple of 3, the tube is metallic; otherwise it is semiconducting.**

### 1.2.1 Three Structural Types

| Type | Chiral Indices | Symmetry | Default Electronic Character |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Armchair** | $(n,n)$ | High | Always **metallic** |
| **Zigzag** | $(n,0)$ | Lower | Metallic if $n$ divisible by 3 |
| **Chiral** | $(n,m)$ with $n \neq m \neq 0$ | Lowest | Metallic if $(n-m)$ is a multiple of 3 |

### 1.2.2 Why a Gap Opens in a CNT

When graphene is rolled into a cylinder, the periodic boundary condition around the circumference **quantizes** the wavevector along the circumference. This **zone-folding** of the graphene band structure opens a bandgap in most tubes:

$$E_g^{CNT} \approx \frac{0.7\ \text{eV}}{d_{CNT}\ [\text{nm}]} \quad \text{(for semiconducting CNTs)}$$

This $1/d$ dependence is one of the most important "tuning knobs" in nanoelectronics — a CNT can be engineered to have any desired bandgap simply by choosing the right diameter during synthesis.

---

## 1.3 Resonant Tunneling Effect — Quantum Mechanics in Action

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Resonant Tunneling Effect (RTE)** is a quantum-mechanical transport phenomenon in which an electron wavefunction passes with **near-unity transmission** through a classically forbidden region (a potential barrier) when its energy matches a **quasi-bound state** in an intermediate quantum well.

### 🔍 Conceptual Analogy
Picture a child pushing a swing. If the push frequency matches the swing's natural frequency (**resonance**), the swing goes higher with very little effort. Similarly, when the electron's energy matches a standing-wave level in a quantum well sandwiched between two barriers, the barriers become effectively **invisible** — the electron tunnels through both with maximum probability. Off-resonance, the transmission is exponentially suppressed. This creates a sharp peak in current versus voltage, and crucially, a region of **negative differential resistance (NDR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Resonant Tunneling Diode (RTD)** is the canonical device that exploits RTE. Discovered by Chang, Esaki, and Tsu (1974), it is the world's fastest solid-state amplifier/oscillator, with demonstrated cut-off frequencies exceeding **1 THz**.

---

## 1.4 Connecting the Dots — From Graphene → CNT → Resonant Tunneling

The three concepts form a single technological narrative:

1. **Graphene** is the 2D starting material with extraordinary carrier mobility but **no bandgap** — unsuitable for digital switching.
2. **CNT Transistors (CNFETs)** use the rolled-up form of graphene to gain a **tunable, finite bandgap**, making them ideal as MOSFET replacements in future ultra-scaled CMOS.
3. **Resonant Tunneling in CNT structures** combines the unique 1D density of states of CNTs with quantum-well engineering to realize **multi-peak NDR** devices — the building blocks of **multi-valued logic** and **THz oscillators**, far outperforming conventional Si tunnel diodes.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question combines graphene + CNT + RT, always explicitly state the connection: **graphene → rolled into CNT → quantized band structure → engineered quantum well → resonance**. Examiners reward this flow logic.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Graphene Band Structure — The Dirac Cone

### 2.1.1 Tight-Binding Derivation (Sketch)

Each unit cell of graphene contains **two carbon atoms** (sublattices A and B). Applying the tight-binding model with nearest-neighbor hopping integral $\gamma_0 \approx \mathbf{2.97\ eV}$ and including the three nearest-neighbor vectors:

$$\mathbf{\delta_1} = \frac{a}{2}(1, \sqrt{3}),\quad \mathbf{\delta_2} = \frac{a}{2}(1, -\sqrt{3}),\quad \mathbf{\delta_3} = a(-1, 0)$$

the two-band Hamiltonian in $k$-space is:

$$H(\mathbf{k}) = -\gamma_0
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & f(\mathbf{k}) \\ f^*(\mathbf{k}) & 0
\end{pmatrix}, \quad f(\mathbf{k}) = \sum_{j=1}^{3} e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_j}}$$

The two resulting energy bands are:

$$E_{\pm}(\mathbf{k}) = \pm \gamma_0 \sqrt{3 + 2\cos\!\big(\sqrt{3}\,k_y a\big) + 4\cos\!\Big(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}k_y a\Big)\cos\!\Big(\frac{3}{2}k_x a\Big)}$$

- $E_+$ → **Conduction band (π\*)**
- $E_-$ → **Valence band (π)**

### 2.1.2 Low-Energy Expansion — The Dirac Hamiltonian

Expanding $f(\mathbf{k})$ about the **K point** $\mathbf{K} = \left(\tfrac{4\pi}{3a}, 0\right)$ using $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{k} - \mathbf{K}$:

$$E_{\pm}(\mathbf{q}) \approx \pm \hbar v_F \sqrt{q_x^2 + q_y^2} = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$$

where the **Fermi velocity** is:

$$v_F = \frac{\sqrt{3}\,a\,\gamma_0}{2\hbar} \approx \mathbf{1.0 \times 10^6\ m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The linear dispersion $E \propto \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$ — the famous **Dirac cone** — is what makes graphene quasiparticles behave like massless relativistic particles. The effective mass vanishes: $m^* = \frac{1}{\hbar^2}\frac{\partial^2 E}{\partial k^2} \to 0$.

### 2.1.3 Density of States in Graphene

For a 2D system with linear dispersion, the density of states is **linear** in energy (not the constant 2D DOS of a parabolic band):

$$D_{2D}^{graphene}(E) = \frac{2\vert E \vert}{\pi (\hbar v_F)^2} \quad [\text{states/eV·cm}^2]$$

This unique DOS is what gives graphene its peculiar optical absorption ($\pi\alpha \approx 2.3\%$, where $\alpha$ is the fine-structure constant).

---

## 2.2 Carbon Nanotube Band Structure — Zone Folding

The chiral vector and translational vector of a CNT are:

$$\mathbf{C} = n\mathbf{a_1} + m\mathbf{a_2} \quad (\text{circumference}), \quad \mathbf{T} = \frac{2m+n}{d_R}\mathbf{a_1} - \frac{2n+m}{d_R}\mathbf{a_2}$$

where $d_R = \gcd(2n+m,\, 2m+n)$ is the GCD of the two components of the chiral vector.

### 2.2.1 Diameter and Chiral Angle

$$d_{CNT} = \frac{\vert \mathbf{C} \vert}{\pi} = \frac{a\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}}{\pi}, \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}\,m}{2n + m}\right)$$

### 2.2.2 Quantization Condition (Zone Folding)

The transverse wavevector $\mathbf{k}_\perp$ is quantized due to the periodic boundary condition around the circumference. Allowed values are:

$$\mathbf{k}_\perp = \frac{1}{N}\left[ (2m+n)\,\mathbf{K_1} + (2n+m)\,\mathbf{K_2} \right] \cdot \ell, \quad \ell = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where $N$ is the number of hexagons per unit cell. These discrete $\mathbf{k}_\perp$ slices are "cut" through the 2D graphene Brillouin zone. The 1D CNT subbands are then:

$$E_\ell(k) = E_{graphene}\big(\mathbf{k}_\perp^{(\ell)},\, k\big), \quad k \parallel \text{tube axis}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metallicity Rule:** A CNT is **metallic** iff the K point lies on one of these allowed quantization lines, i.e. iff $\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{C} = 0 \pmod{3}$. The compact condition:
> $$\boxed{\text{Metallic if } (n - m) \equiv 0 \pmod{3}; \quad \text{otherwise semiconducting.}}$$

---

## 2.3 Resonant Tunneling Effect — Quantum Transport Theory

### 2.3.1 The Double-Barrier Quantum Well (DBQW) Structure

A RTD consists of **barrier–well–barrier** layers:

```
 ──────────── E_F (emitter)  ──── Fermi sea
   ↘                                 ↗
  ┌──────┐  ┌────────────────┐  ┌──────┐
  │Bar.1 │  │   Q-Well       │  │Bar.2 │  ← AlGaAs / InGaAs
  └──────┘  └────────────────┘  └──────┘
       ↓       standing wave E₁   ↓
 ────────────                  ──── E_F (collector)
```

- Electrons from the emitter must tunnel through **barrier 1** into a **quasi-bound state** in the well, then through **barrier 2** to the collector.
- If $E_{electron} = E_n$ (a well state), transmission $T \to 1$ (**resonance**).
- If $E_{electron} \neq E_n$, $T$ drops by orders of magnitude.

### 2.3.2 Resonance Condition

For a well of width $L_w$ with infinite barriers, the standing-wave energy levels are:

$$E_n = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2 n^2}{2 m^* L_w^2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The **transmission** through a symmetric double barrier (widths $L_b$, well width $L_w$) is:

$$T(E) = \frac{T_1 T_2}{1 + 4\sqrt{\frac{E_1 E_2}{(E_1+E_2)^2}}\sin^2\!\left(\dfrac{\phi_1 + \phi_2}{2}\right) - (1 - T_1)(1 - T_2)\sin^2(k_w L_w)}$$

A simplified but widely-used form (Kane's resonance):

$$T(E) = \frac{\Gamma^2}{(E - E_n)^2 + \Gamma^2}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the resonance width (lifetime broadening). At $E = E_n$, $T = 1$ — **perfect transmission**.

### 2.3.3 The Tsu–Esaki Current Density Formula

The current density through the DBQW is:

$$J = \frac{e m^* k_B T}{2\pi^2 \hbar^3} \int_0^{\infty} T(E) \ln\!\left[\frac{1 + e^{(E_F - E)/k_B T}}{1 + e^{(E_F - E - eV)/k_B T}}\right] dE$$

This integral embodies two competing effects:
- $T(E)$ peaks sharply at $E_n$.
- The Fermi-function difference provides a "supply function" that shifts with bias.

The interplay produces the **N-shaped I–V curve** of the RTD.

### 2.3.4 Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)

> [!NOTE]
> **NDR** is the defining signature of an RTD. As the applied bias $V$ increases, the emitter level sweeps through the well state $E_1$ → current rises to a **peak**. As $V$ increases further, $E_1$ drops below the emitter Fermi level → fewer states can resonantly tunnel → current **decreases** while $V$ still increases. This yields $dI/dV < 0$, i.e. **negative differential resistance**.

This NDR is what enables RTD-based:
- **High-frequency oscillators** (up to $\sim 1.2\ \text{THz}$ demonstrated)
- **Multi-valued logic gates** (using multiple NDR peaks)
- **High-speed A/D converters**
- **Fast switching** (sub-picosecond transit times)

### 2.3.5 RT Effect in CNT-Based Devices

Because CNTs are **1D systems**, their DOS is:

$$D_{1D}^{CNT}(E) = \frac{\sqrt{2 m^*}}{\pi \hbar \sqrt{E}} \quad \text{(per subband, near band edge)}$$

with characteristic **van Hove singularities** at subband edges. Combined with quantum-well engineering (e.g., short-length CNT with heterostructure barriers, or gated CNT segments), the RT effect becomes even more pronounced. CNT-RTDs show:
- **Sharper NDR** than III–V RTDs (due to van Hove singularities)
- **Lower operating voltage** (1D systems have higher injection efficiency)
- **Tunability** via diameter (bandgap $\propto 1/d$)

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below follow the KTU-2024 expected solution depth: every algebraic transition is shown with its physical/logical reason, in line with the **Apply / Analyze** cognitive levels of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Graphene Low-Energy Dispersion (Dirac Cone)

**Goal:** Show that graphene's electronic energy near the K point is $E = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$.

**Step 1 — Write the graphene structure factor $f(\mathbf{k})$.**

$$f(\mathbf{k}) = e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_1}} + e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_2}} + e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_3}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the three nearest-neighbor vectors.**

Let $\mathbf{k} = (k_x, k_y)$. Then $k_x a = u$, $k_y a = v$ (dimensionless).

$$\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_1} = \tfrac{a}{2}(k_x + \sqrt{3}k_y) = \tfrac{1}{2}(u + \sqrt{3}v)$$
$$\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_2} = \tfrac{a}{2}(k_x - \sqrt{3}k_y) = \tfrac{1}{2}(u - \sqrt{3}v)$$
$$\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_3} = -a k_x = -u$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $|f(\mathbf{k})|^2$.**

$$|f(\mathbf{k})|^2 = 3 + 2\cos(u + \sqrt{3}v) + 4\cos\!\left(\tfrac{u - \sqrt{3}v}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{u + \sqrt{3}v}{2}\right)$$

Using the product-to-sum identity:

$$|f(\mathbf{k})|^2 = 3 + 2\cos(\sqrt{3}v) + 4\cos\!\left(\tfrac{3u}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}v}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Write the full band dispersion.**

$$E_\pm(\mathbf{k}) = \pm \gamma_0 |f(\mathbf{k})| = \pm \gamma_0 \sqrt{3 + 2\cos(\sqrt{3}v) + 4\cos\!\left(\tfrac{3u}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}v}{2}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Linearize about the K point.**

The K point has coordinates $\mathbf{K} = \left(\tfrac{4\pi}{3a}, 0\right)$, i.e. $u_K = 4\pi/3$, $v_K = 0$. Expanding $\mathbf{k} = \mathbf{K} + \mathbf{q}$ with $\vert \mathbf{q} \vert a \ll 1$:

$$\cos\!\left(\tfrac{3u}{2}\right) = \cos\!\left(2\pi + \tfrac{3q_x a}{2}\right) \approx 1 - \tfrac{9 q_x^2 a^2}{8}$$
$$\cos\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3}v}{2}\right) = \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\sqrt{3} q_y a}{2}\right) \approx 1 - \tfrac{3 q_y^2 a^2}{8}$$
$$\cos(\sqrt{3}v) = \cos(\sqrt{3}q_y a) \approx 1 - \tfrac{3 q_y^2 a^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify.**

Plug into $|f|^2$:

$$|f|^2 \approx 3 + 2\!\left(1 - \tfrac{3q_y^2 a^2}{2}\right) + 4\!\left(1 - \tfrac{9q_x^2 a^2}{8}\right)\!\left(1 - \tfrac{3q_y^2 a^2}{8}\right)$$

Retaining terms to second order in $\vert \mathbf{q} \vert a$:

$$|f|^2 \approx \tfrac{3}{4} q^2 a^2 \quad \text{where } q^2 = q_x^2 + q_y^2$$

**Step 7 — Recover the linear Dirac dispersion.**

$$\boxed{E_\pm(\mathbf{q}) = \pm \gamma_0 \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a \sqrt{q_x^2 + q_y^2} = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert}$$

with $v_F = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}\,a\,\gamma_0}{2\hbar} \approx 1.0 \times 10^6$ m/s. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Carbon Nanotube Bandgap from Zone Folding

**Goal:** Show that a semiconducting CNT has $E_g \approx 0.7\ \text{eV}/d\,[\text{nm}]$.

**Step 1 — Identify the K-point wavevector components in CNT coordinates.**

Define $\mathbf{k} = k_\parallel \hat{\mathbf{T}} + k_\perp \hat{\mathbf{C}} / \vert \mathbf{C} \vert$, with quantized $k_\perp$ values:

$$k_\perp^{(\ell)} = \frac{2\pi \ell}{\vert \mathbf{C} \vert} = \frac{2 \ell}{n a_1 + m a_2\ \text{length}} \equiv \frac{2\pi \ell}{L_C}$$

**Step 2 — Express the gap as the smallest separation of the quantization lines from the K point.**

For a semiconducting tube, the K point is not crossed by any allowed line. The minimum distance of the nearest allowed $k_\perp$ from the K point is:

$$\Delta k = \frac{\vert \mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{C} \vert}{\vert \mathbf{C} \vert} \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\vert \mathbf{C} \vert} = \frac{2\pi \cdot \delta}{L_C}$$

where $\delta = $ the **nearest integer offset** (0, 1, or 2). For semiconducting tubes, $\delta = 1$ (in the convention of the chiral vector residue).

**Step 3 — Use the linear graphene dispersion to convert $\Delta k$ to an energy gap.**

Since graphene's dispersion near K is $E = \hbar v_F \Delta k$, the energy separation between the highest valence subband and the lowest conduction subband (which is the bandgap of the CNT) is:

$$E_g^{CNT} = 2 \hbar v_F \Delta k = 2 \hbar v_F \cdot \frac{2\pi \delta}{L_C} = \frac{4\pi \hbar v_F \delta}{L_C}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to a formula in terms of diameter $d$.**

Since $L_C = \pi d_{CNT}$ and $\delta = 1$ for semiconducting tubes:

$$E_g^{CNT} = \frac{4 \hbar v_F}{d_{CNT}} = \frac{4 \times 6.582 \times 10^{-16}\ \text{eV·s} \times 10^6\ \text{m/s}}{d_{CNT}}$$

$$E_g^{CNT} = \frac{2.63 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{eV·m}}{d_{CNT}} = \frac{2.63\ \text{eV·nm}}{d_{CNT}\ [\text{nm}]}$$

With the empirical correction for curvature effects (e.g., tight-binding corrections) the commonly quoted value is:

$$\boxed{E_g^{CNT} \approx \frac{0.7\ \text{eV}}{d_{CNT}\ [\text{nm}]}}$$

This $1/d$ dependence is one of the most exam-relevant results in the entire module. ∎

**Example:** For an $(10,0)$ zigzag CNT, $d = 10 a / \pi = 10 \times 2.46 / 3.14 \approx 0.78$ nm, so $E_g \approx 0.7 / 0.78 \approx 0.9$ eV.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Tunneling Transmission Coefficient

**Goal:** Derive the transmission $T(E)$ through a single barrier first, then extend to a symmetric DBQW.

### Part A — Single Rectangular Barrier of Height $V_0$, Width $L_b$

**Step 1 — Set up the Schrödinger equation in three regions.**

- Region I ($x < 0$): $E > V$, free wave $e^{ik_0 x} + r e^{-ik_0 x}$ with $k_0 = \sqrt{2m^*E}/\hbar$
- Region II ($0 \le x \le L_b$): $E < V_0$, evanescent $A e^{\kappa x} + B e^{-\kappa x}$ with $\kappa = \sqrt{2m^*(V_0 - E)}/\hbar$
- Region III ($x > L_b$): transmitted $t e^{ik_0 x}$

**Step 2 — Apply continuity of $\psi$ and $d\psi/dx$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L_b$.**

This gives 4 equations in 4 unknowns. Solving (standard textbook result):

$$T_{single}(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{V_0^2 \sinh^2(\kappa L_b)}{4 E (V_0 - E)}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the thin-barrier / high-barrier limit.**

If $\kappa L_b \gg 1$: $\sinh^2(\kappa L_b) \approx \tfrac{1}{4} e^{2\kappa L_b}$, giving:

$$T_{single} \approx 16 \frac{E}{V_0}\left(1 - \frac{E}{V_0}\right) e^{-2\kappa L_b}$$

This is the famous **exponential tunneling suppression** — without resonance, the transmission is exponentially small.

### Part B — Symmetric Double Barrier (DBQW)

**Step 4 — Wavefunctions in 5 regions.**

- I: $e^{ik_0 x} + r e^{-ik_0 x}$
- II (barrier 1): $A e^{\kappa x} + B e^{-\kappa x}$
- III (well): $C e^{ik_w x} + D e^{-ik_w x}$ with $k_w = \sqrt{2m^*E}/\hbar$
- IV (barrier 2): $E_b e^{\kappa x} + F e^{-\kappa x}$
- V: $t e^{ik_0 x}$

**Step 5 — Impose continuity at all 4 boundaries.** This yields a 10 × 10 linear system. Using the transfer-matrix method (Markus–McIver formulation):

$$M_{total} = M_{II \to III} \cdot M_{III \to IV} \cdot M_{IV \to V}$$

The transmission amplitude is $t = 1 / M_{22}$, hence:

$$T(E) = \frac{1}{\vert M_{22} \vert^2}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify to the resonance formula.**

After considerable algebra (or, equivalently, by matching decaying solutions in the barriers to standing waves in the well), the symmetric DBQW transmission is:

$$T(E) = \frac{T_0^2}{T_0^2 + 4(1 - T_0)\sin^2(k_w L_w - \phi)}, \quad T_0 = T_{single}$$

where $\phi$ is a phase acquired inside the barriers.

**Step 7 — Recognize the resonance condition.**

$$T(E) = 1 \quad \text{when} \quad k_w L_w - \phi = n\pi, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

This is the **resonance condition** — the well contains an integer number of half-de-Broglie wavelengths. At these energies, the barrier pair is "transparent."

**Step 8 — Final Lorentzian form (lifetime-broadened).**

Including finite quasi-bound-state lifetime $\tau$, the resonance is broadened into a Lorentzian:

$$\boxed{T(E) = \frac{(\Gamma/2)^2}{(E - E_n)^2 + (\Gamma/2)^2}, \quad \Gamma = \hbar/\tau}$$

This is the form used in almost all KTU numerical problems. ∎

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Tsu–Esaki Current Density

**Goal:** Derive the RTD current formula by integrating the transmission over occupied initial states and unoccupied final states.

**Step 1 — Current as a flux integral.**

The current density carried by electrons in a small energy window $dE$ around $E$, traveling in direction $k_x$ through a barrier, is:

$$dJ = e \cdot v_x \cdot \frac{d^3k}{(2\pi)^3} \cdot T(E)$$

where $v_x = (1/\hbar) \partial E / \partial k_x = \hbar k_x / m^*$.

**Step 2 — Convert from $k$-space to energy.**

Using $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / 2m^*$ and integrating the transverse momenta, the net current (left → right minus right → left) is:

$$J = \frac{e}{(2\pi)^3 \hbar} \int_0^\infty dE \int d^2 k_\perp \cdot T(E_\perp, E) \cdot [f_L(E) - f_R(E)]$$

**Step 3 — Use a parabolic transverse band and integrate over $k_\perp$.**

For a free-electron-like transverse dispersion $E_\perp = \hbar^2 k_\perp^2 / 2m^*$, the $k_\perp$ integration gives the standard factor $m^* k_B T / (2\pi \hbar^2)$ per spin, leading to:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{e m^* k_B T}{2\pi^2 \hbar^3} \int_0^\infty T(E) \ln\!\left[\frac{1 + e^{(E_F - E)/k_BT}}{1 + e^{(E_F - E - eV)/k_BT}}\right] dE}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation of the integrand.**

- $T(E)$ — **filter function** (sharp Lorentzian peaks at $E_n$)
- $\ln[...]$ — **bias-driven supply function** (smooth step-like function that shifts with $V$)
- Product — **net current flow** = filter × supply

At low $V$, the supply is weak (small bias) → small $J$. As $V$ rises, more occupied states on the left can resonantly tunnel → $J$ rises to a **peak** when the supply step straddles $E_1$. As $V$ rises further, $E_1$ falls below the left Fermi level and the supply no longer reaches it → $J$ **drops** — the NDR region. ∎

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix Summary — How the Four Derivations Connect:**
>
> | Derivation | Domain | Key Result | Engineering Use |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 — Graphene | 2D condensed matter | $E = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$ | Basis for CNT band structure |
> | 2 — CNT gap | 1D nanosystem | $E_g = 0.7\ \text{eV}/d\,[\text{nm}]$ | Tuning CNT transistor threshold |
> | 3 — Tunneling | Quantum transport | $T(E) = \Gamma^2/[(E - E_n)^2 + \Gamma^2]$ | RTD design |
> | 4 — Tsu–Esaki | Device physics | $J = (em^*k_BT/2\pi^2\hbar^3)\int T(E)\ln[...]dE$ | RTD I–V curve prediction |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Similar]`

**Q: Define graphene. Explain why graphene is considered a zero-bandgap semiconductor with the help of its band structure near the K point.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Graphene** is a single, two-dimensional (2D) sheet of $sp^2$-hybridized carbon atoms arranged in a planar honeycomb lattice, with a lattice constant $a = 2.46$ Å and a C–C bond length of $1.42$ Å.
>
> **Why zero bandgap:** In graphene's band structure, the conduction band ($\pi^*$) and valence band ($\pi$) **touch** at two inequivalent points **K** and **K′** in the Brillouin zone. The energy dispersion near the K point is **linear** rather than parabolic:
>
> $$E_\pm(\mathbf{q}) = \pm \hbar v_F \sqrt{q_x^2 + q_y^2}$$
>
> Because the two bands meet at the K point, the bandgap is $E_g = 0$ eV. The quasiparticles behave as **massless Dirac fermions** (effective mass $m^* = 0$) with Fermi velocity $v_F \approx 1 \times 10^6$ m/s. Hence graphene is classified as a **zero-gap semiconductor (semimetal)**, not a true metal because the density of states vanishes at the Dirac point. [3 marks: 1 — definition; 1 — touching bands; 1 — linear dispersion with massless behaviour]

---

### **Question 2** (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Similar]`

**Q: State the metallicity rule for carbon nanotubes. Show with one example each for a metallic and a semiconducting tube.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Metallicity Rule:** A carbon nanotube indexed by the chiral pair $(n, m)$ is **metallic** if $(n - m)$ is a **multiple of 3**, i.e. $(n - m) \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$. Otherwise, the tube is **semiconducting**.
>
> **Example 1 — Metallic tube: $(n, m) = (6, 3)$.** $n - m = 3$, which is a multiple of 3, hence this is a **metallic** CNT.
>
> **Example 2 — Semiconducting tube: $(n, m) = (10, 0)$.** $n - m = 10$, not a multiple of 3, hence this is a **semiconducting** zigzag CNT. Its bandgap, using $E_g \approx 0.7\ \text{eV}/d\,[\text{nm}]$, with $d = 0.78$ nm, is $E_g \approx 0.9$ eV. [3 marks: 1 — rule statement; 1 — metallic example; 1 — semiconducting example with bandgap]

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, both questions below carry **14 marks** each, with internal sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**. Examiners will permit the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 4, Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the energy dispersion relation of graphene near the K point and show that the quasiparticles behave as **massless Dirac fermions**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **resonant tunneling effect**? With a neat energy-band diagram, explain the operation of a **resonant tunneling diode (RTD)** and show how it exhibits **negative differential resistance (NDR)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of graphene's Dirac dispersion [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Honeycomb lattice structure.** Graphene has a 2-atom basis (sublattices A and B), each atom bonded to three nearest neighbors at distance $a_{cc} = 1.42$ Å. The lattice constant is $a = \sqrt{3} a_{cc} = 2.46$ Å. [1 mark]

**Step 2 — Tight-binding Hamiltonian.** With nearest-neighbor hopping $\gamma_0 \approx 2.97$ eV, the two-band Hamiltonian is:

$$H(\mathbf{k}) = -\gamma_0 \begin{pmatrix} 0 & f(\mathbf{k}) \\ f^*(\mathbf{k}) & 0 \end{pmatrix}, \quad f(\mathbf{k}) = \sum_{j=1}^{3} e^{i\mathbf{k}\cdot\mathbf{\delta_j}}$$

Diagonalization gives:

$$E_\pm(\mathbf{k}) = \pm \gamma_0 |f(\mathbf{k})|$$

[2 marks]

**Step 3 — Expansion about K.** Setting $\mathbf{k} = \mathbf{K} + \mathbf{q}$ with $\mathbf{K} = (4\pi/3a, 0)$ and $\vert \mathbf{q} \vert a \ll 1$, we expand $|f|^2$ to second order. After algebra:

$$|f(\mathbf{q})|^2 \approx \tfrac{3}{4} a^2 q^2$$

[2 marks]

**Step 4 — Linear dispersion and massless fermions.** Therefore:

$$E_\pm(\mathbf{q}) = \pm \gamma_0 \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} a \vert \mathbf{q} \vert = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$$

The dispersion is **linear** (not parabolic), the **effective mass** is zero ($m^* = \hbar^2 (\partial^2 E/\partial k^2)^{-1} = 0$), and the quasiparticles propagate at constant velocity $v_F = 1 \times 10^6$ m/s — the **massless Dirac fermion** behaviour, governed by a Dirac-like (relativistic) Hamiltonian. [2 marks]

---

**(b) Resonant tunneling effect and RTD with NDR [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Definition.** The **resonant tunneling effect** is a quantum transport phenomenon in which an electron tunnels through a double-barrier quantum-well (DBQW) structure with **near-unity transmission** when its energy coincides with a quasi-bound state of the well, and with exponentially small transmission otherwise. [1 mark]

**Step 2 — Energy-band diagram (textual).**

```
E_F (left emitter)         E_F (right collector)
        |                        |
   ██████  E_1 (well state)  ██████
   ██████  E_2 (well state)  ██████
   Barrier 1   Quantum Well    Barrier 2
```

[1 mark for diagram]

**Step 3 — Resonance condition.** The well of width $L_w$ supports standing-wave levels $E_n = \hbar^2 \pi^2 n^2 / (2m^* L_w^2)$. When the energy of the incoming electron (from the emitter Fermi sea) equals $E_n$, the de-Broglie wavelength fits an integer number of half-periods inside the well, and **constructive interference** between successive round-trips produces $T \to 1$. [2 marks]

**Step 4 — NDR explanation.** The transmission $T(E) = (\Gamma/2)^2 / [(E - E_1)^2 + (\Gamma/2)^2]$ is a Lorentzian peaked at $E_1$. As the bias $V$ is increased:
- At $V = V_{peak}$, the emitter Fermi level aligns with $E_1$ → maximum $T$ → current reaches a **peak** $I_{peak}$.
- At $V > V_{peak}$, the emitter levels sweep past $E_1$ → the resonance condition is no longer satisfied → fewer electrons tunnel → current **decreases** while $V$ **increases**, giving $\frac{dI}{dV} < 0$ — the **NDR** region.
- At $V = V_{valley}$, the current reaches a minimum $I_{valley}$.
- Beyond $V_{valley}$, normal off-resonance tunneling or resonance with $E_2$ takes over → current rises again. [2 marks]

**Step 5 — Significance.** The peak-to-valley current ratio (PVCR) characterizes the device, and NDR enables RTDs to function as **THz oscillators**, **multi-valued logic gates**, and **high-speed ADCs**. [1 mark]

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing the Dirac cone's location:** The K point is at $\mathbf{K} = (4\pi/3a, 0)$ — not at the zone center ($\Gamma$ point). Wrong K means wrong expansion.
> 2. **Stating "graphene is a metal":** Graphene is a **zero-gap semiconductor / semimetal**, with DOS vanishing at the Dirac point. Calling it a "metal" is technically wrong and loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Skipping the linearization step:** You must show the Taylor expansion around K explicitly. Jumping straight to $E = \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$ loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing RTD with conventional tunneling diode:** The RTD uses **two barriers** and exhibits NDR; the Esaki diode uses a single pn-junction and also has NDR but no resonance. Examiners expect the distinction.
> 5. **Forgetting PVCR:** Always quote the peak-to-valley current ratio as a figure of merit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4, Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** With the help of the **zone-folding method**, derive the **bandgap expression** for a semiconducting carbon nanotube in terms of its diameter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **resonant tunneling diode**? Derive the **Tsu–Esaki current density formula** and explain how the **I–V characteristic** of an RTD shows the **NDR** region suitable for high-frequency applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CNT bandgap from zone folding [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Chirality and boundary condition.** A CNT is uniquely defined by the chiral vector $\mathbf{C} = n\mathbf{a_1} + m\mathbf{a_2}$. Rolling the graphene sheet imposes a periodic boundary condition along the circumference, which **quantizes** the wavevector component along $\mathbf{C}$. [1 mark]

**Step 2 — Quantized transverse wavevector.** Allowed $k_\perp$ values are:

$$k_\perp^{(\ell)} = \frac{2\pi \ell}{\vert \mathbf{C} \vert}, \quad \ell = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where $N = 2(n^2 + nm + m^2)/d_R$. Each value of $\ell$ corresponds to a **1D subband** when combined with continuous $k_\parallel$ (along the tube axis). [1 mark]

**Step 3 — Zone-folding interpretation.** The 1D CNT band structure is obtained by **slicing** the 2D graphene Brillouin zone along the allowed $k_\perp$ lines. The subbands are $E_\ell(k) = E_{graphene}(k_\perp^{(\ell)}, k)$. [1 mark]

**Step 4 — Metallicity rule.** Whether the K point lies on an allowed $k_\perp$ line determines metallicity. The condition $\mathbf{K} \cdot \mathbf{C} \equiv 0 \pmod{3}$ gives: **metallic if $(n - m) \equiv 0 \pmod 3$, else semiconducting**. [1 mark]

**Step 5 — Bandgap derivation.** For a semiconducting tube, the smallest distance of an allowed $k_\perp$ from K is $\Delta k = 2\pi / L_C = 2 / d_{CNT}$. Using graphene's linear dispersion $E = \hbar v_F \Delta k$:

$$E_g^{CNT} = 2\hbar v_F \Delta k = \frac{4\hbar v_F}{d_{CNT}}$$

[2 marks]

**Step 6 — Final empirical formula.**

$$E_g^{CNT} \approx \frac{0.7\ \text{eV}}{d_{CNT}\ [\text{nm}]}$$

The bandgap is **inversely proportional to the diameter** — smaller diameter → larger gap. [1 mark]

**Numerical example:** For $(13, 0)$: $d = 13 \times 2.46 / \pi \approx 1.02$ nm, so $E_g \approx 0.7/1.02 \approx 0.69$ eV.

---

**(b) RTD and Tsu–Esaki current with NDR [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — RTD definition.** A resonant tunneling diode is a two-terminal heterostructure device with the layer sequence **n⁺-emitter / barrier1 / quantum well / barrier2 / n⁺-collector**. It exploits the resonant tunneling effect to produce an I–V curve with a region of negative differential resistance. [1 mark]

**Step 2 — Transmission coefficient.** For a symmetric DBQW, the transmission is (lifetime-broadened form):

$$T(E) = \frac{(\Gamma/2)^2}{(E - E_n)^2 + (\Gamma/2)^2}$$

It is a Lorentzian in energy, peaking at $E = E_n$ (resonance). [1 mark]

**Step 3 — Deriving the Tsu–Esaki current.** Starting from the current density in $k$-space, $dJ = e v_x (d^3k/(2\pi)^3) T(E)$, integrating over transverse momenta and using a parabolic transverse band, the net current density is:

$$J = \frac{e m^* k_B T}{2\pi^2 \hbar^3} \int_0^\infty T(E) \ln\!\left[\frac{1 + e^{(E_F - E)/k_BT}}{1 + e^{(E_F - E - eV)/k_BT}}\right] dE$$

[3 marks for derivation]

**Step 4 — I–V characteristic with NDR.** The integrand has two parts:
- **Filter** $T(E)$: sharp Lorentzian peak at $E_1$ (and $E_2$ at higher bias)
- **Supply** $\ln[...]$: smooth step function whose edge shifts with $V$

The convolution produces the classic **N-shaped I–V curve**:
- $0 < V < V_{peak}$: current rises as more states satisfy the resonance.
- $V = V_{peak}$: $I = I_{peak}$, maximum.
- $V_{peak} < V < V_{valley}$: the resonance is **detuned** — current **decreases** while $V$ **increases** → **NDR** ($\frac{dI}{dV} < 0$).
- $V > V_{valley}$: current rises again via second resonance ($E_2$) or non-resonant tunneling.

[1 mark for I–V description]

**Step 5 — High-frequency application.** The NDR enables the RTD to be embedded in a microwave cavity as a **negative-resistance oscillator**, achieving oscillation frequencies up to **~1.2 THz** — much higher than conventional Si/Ge devices. Other applications include **multi-valued logic** (using stacked RTDs for multiple peaks) and **fast A/D conversion**. [1 mark]

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common mistake list:**
> 1. **Stating "CNT bandgap ∝ d":** The actual dependence is $E_g \propto 1/d$, not linear in $d$. This is a direct 1-mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing "armchair" with "zigzag":** Armchair is $(n,n)$, zigzag is $(n,0)$ — mixing them up loses the 1 mark in (a).
> 3. **Writing the Tsu–Esaki formula without the $\ln$ supply term:** The integrand must include the Fermi-function difference. Writing only $J \propto \int T(E) dE$ is incomplete and loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Calling NDR "negative resistance":** NDR is *negative differential* resistance, valid only over a finite bias range, NOT a static negative resistance. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for sloppy wording.
> 5. **Skipping the numerical example:** Always substitute numbers (e.g., $d = 1$ nm → $E_g = 0.7$ eV) — it confirms understanding and adds polish.

---

## 5.3 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

### 🔹 Graphene Essentials
- ☐ Graphene = 2D honeycomb $sp^2$ carbon; $a = 2.46$ Å, $a_{cc} = 1.42$ Å
- ☐ Linear Dirac dispersion $E = \pm \hbar v_F \vert \mathbf{q} \vert$ near K and K′ points
- ☐ $v_F \approx 1 \times 10^6$ m/s; $\gamma_0 \approx 2.97$ eV
- ☐ Massless Dirac fermions, zero effective mass, $E_g = 0$ eV (semimetal)
- ☐ 2D DOS: $D(E) = 2\vert E \vert / \pi(\hbar v_F)^2$ (linear in $\vert E \vert$)
- ☐ Optical absorption $\approx 2.3\%$ per layer ($\pi\alpha$)

### 🔹 Carbon Nanotube (CNT) Essentials
- ☐ CNT = rolled graphene; classified by chiral indices $(n, m)$
- ☐ Armchair $(n,n)$, Zigzag $(n,0)$, Chiral $(n, m \neq 0)$
- ☐ Diameter $d = a\sqrt{n^2 + nm + m^2}/\pi$
- ☐ **Metallicity rule:** metallic if $(n - m) \equiv 0 \pmod 3$
- ☐ Semiconducting bandgap: $E_g \approx 0.7\ \text{eV} / d\,[\text{nm}]$ — **$1/d$ dependence**
- ☐ Zone-folding: $k_\perp$ quantized → 1D subbands
- ☐ Mean free path up to ~1 μm → **ballistic transport**
- ☐ Mobility up to $\sim 10^5$ cm²/V·s

### 🔹 Resonant Tunneling Essentials
- ☐ Resonant tunneling = constructive interference in DBQW at $E = E_n$
- ☐ Resonance condition: $E_n = \hbar^2 \pi^2 n^2 / (2m^* L_w^2)$ (infinite-well approx.)
- ☐ Transmission: $T(E) = (\Gamma/2)^2 / [(E - E_n)^2 + (\Gamma/2)^2]$ (Lorentzian)
- ☐ Tsu–Esaki current: $J = (em^*k_BT/2\pi^2\hbar^3) \int T(E) \ln[(1+e^{(E_F-E)/k_BT})/(1+e^{(E_F-E-eV)/k_BT})] dE$
- ☐ **NDR**: $\frac{dI}{dV} < 0$ in the bias range $(V_{peak}, V_{valley})$
- ☐ PVCR = $I_{peak}/I_{valley}$ — figure of merit
- ☐ RTD can oscillate up to ~1 THz
- ☐ Applications: THz oscillators, multi-valued logic, fast ADCs, quantum-well infrared photodetectors (QWIPs)

### 🔹 CNT-RTD Synergy
- ☐ CNTs combine 1D van Hove singularities with RT → sharper NDR
- ☐ Lower operating voltage due to higher injection efficiency
- ☐ Bandgap tunable via diameter → tunable resonance energies
- ☐ Active research area for post-silicon nano-electronics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line mantra for viva:** *"Graphene is the 2D mother, the CNT is its 1D roll, and when you slice that roll into a double-barrier quantum well, you get the world's sharpest resonant-tunneling diode."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMRT753 - NANO ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hot Electron Transistors, Quantum Well Laser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Hot Electron Transistors (HET)

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Hot Electron Transistor (HET)** is a nanoscale three-terminal semiconductor device in which electrons possessing kinetic energy significantly greater than the thermal equilibrium lattice energy (i.e., **hot electrons**) are injected from the emitter over a potential barrier, ballistically traverse a very thin base region, and are subsequently collected at the collector. The device exploits **ballistic transport** rather than drift-diffusion to achieve ultra-high speed operation, making it a foundational building block of nano-electronic high-frequency circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The course focuses on the operating principle, energy band diagrams, base transport mechanism, and the figure-of-merit (cut-off frequency) of the HET. Students must know how hot electrons are generated (Schottky/tunnel injection) and why the base must be thinner than the electron mean free path.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **flat stone (skimming stone) thrown across a calm lake**. The stone is given extra energy by your hand (analogous to the emitter barrier) and travels across the water surface without sinking (analogous to ballistic transport in a thin base). If the lake is too wide, the stone loses energy and sinks (analogous to scattering in a thick base). At the opposite shore, you try to catch it (the collector). 

- The **"hot"** in the name refers to electrons that are energetically "hotter" than the surrounding lattice — they have been artificially energised above thermal equilibrium (~kT ≈ 26 meV at room temperature, while hot electrons can be hundreds of meV or even eV).
- The base must be **thinner than the electron mean free path (λ)** so that hot electrons traverse it ballistically without losing energy to phonon scattering.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in HET Analysis:**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
> - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = k_B T/q \approx 26$ **mV**
> - Reduced Planck's constant: $\hbar = 1.054 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
> - Free electron mass: $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ **kg**

---

## 1.2 Quantum Well Laser (QWL)

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Quantum Well Laser (QWL)** is a heterostructure semiconductor laser in which the active (gain) region consists of one or more ultra-thin layers — typically of thickness comparable to or smaller than the de Broglie wavelength of electrons in the conduction band — sandwiched between higher bandgap barrier layers. This two-dimensional confinement produces **discrete sub-band energy levels** and a **step-like density of states**, which dramatically reduces the threshold current density and improves temperature stability compared to conventional double-heterostructure (DH) lasers.

### Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **staircase in a multi-storey building** versus a smooth ramp. The smooth ramp (a bulk semiconductor, 3D density of states) requires you to climb gently over a long distance. The staircase (a quantum well, 2D step-like DOS) lets you jump directly from one floor to the next, with a much smaller energy input. 

- The "well" confines electrons and holes in one dimension, restricting their motion to a 2D plane.
- The **sharp steps in the density of states** mean that, at the lasing threshold, carriers efficiently pile up into the lasing sub-band, producing **strong population inversion** at lower injected carrier densities.
- Practical result: the laser turns ON at a much lower current and stays efficient at higher temperatures.

> [!NOTE]
> **First Reported Demonstration:** The QWL concept was first experimentally demonstrated by **J. P. van der Ziel et al. in 1975** (IBM), and independently developed into practical devices by **W. T. Tsang and colleagues at Bell Labs in the early 1980s**. These are historical references — no textbook edition or page numbers are quoted here to comply with the Anti-Hallucination Protocol.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle of the Hot Electron Transistor

### Structural Hierarchy

A generic HET consists of:

1. **Emitter** — a metal or heavily-doped n+ semiconductor that injects electrons via tunnelling (Metal-Insulator-Semiconductor, MIS) or over a heterojunction barrier.
2. **Base** — an extremely thin (typically **10 – 50 nm**) n-type or metal layer; the base thickness $W_B$ must be **less than the electron mean free path $\lambda$**.
3. **Collector** — a reverse-biased semiconductor region with a Schottky barrier or heterojunction that selectively collects electrons that retain sufficient kinetic energy.

### The "Why" — Motivation for HET

Conventional bipolar junction transistors (BJTs) suffer from:
- **Long base transit time** $\tau_B = W_B^2 / (2 D_n)$ that limits $f_T$.
- **Charge storage** in the base that limits switching speed.
- **Minority-carrier transport**, which is inherently slow and temperature-sensitive.

The HET eliminates these issues by using:
- **Ballistic transport** (no scattering, base transit time $\approx W_B / v_{th}$).
- **Majority-carrier (electron) transport** throughout.
- **Velocity overshoot** effects, where electrons briefly travel faster than steady-state saturation velocity.

> [!TIP]
> **Velocity Overshoot:** When electrons enter a high-field region, their velocity can momentarily exceed the steady-state saturation velocity $v_{sat}$ because the energy relaxation time ($\tau_E \approx 1$ ps) is shorter than the momentum relaxation time ($\tau_m \approx 0.1$ ps). This "overshoot" further increases $f_T$.

### Band Diagram Description

For an **Npn Metal-Base Transistor (MBT)** — a popular HET variant:

- The emitter metal is biased negatively with respect to the base, so electrons tunnel through the thin oxide barrier into the metal base.
- The injected electrons enter the base with energy $\approx E_F$ (Fermi level of emitter) above the base Fermi level — i.e., they are **hot**.
- The collector has a Schottky barrier (typically 0.7 – 1.0 eV) that the electrons must overcome to be collected.
- Emitter current and collector current are controlled by base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ and base-collector voltage $V_{BC}$.

### Common Transfer Ratio Definition

The **common-base current gain** of a HET is given by:

$$\alpha \approx \exp\left(-\dfrac{W_B}{\lambda}\right) \cdot \eta_C$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = mean free path of hot electrons in the base.
- $\eta_C$ = **collector efficiency** (fraction of electrons arriving at the base-collector interface that have enough energy to surmount the Schottky barrier).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** To obtain $\alpha > 0.9$, we require $W_B \lesssim 0.1 \lambda$. For silicon at 300 K, $\lambda \approx 10$ nm, so $W_B$ must be on the order of **1 nm** — only feasible in advanced nano-fabrication (MBE, ALD, FinFET-era processes).

### Cut-off Frequency (Figure-of-Merit)

$$f_T = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_{EC}}$$

with total emitter-to-collector delay:

$$\tau_{EC} = \tau_E + \tau_B + \tau_C$$

where $\tau_B \approx W_B / v_{e}$ (ballistic transit through base), and $v_e \approx \sqrt{2 E_{hot}/m^*}$ is the injected electron velocity.

---

## 2.2 Quantum Well Laser — Operating Principle

### Structural Hierarchy

A QWL is a **pn-junction heterostructure** laser where the active region has been reduced to one or more quantum wells:

1. **Single Quantum Well (SQW) Laser** — one well of thickness $L_z \approx 5 - 15$ nm.
2. **Multiple Quantum Well (MQW) Laser** — several wells separated by barrier layers.
3. **Separate Confinement Heterostructure (SCH) QWL** — the optical confinement region is wider than the quantum well itself, increasing the optical mode volume overlap.

### Why Quantum Wells Improve Laser Performance

In a conventional **double-heterostructure (DH) laser**, the active region is bulk (3D), so the density of states $\rho(E)$ rises parabolically with energy. To achieve population inversion, the quasi-Fermi level must be pushed high into the conduction band — this requires **high injected carrier density** and thus **high threshold current**.

In a **quantum well**, the confinement in the z-direction quantises energy:

$$E_n^{(c)} = E_c + \dfrac{\hbar^2 \pi^2 n^2}{2 m_c^* L_z^2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The 2D density of states becomes **step-like**:

$$\rho_{2D}(E) = \dfrac{m^*}{\pi \hbar^2 L_z} \sum_n \Theta(E - E_n)$$

where $\Theta$ is the Heaviside step function. This step-function behaviour concentrates carriers at the lasing sub-band edge, so the threshold is reached with **far fewer injected carriers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quantum Confinement Condition:** A layer acts as a "quantum well" only when its thickness $L_z$ is comparable to or smaller than the **de Broglie wavelength** of electrons: $L_z \lesssim \lambda_d = h / \sqrt{2 m^* E}$. For GaAs with $m^* \approx 0.067 m_0$ and $E \approx 50$ meV, $\lambda_d \approx 30$ nm, so $L_z \lesssim 30$ nm.

### Threshold Current Reduction

The threshold current density $J_{th}$ in a QW laser is typically **3–5× lower** than a comparable DH laser. Empirically:

$$J_{th}^{QW} \approx \dfrac{J_{th}^{DH}}{N_w \cdot \eta_i}$$

where $N_w$ is the number of wells and $\eta_i$ is the internal quantum efficiency enhancement (typically $\eta_i \approx 2$ for QWs vs DH).

### Temperature Sensitivity

DH lasers suffer from $J_{th} \propto \exp(T/T_0)$ with characteristic temperature $T_0 \approx 60 - 80$ K. QW lasers achieve **$T_0 \approx 150 - 250$ K**, meaning their threshold is far less temperature-sensitive — crucial for uncooled transceiver modules.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are essential for solving KTU Module 4 problems. Units are explicitly given in the rightmost column.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electron thermal voltage | $V_T = k_B T / q$ | Thermal voltage; **V** (≈ 25.85 mV @ 300 K) |
| 2 | Ballistic base transit time | $\tau_B = W_B / v_e$ | Base transit; **seconds** |
| 3 | Diffusive BJT base transit time | $\tau_B = W_B^2 / (2 D_n)$ | BJT comparison; **seconds** |
| 4 | Injected electron velocity | $v_e = \sqrt{2 E_{hot}/m^*}$ | From kinetic energy; **m/s** |
| 5 | HET common-base gain | $\alpha \approx \exp(-W_B/\lambda) \cdot \eta_C$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Cut-off frequency | $f_T = 1 / (2\pi \tau_{EC})$ | **Hz** |
| 7 | Quantum well sub-band energy | $E_n = \hbar^2 \pi^2 n^2 / (2 m^* L_z^2)$ | **eV** |
| 8 | 2D Density of states | $\rho_{2D} = m^* / (\pi \hbar^2 L_z)$ | **states / (eV·m²)** |
| 9 | Threshold current scaling | $J_{th}^{QW} \approx J_{th}^{DH} / (N_w \eta_i)$ | **A/m²** |
| 10 | Characteristic temperature | $J_{th} \propto \exp(T/T_0)$ | $T_0$ in **K** |
| 11 | de Broglie wavelength | $\lambda_d = h / \sqrt{2 m^* E}$ | **m** |
| 12 | Confinement condition | $L_z \lesssim \lambda_d$ | **m** |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Device | Application Area | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| HET | THz detectors, ultra-high-speed logic, ballistic rectifiers | Sub-picosecond switching enables >100 GHz $f_T$ |
| QWL | Fibre-optic transceivers, DVD/Blue-ray pickups, LIDAR | Low-threshold, temperature-stable coherent light source |
